questions.stem: APAP toxicity causes liver damage via:

choices.feedback: APAP itself is not directly toxic.

choices.text: NAPQI accumulation when glutathione is depleted
choices.feedback: Correct! CYP2E1 converts APAP → NAPQI (toxic). Normally, glutathione neutralizes NAPQI. In overdose: glutathione depleted → NAPQI accumulates → centrilobular hepatic necrosis (zone 3). NAC works by replenishing glutathione stores and providing alternative substrate for NAPQI conjugation.
APAP does not cause biliary obstruction.
APAP toxicity is direct, not autoimmune.

questions.stem: APAP overdose: patient feels fine at 12 hours. Is this reassuring?

choices.text: Yes — symptoms would have appeared by now
choices.feedback: Dangerously false! Stage I (0-24h) is deceptively asymptomatic.

choices.text: No — Stage I is asymptomatic despite ongoing hepatic damage
choices.feedback: Correct! The 'deceptive well period' is a hallmark of APAP toxicity. Patients feel well during Stage I (0-24h) while NAPQI is silently destroying hepatocytes. By Stage II (24-72h), AST/ALT spike and RUQ pain develops. This is why the nomogram and early NAC are critical — don't wait for symptoms.

choices.text: Only reassuring if liver enzymes are checked
choices.feedback: Liver enzymes may be normal in Stage I despite ongoing damage. APAP level and nomogram guide treatment.

choices.text: Yes, if the ingestion was less than 10g
choices.feedback: Even at 7.5g (150 mg/kg), hepatotoxicity can develop.

questions.stem: NAC is most effective when given within:

choices.feedback: While earlier is always better, the critical window is wider.

Correct! NAC within 8 hours → nearly 100% prevention of hepatotoxicity. Still beneficial after 8h (reduces severity). Even in established liver failure (Stage III), NAC improves outcomes and reduces mortality. Never 'too late' to give NAC if there's evidence of toxicity. Key: don't delay NAC waiting for nomogram results if >8h have passed.
While NAC has benefit even late, efficacy drops significantly after 8h.
NAC works by multiple mechanisms including replenishing glutathione even after NAPQI has formed.

questions.stem: The Rumack-Matthew nomogram is NOT valid for:

choices.text: Single acute ingestions with known time
choices.feedback: This is exactly what the nomogram IS designed for.

choices.text: Chronic/repeated ingestions or unknown time of ingestion
choices.feedback: Correct! The nomogram is only valid for single acute ingestions with known time (plot level at 4-24h post-ingestion). Invalid for: chronic/repeated dosing, unknown time, extended-release APAP, co-ingestion delaying absorption. In these cases → treat empirically with NAC if any concern for significant ingestion.
The nomogram applies regardless of reported dose.
Age doesn't affect nomogram validity.

questions.stem: Which zone of the liver is most affected in APAP toxicity?

choices.feedback: Zone 1 is affected in phosphorus poisoning and eclampsia.

Correct! Zone 3 (centrilobular, around central vein) has the highest concentration of CYP2E1 → most NAPQI production → greatest oxidative damage. Pathology: centrilobular hepatic necrosis. AST/ALT can exceed 10,000 IU/L (very high transaminases are characteristic of APAP toxicity and ischemic hepatitis).
Zone 2 is the least commonly affected in toxicology.
APAP toxicity has a specific centrilobular (zone 3) pattern.

questions.stem: King's College Criteria for liver transplant in APAP-induced ALF include:

choices.feedback: High AST alone doesn't meet criteria.

choices.text: pH <7.30 after resuscitation, OR: INR >6.5 + Cr >3.4 + grade III-IV encephalopathy
choices.feedback: Correct! King's College Criteria for APAP-ALF: arterial pH <7.30 (refractory acidosis despite resuscitation) is independently sufficient. OR all three of: PT >100s (INR >6.5), Cr >3.4, grade III-IV encephalopathy. Meeting criteria → urgent transplant evaluation.
APAP level doesn't directly predict need for transplant.
Age is a factor in non-APAP King's criteria but not the primary APAP criteria.

questions.stem: Chronic alcoholics have increased APAP toxicity risk because:

choices.feedback: Alcohol doesn't significantly increase APAP absorption.

choices.text: CYP2E1 induction increases NAPQI production + chronic glutathione depletion
choices.feedback: Correct! Chronic alcohol: CYP2E1 upregulation (more NAPQI produced) + chronic malnutrition depletes glutathione → double hit. Toxicity can occur at lower APAP doses (<4g/day). Paradoxically, acute alcohol co-ingestion may be protective (competes for CYP2E1). Chronic use is the risk factor.
NAC metabolism is not significantly affected by alcohol use.
The key mechanism is CYP2E1 induction and glutathione depletion.

questions.stem: Anaphylactoid reaction during IV NAC should be managed by:

choices.feedback: Do not permanently discontinue — the benefit of NAC outweighs anaphylactoid risk.

choices.text: Slowing the infusion rate and giving antihistamines
choices.feedback: Correct! IV NAC anaphylactoid reactions (flushing, urticaria, bronchospasm) are rate-related (most common during loading dose). Management: pause or slow infusion, give diphenhydramine ± albuterol for bronchospasm, then restart at slower rate. True IgE-mediated anaphylaxis to NAC is extremely rare. Do not withhold NAC for anaphylactoid symptoms.
Charcoal is for GI decontamination, not a substitute for NAC.

choices.text: Giving epinephrine and stopping all treatment
choices.feedback: Epinephrine may be needed for severe reactions, but NAC should be continued.

questions.stem: What differentiates NSTEMI from unstable angina?

choices.feedback: ST elevation defines STEMI. Both NSTEMI and UA can show ST depression or T-wave changes.
Correct! Troponin elevation = myocardial necrosis = NSTEMI. UA has similar symptoms/EKG but negative troponin (no cell death). High-sensitivity troponin detectable within 1-3 hours.
Both UA and NSTEMI can present with chest pain at rest.
Both conditions involve CAD with plaque instability.

questions.stem: Door-to-balloon time goal for STEMI?

choices.feedback: 30 min is the door-to-needle goal for fibrinolytics.

Correct! PCI door-to-balloon ≤90 min (≤120 min if transfer to PCI-capable facility). If PCI not available within 120 min, give fibrinolytics (door-to-needle ≤30 min). Every minute of delay increases mortality.
While benefit exists up to 12h, the target is ≤90 min.
Far too long. Rapid reperfusion is critical.

questions.stem: Inferior STEMI with hypotension, clear lungs, and JVD. What should be AVOIDED?

choices.feedback: IV fluids are the CORRECT treatment — RV infarct is preload-dependent.

Correct! RV infarct = preload-dependent. Nitroglycerin causes venodilation → decreased preload → hemodynamic collapse. Also avoid morphine and diuretics. Treatment: IV fluids to maintain RV preload. Recognize by: inferior STEMI + hypotension + clear lungs + JVD. Confirm with right-sided EKG (ST elevation V4R).
Aspirin should be given to all ACS patients regardless of RV involvement.
Anticoagulation is still indicated in ACS with RV infarct.

questions.stem: Most IMPORTANT initial medication in ACS?

choices.feedback: Morphine is used cautiously for refractory pain only. Evidence suggests it may worsen outcomes.

Correct! Aspirin is the single most important initial therapy. 325 mg CHEWED (faster absorption vs swallowed). Irreversibly inhibits COX-1 → blocks thromboxane A2 → inhibits platelet aggregation. Reduces mortality by 23% (ISIS-2 trial). Give to ALL suspected ACS unless true allergy.
Nitroglycerin relieves ischemic symptoms but doesn't reduce mortality like aspirin.
P2Y12 inhibitors are important adjuncts but aspirin is the first-line priority.

questions.stem: New harsh holosystolic murmur with thrill on day 4 post-MI. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Papillary muscle rupture causes acute MR (holosystolic murmur at apex radiating to axilla) but typically without a palpable thrill.

Correct! Post-MI VSD presents day 3-5 with a new harsh holosystolic murmur at the left sternal border WITH a palpable thrill (key distinguishing feature from MR). Causes acute left-to-right shunt → heart failure. Diagnosis: echocardiogram shows septal defect with color flow. Treatment: emergent surgical repair.
Dressler = pericarditis (friction rub, fever) occurring weeks post-MI, not a murmur with thrill.
AS causes a systolic crescendo-decrescendo murmur, not a new holosystolic murmur post-MI.

questions.stem: Anterior wall STEMI involves which coronary artery?

choices.feedback: RCA supplies the inferior wall (leads II, III, aVF) in 85% of patients.

Correct! LAD supplies the anterior wall. Anterior STEMI (leads V1-V4) = LAD occlusion. The LAD is called the 'widow-maker' because anterior MI carries the highest mortality and risk of cardiogenic shock/LV dysfunction.
LCx supplies the lateral wall (leads I, aVL, V5-V6).
Left main occlusion is usually rapidly fatal or presents as massive anterior + lateral STEMI.

questions.stem: Fibrinolytics are CONTRAINDICATED in which situation?

choices.feedback: This is an indication, not a contraindication.

Correct! Absolute contraindications to fibrinolytics: aortic dissection (catastrophic hemorrhage), active internal bleeding, prior intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke <3 months, brain tumor, significant head trauma <3 months. Aortic dissection can mimic STEMI (inferior MI from RCA involvement) — always consider before giving lytics.
Advanced age increases bleeding risk but is not an absolute contraindication.
Prior PCI is not a contraindication to fibrinolytics.

questions.stem: Post-ACS, dual antiplatelet therapy (DAPT) should continue for:

choices.feedback: Too short — premature discontinuation increases stent thrombosis risk.

Correct! Standard DAPT (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor) for 12 months post-ACS, regardless of whether PCI/stent was performed. After 12 months, continue aspirin indefinitely. P2Y12 options: ticagrelor (preferred — reversible, superior to clopidogrel per PLATO trial) or clopidogrel. May shorten to 6 months if high bleeding risk.
Lifelong aspirin monotherapy, but DAPT is typically limited to 12 months due to bleeding risk.
Stopping DAPT at discharge dramatically increases recurrent events.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old has periumbilical pain migrating to RLQ over 12 hours, with anorexia and low-grade fever. CT confirms appendicitis. Treatment?

choices.feedback: Antibiotics alone may be considered in select uncomplicated cases, but appendectomy remains standard of care.
Correct! Appendectomy (laparoscopic preferred) is standard for acute appendicitis. Classic migration pattern + CT confirmation warrants surgery. Delay >48-72h increases perforation risk.
CT-confirmed appendicitis warrants surgical intervention, not continued observation.
Colonoscopy has no role in acute appendicitis and risks perforation.

questions.stem: Elderly patient with A-fib has severe abdominal pain but a soft, non-tender abdomen with elevated lactate. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Cholecystitis causes RUQ tenderness and Murphy sign. Pain out of proportion to exam suggests mesenteric ischemia.

Correct! Classic: pain OUT OF PROPORTION to exam + A-fib (embolic source) + elevated lactate. Abdomen surprisingly benign despite severe pain. CT angiography diagnostic. Vascular emergency with 60-80% mortality.
IBS does not cause elevated lactate or acute severe pain in this context.
PUD causes epigastric tenderness. The exam-pain discrepancy is hallmark of mesenteric ischemia.

questions.stem: Free air under the diaphragm on upright CXR indicates:

choices.feedback: Pneumonia shows infiltrates, not subdiaphragmatic free air.
Correct! Pneumoperitoneum = perforation of a hollow viscus. Surgical emergency requiring laparotomy. Most common cause: perforated peptic ulcer.
SBO shows air-fluid levels and dilated loops, not subdiaphragmatic free air.
Appendicitis does not typically cause pneumoperitoneum.

questions.stem: In a reproductive-age female with acute abdominal pain, what test should ALWAYS be obtained first?

choices.feedback: CT involves radiation. Must exclude pregnancy first — ectopic is life-threatening and changes the entire approach.

Correct! ALWAYS obtain β-hCG in reproductive-age females with abdominal pain. Ectopic pregnancy can rupture and is a surgical emergency. A positive test changes differential and imaging approach entirely.
Lipase evaluates pancreatitis but doesn't address the critical need to exclude ectopic pregnancy.
UA helps evaluate UTI/stones but doesn't rule out ectopic pregnancy.

questions.stem: Most RELIABLE physical exam finding indicating peritonitis?

choices.feedback: Rebound is suggestive but less reliable and can be unreliable.
Correct! Involuntary guarding = reflex contraction of abdominal muscles from peritoneal inflammation. Cannot be overcome by distraction. Most reliable sign of peritonitis.
Hyperactive sounds suggest early obstruction. Peritonitis causes absent/hypoactive sounds.
Localized tenderness is less specific than involuntary guarding for generalized peritonitis.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old with sudden back pain, hypotension, and pulsatile abdominal mass. Immediate management?

choices.feedback: Do NOT delay for imaging in an unstable patient with classic ruptured AAA triad.
Correct! Hypotension + pulsatile mass + back pain = ruptured AAA. Unstable → emergent OR. Mortality 80-90% untreated.
Ruptured AAA requires surgery, not observation.
In unstable patients with classic presentation, proceed to OR without imaging delay.

questions.stem: Rovsing, psoas, and obturator signs are associated with:

choices.feedback: Cholecystitis has Murphy sign, not these.
Correct! Rovsing = RLQ pain with LLQ palpation. Psoas = pain with hip extension (retrocecal appendix). Obturator = pain with internal rotation of flexed hip (pelvic appendix).
Diverticulitis causes LLQ pain but these signs are specifically appendicitis signs.
Pancreatitis has Cullen and Grey Turner signs, not these.

questions.stem: LLQ pain in elderly patients is most commonly caused by:

choices.feedback: Volvulus causes obstruction with distension and coffee-bean sign, not typical acute LLQ pain.

Correct! Diverticulitis is the #1 cause of LLQ pain in elderly. CT shows pericolic fat stranding, wall thickening, ± abscess. Uncomplicated: antibiotics + bowel rest. Complicated: percutaneous drainage or surgery.
True left-sided appendicitis only occurs in situs inversus totalis (extremely rare).
Ovarian torsion affects younger women, not typically elderly patients.

questions.stem: A 3-year-old presents with bone pain, bruising, and hepatosplenomegaly. CBC shows WBC 45,000 with 80% blasts that are TdT+, CD10+, and PAS+. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: AML blasts would be MPO+, Auer rods, TdT-negative. This pattern is lymphoid.
Correct! TdT+ (immature lymphoid) + CD10+ (CALLA/common ALL antigen) + PAS+ = B-cell ALL. This is the most common pediatric cancer with peak age 2-5.
T-cell ALL would be CD3/CD7+ and often presents with mediastinal mass. CD10+ points to B-cell.
CML blast crisis occurs in adults with preceding chronic phase. This is a child with de novo disease.

questions.stem: A patient with newly diagnosed AML presents with DIC and scattered Auer rods forming bundles ('faggot cells'). What translocation is expected, and what treatment must be started immediately?

choices.feedback: t(8;21) is M2 AML with good prognosis but doesn't typically present with DIC and faggot cells.

Correct! APL (M3) = t(15;17) PML-RARA with DIC and faggot cells (bundles of Auer rods). ATRA must be started IMMEDIATELY upon clinical suspicion — do NOT wait for genetic confirmation. Delays in ATRA increase mortality from DIC-related hemorrhage.
t(9;22) is Philadelphia chromosome — CML or Ph+ ALL, not APL.
inv(16) is M4eo — favorable prognosis but not the APL/DIC picture.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the most important prognostic factor that distinguishes favorable from unfavorable childhood ALL?

choices.feedback: Gender has minor prognostic significance (males slightly worse due to testicular relapse), but cytogenetics is far more important.
Ethnicity affects some outcomes but is not the most important prognostic factor.

Correct! Cytogenetics is the most important prognostic factor. Hyperdiploidy (>50 chromosomes) and t(12;21) = excellent prognosis. Philadelphia chromosome t(9;22) and hypodiploidy = poor prognosis. Cytogenetics determines risk stratification and treatment intensity.
Hemoglobin is less prognostic than WBC count and cytogenetics.

questions.stem: A patient starting chemotherapy for Burkitt lymphoma develops hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, hyperuricemia, and acute kidney injury within 24 hours. What preventive agent could have reduced this risk?

choices.feedback: Dexamethasone treats differentiation syndrome in APL, not tumor lysis.

Correct! Rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase) converts uric acid to allantoin (water-soluble) and is used for TLS prevention/treatment in high-risk patients. It works faster than allopurinol. Contraindicated in G6PD deficiency (produces hydrogen peroxide).
Filgrastim is G-CSF for neutropenia, not TLS prevention.
Hydroxyurea reduces WBC in leukostasis but doesn't prevent TLS.

questions.stem: Two most common causes of acute pancreatitis?

choices.feedback: Medications are a less common cause overall.

Correct! Gallstones (40%) and alcohol (30%) together account for ~70% of cases. Gallstone: impacted at ampulla → duct obstruction. Alcohol: direct toxic effect + premature enzyme activation. Always get RUQ ultrasound and check triglycerides.
Iatrogenic (ERCP) and trauma are less common causes.
Important but much less common than gallstones and alcohol.

questions.stem: Lipase is preferred over amylase because:

choices.feedback: Cost is not the primary reason.

choices.text: Lipase is more sensitive AND more specific for pancreatitis
choices.feedback: Correct! Lipase rises early, stays elevated longer (up to 14 days vs 3-5 for amylase), and is more specific to the pancreas. Amylase is also produced by salivary glands and can be elevated in many non-pancreatic conditions. Lipase ≥3× ULN is diagnostic.

choices.text: Amylase is not available in most labs
choices.feedback: Amylase is widely available but less reliable.
Neither lipase nor amylase levels correlate with disease severity.

questions.stem: Early enteral nutrition in pancreatitis is preferred over TPN because:

choices.feedback: While true, the clinical rationale is more important.

choices.text: It maintains gut barrier integrity and reduces infectious complications
choices.feedback: Correct! Enteral nutrition maintains intestinal mucosal barrier → prevents bacterial translocation → reduces infected necrosis and sepsis. Start oral feeding within 24h as tolerated. If unable to eat: nasojejunal tube feeding. TPN only as last resort (increases infection risk, cholestasis).
Pain is not the distinguishing factor.
Nutrition doesn't change the disease course directly but reduces complications.

questions.stem: When are antibiotics indicated in acute pancreatitis?

choices.feedback: Prophylactic antibiotics are NOT recommended — no benefit and increase resistant organisms/fungal infection.

choices.text: Confirmed infected necrosis or concurrent cholangitis
choices.feedback: Correct! Antibiotics only for: infected necrosis (fever + gas on CT at 2-4 weeks → FNA confirms → carbapenems), or cholangitis (gallstone pancreatitis + Charcot triad → ERCP + antibiotics). Prophylactic antibiotics in sterile necrosis increase mortality.
Leukocytosis is common in pancreatitis from inflammation alone and doesn't mandate antibiotics.
Lipase level doesn't determine antibiotic need.

questions.stem: Gallstone pancreatitis: when is ERCP indicated?

choices.feedback: ERCP is not routine — only for specific indications.

choices.text: If concurrent cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction
choices.feedback: Correct! Urgent ERCP within 24h if cholangitis (Charcot triad) or persistent common bile duct obstruction. Otherwise manage pancreatitis first, then same-admission cholecystectomy once resolved (prevents recurrence — 30-40% recurrence within 6 weeks without cholecystectomy).

choices.text: Only after pancreatitis fully resolves (4-6 weeks)
choices.feedback: Cholecystectomy should be same-admission. Waiting increases recurrence risk.

choices.text: Never — gallstone pancreatitis is managed medically only
choices.feedback: While most resolve with supportive care, ERCP is needed for cholangitis/obstruction, and cholecystectomy prevents recurrence.

questions.stem: Ranson criteria at admission include all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Glucose >200 is part of GA LAW at admission.

Correct! Calcium <8 is a 48-HOUR criterion (part of C HOBBS). Admission criteria = GA LAW: Glucose >200, Age >55, LDH >350, AST >250, WBC >16K. 48-hour criteria = C HOBBS: Calcium <8, Hct drop >10%, O2 <60, BUN rise >5, Base deficit >4, Sequestration >6L.
Age >55 is an admission criterion.
WBC >16K is an admission criterion.

questions.stem: Pancreatic pseudocyst vs walled-off necrosis — key difference?

choices.feedback: Both are late complications (>4 weeks). Pseudocysts are encapsulated fluid.

choices.text: Pseudocyst = encapsulated FLUID only; WON = encapsulated fluid WITH necrotic tissue
choices.feedback: Correct! Pseudocyst: well-defined wall, homogeneous fluid, no solid debris. Usually after interstitial pancreatitis. WON: mature encapsulated collection containing necrotic tissue. Usually after necrotizing pancreatitis. Both develop >4 weeks. WON has higher infection risk and may need necrosectomy.
They are distinct entities requiring different management approaches.
WON can be sterile or infected. Infection is suspected with new fever/sepsis.

questions.stem: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation in pancreatitis — which fluid?

choices.feedback: D5W provides no volume expansion benefit.

Correct! LR is preferred over normal saline. LR has anti-inflammatory properties (lactate is metabolized to bicarbonate), reduces SIRS, and is associated with lower CRP and better outcomes. NS can cause hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis. Goal-directed: 1.5-2 mL/kg/hr initially, titrate to UOP, HR, BUN.
Albumin is not first-line for initial resuscitation in pancreatitis.
Hypertonic saline is used for hyponatremia/TBI, not pancreatitis resuscitation.

questions.stem: A patient with chronic fatigue, hypotension, and darkening of skin creases has a morning cortisol of 2 mcg/dL and ACTH of 250 pg/mL (elevated). After cosyntropin stimulation, cortisol rises to only 4 mcg/dL. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Secondary AI has LOW ACTH. This patient has elevated ACTH, indicating the pituitary is responding appropriately to low cortisol.

Correct! Low cortisol + HIGH ACTH + failed cosyntropin test + hyperpigmentation = primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison). The adrenals cannot respond to ACTH stimulation. Autoimmune adrenalitis (21-hydroxylase antibodies) is the most common cause.
Cushing would have elevated cortisol, not low cortisol.
Hypothyroidism causes fatigue but not hyperpigmentation or abnormal cortisol/ACTH.

questions.stem: A 24-hour urine free cortisol is 4x the upper limit of normal. ACTH is suppressed (<5 pg/mL). What is the next best test?

choices.feedback: High-dose DST is used to distinguish pituitary from ectopic ACTH sources. This patient has suppressed ACTH, so the source is adrenal, not pituitary.
Correct! Suppressed ACTH = the adrenals are producing cortisol independently (ACTH-independent Cushing). Next step: CT adrenals to look for adenoma or carcinoma.
MRI pituitary is for ACTH-dependent Cushing (high ACTH). This patient has suppressed ACTH.
IPSS is for distinguishing pituitary from ectopic ACTH when ACTH is elevated. Not needed here.

questions.stem: A patient on chronic prednisone for rheumatoid arthritis is hospitalized for pneumonia. They become hypotensive and unresponsive to fluids. What should you give immediately?

choices.feedback: Vasopressors may be needed, but the underlying issue is adrenal crisis from HPA suppression. Address the cause.

Correct! This is adrenal crisis triggered by physiologic stress in a patient with suppressed HPA axis. IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus, then 50 mg q8h. At stress doses, hydrocortisone has adequate mineralocorticoid activity. Oral steroids are inadequate in a crisis.
Oral prednisone may not be absorbed in a critically ill patient, and the dose is too low for crisis.
Pulse-dose methylprednisolone is for conditions like MS or transplant rejection, not adrenal crisis.

questions.stem: What clinical feature distinguishes primary from secondary adrenal insufficiency?

choices.feedback: Fatigue occurs in both primary and secondary AI.
Hypotension can occur in both (though more severe in primary due to aldosterone loss).

Correct! Hyperpigmentation is present in PRIMARY (Addison) because ACTH is elevated. ACTH is cleaved from POMC, which also produces MSH (melanocyte-stimulating hormone). In secondary AI, ACTH is LOW → no hyperpigmentation.
Hyponatremia occurs in both (cortisol loss increases ADH). Hyperkalemia is specific to primary (aldosterone loss).

questions.stem: The hallmark EKG finding in atrial fibrillation is:

choices.feedback: Regular narrow complex = SVT (AVNRT, AVRT). A-fib is IRREGULARLY irregular.

choices.text: Absent P waves with irregularly irregular R-R intervals
choices.feedback: Correct! No organized P waves (chaotic atrial activity creates fibrillatory baseline) + irregularly irregular ventricular response. These two features together are pathognomonic for A-fib.
Sawtooth (flutter waves) = atrial flutter, not A-fib.
A-fib has narrow QRS unless concurrent bundle branch block or aberrant conduction.

questions.stem: A patient with A-fib, HTN, DM, and age 70. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score and management?

choices.feedback: Calculate: HTN(1) + DM(1) + age 65-74(1) = 3, not 2.
Correct! HTN = 1, DM = 1, Age 65-74 = 1. Total = 3. Score ≥2 = anticoagulate. DOACs (apixaban preferred — lowest bleeding in ARISTOTLE trial) are first-line over warfarin for non-valvular A-fib.
Score is 3, not 1. Also, aspirin alone is no longer recommended for stroke prevention in A-fib.
Score is 3. Even at high scores, DOACs are preferred over warfarin unless mechanical valve or mitral stenosis.

questions.stem: When is warfarin preferred over DOACs in A-fib?

choices.feedback: DOACs are safe and preferred even in elderly patients.

choices.text: Mechanical heart valve or moderate-severe mitral stenosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Warfarin is required for 'valvular' A-fib: mechanical prosthetic valves and moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis. DOACs are NOT approved for these — the RE-ALIGN trial (dabigatran in mechanical valves) was stopped early due to increased thromboembolism and bleeding.
Some DOACs (apixaban) can be used with dose adjustment even in severe CKD. Warfarin is an option but not exclusively required.
DOACs are preferred for paroxysmal A-fib with appropriate CHA₂DS₂-VASc score.

questions.stem: A-fib of unknown duration. Before cardioversion, you must:

choices.feedback: Unknown duration could mean >48h → thrombus risk. Must anticoagulate or rule out thrombus.

choices.text: Anticoagulate 3 weeks OR perform TEE to exclude LA thrombus
choices.feedback: Correct! A-fib ≥48h or unknown: 3 weeks anticoagulation → cardiovert → 4 weeks post-cardioversion anticoagulation. OR: TEE → if no LA appendage thrombus → cardiovert immediately with anticoagulation. Either approach acceptable.

choices.text: Give aspirin for 1 week then cardiovert
choices.feedback: Aspirin is insufficient for stroke prevention around cardioversion.

choices.text: Cardioversion is never safe after 48 hours
choices.feedback: Cardioversion is safe after appropriate anticoagulation or TEE clearance.

questions.stem: Rate control medications for A-fib include all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Beta-blockers are first-line rate control agents.

Correct! Flecainide is a rhythm control drug (Class IC antiarrhythmic) used to restore/maintain sinus rhythm, NOT for rate control. It can paradoxically INCREASE ventricular rate in A-fib by organizing atrial activity into flutter (must co-administer with AV nodal blocker). Rate control agents: BBs, non-DHP CCBs, digoxin.
Non-DHP CCBs are effective rate control agents (avoid in HFrEF).
Digoxin is used as adjunct rate control, especially in HF patients.

questions.stem: Most common cause of atrial fibrillation?

choices.feedback: Mitral stenosis is a classic cause but not the most common overall.

Correct! HTN is the #1 cause of A-fib due to left atrial enlargement from chronic pressure overload. Other important causes: valvular disease (especially mitral), HF, thyrotoxicosis (always check TSH in new A-fib), alcohol, PE, post-cardiac surgery, OSA.
Thyrotoxicosis is an important and reversible cause but not the most common.
'Holiday heart' is a well-known cause but less common than HTN.

questions.stem: EAST-AFNET 4 trial showed that early rhythm control:

choices.text: Has no benefit over rate control
choices.feedback: That was the AFFIRM trial conclusion. EAST-AFNET 4 challenged this.

choices.text: Reduces cardiovascular outcomes when initiated within 1 year of diagnosis
choices.feedback: Correct! EAST-AFNET 4 (2020): early rhythm control (within 1 year of A-fib diagnosis) reduced composite of CV death, stroke, and HF hospitalization by 21%. This shifted practice toward earlier rhythm control, especially with catheter ablation becoming safer and more effective.

choices.text: Should only be used in patients under 50
choices.feedback: The trial included patients of various ages and showed benefit across subgroups.
Anticoagulation decisions are based on CHA₂DS₂-VASc score regardless of rhythm control strategy.

questions.stem: Digoxin in A-fib rate control — key limitation?

choices.feedback: Bradycardia is a side effect but not the key limitation.

choices.text: It only controls resting heart rate, not exercise rate
choices.feedback: Correct! Digoxin controls resting HR via vagal enhancement but does NOT control exercise HR (sympathetic activation overrides vagal tone). This is why it's used as an adjunct, not first-line. Best role: HFrEF patients with A-fib (inotropic + rate control). Narrow therapeutic window — monitor for toxicity (visual changes, nausea, arrhythmias).
Digoxin is available in both oral and IV formulations.

choices.text: It converts A-fib to sinus rhythm
choices.feedback: Digoxin does NOT cardiovert — it's purely for rate control.

questions.stem: FeNa <1% with elevated BUN:Cr ratio (>20:1) indicates:

choices.feedback: ATN has FeNa >2% — tubules can't reabsorb sodium.

Correct! FeNa <1% = kidneys are avidly retaining sodium (tubules intact, just underperfused). BUN:Cr >20:1 because urea is reabsorbed more than creatinine in low-flow states. Treatment: volume resuscitation. If FeNa >2% + muddy brown casts → ATN (tubular damage).
Post-renal has variable FeNa and hydronephrosis on US.
CKD is chronic, not acute. BUN:Cr is typically 10-15:1.

questions.stem: Muddy brown granular casts on urinalysis indicate:

choices.feedback: GN shows RBC casts.

Correct! Muddy brown granular casts = sloughed tubular epithelial cells from necrosis. Pathognomonic for ATN. Other ATN clues: FeNa >2%, BUN:Cr 10-15:1, urine osmolality ~300 (isosthenuria — tubules can't concentrate).
AIN shows WBC casts and urine eosinophils.
Pre-renal has bland sediment (hyaline casts at most).

questions.stem: Classic triad of acute interstitial nephritis?

choices.feedback: That's nephritic syndrome (glomerulonephritis).

Correct! AIN: drug-induced hypersensitivity. Classic triad (not always present): fever + maculopapular rash + peripheral eosinophilia. Urine: WBC casts, urine eosinophils (Hansel stain). Common culprits: penicillins, NSAIDs, PPIs, sulfonamides. Treatment: discontinue drug ± steroids.
That's pyelonephritis.
That suggests nephrotic/nephritic syndrome or CHF.

questions.stem: AEIOU indications for emergent dialysis — the 'E' stands for:

choices.feedback: Encephalopathy falls under 'U' (uremia).
Correct! Refractory hyperkalemia (K+ >6.5 with EKG changes not responding to medical therapy) is an emergent dialysis indication. Full AEIOU: Acidosis, Electrolytes, Intoxication, Overload, Uremia.
Embolism is not a dialysis indication.
Edema from fluid overload is 'O' (Overload).

questions.stem: Contrast-induced nephropathy prevention?

choices.feedback: Prophylactic dialysis does not prevent CIN.

choices.text: IV isotonic saline hydration before and after contrast
choices.feedback: Correct! Pre- and post-hydration with IV normal saline or sodium bicarbonate is the most effective prevention strategy. Also: use lowest contrast volume, iso-osmolar contrast, hold metformin (lactic acidosis risk if AKI develops), avoid concurrent nephrotoxins. NAC (N-acetylcysteine) has uncertain benefit.
Steroids prevent contrast allergy reactions, not nephropathy.
Diuretics worsen CIN by causing dehydration.

questions.stem: Post-renal AKI in elderly male is most commonly caused by:

choices.feedback: That's a pre-renal (or renovascular) cause.

Correct! BPH causes bladder outlet obstruction → bilateral hydronephrosis → post-renal AKI. #1 cause of post-renal AKI in older males. Diagnosis: renal ultrasound (bilateral hydronephrosis) + elevated post-void residual. Treatment: Foley catheter for immediate decompression.
NSAIDs cause intrinsic AKI, not post-renal obstruction.
MCD causes nephrotic syndrome, not post-renal obstruction.

questions.stem: ATN typically resolves in:

choices.feedback: Too quick — tubular regeneration takes longer.

Correct! ATN is self-limiting. Tubular epithelial cells regenerate over 1-3 weeks. Polyuric phase follows oliguric phase (recovering tubules initially can't concentrate urine). Management is supportive: maintain euvolemia, avoid nephrotoxins, adjust drug doses, dialysis if indicated.
If recovery takes months, consider chronic kidney disease.
Most ATN episodes recover, though some progress to CKD.

questions.stem: Which drug combination is particularly nephrotoxic?

choices.feedback: This is a safe combination at appropriate doses.

Correct! 'Triple whammy': NSAIDs (constrict afferent arteriole) + ACEi/ARBs (dilate efferent arteriole) + diuretics (volume depletion) → dramatically reduced GFR → AKI. Each drug alone is manageable, but the combination is highly nephrotoxic, especially in elderly or CKD patients. Avoid this combination.
Generally safe combination (metformin held only if AKI develops).
Common and safe cardiovascular combination.

questions.stem: Delirium tremens typically occurs at what time after last drink?

choices.feedback: 6-12h = minor withdrawal symptoms (tremor, anxiety, diaphoresis).

Correct! DT peaks at 48-96h (can be later). Timeline: minor withdrawal (6-12h) → hallucinosis (12-24h) → seizures (24-48h) → DT (48-96h). DT features: delirium + autonomic storm + hallucinations. 5-15% mortality untreated. Requires ICU-level care.
Withdrawal takes hours to develop after cessation.
By 7-10 days, most withdrawal has resolved.

questions.stem: First-line treatment for alcohol withdrawal?

choices.feedback: Phenytoin is NOT effective for alcohol withdrawal seizures.

Correct! BZDs are first-line: cross-tolerance with alcohol at GABA-A receptor. Long-acting (diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) provide smoother withdrawal. Lorazepam preferred in liver failure (no hepatic metabolism). Symptom-triggered (CIWA-based) dosing is preferred over fixed-schedule — lower total dose, shorter treatment.
Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold. Only adjunct for agitation after BZDs.
SSRIs treat depression/anxiety but have no role in acute withdrawal management.

questions.stem: Why give thiamine BEFORE glucose?

choices.feedback: Glucose doesn't destroy thiamine.

choices.text: Glucose metabolism consumes thiamine → can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy
choices.feedback: Correct! Glucose metabolism requires thiamine as a cofactor (pyruvate dehydrogenase, transketolase). IV glucose in a thiamine-depleted patient → accelerates remaining thiamine depletion → acute Wernicke encephalopathy (confusion + ataxia + ophthalmoplegia). Give thiamine 500 mg IV first. This order is critical.
Thiamine doesn't affect glucose absorption.

choices.text: Glucose causes hyperkalemia unless thiamine is given
choices.feedback: This interaction doesn't exist.

questions.stem: Alcoholic hallucinosis differs from DT by:

choices.feedback: Both have hallucinations — that's the name.

choices.text: Hallucinosis: CLEAR sensorium. DT: IMPAIRED sensorium (delirium)
choices.feedback: Correct! Alcoholic hallucinosis (12-24h): vivid hallucinations but patient is oriented, conversational, and knows the hallucinations aren't real. DT (48-96h): TRUE delirium — disorientation, confusion, agitation, fluctuating consciousness, plus hallucinations. DT is far more dangerous.
DT has SEVERE autonomic instability (tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia).
Both can have visual hallucinations.

questions.stem: Which benzodiazepine is preferred in liver failure?

choices.feedback: Diazepam has active metabolites requiring hepatic metabolism (long-acting → accumulation in liver failure).

Correct! LOT mnemonic: Lorazepam, Oxazepam, Temazepam — no hepatic CYP metabolism (glucuronidation only), no active metabolites. Preferred in liver dysfunction. Diazepam and chlordiazepoxide have active hepatically-metabolized metabolites → risk of accumulation and prolonged sedation.
Midazolam is hepatically metabolized via CYP3A4.
Chlordiazepoxide has multiple active metabolites requiring hepatic metabolism.

questions.stem: CIWA-Ar score >20 indicates:

choices.feedback: <10 is mild. >20 is severe.

choices.text: Severe withdrawal with high DT risk — aggressive treatment needed
choices.feedback: Correct! CIWA-Ar >20: severe withdrawal requiring aggressive benzodiazepine treatment. High risk for progression to seizures and DT. ICU monitoring recommended. CIWA-based symptom-triggered dosing: administer BZDs when score ≥10, reassess q1h. This approach results in less total medication use and shorter treatment.

choices.text: Patient is safe for outpatient detox
choices.feedback: >20 is too severe for outpatient management.
BZDs are always first-line. Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold.

questions.stem: Phenobarbital is useful in alcohol withdrawal because:

choices.feedback: Phenobarbital is a potent sedative.

choices.text: It directly activates GABA receptors even when BZD-resistant
choices.feedback: Correct! Phenobarbital is a barbiturate that directly opens GABA-A chloride channels (allosteric agonist) — works even when BZD binding sites are downregulated in severe withdrawal. Increasingly used as first-line or adjunct in severe/refractory withdrawal. Longer half-life provides steady-state coverage. Risk: respiratory depression.
It treats acute withdrawal but doesn't prevent seizures permanently.

choices.text: It treats the underlying liver disease
choices.feedback: Phenobarbital has no effect on liver disease treatment.

questions.stem: Kindling effect in alcohol withdrawal refers to:

choices.feedback: Kindling is not related to temperature.

choices.text: Each successive withdrawal episode becomes more severe due to neuronal sensitization
choices.feedback: Correct! Repeated withdrawal episodes → progressive neuronal sensitization (NMDA receptor upregulation) → each subsequent withdrawal is more severe with higher seizure risk and DT risk. This is why patients with multiple prior detoxifications are at highest risk. Supports the argument for adequate treatment of each withdrawal episode.

choices.text: A specific type of seizure unique to alcoholism
choices.feedback: Kindling is a concept, not a seizure type.

choices.text: The calming effect of the first drink
choices.feedback: That's tolerance/dependence, not kindling.

questions.stem: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! ABS occurs when the amnion ruptures, releasing fibrous bands that entangle fetal parts. The bands constrict limbs (rings), amputate digits, or cause facial clefts. KEY features: ASYMMETRIC, do NOT follow developmental patterns or dermatomal distributions, random combination of defects. Not hereditary. The fetus becomes entangled in the sticky mesodermal surface of the chorion.
Thalidomide causes SYMMETRIC bilateral limb reduction (phocomelia/amelia). ABS is characteristically asymmetric.
Adams-Oliver causes terminal transverse limb defects + aplasia cutis congenita (scalp defects). It follows Mendelian inheritance. ABS is sporadic and has constriction rings.
Limb-body wall complex is more severe (thoracoabdominal wall defects, limb-body attachment) and may overlap with ABS but is a distinct entity.

questions.stem: ABS is classified as a DISRUPTION. What does this mean for recurrence risk?

choices.text: Low recurrence risk — it was an extrinsic random event, not genetic
choices.feedback: Correct! Disruption = breakdown of a previously normally developing structure by an external destructive process. Since the fetal genome was normal and the event (amnion rupture) was random, recurrence risk is LOW (not genetic). Contrast: Malformation = intrinsically abnormal development (often genetic, may recur). Deformation = abnormal form/position from mechanical forces on normal tissue (oligohydramnios → Potter sequence). Dysplasia = abnormal tissue organization (skeletal dysplasias).
ABS is not genetic. It's a random event caused by amnion rupture.
ABS has no genetic basis. It's a disruption, not a malformation.
While some maternal factors (trauma, amniocentesis) may rarely contribute, recurrence risk is still very low because the event is stochastic.

questions.stem: What is the surgical approach to a deep circumferential constriction ring?

choices.text: Staged Z-plasty release — never release the full circumference at once
choices.feedback: Correct! Deep constriction rings are released with Z-plasty in TWO stages: release one half of the circumference first, then the other half 6-12 weeks later. Releasing the full circumference at once risks vascular compromise. Z-plasty lengthens the scar and breaks up the linear constriction. If acute vascular compromise exists, emergent release may be needed. Shallow grooves may be observed if not causing functional problems.

choices.text: Complete circumferential excision in one stage
choices.feedback: Full circumferential release risks vascular compromise of the distal limb. Staged approach is standard.

choices.text: Observation only — rings always resolve spontaneously
choices.feedback: Deep rings with neurovascular compromise do NOT resolve and require surgical release.
Amputation is a last resort for non-salvageable limbs. Many constriction rings can be successfully released with staged Z-plasty.

questions.stem: A baby is born with BILATERAL symmetric absence of both upper limbs (amelia). No constriction rings are present. What category of defect is this most likely?

choices.text: Teratogenic or genetic malformation — symmetric bilateral defects suggest intrinsic cause
choices.feedback: Correct! Symmetric bilateral limb defects suggest an intrinsic cause: teratogenic (thalidomide, misoprostol) or genetic (Roberts syndrome, Holt-Oram). ABS causes ASYMMETRIC defects with constriction rings. Key rule: SYMMETRIC suggests intrinsic (malformation/teratogen), ASYMMETRIC without pattern suggests disruption (ABS, vascular accident).

choices.text: Amniotic band syndrome — bands can affect both sides equally
choices.feedback: ABS characteristically causes ASYMMETRIC, random defects. Perfect bilateral symmetry argues strongly against ABS.
Oligohydramnios causes positional deformities (clubfoot, hip dysplasia) but does not cause limb absence/amputations.
Symmetric bilateral vascular events affecting identical structures would be extremely unlikely. Symmetry points to intrinsic causes.

questions.stem: In Potter sequence, why does bilateral renal agenesis cause pulmonary hypoplasia?

choices.text: No kidneys → no fetal urine → oligohydramnios → no fluid for fetal breathing → pulmonary hypoplasia
choices.feedback: Correct! Fetal urine is the major source of amniotic fluid in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters. No kidneys → no urine → severe oligohydramnios. Fetal breathing movements of amniotic fluid are essential for lung development — the fluid mechanically stretches the lungs and stimulates growth. Without adequate fluid volume, the lungs cannot expand properly → pulmonary hypoplasia. Additional effects: limb compression → contractures, facial compression → Potter facies (flat nose, recessed chin, low-set ears).

choices.text: The kidneys produce a growth factor needed for lung development
choices.feedback: While some hormonal interactions exist, the primary mechanism is mechanical — lack of amniotic fluid prevents lung expansion.

choices.text: Renal agenesis and pulmonary hypoplasia share a common genetic cause
choices.feedback: In Potter sequence, the lung problem is SECONDARY to the kidney problem (via oligohydramnios). It's a sequence, not a syndrome with independent organ involvement.

choices.text: Compression of the thorax by the uterus
choices.feedback: While oligohydramnios does cause external compression, the primary mechanism for pulmonary hypoplasia is the lack of fluid for fetal breathing, not chest wall compression.

questions.stem: What is the underlying embryological defect?

choices.feedback: This describes a simple covered anus (low malformation). But the flat perineum suggests a more significant defect — failure of the urorectal septum to divide the cloaca properly.

Correct! The cloaca (common endodermal chamber) is divided by the urorectal septum into the urogenital sinus (anteriorly) and anorectal canal (posteriorly) by week 7. Failure of this septum to descend completely causes anorectal malformations, often with fistulas to the urogenital tract.
Neural crest migration failure causes Hirschsprung disease (aganglionosis), not imperforate anus. Different embryological mechanism entirely.
Vascular accidents cause jejunal/ileal atresia, not anorectal malformations. ARM is caused by abnormal cloacal development.

questions.stem: What association should be systematically evaluated?

choices.feedback: Correct! ARM is the A in VACTERL. Up to 50-60% of patients with ARM have at least one additional VACTERL anomaly. Must screen: spinal X-ray/US, echocardiogram, renal US, esophageal patency (pass NG tube), limb exam.
CHARGE includes choanal atresia, not anorectal. ARM is classically associated with VACTERL.
Down syndrome is associated with duodenal atresia, Hirschsprung, and AVSD — not typically anorectal malformations.
Pierre Robin involves the mandible and airway, not anorectal development.

questions.stem: In females with high ARM, what is the most common fistula type?

choices.feedback: While rectovaginal fistulas exist, they are actually less common than rectovestibular fistulas. The vestibular fistula (opening in the vestibule between vagina and perineum) is the most common female ARM.

Correct! Rectovestibular fistula is the most common ARM in females. The rectal opening is in the vestibule (between the hymen and perineum). In males, rectourethral (prostatic or bulbar) is most common. Persistent cloaca (single perineal opening for rectum, vagina, and urethra) is the most complex female variant.
Rectoperineal fistulas occur in both sexes but represent LOW malformations, not high. They have good prognosis and often need only a simple anoplasty.
Rectobladder (vesical) fistulas are the highest and rarest type. They carry the worst prognosis for continence.

questions.stem: What is the critical structure the PSARP must preserve for continence?

choices.feedback: The internal sphincter provides resting tone but is often abnormal in ARM. The external sphincter complex (including puborectalis) is more critical for voluntary continence.

Correct! The PSARP (developed by Dr. Alberto Peña) approaches through the posterior sagittal midline, splitting the sphincter complex exactly in the midline to identify and place the rectum within the muscle complex. The puborectalis sling provides the anorectal angle critical for continence. Electrical stimulation guides the midline approach.
The levator ani is important for pelvic floor support, but the puborectalis (part of the levator complex) is the specific muscle that creates the anorectal angle for continence.
While blood supply matters for healing, continence depends on the sphincter complex, not the vascular supply.

questions.stem: What is the most common long-term functional outcome after ARM repair?

choices.feedback: Permanent colostomy is rarely needed. Most patients achieve social continence with bowel management programs, even if they don't have perfect voluntary control.

choices.text: Full voluntary continence in all patients
choices.feedback: Unfortunately, full voluntary continence is achieved in only about 25-30% of high ARM patients. Most require some form of bowel management.

Correct! Constipation is the #1 long-term problem after ARM repair. The anorectum often has abnormal motility. Bowel management programs (daily enemas or antegrade continence enemas via Malone appendicostomy) allow social continence in >90% of patients. Soiling/incontinence is less common than constipation.

choices.text: Stricture at the repair site requiring dilation
choices.feedback: Strictures can occur early post-op and require dilation, but constipation is the most common LONG-TERM issue.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman has 3 early miscarriages and a DVT. Lupus anticoagulant is positive twice, 14 weeks apart. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Factor V Leiden causes hypercoagulability but does not explain the recurrent pregnancy losses + positive lupus anticoagulant pattern. APS is the unifying diagnosis.

Correct! APS diagnostic criteria met: clinical criterion (recurrent pregnancy losses + DVT) + lab criterion (lupus anticoagulant positive twice ≥12 weeks apart). Treatment: lifelong warfarin for DVT + LMWH + aspirin for future pregnancies.
DIC is an acute consumptive coagulopathy with bleeding, not chronic thrombosis and pregnancy loss.
While APS can be secondary to SLE, the positive lupus anticoagulant with thrombosis and pregnancy morbidity specifically meets APS criteria.

questions.stem: A patient with APS has a prolonged aPTT. A mixing study does NOT correct the aPTT. This is most consistent with:

choices.feedback: Factor deficiencies CORRECT on mixing study (adding normal plasma provides the missing factor). Failure to correct = inhibitor present.

Correct! Lupus anticoagulant is a phospholipid-dependent inhibitor. It prolongs aPTT and does NOT correct with a mixing study (unlike factor deficiencies, which correct). Despite prolonging aPTT in vitro, LA causes thrombosis in vivo — the classic APS paradox.
Vitamin K deficiency affects multiple factors and corrects on mixing study (factors are simply deficient, not inhibited).
Heparin contamination prolongs aPTT but can be identified by thrombin time and would correct with protamine. The clinical context (APS with thrombosis) points to lupus anticoagulant.

questions.stem: Why are DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban) NOT recommended for APS?

choices.text: They are not indicated for venous thrombosis
choices.feedback: DOACs are indicated for VTE treatment generally. The concern is specifically about APS.

choices.text: Trials showed higher thrombosis rates compared to warfarin in APS
choices.feedback: Correct! The TRAPS trial (triple-positive APS) and ASTRO-APS trial demonstrated higher rates of arterial thrombosis (particularly stroke) with rivaroxaban vs warfarin. DOACs are therefore NOT recommended for APS, especially triple-positive APS. Warfarin remains the standard of care.

choices.text: DOACs interact with lupus anticoagulant testing
choices.feedback: While DOACs can interfere with coagulation testing, this is not the primary reason they're contraindicated in APS.

choices.text: DOACs cause more bleeding in APS patients
choices.feedback: The concern is inadequate protection from thrombosis, not increased bleeding risk.

questions.stem: What is the recommended treatment during pregnancy for a woman with obstetric APS?

choices.feedback: Warfarin is TERATOGENIC (crosses placenta, causes nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses, CNS abnormalities — warfarin embryopathy). Contraindicated in pregnancy.

Correct! LMWH + aspirin 81 mg daily is the standard treatment for obstetric APS. Heparin does NOT cross the placenta (safe for fetus). Continue through pregnancy + 6-12 weeks postpartum. This regimen increases live birth rates from ~20% to ~70-80%.
Dual antiplatelet therapy is not the standard for obstetric APS. Anticoagulation (LMWH) is required, not just antiplatelet therapy.
Untreated obstetric APS has ~80% pregnancy loss rate. Treatment with LMWH + aspirin dramatically improves outcomes.

questions.stem: Catastrophic APS (CAPS) is defined by thrombosis of how many organs within one week?

choices.feedback: Single-organ thrombosis is typical APS, not catastrophic.
Two organs do not meet CAPS criteria, which requires multi-organ involvement.

Correct! CAPS = rapid (<1 week) thrombosis of ≥3 organ systems, typically small vessel occlusion, with histopathologic confirmation of microthrombi + confirmed aPL antibodies. Mortality ~50%. Treatment requires the 'triple therapy + one' approach: anticoagulation + steroids + plasma exchange + IVIG.
CAPS requires ≥3 organs. Five or more would be CAPS but the threshold is 3.

questions.stem: Which antiphospholipid antibody profile carries the HIGHEST thrombotic risk?

choices.feedback: Isolated IgM anti-cardiolipin has the lowest clinical significance and thrombotic risk among the aPL antibody profiles.

Correct! Triple positivity (all three antibody types positive) carries the highest thrombotic risk — up to 5.3% annual recurrence rate even on anticoagulation. These patients require lifelong warfarin (NOT DOACs), and the threshold for prophylaxis is lower. Triple positivity also has the highest pregnancy morbidity risk.
Isolated LA carries significant thrombotic risk but triple positivity is higher.
Isolated anti-β2GP1 carries moderate risk but is surpassed by triple positivity.

questions.stem: Livedo reticularis in a patient with thrombosis and recurrent miscarriages suggests:

choices.feedback: Raynaud causes episodic triphasic color changes in digits (white → blue → red), not the reticular/lace-like skin pattern of livedo reticularis.

Correct! Livedo reticularis (lace-like, purplish, reticular/net-like skin discoloration) is a common dermatologic finding in APS, present in ~25% of patients. Combined with thrombosis and recurrent pregnancy loss, it strongly suggests APS. Also seen in Sneddon syndrome (livedo reticularis + stroke + aPL antibodies).
PAN can cause livedo reticularis but typically presents with systemic vasculitis symptoms (fever, weight loss, renal/mesenteric involvement, HBV association), not recurrent miscarriages.
Cryoglobulinemia can cause purpura and livedo but is associated with hepatitis C, not the thrombosis/pregnancy loss pattern of APS.

questions.stem: A patient with APS and mild thrombocytopenia (platelet count 75,000) develops a DVT. Should anticoagulation be withheld?

choices.feedback: Mild thrombocytopenia in APS is common (30-40% of patients) and is NOT a contraindication to anticoagulation. The platelet count is adequate for anticoagulation.

choices.text: No — mild thrombocytopenia in APS does not contraindicate anticoagulation
choices.feedback: Correct! Thrombocytopenia in APS is typically mild (50,000-100,000) and is NOT a reason to withhold anticoagulation. The prothrombotic risk far outweighs the bleeding risk at these platelet levels. Anticoagulation should be initiated for the DVT. Only severe thrombocytopenia (<20,000-30,000) with active bleeding warrants holding anticoagulation.

choices.text: Only if the patient is also on aspirin
choices.feedback: Dual therapy (warfarin + aspirin) decisions are based on thrombotic risk, not platelet count at this level.

choices.text: Yes — treat thrombocytopenia first with steroids
choices.feedback: The thrombocytopenia is mild and does not require treatment. The DVT requires immediate anticoagulation.

questions.stem: A patient has excessive worry about multiple topics (finances, health, relationships) with muscle tension, insomnia, and restlessness for 8 months. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Panic disorder features recurrent discrete panic attacks, not chronic pervasive worry about multiple domains.

Correct! GAD = excessive worry about multiple domains for ≥6 months with ≥3 somatic symptoms (restlessness, muscle tension, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, irritability, sleep disturbance). The chronic, pervasive nature distinguishes GAD from other anxiety disorders.
Adjustment disorder requires an identifiable stressor and resolves within 6 months of stressor resolution. This has persisted 8 months with no specific stressor.
Social anxiety is specifically about fear of social/performance situations, not broad worry about finances, health, and relationships.

questions.stem: What is the BEST pharmacologic choice for a patient who gives a public speech quarterly and experiences severe performance anxiety?

choices.feedback: Daily SSRI is appropriate for generalized social anxiety disorder, but for isolated performance anxiety occurring quarterly, an as-needed approach is preferred.

Correct! Propranolol (beta-blocker) is ideal for performance-only social anxiety. It blocks peripheral sympathetic symptoms (tremor, tachycardia, sweating) without sedation or dependence. Take 10-40 mg 30-60 minutes before the event. Does NOT treat generalized social anxiety — only performance-specific subtype.
Daily benzodiazepine for quarterly performance anxiety is excessive and carries dependence risk.
Buspirone requires 2-4 weeks of daily use to be effective. It does not work on an as-needed basis.

questions.stem: OCD treatment requires SSRIs at what doses compared to depression treatment?

choices.feedback: OCD actually requires higher doses than depression. Subtherapeutic doses for OCD would be effective for depression but insufficient for OCD.

Correct! OCD typically requires SSRIs at the HIGH end of dosing (e.g., fluoxetine 40-80 mg vs. 20 mg for depression, fluvoxamine 200-300 mg). Response time is also longer (8-12 weeks vs 4-6 weeks for depression). If SSRIs fail at maximum doses, clomipramine (TCA with strong serotonergic activity) is the next pharmacologic option.
OCD requires higher SSRI doses and longer treatment duration than depression.

choices.text: SSRIs are not used for OCD
choices.feedback: SSRIs are first-line pharmacotherapy for OCD, alongside ERP psychotherapy.

questions.stem: What is the gold standard psychotherapy for OCD?

choices.feedback: Psychodynamic therapy explores unconscious conflicts and is not effective for OCD.

Correct! ERP = systematic exposure to obsession triggers while preventing compulsive response. Gradual habituation reduces anxiety. ERP has the strongest evidence for OCD treatment and is effective in 60-70% of patients. Combined ERP + SSRI is often the most effective approach for moderate-severe OCD.
Supportive therapy provides emotional support but does not address the obsession-compulsion cycle.
DBT is primarily used for borderline personality disorder and emotion dysregulation, not OCD.

questions.stem: What medication is indicated for PTSD-related nightmares?

choices.feedback: Trazodone is used off-label for insomnia but is not specifically indicated for PTSD nightmares.

Correct! Prazosin (alpha-1 adrenergic antagonist) reduces PTSD-related nightmares by blocking noradrenergic hyperarousal during sleep. Start low (1 mg at bedtime), titrate up. Monitor for orthostatic hypotension (first-dose effect). Some recent trials have shown mixed results, but it remains a clinical staple for PTSD nightmares.
Melatonin helps with sleep initiation but does not specifically target trauma-related nightmares.
Benzodiazepines are NOT recommended for PTSD — they may worsen outcomes, interfere with trauma processing, and carry dependence risk.

questions.stem: Benzodiazepines are NOT recommended for chronic treatment of which anxiety conditions?

choices.feedback: Benzos are problematic in most chronic anxiety treatment, not just phobias.

Correct! Chronic benzodiazepine use is discouraged in GAD (use SSRIs/buspirone instead), panic disorder (SSRIs for maintenance, benzos only for acute rescue), and PTSD (may worsen outcomes and prevent trauma processing). Benzos carry tolerance, dependence, withdrawal seizure risk, cognitive impairment, and fall risk in elderly. Short-term or as-needed use is acceptable in select patients.
Benzos are problematic across multiple anxiety disorders, not just social anxiety.

choices.text: None — they are safe for chronic use in all anxiety disorders
choices.feedback: This is incorrect. Chronic benzo use carries significant risks and is not recommended as first-line maintenance therapy for any anxiety disorder.

questions.stem: What distinguishes acute stress disorder from PTSD?

choices.text: Acute stress disorder involves less severe symptoms
choices.feedback: Symptom severity can be similar. The distinction is duration, not severity.

choices.text: Duration: ASD = 3 days to 1 month; PTSD = >1 month after trauma
choices.feedback: Correct! Both require exposure to traumatic event with intrusion, avoidance, and arousal symptoms. ASD: 3 days to 1 month post-trauma. PTSD: symptoms persist >1 month. ASD may transition to PTSD if symptoms don't resolve. Early CBT can prevent this transition.

choices.text: PTSD only occurs in combat veterans
choices.feedback: PTSD can result from any traumatic event — sexual assault, accidents, natural disasters, medical trauma, etc. It is not limited to military experience.
ASD does not require hospitalization by definition. The distinction from PTSD is purely temporal.

questions.stem: Buspirone has which advantage over benzodiazepines for GAD?

choices.feedback: Buspirone takes 2-4 weeks for full effect — SLOWER than benzos, which act within minutes.

choices.text: No dependence, tolerance, or withdrawal risk
choices.feedback: Correct! Buspirone (5-HT1A partial agonist) is non-addictive — no dependence, tolerance, withdrawal, sedation, or cognitive impairment. Ideal for patients with substance use history. Disadvantage: slow onset (2-4 weeks), must take daily (not effective as-needed), less potent anxiolysis than benzos. Does not treat panic attacks.

choices.text: Can be used as-needed for acute anxiety
choices.feedback: Buspirone must be taken daily for 2-4 weeks before therapeutic effect. It does NOT work on an as-needed basis.
Buspirone is NOT effective for panic disorder. It works for GAD only.

questions.stem: Stanford Type A aortic dissection involves the:

choices.feedback: Descending only = Type B.

Correct! Type A = any involvement of ascending aorta (regardless of descending extension). Surgical emergency — mortality 1-2% per hour without surgery. Type B = descending only (distal to left subclavian) — medical management unless complicated.
Isolated abdominal aortic dissection is extremely rare.

choices.text: Aortic arch only, sparing the ascending
choices.feedback: Arch involvement with ascending = Type A.

questions.stem: First-line antihypertensive for aortic dissection?

choices.feedback: Nitroprusside causes reflex tachycardia → increased shear stress. Must give BB first.

Correct! IV BB first: reduces HR (target <60) and BP (SBP 100-120). Esmolol (titratable, short-acting) or labetalol (alpha + beta). BB BEFORE any vasodilator to prevent reflex tachycardia. Then add nicardipine or nitroprusside if needed.
Too slow onset for acute dissection emergency.
Hydralazine causes reflex tachycardia — worsens dissection.

questions.stem: Why must beta-blockers be given BEFORE vasodilators in dissection?

choices.text: Vasodilators are not effective in dissection
choices.feedback: Vasodilators effectively lower BP but have a dangerous side effect when used alone.

choices.text: Vasodilators alone cause reflex tachycardia → increased aortic shear stress
choices.feedback: Correct! Vasodilators (nitroprusside, hydralazine) lower BP but trigger baroreceptor-mediated reflex tachycardia → increased dP/dt (rate of aortic pressure rise) → more wall stress → propagates dissection. BB first eliminates reflex tachycardia before adding vasodilators.
This is not a pharmacokinetic interaction.

choices.text: Vasodilators cause coronary steal in dissection
choices.feedback: Coronary steal is not the primary concern here.

questions.stem: Imaging gold standard for diagnosing aortic dissection?

choices.feedback: CXR shows widened mediastinum in 60-90% but is insufficiently sensitive to confirm or exclude.

Correct! CTA: >95% sensitivity and specificity. Shows intimal flap separating true and false lumens, extent of dissection, branch vessel involvement. Fast and widely available. TEE is the best bedside test for unstable patients who cannot go to CT.
TTE has limited sensitivity for dissection. TEE is much better.
Has no role in aortic dissection diagnosis.

questions.stem: Aortic dissection can mimic STEMI when it involves:

choices.feedback: Descending dissection does not typically involve coronary arteries.

choices.text: The coronary ostia (usually RCA → inferior STEMI)
choices.feedback: Correct! Type A dissection can extend into the right coronary ostium → RCA occlusion → inferior STEMI. This is catastrophic if misdiagnosed as primary ACS and treated with fibrinolytics (causes fatal hemorrhage into the dissection). Always consider dissection in chest pain with aortic regurgitation, pulse deficits, or tearing back pain.
Brachiocephalic involvement causes stroke symptoms, not STEMI.
Renal involvement causes renal malperfusion (AKI), not STEMI.

questions.stem: A patient with Marfan syndrome presents with sudden tearing chest pain. Which risk factor predisposes them?

choices.feedback: Atherosclerosis is less relevant in Marfan — the connective tissue defect is key.

choices.text: Cystic medial necrosis from fibrillin-1 deficiency
choices.feedback: Correct! Marfan syndrome = fibrillin-1 (FBN1) gene mutation → defective elastic tissue → cystic medial necrosis → weakened aortic wall → dissection and aneurysm. Other connective tissue disorders: Ehlers-Danlos (type IV — vascular), Loeys-Dietz, Turner syndrome. These patients need lifelong aortic surveillance.
Calcification is degenerative, not the mechanism in Marfan.
Marfan affects connective tissue structure, not coagulation.

questions.stem: Type B dissection is treated medically UNLESS which complication occurs?

choices.feedback: Mild HTN is expected and managed with anti-impulse therapy.

Correct! Uncomplicated Type B: medical therapy (anti-impulse). Complicated Type B requires endovascular repair (TEVAR): organ malperfusion (renal failure, mesenteric ischemia, limb ischemia), aortic rupture, refractory pain despite optimal medical therapy, rapid aortic expansion.
Persistent pain may indicate complication but isolated pain is managed medically with reassessment.
Age alone does not change the medical management strategy for uncomplicated Type B.

questions.stem: Chest pain that is maximal at onset with tearing quality suggests dissection over MI because:

choices.feedback: MI pain can be severe but typically crescendos over minutes.

choices.text: Dissection pain peaks instantly (intimal tear) while MI crescendos gradually (progressive ischemia)
choices.feedback: Correct! Dissection: sudden maximal pain from the moment of intimal tear — patients can tell you the exact second. MI: gradual buildup as ischemia progresses. Dissection radiates to the back (interscapular), MI to jaw/arm. This temporal distinction is high-yield for distinguishing the two.
Dissection CAN cause EKG changes (coronary involvement → STEMI, pericardial effusion → low voltage).

choices.text: MI always presents with shortness of breath first
choices.feedback: MI presents with chest pain/pressure as the primary symptom.

questions.stem: ARDS Berlin criteria require all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: This IS required — bilateral opacities not explained by other causes.

Correct! ARDS requires PCWP ≤18 (non-cardiogenic). Elevated PCWP (>18) indicates cardiogenic pulmonary edema, not ARDS. Need to exclude heart failure with echo/BNP. Other criteria: within 1 week, bilateral opacities, P/F ratio classification on PEEP ≥5.
This IS required.
This IS required for severity grading.

questions.stem: ARDSNet lung-protective ventilation uses tidal volume of:

choices.feedback: This is the traditional (harmful) tidal volume that causes volutrauma.

Correct! Low tidal volume (6 mL/kg IBW) is the cornerstone of ARDS management. ARDSNet trial: reduced mortality from 40% to 31%. Target plateau pressure ≤30 cmH₂O. Accept permissive hypercapnia (pH >7.25). Calculate IBW from height, not actual weight.
Too low — causes inadequate ventilation and severe hypercapnia.
Far too high — causes barotrauma and worsens ARDS.

questions.stem: Prone positioning in ARDS is indicated when P/F ratio is:

choices.feedback: Mild ARDS does not require proning.

Correct! PROSEVA trial: prone positioning for ≥16h/day in severe ARDS (P/F <150) reduced 28-day mortality from 33% to 16% (~50% reduction). Most impactful non-ventilator intervention. Mechanism: improves V/Q matching, reduces compression atelectasis, improves secretion drainage.
Proning is reserved for moderate-severe ARDS, not mild.
P/F >300 doesn't meet ARDS criteria.

questions.stem: #1 cause of ARDS:

choices.feedback: Trauma is an indirect cause but not #1.

Correct! Pneumonia is the #1 direct (pulmonary) cause. Sepsis is the #1 indirect (extrapulmonary) cause. Together they account for the majority of ARDS cases. Other causes: aspiration, pancreatitis, massive transfusion (TRALI), burns.
Heart failure causes cardiogenic pulmonary edema, which must be excluded to diagnose ARDS.
PE can contribute but is not a primary cause of ARDS.

questions.stem: Conservative vs liberal fluid strategy in ARDS (FACTT trial):

choices.feedback: Liberal fluids worsen pulmonary edema in ARDS.

Correct! FACTT trial: conservative fluid strategy improved oxygenation and shortened ventilator days without increasing organ failure. ARDS lungs are 'leaky' — extra fluid worsens edema. Target CVP <4 or PCWP <8. Diuretics (furosemide) when hemodynamically stable.
Conservative strategy showed significant benefit.
The trial compared fluid volume strategies, not fluid types.

questions.stem: P/F ratio of 80 on PEEP 10 classifies ARDS as:

choices.feedback: Correct! P/F ≤100 on PEEP ≥5 = severe ARDS (mortality ~45%). P/F ratio = PaO2 ÷ FiO2. Example: PaO2 80 on FiO2 1.0 = P/F 80 = severe. This patient needs: low tidal volume, prone positioning, possible paralysis, consider ECMO if refractory.
Moderate = P/F 100-200.
P/F 80 with bilateral opacities and appropriate timing meets severe ARDS criteria.

questions.stem: ECMO in ARDS is reserved for:

choices.feedback: ECMO is resource-intensive and reserved for refractory cases.

choices.text: Refractory hypoxemia despite optimal conventional management
choices.feedback: Correct! ECMO (veno-venous for respiratory failure) is a rescue therapy when optimal ventilation, proning, paralysis, and inhaled vasodilators fail. EOLIA trial showed trend toward benefit. Consider when P/F <80 despite ≥6h optimal management. Best outcomes at experienced ECMO centers.
ECMO is rescue, not first-line. Start with ARDSNet ventilation + proning.
ECMO is never prophylactic.

questions.stem: Permissive hypercapnia in ARDS means accepting:

choices.feedback: Low tidal volumes may lead to high (not low) pCO2.

choices.text: Higher pCO2 levels to keep tidal volumes low (pH >7.25)
choices.feedback: Correct! Low tidal volumes (6 mL/kg) may not fully clear CO2 → pCO2 rises. This is acceptable as long as pH stays >7.25. The mortality benefit of low tidal volumes far outweighs the risk of mild hypercapnia. Exception: contraindicated in elevated ICP (hypercapnia → cerebral vasodilation → raised ICP).

choices.text: Normal pCO2 with high tidal volumes
choices.feedback: High tidal volumes cause volutrauma — the entire point is to accept higher pCO2 for lung protection.
Impossible and not a goal.

questions.stem: A 'silent chest' during asthma exacerbation indicates:

choices.feedback: Silent chest is an ominous sign, not improvement.

choices.text: Severe bronchospasm with minimal air movement (imminent respiratory failure)
choices.feedback: Correct! Silent chest = airways so constricted that insufficient air moves to produce wheezing. This is a pre-arrest sign. Paradox: LESS wheezing = WORSE asthma (not better). Requires aggressive intervention ± intubation.
PTX can cause absent breath sounds but unilaterally; silent chest in asthma is bilateral.
Not related to the acute finding of silent chest.

questions.stem: Normal or rising PaCO2 in asthma exacerbation suggests:

choices.feedback: In asthma, patients hyperventilate → PaCO2 should be LOW. Normal/rising = failure.

choices.text: Respiratory muscle fatigue and impending failure
choices.feedback: Correct! During an asthma attack, hyperventilation → LOW PaCO2 is expected. Normal or RISING PaCO2 = respiratory muscles fatiguing, can no longer maintain increased ventilation. This is a sign of impending respiratory arrest — prepare for intubation.
Resolution would show normalizing respiratory rate with improving symptoms, not isolated PaCO2 rise.
Oxygen is not the cause of rising PaCO2 in asthma.

questions.stem: IV magnesium in acute asthma works by:

choices.feedback: Magnesium is a bronchodilator, not an antibiotic.

Correct! IV MgSO4 2g over 20 min causes bronchial smooth muscle relaxation. Used for severe exacerbations not responding to initial albuterol/steroids. Most effective in severe attacks (FEV1 <25% predicted). Works within minutes.
That's the role of corticosteroids, not magnesium.
Surfactant is relevant to neonatal RDS, not asthma.

questions.stem: Samter triad consists of:

choices.feedback: That's the atopic triad.

Correct! Samter triad (aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease). COX-1 inhibition by NSAIDs shifts arachidonic acid to leukotriene pathway → bronchospasm. Treatment: aspirin desensitization, leukotriene receptor antagonists (montelukast). These patients can typically tolerate acetaminophen and COX-2 selective inhibitors.
That pattern can occur with drug reactions but isn't Samter triad.
These commonly coexist but don't constitute Samter triad.

questions.stem: Key spirometry finding distinguishing asthma from COPD?

choices.feedback: Fixed obstruction is COPD. Asthma is reversible.

choices.text: Reversible obstruction (≥12% and ≥200 mL FEV1 improvement with bronchodilator)
choices.feedback: Correct! Asthma shows significant bronchodilator reversibility. COPD: FEV1/FVC <0.70 post-bronchodilator with minimal reversibility. Note: some COPD patients show partial reversibility (asthma-COPD overlap), and long-standing asthma can develop fixed obstruction.

choices.text: Reduced total lung capacity in asthma
choices.feedback: TLC is normal or increased in asthma (air trapping), not reduced.
DLCO is typically normal in asthma; it's reduced in emphysema.

questions.stem: Controller medication backbone for persistent asthma?

choices.feedback: Albuterol is a rescue medication, not a controller.

Correct! ICS is the cornerstone of asthma controller therapy from Step 2 onward. Reduces airway inflammation, prevents exacerbations, improves lung function. LABA added at Step 3+ (never LABA alone — increases mortality without ICS per SMART trial). Side effects: oral candidiasis, dysphonia (rinse mouth after use).
Theophylline is rarely used now due to narrow therapeutic window and side effects.
Cromolyn is less effective than ICS and rarely used in adults.

questions.stem: Preferred sedation agent if intubating an asthmatic?

choices.feedback: Propofol is acceptable but ketamine has additional bronchodilator properties.

Correct! Ketamine has bronchodilatory properties (catecholamine release + direct smooth muscle relaxation) making it the preferred induction agent for intubation in severe asthma. Also provides analgesia and amnesia. Avoid: morphine (histamine release → bronchospasm), succinylcholine (can trigger bronchospasm in some).
Morphine causes histamine release → can worsen bronchospasm. Avoid in asthma.
Midazolam provides sedation but lacks the bronchodilatory benefit of ketamine.

questions.stem: Biologics for severe asthma target which pathway?

choices.feedback: Biologics target immune/inflammatory pathways, not bacteria.

Correct! Omalizumab: anti-IgE for severe allergic asthma. Mepolizumab/benralizumab: anti-IL-5 for severe eosinophilic asthma. Dupilumab: anti-IL-4Rα (blocks IL-4 and IL-13). Tezepelumab: anti-TSLP. Used for Step 5 severe asthma uncontrolled on high-dose ICS-LABA.
Neutrophilic pathways are more relevant to COPD than asthma.
Antihistamines treat allergic rhinitis/urticaria, not the eosinophilic asthma pathway.

questions.stem: PALM-COEIN: what does the 'O' stand for?

choices.feedback: O stands for Ovulatory dysfunction.

Correct! PALM-COEIN: Polyp, Adenomyosis, Leiomyoma, Malignancy (structural causes) + Coagulopathy, Ovulatory dysfunction, Endometrial, Iatrogenic, Not yet classified (non-structural). Ovulatory dysfunction (anovulation) is one of the most common causes of AUB, especially PCOS.
Ovarian cysts are not part of PALM-COEIN.
Malignancy is represented by M, not O.

questions.stem: First test in a reproductive-age woman with AUB?

choices.feedback: US is important but not first. Rule out pregnancy first.

Correct! ALWAYS rule out pregnancy first in reproductive-age AUB. Ectopic pregnancy, threatened/inevitable abortion, and molar pregnancy can all present with abnormal bleeding. After negative β-hCG: CBC, TSH, coagulation studies (if indicated), pelvic US.
Biopsy is indicated if >45 or with risk factors, but pregnancy test comes first.
CT is not first-line for AUB evaluation.

questions.stem: Most effective MEDICAL treatment for menorrhagia?

choices.feedback: OCPs reduce bleeding ~40% but are not the most effective medical option.

Correct! LNG-IUD reduces menstrual blood loss by >90%, superior to all other medical therapies. Causes endometrial atrophy. Also treats endometrial hyperplasia without atypia. Comparable to hysterectomy for quality of life improvement while preserving fertility. First-line medical therapy for heavy menstrual bleeding.
Tranexamic acid reduces bleeding ~40% — effective but less than LNG-IUD.
Iron treats the resulting anemia but doesn't reduce bleeding.

questions.stem: #1 cause of anovulatory AUB in reproductive-age women?

choices.feedback: Thyroid dysfunction causes AUB but is not the #1 cause of anovulation.

Correct! PCOS is the most common cause of anovulatory bleeding in reproductive-age women. Features: irregular periods, hyperandrogenism (acne, hirsutism), polycystic ovaries on US. Rotterdam criteria: 2 of 3 (oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries). Associated with insulin resistance, obesity, type 2 DM risk.
Endometriosis causes dysmenorrhea and pelvic pain, not typically anovulatory bleeding.
Cervical cancer causes postcoital or intermenstrual bleeding, not anovulation.

questions.stem: Submucosal fibroids cause the MOST bleeding because:
