choices.feedback: Size doesn't determine which type bleeds most.

choices.text: They distort the endometrial cavity and increase surface area
choices.feedback: Correct! Submucosal fibroids project into the uterine cavity, distorting the endometrium and increasing endometrial surface area → heavier menstrual bleeding. Subserosal fibroids (projecting outward) cause bulk symptoms (pressure, urinary frequency) but less bleeding. Intramural (within the wall) cause intermediate symptoms.

choices.text: They have the highest malignant potential
choices.feedback: Malignant transformation is extremely rare for all fibroid types.
Fibroids are estrogen-responsive but don't produce estrogen.

questions.stem: Adolescent with heavy menstrual bleeding since menarche. Screen for:

choices.feedback: Endometrial cancer is rare in adolescents.

Correct! ~20% of adolescents hospitalized for menorrhagia have an underlying bleeding disorder, most commonly von Willebrand disease (vWD — type 1 most common). Screen: CBC, PT/PTT, vWF antigen, vWF activity (ristocetin cofactor), factor VIII. Heavy periods since menarche = red flag for coagulopathy.
BRCA screening is not indicated for menorrhagia.
Ovarian cancer is extremely rare in adolescents.

questions.stem: Postmenopausal AUB — #1 cause?

choices.feedback: Cancer is the most concerning cause but not the most common.

Correct! Atrophy is the #1 cause of postmenopausal bleeding (60-80%). Due to estrogen deficiency. However, endometrial cancer must always be excluded. Workup: TVUS → if endometrial thickness >4mm → endometrial biopsy. ~10% of postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer.
Fibroids shrink after menopause (estrogen-dependent). Rarely cause postmenopausal bleeding.
PCOS is a reproductive-age diagnosis. Not relevant postmenopausally.

questions.stem: Rapidly growing fibroid in a postmenopausal woman raises concern for:

choices.feedback: Fibroids should SHRINK after menopause due to estrogen decline. Growth is concerning.

Correct! Fibroids are estrogen-dependent and should not grow after menopause. Rapid growth postmenopausally raises concern for leiomyosarcoma (uterine sarcoma). Imaging cannot reliably distinguish benign from malignant. Surgical excision recommended. Overall malignant transformation of fibroids is rare (<1%) but must be considered in this scenario.
Endometriosis also regresses postmenopausally.
Torsion presents acutely with pain, not as gradual growth.

questions.stem: RUQ pain, fever, jaundice, hypotension. Most likely diagnosis and emergent intervention?

choices.feedback: Cholecystitis typically does NOT cause jaundice or hypotension. Charcot triad + shock = cholangitis.
Correct! Reynolds pentad: pain + fever + jaundice + AMS + hypotension. Emergent ERCP for biliary decompression. Without it, mortality >50%.
Pancreatitis causes epigastric pain with elevated lipase, not this pattern.
Hepatitis causes jaundice but not acute RUQ pain + fever + shock.

questions.stem: First imaging for suspected gallbladder disease?

choices.feedback: CT is less sensitive (~80%) for gallstones than US.
Correct! First-line: 95% sensitivity for stones, shows wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, CBD dilation, sonographic Murphy sign. Non-invasive, no radiation.
Second-line for cholecystitis if US equivocal.
MRCP evaluates bile ducts, not primary GB test.

questions.stem: Non-visualization of gallbladder on HIDA scan indicates:

choices.feedback: Normal HIDA fills GB within 1 hour. Non-visualization is abnormal.
Correct! Tracer taken up by hepatocytes, excreted into bile. GB non-filling at 4h = obstructed cystic duct = cholecystitis. 97% sensitivity. Gold standard when US equivocal.
CBD obstruction → non-visualization of duodenum, not specifically the GB.
While possible, non-visualization primarily indicates cholecystitis in this clinical context.

questions.stem: Acalculous cholecystitis typically occurs in:

choices.feedback: Young women get symptomatic gallstones. Acalculous is an ICU disease.
Correct! ICU patients (sepsis, burns, TPN, ventilation, trauma). GB ischemia + stasis without stones. 30-50% mortality. Treatment: percutaneous cholecystostomy.
That's stone-related. Acalculous = no stones by definition.
SCD → pigmented gallstones from hemolysis, not acalculous disease.

questions.stem: After ERCP for CBD stones, what additional procedure same hospitalization?

choices.feedback: Only if stones incompletely removed. The key is preventing recurrence.
Correct! Same-admission cholecystectomy prevents 25-30% recurrence rate within 6 weeks. Reduces readmission and recurrent biliary events.
Not indicated after routine CBD stone removal.
Increases bleeding/perforation risk without benefit.

questions.stem: Porcelain gallbladder requires:

choices.feedback: Surveillance is insufficient given the cancer risk.
Correct! Porcelain gallbladder (wall calcification) carries increased gallbladder carcinoma risk. Cholecystectomy is standard recommendation.
ERCP evaluates bile ducts, not GB wall.
Ursodiol dissolves cholesterol stones, not wall calcification.

questions.stem: Most common complication of ERCP?

choices.feedback: Perforation occurs in <1% — rare but serious.
Correct! Occurs in 5-10%. Prevention: rectal indomethacin before/after procedure reduces risk by 50%. Prophylactic pancreatic duct stent also helps.
More commonly the indication for ERCP than a complication.
ERCP focuses on bile duct, not GB.

questions.stem: What distinguishes biliary colic from acute cholecystitis?

choices.feedback: Both can cause RUQ/epigastric pain.

choices.text: Duration >6h, fever, and positive Murphy sign indicate cholecystitis
choices.feedback: Correct! Biliary colic: 30 min-6h, self-resolving, no fever, negative Murphy. Cholecystitis: constant >6h, fever, positive Murphy, leukocytosis, US wall thickening. The 6-hour cutoff is key.
Both have stones. Stones alone don't distinguish them.
Both typically have normal LFTs. Elevation suggests choledocholithiasis.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old presents with 5 days of decreased sleep (feels 'amazing' after 2 hours), pressured speech, spending $10,000 on a shopping spree, and grandiose beliefs that he is a famous musician. He was hospitalized. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: This presentation shows elevated/expansive mood with grandiosity, not depression.

Correct! Elevated mood ≥7 days (or hospitalization) with ≥3 DIG FAST criteria (decreased sleep, impulsivity/spending, grandiosity, pressured speech) = manic episode. One manic episode = Bipolar I disorder. Hospitalization alone qualifies for mania duration criterion regardless of actual days.
Bipolar II has hypomania only — no psychotic features, no hospitalization, no marked impairment. This patient's hospitalization and grandiose delusions indicate full mania.
Cyclothymia involves subthreshold mood symptoms for ≥2 years. This is a full manic episode.

questions.stem: Which psychiatric medication is the ONLY one proven to reduce suicide risk?

choices.feedback: SSRIs treat depression but have not been specifically proven to reduce suicide rates. They may increase suicidality in young adults during initial weeks.

Correct! Lithium is the only psychiatric medication with robust evidence for reducing suicide risk in bipolar disorder (and possibly unipolar depression). This anti-suicidal effect is independent of its mood-stabilizing properties and persists even at subtherapeutic levels. This is a major reason to favor lithium in bipolar patients with suicide risk.
Clozapine reduces suicide risk in schizophrenia specifically (indicated for this), but lithium has broader evidence across mood disorders.
Valproate is an effective mood stabilizer but has not been proven to reduce suicide risk.

questions.stem: A patient on lithium develops tremor, vomiting, ataxia, and confusion. Lithium level is 2.8 mEq/L. What is the management?

choices.feedback: The level is toxic (therapeutic range 0.6-1.2). Increasing the dose would be dangerous.

choices.text: Stop lithium + IV fluids + consider hemodialysis
choices.feedback: Correct! Lithium toxicity (level >1.5, severe >2.5). Mild: tremor, GI. Moderate: ataxia, confusion, fasciculations. Severe: seizures, coma, cardiac arrhythmias. Treatment: stop lithium, aggressive IV normal saline (NS — lithium is renally excreted), hemodialysis for severe toxicity (level >2.5 with symptoms, AKI, or altered consciousness).
While propranolol can treat lithium-induced tremor at therapeutic levels, this patient has severe toxicity requiring lithium discontinuation and aggressive management.
Charcoal does NOT bind lithium (it's a small ion). It is ineffective for lithium toxicity.

questions.stem: Why is valproate absolutely contraindicated in women of childbearing age?

choices.feedback: While valproate is associated with polycystic ovary-like changes, this is not the primary contraindication.

choices.text: It is a potent teratogen causing neural tube defects
choices.feedback: Correct! Valproate causes neural tube defects (spina bifida) in 1-2% of exposed pregnancies (10-20x baseline risk), plus cardiac defects and neurodevelopmental impairment. boxed warning. Must use reliable contraception if prescribed. Lamotrigine or quetiapine are preferred alternatives in women of reproductive age.
Lactation suppression is not the primary concern with valproate.
Valproate may affect fertility but the primary contraindication is its severe teratogenicity.

questions.stem: What distinguishes hypomania from mania?

choices.feedback: Hypomania has a SHORTER minimum duration (≥4 days) than mania (≥7 days).

choices.text: Hypomania has no marked functional impairment and no psychosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Hypomania = elevated mood ≥4 days with DIG FAST symptoms BUT: 1) No marked impairment in functioning, 2) No psychosis, 3) No hospitalization required. If ANY of these occur, it becomes mania. Hypomania may actually feel productive to the patient, making it harder to diagnose.
Hypomania is a distinct elevated mood state. Depressive symptoms would suggest mixed features.
Both mania and hypomania can involve decreased need for sleep.

questions.stem: Lamotrigine is most effective for which aspect of bipolar disorder?

choices.feedback: Lamotrigine is NOT effective for acute mania. It is not indicated for and should not be used for acute manic episodes.

Correct! Lamotrigine's primary role is preventing depressive episodes in bipolar disorder. It has modest acute antidepressant effects and strong evidence for maintenance against depressive relapse. Must be titrated slowly over 6-8 weeks due to risk of Stevens-Johnson syndrome (SJS), especially with concurrent valproate (which doubles lamotrigine levels).
Valproate and atypical antipsychotics are more effective for rapid cycling than lamotrigine.
Psychotic features require antipsychotics. Lamotrigine does not treat psychosis.

questions.stem: An antidepressant is needed for bipolar depression. What is the critical prescribing rule?

choices.text: Use the highest effective SSRI dose for rapid response
choices.feedback: High-dose antidepressants increase mania/switching risk in bipolar disorder.

choices.text: Always combine with a mood stabilizer
choices.feedback: Correct! Antidepressant monotherapy in bipolar disorder is contraindicated — it can trigger mania, rapid cycling, or mixed episodes. If an antidepressant is necessary for bipolar depression, ALWAYS pair it with a mood stabilizer (lithium, valproate) or atypical antipsychotic. Preferred bipolar depression treatments (quetiapine, lurasidone, lamotrigine) often don't require adding an antidepressant.

choices.text: MAOIs are first-line for bipolar depression
choices.feedback: MAOIs are rarely used and carry significant dietary and drug interaction risks. They are not first-line for bipolar depression.

choices.text: Tricyclics are safer than SSRIs in bipolar disorder
choices.feedback: TCAs actually have a HIGHER rate of inducing mania than SSRIs. If an antidepressant is used, SSRIs or bupropion are preferred.

questions.stem: Ebstein anomaly in a newborn is associated with maternal use of which medication during pregnancy?

choices.feedback: Valproate causes neural tube defects (spina bifida), not cardiac anomalies like Ebstein.

Correct! Lithium exposure in the first trimester is associated with Ebstein anomaly — downward displacement of the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle. The absolute risk is lower than historically believed (~1 in 1000 vs. previously quoted 1 in 20), but fetal echocardiography at 18-20 weeks is recommended if lithium was used in the first trimester.
Carbamazepine is associated with neural tube defects and craniofacial abnormalities, not Ebstein anomaly.
Lamotrigine has relatively low teratogenicity and is considered one of the safer mood stabilizers in pregnancy.

questions.stem: #1 cause of small bowel obstruction?

choices.feedback: Cancer is the #1 cause of LARGE bowel obstruction.

Correct! Post-surgical adhesions cause ~60% of SBO. Even a single prior abdominal surgery creates adhesion risk. In patients without prior surgery, hernias are the #1 cause. Always ask about surgical history in a patient with SBO symptoms.
Crohn strictures can cause SBO but account for only ~5%.
Intussusception is more common in children (lead point from Meckel diverticulum or lymphoma in adults).

questions.stem: Sigmoid volvulus on X-ray shows:

choices.feedback: This suggests SBO, not sigmoid volvulus.

Correct! Sigmoid volvulus: massively dilated sigmoid loop rising from the pelvis, creating a 'coffee bean' or inverted U shape. The loop often extends up toward the diaphragm. Inner wall creates the 'crease' of the coffee bean. First-line treatment in stable patient: sigmoidoscopic decompression.
Apple-core = annular colorectal carcinoma on barium enema.
Free air suggests perforation, a complication rather than volvulus itself.

questions.stem: Cecal volvulus treatment differs from sigmoid because:

choices.feedback: Cecal volvulus does NOT resolve spontaneously and requires intervention.

choices.text: It cannot be decompressed endoscopically — requires surgery
choices.feedback: Correct! Unlike sigmoid volvulus (which can be decompressed via rigid sigmoidoscopy), cecal volvulus is treated surgically (right hemicolectomy or cecopexy). Endoscopic decompression of cecal volvulus has high failure rate and risk of perforation. Delay in surgery increases mortality.
Antibiotics don't treat mechanical obstruction.
Cecal volvulus occurs in adults with a mobile cecum.

questions.stem: Ogilvie syndrome treatment?

choices.feedback: Surgery is reserved for failure of medical therapy or perforation.

Correct! Neostigmine inhibits acetylcholinesterase → increases parasympathetic tone → stimulates colonic motility. ~90% response rate. Monitor with telemetry (risk of bradycardia — have atropine ready). If neostigmine fails: colonoscopic decompression. Surgery for perforation or failed conservative measures.
Ogilvie's is a motility disorder, not a simple constipation problem.
Antibiotics don't treat pseudo-obstruction.

questions.stem: Strangulated bowel obstruction — key clinical finding?

choices.feedback: Colicky pain suggests simple obstruction. Strangulation causes continuous pain.

choices.text: Continuous (non-colicky) pain with peritoneal signs, fever, and acidosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Strangulation = compromised mesenteric blood supply → ischemia → necrosis → perforation. Changes from colicky to continuous pain. Peritoneal signs (guarding, rebound), fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, lactic acidosis. CT: bowel wall thickening, mesenteric haziness, pneumatosis. EMERGENT surgery required.
Pain relief in obstruction can paradoxically indicate perforation (pressure release), not improvement.
Painless vomiting suggests gastroparesis, not strangulated obstruction.

questions.stem: Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) in SBO is both:

choices.text: Diagnostic for cancer and therapeutic for adhesions
choices.feedback: Gastrografin doesn't diagnose cancer specifically.

choices.text: Therapeutic (osmotic effect aids resolution) AND prognostic (contrast in colon at 24h predicts resolution)
choices.feedback: Correct! Gastrografin has osmotic properties that draw fluid into the bowel lumen → reduces edema → may help resolve partial adhesive SBO. If contrast reaches colon within 24h = likely to resolve without surgery. If not = higher likelihood of needing operative intervention.

choices.text: Only diagnostic with no therapeutic benefit
choices.feedback: Gastrografin has both diagnostic and therapeutic properties.
Water-soluble contrast is safe. BARIUM is relatively contraindicated (risk of barium peritonitis if perforation).

questions.stem: Cecal diameter >9 cm on imaging indicates:

choices.feedback: Cecal diameter >9 cm is never normal.

choices.text: Risk of perforation — urgent decompression needed
choices.feedback: Correct! Rule of 3-6-9: small bowel >3 cm, colon >6 cm, cecum >9 cm = abnormally dilated. Cecum >9 cm (some say >12 cm) = high risk of perforation due to Law of Laplace (wall tension increases with diameter). Requires urgent decompression.
While volvulus causes dilation, diameter alone doesn't confirm the diagnosis.
Cecal dilation is an acute finding, not a screening indication.

questions.stem: Closed-loop obstruction requires emergent surgery because:

choices.feedback: Closed-loop can occur from volvulus, hernia, or adhesive bands.

choices.text: Bowel is obstructed at two points → rapid pressure rise → ischemia/perforation
choices.feedback: Correct! Two points of obstruction trap a bowel segment → intraluminal pressure rises rapidly (no decompression possible) → venous then arterial compromise → ischemia → gangrene → perforation. CT shows C-shaped or U-shaped loop with mesenteric whirl sign. This is a surgical emergency.
Antibiotic penetration is not the issue.

choices.text: It only occurs in the small bowel
choices.feedback: Closed-loop can occur in both small and large bowel.

questions.stem: 22-year-old: firm, rubbery, mobile breast mass. US: well-circumscribed solid. Most likely?

choices.feedback: Cancer at 22 uncommon. Mobile, well-circumscribed = benign.
Correct! Most common mass in women <35. Firm, rubbery, painless, highly mobile. Observe if <2 cm and stable.
Papilloma presents with bloody discharge, not discrete mobile mass.
Phyllodes: rapidly growing, large, older women (40s).

questions.stem: Most common cause of bloody nipple discharge?

choices.feedback: Can cause bloody discharge but papilloma is more common.
Correct! #1 cause of unilateral bloody discharge from single duct. Benign subareolar tumor. Treatment: duct excision.
Presents as solid mass, not discharge.
Causes pain and clear/straw discharge, not bloody.

questions.stem: Lumpectomy + radiation equals mastectomy for early-stage breast cancer. Key advantage?

choices.feedback: Cost varies with radiation follow-up requirements.
Correct! NSABP B-06, Milan trials: lumpectomy + XRT = equal overall survival to mastectomy for stage I-II. Key advantage: breast conservation.
Systemic therapy based on tumor biology, not surgical approach.
Local recurrence slightly higher with lumpectomy, but OS equivalent.

questions.stem: Complication to monitor with trastuzumab (Herceptin)?

choices.feedback: That's cisplatin.
Correct! HER2 receptors on cardiomyocytes. Reversible cardiomyopathy. Echo every 3 months. Hold if LVEF drops >15% or below 50%.
That's bleomycin.
That's platinum agents and taxanes.

questions.stem: ER+/PR+ breast cancer in a premenopausal woman — endocrine therapy?

choices.feedback: AIs only work in postmenopausal women (block peripheral estrogen conversion). Premenopausal ovaries still produce estrogen.

Correct! Tamoxifen = selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM). First-line endocrine therapy in premenopausal women. Risks: endometrial cancer, VTE. AIs (anastrozole, letrozole) are for postmenopausal patients only.
Trastuzumab targets HER2, not estrogen receptors.
Chemotherapy agent, not endocrine therapy.

questions.stem: Inflammatory breast cancer presents with:

choices.feedback: Inflammatory breast cancer characteristically has NO discrete mass.

choices.text: Erythema, peau d'orange, warmth, NO mass
choices.feedback: Correct! Inflammatory = dermal lymphatic invasion. Skin erythema, edema (peau d'orange = orange peel skin), warmth. Often mistaken for mastitis/cellulitis. NO discrete mass. Worst prognosis of all breast cancers. Biopsy of skin shows tumor emboli in dermal lymphatics.
Bilateral discharge suggests hormonal cause (prolactinoma), not cancer.
Isolated LAD without breast changes is less typical of inflammatory cancer.

questions.stem: BRCA1/2 mutation carriers have what lifetime breast cancer risk?

choices.feedback: This is the general population risk, not BRCA carriers.

Correct! BRCA1/2 mutations: 60-80% lifetime breast cancer risk, 20-40% ovarian cancer risk. Management: enhanced screening (MRI + mammography starting age 25-30), risk-reducing mastectomy, risk-reducing salpingo-oophorectomy, chemoprevention (tamoxifen).
Far below the actual BRCA carrier risk.
Not all BRCA carriers develop cancer, though risk is very high.

questions.stem: Sentinel lymph node biopsy: if SLN is negative, what next?

choices.feedback: Negative SLN = no further dissection needed. This is the whole point of SLNBx.

Correct! Negative SLN means the cancer has not spread to axillary nodes. No axillary dissection → avoids lymphedema, nerve injury, shoulder dysfunction. Even with 1-2 positive SLN, ACOSOG Z0011 showed no benefit to complete dissection in lumpectomy + radiation patients.
No indication for repeat biopsy if initial SLN is negative.
Not standard practice.

questions.stem: Painless, dry, white/leathery wound. Burn depth?

choices.feedback: Superficial burns are painful and red.
Correct! Destroys epidermis, dermis, AND nerves → painless, leathery, white. Cannot heal spontaneously — needs excision + graft.
Superficial partial = very painful with blisters.
Sunburns are red. White/leathery = full thickness.

questions.stem: Parkland formula: first 8h fluids for 70 kg, 30% TBSA?

choices.feedback: Correct! 4 × 70 × 30 = 8,400 total. Half in first 8h = 4,200 mL LR. Monitor UOP 0.5-1 mL/kg/hr.
That's the 24h total, not first 8h.
Only one-quarter of total — underestimates need.
Far below calculated requirement.

questions.stem: Circumferential full-thickness burns with absent pulses. Treatment?

choices.feedback: Fasciotomy is for non-burn compartment syndrome. Burns need escharotomy.
Correct! Rigid eschar cannot expand → compartment syndrome. Escharotomy = incision through eschar (painless in full-thickness burns) to release constriction.
Insufficient for compartment syndrome with absent pulses.
Grafting is definitive wound treatment, not acute compartment management.

questions.stem: #1 cause of death in burn patients after initial resuscitation?

choices.feedback: Occurs but not the leading cause.
Correct! Loss of skin barrier + immunosuppression. Pseudomonas and S. aureus most common. Prevention: topical antimicrobials, early excision/grafting.
Acute injury at time of burn, not delayed.
VTE risk elevated but not #1 cause of death.

questions.stem: House fire: singed nasal hair, hoarseness, carbonaceous sputum. Priority?

choices.feedback: Helpful for assessment but securing airway comes first.
Correct! Airway edema progresses rapidly — intubatable now may not be later. Most time-sensitive burn intervention.
NC doesn't protect airway from progressive edema.
Imaging not the priority when airway compromise suspected.

questions.stem: Curling ulcer occurs in which location?

choices.feedback: Cushing ulcer (brain injury) = stomach. Curling = duodenum.
Correct! Curling ulcer = duodenal stress ulcer in severe burns. Prophylaxis: PPI/H2 blocker. Compare: Cushing ulcer = gastric, from brain injury.
Burn stress ulcers are duodenal.
Curling ulcers specifically affect duodenum.

questions.stem: CO poisoning treatment?

choices.feedback: That treats methemoglobinemia, not CO poisoning.

Correct! CO has 200x affinity for Hb. 100% O2 reduces CO half-life from 4-6h to 60-90 min. Pulse ox unreliable (reads COHb as oxyHb). Check ABG co-oximetry. Hyperbaric for COHb >25%, neuro symptoms, pregnancy.
NAC treats acetaminophen toxicity.
That treats cyanide poisoning.

questions.stem: Silver sulfadiazine should be AVOIDED on:

choices.feedback: Commonly used on extremities without issue.
Correct! Avoided on face due to permanent staining. Use bacitracin instead. Also avoid in: sulfa allergy, pregnancy (kernicterus risk), neonates.
Trunk burns routinely treated with silver sulfadiazine.
Still widely used, just with specific restrictions.

questions.stem: CO binds hemoglobin with what relative affinity compared to O2?

choices.feedback: Much higher than that.

Correct! CO's extreme affinity for Hb means even low concentrations displace significant O2. Additionally, CO shifts the oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve LEFT → remaining O2 is held more tightly → less O2 released to tissues. Double insult: reduced O2 carrying capacity + impaired O2 unloading.
If equal, CO wouldn't be dangerous at low concentrations.
The affinity is much higher — 200-250×.

questions.stem: Why is pulse oximetry unreliable in CO poisoning?

choices.text: CO destroys the pulse oximeter sensor
choices.feedback: The device functions normally; it just misreads.

choices.text: Standard pulse ox cannot distinguish COHb from OxyHb → reads falsely normal
choices.feedback: Correct! Standard pulse oximeters measure light absorption at 2 wavelengths — COHb absorbs similarly to OxyHb at these wavelengths. SpO2 reads normal even with lethal COHb levels. Diagnosis requires CO-OXIMETRY (ABG with co-oximeter) — measures COHb, OxyHb, MetHb separately using multiple wavelengths.

choices.text: CO interferes with the infrared signal
choices.feedback: The issue is wavelength overlap, not interference.

choices.text: Pulse ox only works on arterial blood
choices.feedback: Pulse ox does measure arterial saturation — it just can't distinguish CO-bound Hb.

questions.stem: Multiple family members present with headache and nausea in winter. Suspect:

choices.feedback: While possible, the simultaneous presentation + winter + headache pattern is classic for CO.

Correct! Classic scenario: multiple household members (including pets) ill with headache, nausea, dizziness — especially in winter with heating systems. CO is odorless and colorless. Ask about furnaces, space heaters, generators. Evacuate immediately. Check co-oximetry. 'Headache improving when leaving home' is pathognomonic.
Food poisoning typically has more GI symptoms and wouldn't affect pets the same way.
Lead is chronic, not acute simultaneous presentation.

questions.stem: COHb half-life on 100% O2 via NRB?

choices.feedback: 4-6 hours is the half-life on room air.

Correct! Room air: 4-6h. 100% NRB: 60-90 min. Hyperbaric O2: 20-30 min. High-flow 100% O2 is the immediate first-line treatment — start before co-oximetry results. Continue until COHb <3-5% and symptoms resolve.
20-30 min is the hyperbaric O2 half-life.
That would be dangerously long.

questions.stem: Indications for hyperbaric oxygen in CO poisoning include:

choices.feedback: Mild exposures respond well to 100% NRB alone.

choices.text: LOC, seizures, COHb >25%, cardiac ischemia, pregnancy with significant exposure
choices.feedback: Correct! HBO indications: loss of consciousness/coma, seizures, focal neurologic deficits, COHb >25% (>15% in pregnancy), cardiac ischemia, persistent symptoms despite NRB. HBO reduces COHb half-life to 20-30 min and may reduce delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome risk.
Indications are based on severity, not exposure source.

choices.text: Only if pulse oximetry is low
choices.feedback: Pulse ox is unreliable in CO poisoning — cannot guide treatment decisions.

questions.stem: Delayed neuropsychiatric syndrome after CO exposure occurs:

choices.feedback: DNS is a delayed phenomenon, not immediate.

choices.text: 2-40 days post-exposure: cognitive deficits, personality changes, parkinsonism
choices.feedback: Correct! DNS affects 15-40% of significant exposures. Symptoms: memory loss, confusion, personality changes, movement disorders (parkinsonism), depression. May be permanent. Mechanism: delayed demyelination and neuronal apoptosis. HBO therapy may reduce risk. All significant CO exposure patients need cognitive follow-up at 1-2 weeks.
DNS can occur even with moderate exposures that don't cause initial coma.
Onset is typically within 2-40 days.

questions.stem: Cherry-red skin in CO poisoning is:

choices.text: The earliest and most reliable sign
choices.feedback: This is a dangerous misconception.

choices.text: A late/postmortem finding — not reliable for clinical diagnosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Cherry-red skin/lips are classically taught but are actually a LATE finding, often postmortem. Most living CO patients appear normal or pale/cyanotic. Do not wait for or rely on cherry-red skin. Diagnose with co-oximetry. Clinical suspicion based on context (winter, multiple affected, headache) is more important.

choices.text: Present in all cases with COHb >10%
choices.feedback: Many patients with significant COHb never develop cherry-red skin.

choices.text: A sign unique to cyanide poisoning
choices.feedback: Cherry-red is classically associated with both CO and cyanide, but is unreliable for both.

questions.stem: CO poisoning in pregnancy is especially dangerous because:

choices.feedback: CO freely crosses the placenta.

choices.text: Fetal hemoglobin binds CO with even greater affinity + slower clearance
choices.feedback: Correct! Fetal Hb (HbF) has higher CO affinity than adult Hb → fetal COHb levels are 10-15% higher than maternal levels, and clearance is much slower. Fetal tissue hypoxia occurs at maternal levels that may seem only moderate. Lower threshold for HBO in pregnancy. Consider HBO for any pregnant patient with symptoms or COHb >15%.
100% O2 is safe and essential in pregnant CO patients.

choices.text: CO only affects the mother, not the fetus
choices.feedback: The fetus is actually MORE vulnerable than the mother.

questions.stem: What is the critical immediate intervention?

choices.feedback: Bag-mask ventilation worsens CDH by forcing air into herniated bowel in the chest, further compressing the lung. This is a key teaching point.
Correct! Immediate intubation is critical. Bag-mask ventilation is contraindicated because it inflates bowel in the chest, worsening pulmonary compression. Place an OG tube to decompress the stomach.
The findings suggest CDH, not pneumothorax. Needle decompression could injure herniated abdominal organs. The scaphoid abdomen is the key distinguishing feature.
This is a full-term baby. The problem is mechanical (herniated bowel), not surfactant deficiency. The scaphoid abdomen + absent left breath sounds = CDH.

questions.stem: What embryologic structure fails to form in Bochdalek hernia?

choices.feedback: The septum transversum forms the central tendon (anterior diaphragm). Bochdalek hernias are POSTEROLATERAL.

Correct! The pleuroperitoneal membranes close the posterolateral canals by week 8. Left closes later than right (explaining left-sided predominance). Failure → Bochdalek hernia. The diaphragm has 4 embryologic components: septum transversum, pleuroperitoneal membranes, body wall mesoderm, and esophageal mesentery.
The esophageal mesentery forms the crura (posterior diaphragm around esophagus), not the posterolateral portion.
Body wall mesoderm contributes the peripheral muscular rim of the diaphragm, not the area where Bochdalek hernias occur.

questions.stem: Why is pulmonary hypertension so severe in CDH?

choices.feedback: While the hypoplastic lung has reduced surfactant, the primary problem is the vascular bed.

Correct! CDH causes: (1) fewer pulmonary arteries (hypoplasia); (2) thickened arterial walls with excessive smooth muscle (vascular remodeling); (3) increased vascular reactivity. This 'two-hit' of fewer vessels + higher resistance creates severe, often refractory pulmonary hypertension.
There is mediastinal shift, but the primary driver of pulmonary hypertension is the abnormal pulmonary vascular bed, not cardiac compression.
While pulmonary vasoconstriction contributes, CDH pulmonary hypertension is primarily structural (fewer, thicker vessels), not just functional.

questions.stem: When should surgical repair be performed?

choices.feedback: This was the old approach. Current evidence shows delayed repair after physiologic stabilization improves survival.

Correct! Current practice is delayed repair (typically 24-96 hours or more) after pulmonary hypertension is managed. Surgery does not improve the pulmonary hypertension — the vascular bed is structurally abnormal. Operating on a hemodynamically unstable patient worsens outcomes.
Waiting a full month is too long. Once stable, repair should proceed to allow the lung to expand and develop.

choices.text: Only if ECMO is not needed
choices.feedback: Even patients requiring ECMO undergo repair — either on ECMO or shortly after decannulation.

questions.stem: Which prenatal finding is the strongest predictor of CDH outcome?

choices.text: Side of the hernia (left vs right)
choices.feedback: Right-sided CDH can be more severe (liver herniation), but the strongest predictor is lung-to-head ratio.

Correct! O/E LHR is the best prenatal predictor. Measured on ultrasound: contralateral lung area / head circumference, compared to expected. O/E LHR <25% = severe (survival ~15-20%). O/E LHR >45% = mild (survival >90%). Liver herniation into the chest is also a negative predictor.
Earlier detection may correlate with severity but is not the strongest quantitative predictor.
Bowel volume matters less than the degree of lung hypoplasia, which is directly measured by LHR.

questions.stem: Most important risk factor for C. difficile infection?

choices.feedback: Advanced age increases risk but is not the #1 factor.

Correct! Antibiotics disrupt normal colonic flora → C. diff overgrowth and toxin production. Highest risk: fluoroquinolones, clindamycin, cephalosporins, carbapenems. Risk persists up to 3 months after antibiotic exposure. Can occur with ANY antibiotic, even a single dose.
Diet is not a major risk factor for C. diff.
Immunosuppression increases risk but antibiotics are the primary driver.

questions.stem: C. diff treatment — metronidazole is:

choices.feedback: This is outdated. Metronidazole is no longer first-line per current guidelines.

choices.text: No longer first-line (fidaxomicin or oral vancomycin preferred)
choices.feedback: Correct! 2021 IDSA update: fidaxomicin preferred (lower recurrence rate) or oral vancomycin for initial non-severe and severe CDI. Metronidazole only as adjunct IV in fulminant CDI (when oral vancomycin can't reach colon due to ileus). Metronidazole was inferior to vancomycin in clinical trials.
IV metronidazole is only added in fulminant cases as adjunct. It does not achieve adequate colonic levels alone.

choices.text: Used only for recurrent C. diff
choices.feedback: Recurrent CDI is managed with fidaxomicin, vancomycin taper, or FMT.

questions.stem: Why must vancomycin be given ORALLY (not IV) for C. diff?

choices.text: IV vancomycin is toxic to the colon
choices.feedback: IV vancomycin isn't directly toxic to the colon.

choices.text: IV vancomycin does not reach adequate colonic concentrations
choices.feedback: Correct! Vancomycin given IV achieves negligible colonic luminal concentrations — it's not excreted into the GI tract. Oral vancomycin (or via NG tube) delivers drug directly to the site of infection. IV vancomycin is used for systemic infections like MRSA bacteremia. IV metronidazole DOES reach the colon (hepatic excretion into bile), which is why it's used as IV adjunct in fulminant CDI.

choices.text: IV vancomycin causes red man syndrome only in GI infections
choices.feedback: Red man syndrome is related to rapid IV infusion, not route specificity for infections.
Cost is not the reason — it's pharmacokinetic necessity.

questions.stem: Most effective treatment for recurrent C. diff (≥2 episodes)?

choices.feedback: Extended courses help but recurrence rates remain high.

Correct! FMT restores normal colonic microbiome → competitive exclusion of C. diff. ~90% effective for recurrent CDI after ≥2 recurrences. Delivered via colonoscopy, enema, or oral capsules. approved standardized microbiota products (Rebyota, Vowst) now available. Bezlotoxumab (anti-toxin B mAb) can also reduce recurrence.
Prolonged IV metronidazole has neurotoxicity risk and poor colonic levels.
Probiotics have limited evidence for CDI treatment (may have role in prevention).

questions.stem: E. coli O157:H7 — why should antibiotics be AVOIDED?

choices.text: Antibiotics are ineffective against E. coli
choices.feedback: Antibiotics can kill EHEC but shouldn't be used for this specific strain.

choices.text: Antibiotics increase toxin release → higher risk of HUS
choices.feedback: Correct! Antibiotics lyse EHEC bacteria → massive Shiga toxin release → increased risk of HUS (hemolytic uremic syndrome: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + AKI). HUS is the #1 cause of AKI in children. Triad of bloody diarrhea → HUS. Treatment: supportive (fluids, transfusion, dialysis if needed). Avoid anti-motility agents too.

choices.text: E. coli O157:H7 is a virus
choices.feedback: It's a gram-negative bacterium.
The concern is specifically increased HUS risk from toxin release.

questions.stem: Hand hygiene for C. diff requires:

choices.feedback: Alcohol does NOT kill C. diff spores.

choices.text: Soap and water hand washing (alcohol-based sanitizer ineffective against spores)
choices.feedback: Correct! C. diff spores are resistant to alcohol, quaternary ammonium, and most disinfectants. Mechanical removal via soap-and-water hand washing is required. Environmental cleaning with bleach-based (hypochlorite) solutions. Contact precautions: gown + gloves for all patient interactions.
C. diff requires strict contact precautions + hand washing.
Betadine is not the standard recommendation for C. diff hand hygiene.

questions.stem: Campylobacter jejuni is important because it triggers:

choices.feedback: HUS is triggered by EHEC (E. coli O157:H7), not Campylobacter.

Correct! Campylobacter is the #1 trigger for GBS. Lipooligosaccharide on C. jejuni mimics gangliosides on peripheral nerves → autoimmune demyelination 1-4 weeks after GI illness. Campylobacter-associated GBS tends to be more severe (axonal variant). Also: reactive arthritis.
Toxic megacolon is associated with UC and C. diff, not Campylobacter.
Liver abscess is associated with E. histolytica (amebic) or Klebsiella.

questions.stem: Which infectious diarrhea should ALWAYS be treated with antibiotics?

choices.feedback: NEVER give antibiotics for EHEC — increases HUS risk.

choices.text: Shigella (always treat — highly infectious, low inoculum)
choices.feedback: Correct! Shigella should always be treated: highly contagious (as few as 10-100 organisms), causes invasive bloody diarrhea, can cause seizures in children (Shiga toxin), prevents transmission. Treat with azithromycin or fluoroquinolone. Salmonella: treat only if severe, bacteremia, or immunocompromised (otherwise self-limited).
Norovirus is viral — antibiotics are ineffective. Self-limited.
Rotavirus is viral — supportive care only. Vaccine prevention.

questions.stem: Best initial screening test for celiac disease?

choices.feedback: Anti-EMA is highly specific but less sensitive and more expensive than tTG-IgA.

Correct! tTG-IgA: ~95% sensitive, ~95% specific. Best screening test. MUST check total IgA simultaneously — IgA deficiency (2-3% of celiac patients) causes false-negative tTG-IgA. If IgA deficient: use IgG-based tests (tTG-IgG or deamidated gliadin peptide IgG). Patient must be eating gluten at time of testing.
HLA testing has high NPV (rules out celiac if negative) but poor PPV (30% of general population positive). Not used for initial screening.
Fecal fat assesses malabsorption but is not specific for celiac.

questions.stem: Gold standard for celiac diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Serology screens but biopsy confirms.

Correct! Biopsy shows villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and intraepithelial lymphocytosis (Marsh classification). Must biopsy WHILE patient is on gluten-containing diet. Some guidelines allow diagnosis without biopsy in children with very high tTG-IgA (>10× ULN) + positive EMA + symptoms.
Hydrogen breath test diagnoses lactose intolerance or SIBO, not celiac.
CT is not useful for celiac diagnosis.

questions.stem: Dermatitis herpetiformis is pathognomonic for celiac and shows:

choices.feedback: EN = tender red nodules on shins, associated with IBD/sarcoidosis/infections.

choices.text: Granular IgA deposits at dermal papillae on uninvolved skin biopsy
choices.feedback: Correct! DH: intensely pruritic vesicles on extensor surfaces. Direct immunofluorescence of UNINVOLVED perilesional skin shows granular IgA at dermal papillae. Present even without GI symptoms (but most have villous atrophy on intestinal biopsy). Treatment: dapsone for rapid symptom relief + lifelong GFD.
Linear IgA = linear IgA bullous dermatosis (different condition, often drug-induced).
Anti-desmoglein = pemphigus vulgaris.

questions.stem: IgA deficiency in celiac testing causes:

choices.feedback: IgA deficiency causes FALSE NEGATIVE, not elevated.

Correct! IgA deficiency (prevalence 2-3% in celiac, 10-15× general population) → no IgA production → tTG-IgA, EMA-IgA are falsely negative. Always check total IgA. If deficient: use tTG-IgG or deamidated gliadin peptide (DGP) IgG.
IgA deficiency significantly impacts IgA-based serologic tests.
IgA deficiency doesn't affect HLA genotyping.

questions.stem: PAS-positive macrophages on small bowel biopsy. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Celiac shows villous atrophy and intraepithelial lymphocytes, not PAS-positive macrophages.

Correct! Whipple disease (Tropheryma whipplei): classic triad of arthralgias + diarrhea/malabsorption + weight loss. PAS-positive macrophages on duodenal biopsy are pathognomonic (intracellular bacteria). Also: cardiac, neurologic, ocular involvement. Treatment: IV ceftriaxone → long-term TMP-SMX (1 year).
Crohn has non-caseating granulomas, not PAS-positive macrophages.
Tropical sprue has villous atrophy similar to celiac but no PAS-positive macrophages.

questions.stem: Most common presentation of celiac in adults?

choices.feedback: Classic childhood presentation. Adult presentation is more subtle.

Correct! IDA is the #1 presentation in adults (proximal duodenal/jejunal involvement impairs iron absorption). Many adults have no/minimal GI symptoms ('silent celiac'). Other adult presentations: osteoporosis, unexplained transaminitis, infertility, peripheral neuropathy. Screen for celiac in unexplained IDA.
Weight loss can occur but IDA is more common in adults.
DH is pathognomonic but present in only ~15-25% of celiac patients.

questions.stem: Terminal ileum resection/disease causes deficiency of:

choices.feedback: Iron and folate are absorbed in the duodenum/jejunum (proximal).

Correct! Terminal ileum is the exclusive absorption site for B12 (intrinsic factor-B12 complex) and bile salts. Crohn disease or surgical resection → B12 deficiency (megaloblastic anemia, neurologic deficits) + bile salt malabsorption → diarrhea + fat malabsorption → fat-soluble vitamin deficiency (A, D, E, K).
Vitamin C is absorbed throughout the small bowel and colon.
Zinc is absorbed in duodenum/jejunum.

questions.stem: SIBO (small intestinal bacterial overgrowth) is diagnosed by:

choices.feedback: Stool culture evaluates colonic pathogens, not small bowel overgrowth.

choices.text: Hydrogen/methane breath test (or jejunal aspirate >10⁵ CFU/mL)
choices.feedback: Correct! Glucose or lactulose breath test: bacteria in small bowel ferment substrate → early rise in hydrogen/methane. Gold standard: jejunal aspirate culture (>10⁵ CFU/mL). Risk factors: anatomic (blind loops, strictures, post-surgical), motility disorders (diabetes, scleroderma), PPI use. Treatment: rifaximin (preferred) or ciprofloxacin + metronidazole.
tTG-IgA screens for celiac disease, not SIBO.
Barium studies evaluate anatomy, not bacterial overgrowth.

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Chiari I = cerebellar tonsils herniating ≥5mm below the foramen magnum. Classic presentation: occipital headache worsened by Valsalva maneuvers (cough, strain, laugh). Hand numbness suggests an associated syringomyelia (fluid-filled cavity in the spinal cord).
Chiari II is nearly always associated with myelomeningocele and is diagnosed in infancy/prenatally. This 12-year-old has no spinal dysraphism.
Dandy-Walker involves cerebellar vermis hypoplasia and a large posterior fossa cyst, not tonsillar herniation.
While posterior fossa tumors cause similar headaches, the MRI shows tonsillar herniation without a mass. The Valsalva-worsening pattern is classic for Chiari I.

questions.stem: What is the proposed embryologic basis for the small posterior fossa in Chiari I?

choices.feedback: Correct! The posterior fossa is formed from occipital somites (paraxial mesoderm). If these somites contribute insufficient bone growth, the posterior fossa is too small for the cerebellum → the tonsils herniate through the foramen magnum. This is different from Chiari II, where the small fossa results from CSF diversion through the open spinal defect.
Neural tube closure defects cause spina bifida/anencephaly. Chiari I has a normally closed neural tube — the problem is the bony fossa, not the neural tube.
The cerebellum is normal-sized in Chiari I. The fossa is too small for it — it's a container problem, not a contents problem.
Hydrocephalus can worsen Chiari I but is not the primary cause. The underlying problem is the small posterior fossa.

questions.stem: Why does syringomyelia cause dissociated sensory loss?

choices.feedback: Dorsal columns carry proprioception and vibration, not pain/temperature. Dorsal column damage would cause loss of position sense, not the cape-like pain/temp loss seen here.

choices.text: Disrupts crossing spinothalamic fibers at the anterior commissure
choices.feedback: Correct! The syrinx expands from the central canal, first disrupting the anterior white commissure where spinothalamic (pain/temperature) fibers cross. Light touch is carried by both the dorsal columns AND the anterior spinothalamic tract, so it's relatively preserved. This creates the classic 'dissociated sensory loss' — lost pain/temp with preserved light touch.
The corticospinal tract is lateral, not central. It's affected later as the syrinx expands, causing weakness — but the initial finding is the central sensory dissociation.
DRG are outside the spinal cord. The syrinx is an intramedullary (within the cord) lesion.

questions.stem: What is the primary goal of posterior fossa decompression?

choices.feedback: While some surgeons do cauterize/shrink the tonsils, the primary goal is not tissue removal — it's restoring CSF dynamics.

choices.text: Restore normal CSF flow at the craniocervical junction
choices.feedback: Correct! The surgery enlarges the posterior fossa by removing bone (suboccipital craniectomy + C1 laminectomy) and often opening/patching the dura. This restores CSF flow around the foramen magnum, which reduces the pressure gradient driving syrinx formation. In most cases, the syrinx will decrease or stabilize without direct drainage.
Syrinx shunting is a secondary procedure reserved for cases where the syrinx doesn't improve after PF decompression. Direct drainage addresses the effect, not the cause.
Herniation is typically stable in Chiari I. The surgery addresses the obstructed CSF flow, which is what causes symptoms and syringomyelia.

questions.stem: What causes the brainstem symptoms in Chiari II?

choices.text: Brainstem compression and lower cranial nerve traction
choices.feedback: Correct! In Chiari II, the brainstem herniates along with the cerebellar vermis, creating a characteristic 'kink' at the cervicomedullary junction. This compresses the brainstem and stretches cranial nerves IX, X, XI, XII → stridor (vocal cord paralysis via CN X), apnea (respiratory center compression), dysphagia (CN IX, X). This is a neurosurgical emergency.
While hydrocephalus is common in Chiari II, the brainstem symptoms are directly from the herniation and kinking, not just ICP. However, ensuring VP shunt function is part of the workup.
Syringobulbia can occur in Chiari II but is not the primary cause of acute brainstem symptoms in a neonate. The kinking and compression are the main issue.

choices.text: Infection from the MMC repair site
choices.feedback: Meningitis would cause fever, bulging fontanelle, and systemic signs. The progressive stridor and apnea pattern here is classic for Chiari II compression.

questions.stem: First-line imaging for suspected acute cholecystitis?

choices.feedback: CT is less sensitive than US for gallstones and cholecystitis.

Correct! RUQ US is first-line: fast, no radiation, detects gallstones (95% sensitivity), wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy sign. HIDA scan is gold standard if US is equivocal (non-visualization of GB = cystic duct obstruction).
MRCP evaluates bile ducts (choledocholithiasis), not primary cholecystitis.
Only 10-15% of gallstones are radiopaque. X-ray is insensitive.

questions.stem: What distinguishes biliary colic from acute cholecystitis?

choices.feedback: Both cause RUQ pain. Location alone doesn't differentiate.

Correct! Biliary colic: episodic RUQ pain <6h after fatty meal, self-resolves, no fever/Murphy sign. Cholecystitis: constant pain >6h, fever, positive Murphy sign, leukocytosis. Cholecystitis = inflammation, biliary colic = transient obstruction without inflammation.
Stone type doesn't differentiate the two clinically.
Both occur in similar age groups.

questions.stem: Acalculous cholecystitis — classic setting?

choices.feedback: That's classic for symptomatic cholelithiasis, not acalculous.

choices.text: Critically ill ICU patient (sepsis, TPN, major surgery)
choices.feedback: Correct! Acalculous cholecystitis: GB inflammation without stones in critically ill patients. Mechanism: biliary stasis + GB ischemia from hypoperfusion. Mortality 30-50%. Treatment: percutaneous cholecystostomy (temporizing — many are too sick for OR). HIDA scan best diagnostic test.
Pregnancy increases cholesterol stone risk, not acalculous cholecystitis.
Post-ERCP pancreatitis is more common than post-ERCP acalculous cholecystitis.

questions.stem: Charcot triad for cholangitis:

choices.feedback: These are nonspecific GI symptoms.

Correct! Charcot triad = cholangitis (infected bile duct, usually from CBD stone). RUQ pain + fever + jaundice. Present in ~70% of cases. Reynolds pentad adds AMS + hypotension (septic cholangitis → emergent ERCP for biliary drainage). Do not delay decompression.
This describes pulmonary embolism (Virchow triad concepts).
This describes meningitis.

questions.stem: Gallstone ileus — classic imaging triad?

choices.feedback: This describes biliary obstruction with decompensation, not gallstone ileus.

choices.text: SBO + pneumobilia (air in biliary tree) + ectopic gallstone
choices.feedback: Correct! Rigler triad: SBO (dilated small bowel) + pneumobilia (air in biliary tree from cholecystoenteric fistula) + ectopic gallstone (usually impacted at ileocecal valve — narrowest part of small bowel). Gallstone erodes through GB wall into duodenum → travels distally → impacts. Rare but classic.
Coffee-bean = sigmoid volvulus.
This suggests bowel ischemia with perforation.

questions.stem: Emphysematous cholecystitis — key association?

choices.feedback: Emphysematous cholecystitis is most common in diabetic males.

choices.text: Diabetic men — gas-forming organisms in GB wall
choices.feedback: Correct! Emphysematous cholecystitis: gas-producing organisms (Clostridium, E. coli) infect an ischemic GB wall. Risk: diabetes, male sex, elderly. Imaging: air in GB wall/lumen. 15-20% perforation rate. Treatment: EMERGENT cholecystectomy (high mortality without surgery).
While immunosuppression increases risk, diabetes is the classic association.
PPIs are not associated with emphysematous cholecystitis.

questions.stem: Reynolds pentad adds which TWO features to Charcot triad?

choices.feedback: GI symptoms don't define Reynolds pentad.

choices.text: Altered mental status + hypotension (= septic cholangitis)
choices.feedback: Correct! Reynolds pentad = Charcot triad (RUQ pain + fever + jaundice) + AMS + hypotension. Indicates septic cholangitis — biliary sepsis. This is an emergency requiring immediate ERCP for biliary decompression. Mortality is high without drainage.
While present, these are not the defining additions to Reynolds pentad.
These suggest chronic liver disease, not acute cholangitis.

questions.stem: Timing of cholecystectomy in acute cholecystitis?

choices.text: Delay 6-8 weeks for inflammation to resolve
choices.feedback: Studies show early surgery is superior — delayed surgery has higher complications.

Correct! Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy (within 72h) is preferred over delayed. Benefits: shorter total hospital stay, lower morbidity, lower conversion to open rate, cost-effective. Antibiotics + early surgery is standard of care.

choices.text: Only if antibiotics fail after 2 weeks
choices.feedback: Surgery is first-line, not a fallback after antibiotic failure.
Cholecystectomy is definitive. Antibiotics alone have high recurrence rates.

questions.stem: AST:ALT ratio >2:1 suggests:

choices.feedback: Viral hepatitis typically has ALT > AST (ratio <1).

Correct! AST:ALT >2:1 is characteristic of alcoholic hepatitis. Alcohol damages mitochondria (where AST is concentrated) + depletes pyridoxal phosphate (cofactor for ALT synthesis). ALT rarely exceeds 300 in alcoholic hepatitis. Elevated GGT supports alcohol etiology.
Acetaminophen causes massive transaminase elevation (often >1000) with AST≈ALT.
MASLD typically has ALT > AST (ratio <1) until cirrhosis develops.

questions.stem: SAAG ≥1.1 g/dL indicates:

choices.feedback: Malignant ascites has SAAG <1.1 (exudative).

Correct! Serum-Ascites Albumin Gradient ≥1.1 = portal HTN (cirrhosis, heart failure, Budd-Chiari). SAAG <1.1 = non-portal HTN (peritoneal carcinomatosis, TB peritonitis, nephrotic syndrome, pancreatitis). SAAG is superior to the old transudate/exudate classification for ascites.
Nephrotic ascites has SAAG <1.1.
TB peritonitis has SAAG <1.1 with high protein and lymphocyte predominance.

questions.stem: Diagnosis of SBP requires:

choices.feedback: Culture is positive in only ~60% of SBP. PMN count is diagnostic.

Correct! PMN >250 = SBP. Start empiric antibiotics immediately (ceftriaxone/cefotaxime) — don't wait for culture. Also give IV albumin on days 1 and 3 (1.5 g/kg day 1, 1 g/kg day 3) to prevent hepatorenal syndrome. Most common organism: E. coli.

choices.text: Blood cultures positive for gram-negative rods
choices.feedback: Blood cultures support but don't define SBP.
CT is not used to diagnose SBP.

questions.stem: First-line treatment for hepatic encephalopathy?

choices.feedback: Neomycin (oral antibiotic) was historically used but carries ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity risk. Not first-line.

Correct! Lactulose is first-line: osmotic laxative metabolized by colonic bacteria → produces acidic environment → converts NH3 (absorbed) to NH4+ (trapped, excreted). Titrate to 2-3 soft stools/day. Add rifaximin for recurrent episodes (reduces ammonia-producing bacteria). Always identify and treat the precipitant.
IV sodium benzoate/phenylacetate are used in urea cycle defects, not routine HE.
Flumazenil (BZD antagonist) is for BZD overdose, not HE.

questions.stem: Hepatorenal syndrome is characterized by:

choices.feedback: ATN has FENa >2% and muddy brown casts. HRS has bland sediment.

choices.text: Functional renal failure with FENa <1% and bland sediment in a cirrhotic
choices.feedback: Correct! HRS = intense renal vasoconstriction from splanchnic vasodilation in portal HTN. Kidneys are structurally normal (would work if transplanted). FENa <1%, bland sediment, no improvement with volume (distinguishes from prerenal). Type 1: rapidly progressive (doubling of Cr to >2.5 in <2 weeks). Definitive treatment: liver transplant.
RAS is a separate entity from HRS.
Hep B can cause membranous nephropathy but this is different from HRS.

questions.stem: HCC screening in cirrhotics:

choices.feedback: CT is not used for screening due to cost and radiation.

Correct! All cirrhotics need surveillance: abdominal US ± AFP every 6 months. Early detection allows curative treatment (resection, ablation, transplant within Milan criteria). AFP >400 in a cirrhotic strongly suggests HCC. Confirmed with multiphase CT or MRI showing arterial enhancement with portal venous washout.
Colonoscopy screens for colorectal cancer, not HCC.
Biopsy is not a screening tool.

questions.stem: MELD score components include:

choices.feedback: That describes the Child-Pugh score, not MELD.

Correct! MELD uses bilirubin (liver synthetic function), creatinine (renal function — reflects HRS risk), and INR (coagulation). Predicts 3-month mortality. Used for transplant allocation. MELD-Na adds sodium (hyponatremia worsens prognosis). Higher MELD = higher priority on transplant list.
Transaminases are not part of the MELD score.
Blood counts are not MELD components.

questions.stem: Most common primary liver malignancy?

choices.feedback: CCA is the second most common primary liver cancer.

Correct! HCC is the most common primary liver cancer. Risk factors: cirrhosis (any cause), chronic HBV (can develop HCC even without cirrhosis), aflatoxin B1 (Aspergillus on grains/peanuts). Fibrolamellar variant: young patients WITHOUT cirrhosis, better prognosis.
Adenoma is benign, associated with OCP use.
Hemangioma is the most common BENIGN liver tumor.

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Clubfoot (CTEV) = rigid foot deformity with 4 components remembered by CAVE: Cavus (high arch), Adductus (forefoot turned medially), Varus (heel inverted), Equinus (foot pointed down). The key word is RIGID — it doesn't correct with manipulation. This distinguishes true clubfoot from positional deformity.
Metatarsus adductus involves only the forefoot (medial deviation) with a normal hindfoot. It's usually flexible and self-correcting. Clubfoot involves the entire foot with hindfoot deformity.
Calcaneovalgus is the OPPOSITE of clubfoot — the foot is dorsiflexed (not equinus) and everted (not varus). It's usually positional and benign.
Vertical talus (congenital convex pes valgus) has a rocker-bottom appearance with the sole convex. It's much rarer than clubfoot and is associated with chromosomal abnormalities.

questions.stem: Which structure controls anterior-posterior patterning of the limb (e.g., thumb vs pinky)?

choices.feedback: AER controls PROXIMAL-DISTAL growth (shoulder → fingers). It produces FGFs to maintain the underlying mesenchyme (progress zone) proliferating.

Correct! The ZPA in the posterior limb bud mesenchyme produces Sonic Hedgehog (SHH), which creates an anterior-posterior concentration gradient. High SHH = posterior structures (pinky side). Low SHH = anterior structures (thumb side). Duplicating the ZPA → mirror-image polydactyly (extra digits on the radial/preaxial side).
Lateral plate mesoderm contributes to bone and connective tissue of the limb but doesn't control A-P patterning.
Somites (specifically myotome) provide the muscle precursors that migrate into the limb bud, but they don't pattern the A-P axis.

questions.stem: Why is equinus corrected LAST in the Ponseti sequence?

choices.feedback: Equinus is actually the most resistant component. It's corrected last for anatomical reasons, not because it's mild.

choices.text: Correcting equinus before varus creates a rocker-bottom foot
choices.feedback: Correct! If you try to dorsiflex (correct equinus) while the heel is still in varus, the force goes through the midfoot instead of the ankle → iatrogenic rocker-bottom deformity. The cavus, adductus, and varus must be corrected first so that the calcaneus is properly aligned. Then the Achilles tenotomy can safely correct the equinus.

choices.text: The Achilles tendon needs time to stretch
choices.feedback: The equinus is typically corrected with a percutaneous Achilles tenotomy, not gradual stretching. The tenotomy is done after the other components are corrected.
Equinus does not resolve spontaneously. It requires active correction via Achilles tenotomy as the final step of the Ponseti method.

questions.stem: How does the Achilles tendon heal after tenotomy?

choices.text: Regeneration at a lengthened position over 3 weeks
choices.feedback: Correct! After tenotomy, the tendon ends separate by ~1cm. The gap fills with granulation tissue that matures into functional tendon over 3 weeks (while in the cast). The tendon regenerates at its new, lengthened position, providing the additional dorsiflexion needed. Ultrasound studies confirm full tendon continuity by 6 weeks.

choices.text: Permanent tendon discontinuity compensated by other muscles
choices.feedback: The tendon DOES heal. Full continuity is restored. Infants have remarkable tendon regeneration capacity.

choices.text: Scar tissue formation creating a rigid bridge
choices.feedback: The regenerated tissue is functional tendon, not rigid scar. Histologically, it remodels into organized collagen with normal tensile properties.

choices.text: It doesn't heal — that's why bracing is lifelong
choices.feedback: The bracing is to prevent RELAPSE of the clubfoot deformity, not because the tendon failed to heal. The tendon heals fully.

questions.stem: What is the relapse rate if bracing is not followed?

choices.feedback: Without bracing, relapse is the norm, not the exception. The correction is maintained by the brace during the critical growth period.
Higher than this. Non-compliance is the leading cause of relapse.

Correct! Without bracing compliance, relapse rates reach 60-80%. WITH proper bracing, relapse drops to ~10-15%. This is the most critical counseling point for parents — the casts correct the foot, but the brace MAINTAINS the correction during the growth years when the foot would otherwise revert.
Not 100%, but the majority (60-80%) do relapse. Some mild cases may hold correction, but it's unpredictable.

questions.stem: What does the 'sunset sign' indicate?

choices.text: Parinaud syndrome from pressure on the tectal plate
choices.feedback: Correct! The sunset sign (eyes deviated downward, upper sclera visible above the iris) results from pressure on the tectal plate/superior colliculus by the dilated third ventricle or suprapineal recess. This causes paralysis of upward gaze (Parinaud syndrome). It's one of the most recognizable signs of increased intracranial pressure in infants.
Optic nerve compression would cause vision loss, not eye position changes. The sunset sign is from midbrain compression affecting vertical gaze centers.
CN VI palsy causes failure of abduction (can't look laterally), not downward deviation. CN VI palsy can occur with raised ICP but produces a different finding.
Retinal detachment doesn't cause the sunset sign. The finding is from midbrain compression, not a retinal problem.

questions.stem: What type of hydrocephalus is this?

choices.feedback: Correct! The dilated lateral + 3rd ventricles with a NORMAL 4th ventricle = obstruction at the cerebral aqueduct (aqueductal stenosis). This is NON-COMMUNICATING (obstructive) because CSF cannot flow freely through the ventricular system. Aqueductal stenosis is the most common cause of congenital hydrocephalus.

Communicating hydrocephalus has ALL ventricles dilated (including 4th) because CSF flows freely through the system but isn't absorbed properly. The normal-sized 4th ventricle here indicates a blockage upstream.
NPH is an adult condition (Hakim triad: gait disturbance, dementia, urinary incontinence). This is an infant with clearly elevated ICP.
External hydrocephalus (benign enlargement of subarachnoid spaces) has normal-sized ventricles with enlarged extra-axial spaces. This infant has massive ventricular dilation.

questions.stem: What is the significance of the L1CAM mutation?

choices.feedback: Correct! L1CAM mutations cause X-linked hydrocephalus with stenosis of the aqueduct of Sylvius (HSAS). L1CAM is a neural cell adhesion molecule critical for axon guidance and neuronal migration. The aqueduct is narrowed due to abnormal development of the surrounding neural tissue. HSAS also includes spastic paraplegia, adducted thumbs, and aphasia (mnemonic: CRASH — Corpus callosum hypoplasia, Retardation, Adducted thumbs, Spastic paraplegia, Hydrocephalus).
While aqueductal webs exist, L1CAM mutations cause X-linked recessive hydrocephalus through abnormal neural development, not a structural web.
L1CAM is on the X chromosome (Xq28), making it X-linked recessive. Affected males inherit from carrier mothers. It's not mitochondrial.
L1CAM is a cell adhesion molecule gene, not a tumor suppressor. Posterior fossa tumors are a different cause of hydrocephalus.

questions.stem: What is the most common complication of VP shunts?

choices.feedback: Correct! Shunt malfunction/obstruction is the most common complication, occurring in 30-40% of shunts within the first year. Proximal obstruction (choroid plexus or brain tissue blocking the ventricular catheter) is more common than distal obstruction (peritoneal catheter). Signs: recurrence of hydrocephalus symptoms (bulging fontanelle, sunset sign, irritability, vomiting).
Infection is the SECOND most common complication (5-10% per procedure). It typically occurs within 6 months and is most commonly caused by Staph epidermidis. But malfunction is more common overall.
Over-drainage causing slit ventricles is a recognized complication but less common than obstruction. Programmable valves help prevent this.
Pseudocysts occur but are less common than obstruction. They may indicate low-grade infection.

questions.stem: Which patients are the best candidates for ETV?

choices.feedback: Post-hemorrhagic hydrocephalus is COMMUNICATING (impaired absorption from blood products), so bypassing the aqueduct with ETV doesn't address the underlying problem. ETV works best for obstructive hydrocephalus.

Correct! ETV works best for OBSTRUCTIVE (non-communicating) hydrocephalus in children >6-12 months. Success rate: ~70-80% in children >1 year with aqueductal stenosis. ETV is LESS effective in infants <6 months (higher failure rate due to immature CSF absorption pathways) and in communicating hydrocephalus (the problem isn't at the aqueduct).

choices.text: All hydrocephalus patients regardless of type
choices.feedback: ETV is not appropriate for all types. Communicating hydrocephalus and very young infants do better with VP shunts.
NPH is communicating hydrocephalus. ETV has limited efficacy for communicating types. NPH typically responds to large-volume lumbar puncture and LP shunting.

questions.stem: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! CLE (also called congenital lobar overinflation) = progressive hyperinflation of a pulmonary lobe. Most common in LUL (43%), then RML (32%), then RUL (21%). Air enters on inspiration but traps on expiration (ball-valve mechanism). Causes: deficient bronchial cartilage (50%), external compression, idiopathic. More common in males.
Pneumothorax shows absent lung markings in the affected hemithorax. CLE shows hyperinflated lung WITH bronchovascular markings preserved.
CPAM typically shows cystic changes, not diffuse lobar hyperinflation. CLE has overinflated but structurally normal alveoli.
CDH shows bowel loops in the chest, typically left-sided. CLE shows hyperinflated LUNG tissue with bronchovascular markings.

questions.stem: During which stage of lung development does branching morphogenesis (airway formation) primarily occur?

choices.feedback: The embryonic stage involves initial lung bud formation from the foregut, not the detailed branching of airways.

Correct! The pseudoglandular stage (weeks 5-17) completes all conducting airway branching (through terminal bronchioles). Called 'pseudoglandular' because the histology resembles a gland. By week 17, the airway tree is complete — no new airways form after this. The lung looks like a gland with no gas exchange capability yet.
The canalicular stage involves vascularization and beginning of respiratory bronchiole/primitive alveoli formation. Conducting airways are already complete.
The saccular stage forms terminal sacs that will become alveoli. Airways are already fully formed by this point.

questions.stem: What feature distinguishes bronchopulmonary sequestration from CPAM?

choices.text: Sequestration has a systemic arterial blood supply (from the aorta)
choices.feedback: Correct! Sequestration = non-functioning lung tissue with a SYSTEMIC arterial supply (usually from the thoracic or abdominal aorta), NOT connected to the normal tracheobronchial tree. Intralobar (75%): within visceral pleura, drains via pulmonary veins — presents with recurrent pneumonia. Extralobar (25%): own pleural covering, systemic venous drainage — often found incidentally or with CDH.

choices.text: Sequestration always occurs on the right side
choices.feedback: Both CPAM and sequestration can occur on either side, though sequestrations are more common on the left lower lobe. Laterality alone doesn't distinguish them.

choices.text: CPAM has malignant potential but sequestration does not
choices.feedback: Both can have malignant potential (CPAM → pleuropulmonary blastoma; sequestration → rarely). But the defining feature of sequestration is the systemic blood supply.

choices.text: Sequestration communicates with the airway; CPAM does not
choices.feedback: It's the opposite — sequestration typically does NOT communicate with the tracheobronchial tree (that's what makes it 'sequestered').

questions.stem: At what gestational age is surfactant first produced, and when is it adequate for extrauterine life?

choices.text: First at 28 weeks, adequate at 36 weeks
choices.feedback: Surfactant production begins much earlier than 28 weeks, though it doesn't reach adequate levels until later.

choices.text: First at 20 weeks, adequate at 35-36 weeks
choices.feedback: Correct! Type II pneumocytes first produce surfactant at ~20 weeks (canalicular stage), but adequate levels for extrauterine viability aren't reached until 35-36 weeks. Lecithin/sphingomyelin (L/S) ratio ≥2 and presence of phosphatidylglycerol (PG) indicate lung maturity. Antenatal steroids (betamethasone) accelerate surfactant production if preterm delivery is anticipated.

choices.text: First at 35 weeks, adequate at birth
choices.feedback: Surfactant begins much earlier (20 weeks). The canalicular stage is when Type II cells differentiate.

choices.text: First at 12 weeks, adequate at 24 weeks
choices.feedback: 12 weeks is during the pseudoglandular stage — Type II cells haven't yet differentiated. Surfactant begins at the canalicular stage (~20 weeks).

questions.stem: The trachea and esophagus both develop from which embryological structure?

choices.feedback: Neural crest contributes cartilage and connective tissue to the trachea, but the epithelial lining (the tube itself) is endoderm from the foregut.

Correct! Both the trachea and esophagus arise from the foregut. During week 4, the respiratory diverticulum (lung bud) sprouts from the ventral foregut. The tracheoesophageal septum grows caudal-to-cranial to separate the ventral trachea from the dorsal esophagus. Failure of this septum → tracheoesophageal fistula (TEF). The most common type (85%) = esophageal atresia with distal TEF.
Mesoderm forms the muscular wall and cartilage, but the epithelial lining of both structures is endoderm from the foregut.
The notochord induces the neural plate and floor plate, not the respiratory or GI tract epithelium.

questions.stem: Most effective reversible contraception?

choices.feedback: OCPs have 7% typical-use failure rate. LARCs are superior.

Correct! Long-acting reversible contraceptives: subdermal implant (<0.1% failure) and IUDs (copper <1%, LNG-IUD <1%). 'Set and forget' — no adherence issues. ACOG recommends as first-line for all women including nulliparous and adolescents.
DMPA has 4-6% typical-use failure rate.
Condoms have ~13% typical-use failure. Important for STI prevention but less effective for pregnancy prevention alone.

questions.stem: Estrogen-containing contraceptives are CONTRAINDICATED in migraine with aura because:

choices.feedback: The concern is not headache frequency.

Correct! Migraine with aura is an independent stroke risk factor. Adding estrogen (which increases thrombotic risk) creates unacceptable stroke risk. Migraine WITHOUT aura = combined contraceptives are acceptable. Migraine WITH aura = progestin-only methods only. This is one of the most commonly tested contraindications.

choices.text: Estrogen causes seizures in migraine patients
choices.feedback: Estrogen does not cause seizures.
The risk is inherent to the combination of aura + estrogen, not a drug interaction.

questions.stem: Most effective emergency contraception?

choices.feedback: Plan B is ~89% effective and decreases with time and weight.

Correct! Copper IUD is >99% effective as emergency contraception when placed within 5 days of unprotected intercourse. Also serves as ongoing contraception for 10-12 years. Ulipristal (Ella) is second-most effective (~98%, up to 5 days). Plan B is least effective of the three options.
Yuzpe method is older and less effective with more side effects.
Mifepristone is used for medical abortion, not standard emergency contraception in the US.

questions.stem: Which contraceptive method also protects against STIs?

choices.feedback: IUDs prevent pregnancy but not STIs.

Correct! Condoms are the ONLY contraceptive method that provides STI protection (including HIV). Dual method use (condom + LARC or hormonal method) recommended for both pregnancy and STI prevention. Female condoms (internal condoms) also provide STI protection.
OCPs provide no STI protection.
DMPA prevents pregnancy only.

questions.stem: A breastfeeding mother <6 weeks postpartum should AVOID:

choices.feedback: Copper IUD is safe during breastfeeding (hormone-free).

Correct! Estrogen can decrease breast milk supply, especially in early postpartum. Avoid combined hormonal contraceptives (OCP, patch, ring) until ≥6 weeks postpartum (some guidelines say ≥6 months). Progestin-only methods are safe: mini-pill, implant, LNG-IUD, DMPA.
Progestin-only methods are safe and do not affect milk supply.
Barrier methods are always safe.

questions.stem: Bilateral salpingectomy is increasingly preferred over tubal ligation because:

choices.feedback: Neither procedure is easily reversible. Bilateral salpingectomy is actually less reversible.

choices.text: It reduces ovarian cancer risk (serous carcinoma arises from fallopian tube)
choices.feedback: Correct! Growing evidence that high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma originates from the fallopian tube fimbriae. Bilateral salpingectomy removes this tissue, potentially reducing ovarian cancer risk by 40-65%. ACOG supports opportunistic salpingectomy when permanent sterilization is desired.

choices.text: It is less invasive than tubal ligation
choices.feedback: Salpingectomy is slightly more extensive surgically.
Both are permanent sterilization methods.

questions.stem: Plan B (levonorgestrel) efficacy decreases with:

choices.feedback: Age is not the primary factor affecting efficacy.

choices.text: Body weight >75 kg and time delay
choices.feedback: Correct! Levonorgestrel efficacy decreases significantly with higher BMI (body weight >75 kg / BMI >25). Also less effective after 72h. For overweight patients or if >72h: ulipristal (Ella) or copper IUD are better options. Copper IUD is the most effective regardless of weight.
Most antibiotics don't interact with Plan B (rifampin is an exception).
Repeated use doesn't decrease efficacy.

questions.stem: Copper IUD mechanism of contraception:

choices.feedback: Copper IUD is hormone-free and does not affect ovulation.

choices.text: Copper ions are toxic to sperm, creating an inflammatory intrauterine environment that prevents fertilization
choices.feedback: Correct! Copper ions create a sterile inflammatory reaction toxic to sperm and ova, preventing fertilization. Also alters endometrial lining to prevent implantation. No hormonal effects → no systemic side effects. Can increase menstrual bleeding/cramping. Effective for 10-12 years.
The IUD doesn't physically block the cervix.
Cervical mucus thickening is a progestin effect. Copper IUD is hormone-free.

questions.stem: SpO2 target in COPD exacerbation?

choices.feedback: Excessive O2 in COPD suppresses hypoxic ventilatory drive → CO2 retention → respiratory acidosis.
Correct! Target 88-92% — enough to prevent tissue hypoxia without suppressing respiratory drive. Use Venturi mask for precise FiO2 delivery. Higher targets increase mortality in COPD (DECAF study).
Too low — would cause significant tissue hypoxia.
Appropriate for most patients but NOT for COPD with chronic CO2 retention.

questions.stem: BiPAP (NIV) in COPD exacerbation is indicated when:

choices.feedback: SpO2 90% may be adequate for COPD. BiPAP is for respiratory acidosis.

Correct! BiPAP reduces work of breathing, improves ventilation, corrects acidosis. Reduces intubation by 50% and mortality. Contraindications: hemodynamic instability, AMS/inability to protect airway, facial trauma, copious secretions.

choices.text: Any COPD patient presenting to ED
choices.feedback: Not all exacerbations need BiPAP — only those with respiratory acidosis.
BiPAP is used to PREVENT intubation, not after it fails.

questions.stem: Only TWO interventions reduce mortality in stable COPD:

choices.feedback: Inhalers reduce symptoms/exacerbations but do NOT reduce mortality.

choices.text: Long-term supplemental O2 and smoking cessation
choices.feedback: Correct! NOTT/MRC trials: O2 for ≥15 h/day improves survival when PaO2 ≤55 (or ≤59 with polycythemia/cor pulmonale/edema). Smoking cessation is the only intervention that slows FEV1 decline. All inhalers are symptom management only.
Theophylline has minimal mortality benefit. Pulmonary rehab improves exercise capacity and quality of life.
These reduce exacerbation frequency but are not proven to reduce mortality.

questions.stem: #1 cause of COPD?

choices.feedback: Air pollution contributes but is not the primary cause.

Correct! Cigarette smoking causes 85-90% of COPD. The toxic particles/gases cause chronic inflammation → mucus hypersecretion, airway remodeling, and alveolar destruction. Second most common cause: alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency (young, non-smoker, lower lobe panacinar emphysema).
Asthma and COPD are distinct diseases, though asthma-COPD overlap exists.
AAT deficiency causes 1-3% of COPD, not the majority.

questions.stem: Systemic steroid regimen for AECOPD?

choices.feedback: That dose is for MS relapses. COPD uses much lower doses.
