Correct! REDUCE trial: 5-day course of prednisone 40 mg is as effective as 14 days with fewer side effects. Reduces treatment failure and accelerates recovery. Longer courses increase hyperglycemia and infection risk without additional benefit.
Hydrocortisone stress dosing is for adrenal crisis, not COPD.
Single-dose steroids insufficient for AECOPD treatment.

questions.stem: Alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency should be suspected when:

choices.text: Elderly smoker with upper lobe emphysema
choices.feedback: That's typical smoking-related COPD. AAT deficiency has a different pattern.

choices.text: Young non-smoker with lower lobe panacinar emphysema
choices.feedback: Correct! AAT deficiency: onset <45 years, minimal/no smoking history, lower lobe predominant, panacinar pattern (vs centrilobular in smoking). Also causes liver disease (cirrhosis). Diagnosis: serum AAT level + phenotype testing. Treatment: IV augmentation therapy.
Chronic cough has many causes; AAT deficiency is specific to early-onset emphysema.
Upper lobe cavitation suggests TB or Klebsiella, not AAT deficiency.

questions.stem: #1 bacterial cause of COPD exacerbation?

choices.feedback: Pseudomonas is important in severe COPD/bronchiectasis but not #1 overall.

Correct! H. influenzae is the most common bacterial pathogen in AECOPD. Others: M. catarrhalis, S. pneumoniae. Note: most exacerbations are triggered by viral URIs (rhinovirus, influenza). Antibiotics indicated for purulent sputum, severe exacerbation, or mechanical ventilation.
S. aureus is more common in HAP/VAP than AECOPD.
Klebsiella is more associated with aspiration/alcoholism.

questions.stem: Spirometry finding that defines COPD?

choices.feedback: That's normal or restrictive pattern. Obstruction shows reduced ratio.

Correct! Post-bronchodilator FEV1/FVC <0.70 = obstruction. 'Post-bronchodilator' distinguishes COPD (fixed obstruction) from asthma (reversible obstruction). GOLD classification uses FEV1 % predicted for severity: GOLD 1 ≥80%, GOLD 2 50-79%, GOLD 3 30-49%, GOLD 4 <30%.
Reduced TLC = restrictive disease. COPD shows increased TLC (air trapping).
Significant improvement (≥12% and 200 mL) suggests asthma, not COPD.

questions.stem: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! The absent cavum septum pellucidum, parallel ventricles (instead of converging anteriorly where the genu of CC should be), colpocephaly (dilated occipital horns), and elevated 3rd ventricle (riding between the hemispheres) are the classic prenatal signs of ACC.
HPE has FUSED ventricles (single monoventricle) with absent falx. ACC has SEPARATED but parallel ventricles with a present falx. The hemispheres cleave normally in ACC.
Hydrocephalus dilates ALL parts of the ventricles uniformly. Colpocephaly specifically dilates the OCCIPITAL horns while the frontal horns are narrow — this pattern is specific to ACC.
SOD includes absent CSP but has NORMAL corpus callosum (usually). The parallel ventricles and elevated 3rd ventricle point to ACC.

questions.stem: In what order does the corpus callosum develop?

choices.feedback: The CC develops anterior to posterior, not posterior to anterior.

Correct! The CC develops from front to back: genu (week 12) → body → splenium (week 18) → rostrum (week 20, forms LAST by bending back). This explains why PARTIAL agenesis typically affects the posterior portions (splenium and rostrum) — if development stops early, the anterior parts may form but the posterior parts don't.
Development is sequential, not simultaneous. This is clinically important for interpreting partial ACC.
Despite being the most anterior part, the rostrum forms LAST by retroflexion. The genu develops first.

questions.stem: Which syndrome classically includes agenesis of the corpus callosum?

choices.feedback: T21 is associated with AVSD, duodenal atresia, and Brushfield spots, not typically ACC.

Correct! Aicardi syndrome = the classic triad of ACC + infantile spasms (West syndrome) + chorioretinal lacunae (distinctive eye finding). It's X-linked dominant (lethal in males, seen almost exclusively in females and XXY males). Other ACC syndromes: Andermann (ACC + peripheral neuropathy), FG syndrome (ACC + intellectual disability + hypotonia).
Turner is associated with coarctation of the aorta, horseshoe kidney, and cystic hygroma, not ACC.
Marfan involves aortic root dilation, lens subluxation, and skeletal features, not ACC.

questions.stem: What are Probst bundles?

choices.text: Callosal axons that failed to cross and run longitudinally instead
choices.feedback: Correct! In ACC, the axons that would normally cross the midline through the CC instead form thick longitudinal bundles (Probst bundles) that run along the medial walls of the lateral ventricles. They connect ipsilateral frontal to occipital regions instead of crossing to the opposite hemisphere. This is why the lateral ventricles have their characteristic parallel, widely-spaced appearance in ACC.

choices.text: Ectopic gray matter nodules along the ventricles
choices.feedback: Periventricular heterotopia is ectopic gray matter from failed neuronal migration. Probst bundles are WHITE matter (misdirected axonal tracts).

choices.text: Thickened ependymal lining from CSF pressure
choices.feedback: Probst bundles are structural white matter tracts, not reactive changes. They're axons that took the wrong path.

choices.text: Remnants of the fetal glial sling
choices.feedback: The glial sling is a guidance structure that normally degrades. Probst bundles are misdirected callosal axons, not glial remnants.

questions.stem: What cognitive profile is typical of isolated ACC?

choices.feedback: Severe ID occurs in syndromic ACC but is NOT typical of isolated ACC. Many individuals with isolated ACC have normal or near-normal IQ.

choices.text: Normal IQ with deficits in complex processing and social cognition
choices.feedback: Correct! Isolated ACC typically shows: normal basic IQ but impaired complex/novel problem-solving, difficulty with social inference and humor comprehension, reduced processing speed, challenges with abstract language. These deficits reflect the CC's role in interhemispheric integration — combining information processed differently by each hemisphere.
While some individuals with ACC are remarkably compensated, careful neuropsychological testing usually reveals subtle deficits in complex integration tasks.

choices.text: Isolated motor deficits with preserved cognition
choices.feedback: The CC primarily connects association cortices. Motor deficits are not the primary issue — cognitive integration deficits are more characteristic.

questions.stem: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! CPAM (formerly CCAM) Type 1 has large cysts (>2cm, often dominant cyst), accounts for 65% of CPAMs, and has the best prognosis. The pulmonary arterial supply distinguishes it from sequestration (which has systemic/aortic supply). Fetal hydrops from mediastinal shift is the most concerning prenatal complication.
CDH shows bowel loops (not cysts) in the chest with absent diaphragm. Multicystic appearance with pulmonary arterial supply = CPAM.
Sequestration receives SYSTEMIC (aortic) supply. This lesion has PULMONARY arterial supply → CPAM. However, 'hybrid lesions' (CPAM + sequestration features) do exist.
CLE shows a hyperinflated (not cystic) lobe, typically diagnosed postnatally when the lobe fails to deflate.

questions.stem: What prenatal metric best predicts the risk of fetal hydrops from CPAM?

choices.feedback: Correct! CVR = (length × height × width × 0.52) / head circumference. CVR >1.6 indicates high risk (~80%) of developing hydrops. CVR <1.6 has ~<5% hydrops risk. Serial CVR measurements guide management: if CVR rising and approaching 1.6, closer surveillance is needed. If hydrops develops, fetal intervention (steroids for microcystic, thoracoamniotic shunt for macrocystic) may be lifesaving.
The number of cysts is less important than the total volume of the lesion relative to the fetus (CVR). One large cyst can be more dangerous than many small ones.
AFP is not used for CPAM monitoring. AFP is elevated in open neural tube defects and certain other conditions, not lung lesions.
While Doppler is useful for monitoring fetal wellbeing, the CVR is the specific metric for CPAM hydrops risk prediction.

questions.stem: During which stage of lung development do alveoli begin to form?

choices.feedback: The pseudoglandular stage creates the conducting airways (bronchi/bronchioles) but no gas exchange structures. The lung looks like a gland histologically.
The canalicular stage forms respiratory bronchioles and begins vascularization, but TRUE alveoli don't appear until later.
The saccular stage forms terminal sacs (primitive air spaces) and type II pneumocytes begin producing surfactant at ~24 weeks, but mature alveoli form later.

Correct! TRUE alveoli with mature thin walls begin forming at ~36 weeks and continue multiplying until age 8. At birth: ~50 million alveoli. By age 8: ~300-400 million. This is why premature infants have limited gas exchange surface area and why lung development continues postnatally.

questions.stem: What is a bronchogenic cyst embryologically?

choices.text: Foregut duplication from abnormal ventral budding
choices.feedback: Correct! Bronchogenic cysts are foregut duplication cysts arising from abnormal budding of the tracheobronchial tree during early development (weeks 3-7). The aberrant bud separates and develops into a cyst lined by respiratory epithelium with cartilage, smooth muscle, and sometimes mucous glands in its wall — recapitulating normal airway wall structure.
Vitelline duct remnants produce Meckel diverticulum in the ileum, not mediastinal cysts.
Lymphatic malformations (cystic hygroma) are lined by endothelium, not respiratory epithelium, and don't have cartilage in their walls.
Thymic cysts are in the anterior mediastinum and lined by thymic epithelium. Bronchogenic cysts are middle/posterior mediastinum with respiratory epithelium.

questions.stem: Which congenital lung lesion has a recognized risk of malignancy?

choices.feedback: Correct! CPAM (especially Type 1 and Type 4) carries a small risk (~2-4%) of malignancy, specifically pleuropulmonary blastoma (PPB) and bronchioloalveolar carcinoma. Type 4 CPAM and Type I PPB can be histologically identical on prenatal imaging — only pathology distinguishes them. This malignancy risk is the primary argument for elective resection of even asymptomatic CPAMs.
Sequestrations have minimal malignancy risk. They're resected for recurrent infections, not cancer prevention.

choices.text: Congenital lobar emphysema — risk of lymphoma
choices.feedback: CLE has no significant malignancy risk. It's managed for respiratory symptoms, not cancer prevention.

choices.text: None — congenital lung lesions have no malignancy risk
choices.feedback: CPAM does have a recognized malignancy risk, particularly for pleuropulmonary blastoma.

questions.stem: What is the embryological basis of craniosynostosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Craniosynostosis = premature fusion of one or more cranial sutures. Growth is restricted PERPENDICULAR to the fused suture (Virchow's law) and compensates by growing PARALLEL to it. Sagittal synostosis (most common, 40-55%) → scaphocephaly because the skull cannot widen but grows excessively front-to-back.

choices.text: Failure of neural crest migration to calvarium
choices.feedback: Neural crest cells form the frontal and parietal bones, but craniosynostosis is PREMATURE fusion, not failure of formation.
Increased ICP can result FROM craniosynostosis (restricted brain growth), but it's not the cause. The suture fuses first.
Positional plagiocephaly (flat head from sleep position) does NOT involve suture fusion — the sutures remain open. This is an important distinction.

questions.stem: A baby has a triangular forehead shape (trigonocephaly) with a palpable ridge along the midline forehead. Which suture is prematurely fused?

choices.feedback: Sagittal fusion causes scaphocephaly (long/narrow head), not triangular forehead.

Correct! Metopic synostosis → trigonocephaly (triangular forehead with midline frontal ridge, hypotelorism/close-set eyes). The metopic suture normally closes by age 2 (the only suture that closes in infancy). Premature closure before age 2 causes trigonocephaly. Metopic synostosis is the second most common single-suture craniosynostosis.
Unilateral coronal synostosis causes anterior plagiocephaly (asymmetric forehead with ipsilateral flattening and contralateral bossing), not trigonocephaly.
Lambdoid synostosis causes posterior plagiocephaly (unilateral occipital flattening). It's the rarest single-suture synostosis.

questions.stem: Which finding would suggest a SYNDROMIC craniosynostosis requiring genetic evaluation?

choices.text: Isolated sagittal synostosis in an otherwise normal infant
choices.feedback: Isolated single-suture synostosis is usually non-syndromic and sporadic. Genetic testing is not routinely needed unless there are additional features.

choices.text: Multiple suture synostosis with limb anomalies (syndactyly)
choices.feedback: Correct! Multi-suture synostosis + limb anomalies (especially syndactyly) = syndromic craniosynostosis. Think Apert syndrome (FGFR2 mutation): bilateral coronal synostosis + symmetric syndactyly of hands and feet (mitten hands). Also Crouzon syndrome (FGFR2): multi-suture without limb involvement. Pfeiffer syndrome (FGFR1/2): broad thumbs/great toes.

choices.text: Sagittal synostosis with an open anterior fontanelle
choices.feedback: The anterior fontanelle can remain open even with sagittal synostosis — they are different structures. This alone doesn't suggest a syndrome.

choices.text: Family history of large head size
choices.feedback: Familial macrocephaly is a benign condition unrelated to craniosynostosis.

questions.stem: What is the PRIMARY goal of surgical treatment for craniosynostosis?

choices.feedback: Cosmesis is important but NOT the primary goal. The main concern is preventing increased intracranial pressure from restricted brain growth.

choices.text: Allow normal brain growth and prevent increased ICP
choices.feedback: Correct! The primary goal is to allow normal brain growth by removing the restriction. Untreated multi-suture synostosis can cause increased ICP → papilledema, developmental delay, and cognitive impairment. Single-suture synostosis has lower ICP risk but can still cause problems. Cosmetic improvement is an important secondary benefit.
Craniosynostosis does not directly cause seizures. ICP elevation can cause developmental issues, but seizure prevention is not the primary surgical indication.
The fontanelle is not closed surgically — it closes naturally. Surgery REMOVES fused bone to allow growth.

questions.stem: What clinical finding distinguishes positional plagiocephaly from lambdoid synostosis?

choices.text: CT scan is required — clinical exam cannot distinguish them
choices.feedback: Clinical exam CAN often distinguish them. CT is reserved for equivocal cases to avoid radiation. The ear position is the key clinical finding.

choices.text: In positional plagiocephaly, the ipsilateral ear shifts ANTERIOR; in lambdoid synostosis, it shifts POSTERIOR
choices.feedback: Correct! Positional plagiocephaly: parallelogram shape — ipsilateral ear shifts ANTERIORLY, ipsilateral forehead bossing. Lambdoid synostosis: trapezoid shape — ipsilateral ear shifts POSTERIORLY and inferiorly, ipsilateral mastoid bulge. Positional is common (1 in 300). Lambdoid synostosis is very rare (<5% of all synostosis).

choices.text: Positional plagiocephaly causes raised intracranial pressure
choices.feedback: Positional plagiocephaly does NOT cause raised ICP because all sutures are open. It's a cosmetic issue that improves with repositioning.
Positional plagiocephaly is managed conservatively: tummy time, repositioning, helmet therapy if severe. Surgery is NOT needed.

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Dandy-Walker malformation = the classic triad: (1) Cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle, (2) Complete or partial agenesis of the cerebellar vermis, (3) Enlarged posterior fossa with upward displacement of the tentorium. This is distinct from a mega cisterna magna (normal vermis, normal 4th ventricle) and Dandy-Walker variant (partial vermian hypoplasia, normal posterior fossa size).
Arachnoid cysts are extra-axial and don't communicate with the fourth ventricle. They don't cause vermian hypoplasia.
Mega cisterna magna has an enlarged cisterna magna but NORMAL cerebellar vermis and NORMAL fourth ventricle size. It's a benign normal variant.
Chiari II has a SMALL posterior fossa with herniation downward. Dandy-Walker has an ENLARGED posterior fossa with a cystic 4th ventricle expanding it. Opposite directions.

questions.stem: What is the proposed embryologic mechanism for Dandy-Walker malformation?

choices.text: Failed development of the roof of the fourth ventricle and cerebellar vermis
choices.feedback: Correct! The cerebellar vermis develops from the fusion of the two rhombic lips across the midline. In DWM, this fusion fails → vermian agenesis/hypoplasia. The roof of the fourth ventricle (formed by the anterior medullary velum and choroid plexus) also fails to develop properly → the fourth ventricle expands cystically and balloons the posterior fossa.

choices.text: Obstruction of the foramen of Magendie
choices.feedback: This was the old theory (that DWM was caused by atresia of the foramina of Magendie and Luschka). Modern understanding is that the primary defect is in cerebellar/roof development, not just CSF obstruction.
DWM is a malformation (abnormal development), not a disruption (vascular destruction). Cerebellar disruptions exist but have different imaging patterns.

choices.text: Excessive CSF production by choroid plexus
choices.feedback: CSF overproduction is extremely rare (choroid plexus papilloma). DWM is a structural malformation of the cerebellum and 4th ventricle roof, not a CSF dynamics problem.

questions.stem: Which of the following is most commonly associated with Dandy-Walker malformation?

choices.feedback: MMC is associated with Chiari II, not Dandy-Walker. DWM and Chiari II are separate entities with different posterior fossa sizes (large vs small).

choices.text: Cardiac defects and other CNS anomalies
choices.feedback: Correct! DWM associations: CNS anomalies in 50-70% (agenesis of corpus callosum most common at 25%, heterotopias, occipital encephalocele), cardiac defects in 20-30% (VSD, ASD, TOF), face/palate anomalies. Chromosomal: trisomy 13, 18, triploidy. Syndromes: Walker-Warburg, Joubert (vermian aplasia).
Renal agenesis is associated with Potter sequence and VACTERL, not specifically with DWM.
Polydactyly can occur with trisomy 13 (which can include DWM), but it's not a primary DWM association.

questions.stem: What percentage of DWM patients develop hydrocephalus requiring treatment?

choices.feedback: Hydrocephalus is one of the most common complications of DWM, occurring in a majority of cases.

Correct! 75-90% of DWM patients develop hydrocephalus requiring treatment. The mechanism is multifactorial: obstruction of CSF flow by the cystic 4th ventricle, stenosis of the aqueduct, and impaired CSF absorption. VP shunt is the standard treatment. Some patients also need posterior fossa cyst-peritoneal shunting if the supratentorial and infratentorial compartments don't communicate well.
Higher than 50%. The large majority of DWM patients need CSF diversion.
Not quite universal — about 10-25% may have stable ventricle sizes. But the majority do need intervention.

questions.stem: What is the strongest predictor of neurodevelopmental outcome in DWM?

choices.text: Size of the posterior fossa cyst
choices.feedback: Cyst size alone doesn't predict outcomes. Some large cysts are associated with reasonable development.

choices.text: Presence and severity of associated anomalies
choices.feedback: Correct! The strongest predictors are: (1) Associated CNS anomalies (corpus callosum agenesis, heterotopias); (2) Chromosomal abnormalities; (3) Degree of vermian hypoplasia; (4) Complications of hydrocephalus/shunting. Isolated DWM without other anomalies can have near-normal intelligence. DWM with multiple anomalies has significantly worse outcomes.
Age at diagnosis doesn't independently predict outcome. What matters is the severity of the malformation and associated anomalies.
The surgical approach matters for managing hydrocephalus but doesn't fundamentally change the neurodevelopmental outcome, which is driven by brain malformation severity.

questions.stem: What does a positive Ortolani test indicate?

choices.feedback: Correct! Ortolani = the 'entry' test. It reduces a dislocated hip back into the socket. The clunk you feel is the femoral head sliding over the posterior acetabular rim INTO the acetabulum. Barlow (the 'exit' test) does the opposite — it dislocates a reducible hip OUT of the socket.

choices.text: Stable hip with normal variant click
choices.feedback: A 'click' is often benign, but a 'clunk' is pathologic. The distinction matters: clicks are high-pitched, clunks are palpable shifts of the femoral head.
If the hip were irreducible, Ortolani would be NEGATIVE (no clunk, because the head can't reduce). A positive Ortolani means it IS reducible.
Acetabular fracture from delivery is extremely rare. The clunk of Ortolani is from soft tissue and capsular laxity, not fracture.

questions.stem: Why is ultrasound preferred over X-ray for DDH in neonates?

choices.feedback: Correct! The femoral head ossification center doesn't appear on X-ray until 4-6 months of age. Before then, the hip is mostly cartilage, which ultrasound visualizes perfectly. The alpha angle measures the bony acetabular coverage: normal >60°, dysplastic <50°.
While radiation avoidance is a benefit, the primary reason is that X-ray simply can't see the cartilaginous femoral head in neonates.

choices.text: Better detection of soft tissue inflammation
choices.feedback: DDH is not an inflammatory condition. Ultrasound is used because of the cartilaginous anatomy, not for inflammation assessment.
Cost is not the primary driver. Anatomic visualization of the cartilaginous structures is the key reason.

questions.stem: What is the primary mechanism by which the Pavlik harness corrects DDH?

choices.text: Forces the head into the socket mechanically
choices.feedback: The Pavlik harness is NOT rigid. Forced abduction can cause avascular necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head. The harness allows GENTLE guided positioning.

choices.text: Maintains the femoral head in the acetabulum to stimulate remodeling
choices.feedback: Correct! The acetabulum needs the femoral head present to develop its normal concave shape. The Pavlik keeps the head centered in the acetabulum, providing the mechanical stimulus for the acetabulum to deepen and the capsule to tighten. This is why early treatment (before 6 months) has the best outcomes — the acetabulum is still actively forming.
Adductor tightness can contribute, but the primary mechanism is acetabular remodeling stimulated by concentric femoral head positioning.

choices.text: Compresses growth plates to redirect growth
choices.feedback: This is not the mechanism. The harness is gentle and doesn't compress growth plates.

questions.stem: What is the most feared complication of DDH treatment?

choices.text: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
choices.feedback: Correct! AVN of the femoral head is the most serious complication, occurring in 5-15% of treated DDH cases. The medial femoral circumflex artery is vulnerable during reduction maneuvers and in extreme abduction. AVN causes femoral head deformity, leg length discrepancy, and early osteoarthritis.
Femoral neck fracture is exceedingly rare with modern techniques. AVN is far more common and clinically significant.
Nerve palsy can occur but is rare and usually transient. AVN has permanent consequences.
Growth plate arrest can occur secondary to AVN but is not the primary feared complication itself.

questions.stem: What is the next diagnostic step?

choices.feedback: After 4-6 months, the ossified femoral head makes ultrasound less useful. X-ray is preferred in the walking child.

Correct! After 6 months, the femoral head ossification center is visible on X-ray. AP pelvis will show: superolateral displacement of the femoral head, shallow acetabulum, disrupted Shenton's line, and increased acetabular index. This presentation — painless limp + leg length discrepancy + limited abduction — is classic for missed/late DDH.
MRI is used for surgical planning (after closed reduction to check position), not for initial diagnosis in a walking child. X-ray is first-line.
This painless limp with asymmetric folds and limited abduction is classic DDH, not infection or tumor. Start with X-ray.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old post-hip surgery patient is alert in the morning but becomes confused and agitated at night, pulling at IV lines and seeing 'bugs on the wall.' What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Dementia has insidious onset and stable day-to-day course. This patient's acute, fluctuating course with visual hallucinations is classic delirium.

Correct! Acute onset, fluctuating course (lucid AM → confused PM = 'sundowning'), visual hallucinations, and post-surgical setting are classic delirium. Post-operative delirium occurs in 15-50% of elderly surgical patients.
New-onset schizophrenia is extremely rare in an 82-year-old. Schizophrenia typically presents in late teens/twenties and has auditory (not visual) hallucinations.
Brief psychotic disorder is a primary psychiatric diagnosis. In a post-surgical elderly patient, altered mental status should always be attributed to delirium until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: Which subtype of delirium is MOST COMMONLY missed?

choices.feedback: Hyperactive delirium with agitation is easily noticed and diagnosed (though it accounts for only ~25% of cases).

Correct! Hypoactive delirium is the most common subtype (~50%) AND the most missed. Patients are quiet, withdrawn, and lethargic — often misdiagnosed as depression or simply 'tired.' Hypoactive delirium carries WORSE prognosis than hyperactive delirium.
Mixed delirium alternates between types and is recognized during hyperactive episodes.
Catatonia is a separate syndrome, not a subtype of delirium (though they can co-occur).

questions.stem: Which medication class is the #1 pharmacologic cause of delirium in elderly patients?

choices.feedback: Opioids (especially meperidine) cause delirium, but anticholinergics are the most common pharmacologic cause overall.

Correct! Anticholinergic medications are the leading pharmacologic cause of delirium. Examples: diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, TCAs, scopolamine. Acetylcholine is critical for attention and arousal. Anticholinergic burden is cumulative — the more drugs with anticholinergic properties, the higher the risk.
SSRIs rarely cause delirium. They can cause serotonin syndrome at toxic levels but are not a common delirium trigger.
Statins are not significantly associated with delirium.

questions.stem: When is it appropriate to use benzodiazepines for delirium?

choices.feedback: Benzodiazepines WORSEN most forms of delirium by increasing sedation, paradoxical agitation, and respiratory depression.

Correct! Benzodiazepines are specifically indicated for delirium caused by alcohol or benzodiazepine withdrawal (delirium tremens). In withdrawal states, GABAergic tone is dangerously low, and benzos (lorazepam preferred) are life-saving. In all other delirium types, benzos make things worse.
If haloperidol is contraindicated, quetiapine or olanzapine are better alternatives. Benzos should be avoided in non-withdrawal delirium.

choices.text: For hypoactive delirium to prevent agitation
choices.feedback: Hypoactive delirium does not require sedating medications. Treatment focuses on finding and treating the underlying cause.

questions.stem: The CAM (Confusion Assessment Method) requires which features for delirium diagnosis?

choices.text: Chronic progressive memory loss + executive dysfunction
choices.feedback: This describes dementia, not the CAM criteria for delirium.

choices.text: Acute onset with fluctuating course + inattention + EITHER disorganized thinking OR altered consciousness
choices.feedback: Correct! The CAM requires: 1) Acute onset and fluctuating course AND 2) Inattention AND EITHER 3a) Disorganized thinking OR 3b) Altered level of consciousness. All four features (1+2+3a or 1+2+3b) must be present. The CAM has ~95% sensitivity and ~90% specificity.
This describes psychosis criteria, not the CAM for delirium.
These are criteria for major depressive disorder, not delirium.

questions.stem: A patient with Lewy body dementia develops agitation. Which medication should be AVOIDED?

choices.feedback: Quetiapine is actually one of the SAFER antipsychotics in Lewy body dementia if medication is absolutely needed, due to lower D2 receptor affinity.

Correct! Typical antipsychotics (haloperidol) are CONTRAINDICATED in Lewy body dementia. LBD patients have severe neuroleptic sensitivity — even low doses can cause severe parkinsonism, rigidity, NMS-like reactions, and death. If antipsychotic is necessary, quetiapine or pimavanserin are safer options.
Melatonin is safe and may help with sleep disturbance in delirium/dementia.
Acetaminophen is safe for pain management and may actually reduce delirium by treating undertreated pain.

questions.stem: What is the MOST IMPORTANT intervention for delirium management?

choices.feedback: Antipsychotics are only for severe agitation endangering the patient. They do not treat the underlying cause and have significant side effects.

choices.text: Identify and treat the underlying cause
choices.feedback: Correct! Delirium is a symptom, not a diagnosis. The most important step is finding and treating the cause: infection → antibiotics, medication → discontinue, metabolic → correct, urinary retention → catheterize, pain → treat. Cause-directed treatment resolves delirium faster than any medication.
Restraints should be avoided — they increase agitation, injury risk, and worsen delirium. Use de-escalation and environmental modifications instead.
CBT is for psychiatric disorders (depression, anxiety). Delirium requires medical treatment, not psychotherapy.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old develops delirium 48 hours after hospital admission. Which of the following is the LEAST likely cause?

choices.feedback: UTIs are one of the most common causes of delirium in elderly hospitalized patients.

Correct! New-onset schizophrenia at age 75 is extremely rare (typical onset late teens to early 30s). In elderly patients, acute confusion should ALWAYS be attributed to delirium from a medical cause until proven otherwise. Late-onset psychosis can occur but requires exclusion of delirium first.
Urinary retention, catheter discomfort, and UTIs from catheters are common delirium precipitants.
Diphenhydramine (anticholinergic) is a classic and common cause of iatrogenic delirium in hospitalized elderly patients.

questions.stem: A patient meets 6 of 9 SIG E CAPS criteria for 3 weeks but does NOT have depressed mood or anhedonia. Can MDD be diagnosed?

choices.text: Yes — 6 criteria exceeds the threshold of 5
choices.feedback: Meeting ≥5 criteria is necessary but not sufficient. At least ONE must be depressed mood or anhedonia.

choices.text: No — either depressed mood or anhedonia must be present
choices.feedback: Correct! DSM-5 requires ≥5 of 9 criteria for ≥2 weeks AND at least one must be depressed mood or anhedonia (loss of interest/pleasure). Without either gateway symptom, MDD cannot be diagnosed regardless of how many other criteria are met.

choices.text: Yes — if a psychiatrist confirms the diagnosis
choices.feedback: Diagnostic criteria are standardized. Even expert clinical judgment requires the gateway symptoms.

choices.text: Only if psychomotor retardation is prominent
choices.feedback: Psychomotor retardation is one criterion but cannot substitute for the required gateway symptoms.

questions.stem: Which SSRI is preferred for a pregnant patient with depression who plans to breastfeed?

choices.feedback: Paroxetine is AVOIDED in pregnancy (associated with cardiac malformations — category D) and has the worst discontinuation syndrome.

Correct! Sertraline is the best-studied SSRI in pregnancy and breastfeeding, with the lowest transfer into breast milk. It also has the fewest drug interactions. Fluoxetine is an alternative but has a long half-life and more drug interactions.
Fluvoxamine has significant drug interactions and less safety data in pregnancy/lactation compared to sertraline.
Citalopram has more QTc prolongation concerns and less breastfeeding safety data than sertraline.

questions.stem: What is the MOST EFFECTIVE treatment for depression overall?

choices.feedback: SSRIs are first-line pharmacotherapy but are not the most effective treatment. Response rates are ~50-60%.
CBT is effective and comparable to medication for mild-moderate depression but not the most effective treatment available.

Correct! ECT is the most effective treatment for depression, with response rates of 80-90%. Indications: treatment-resistant depression, psychotic depression, severe suicidality, catatonia, and situations requiring rapid response. Main side effect is transient retrograde amnesia.
Ketamine has rapid onset (hours) which is valuable, but ECT has higher overall remission rates for treatment-resistant depression.

questions.stem: A patient on fluoxetine is prescribed tramadol for pain. 24 hours later they develop agitation, tremor, hyperreflexia, clonus, diaphoresis, and fever. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: NMS presents with lead-pipe rigidity, hyperthermia, and elevated CK — typically from antipsychotics, not serotonergic drugs. Clonus and hyperreflexia point to serotonin syndrome.

Correct! Fluoxetine (SSRI) + tramadol (serotonin reuptake inhibitor + weak opioid) = serotonin syndrome. Classic triad: mental status changes + autonomic instability (fever, diaphoresis, tachycardia) + neuromuscular hyperactivity (clonus, hyperreflexia, tremor). Treatment: stop both drugs, supportive care, cyproheptadine.
Malignant hyperthermia occurs with volatile anesthetics + succinylcholine, not serotonergic medications.
Anticholinergic toxicity causes dry skin, urinary retention, mydriasis, decreased bowel sounds — the opposite of the diaphoresis and hyperreflexia seen here.

questions.stem: Bupropion is CONTRAINDICATED in which conditions?

choices.feedback: Bupropion does not have specific cardiac or renal contraindications (unlike venlafaxine, which requires dose adjustment).

Correct! Bupropion lowers the seizure threshold dose-dependently. Contraindicated in: seizure disorders, bulimia/anorexia (electrolyte abnormalities further lower threshold), concurrent MAOI use, and abrupt alcohol/benzo withdrawal. It is unique among antidepressants for having no sexual dysfunction and no weight gain.
Bupropion is actually weight-neutral or promotes slight weight loss — it is not contraindicated in metabolic conditions.
Bupropion is not absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy (category C). The specific contraindications are seizures and eating disorders.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old with depression and insomnia also has poor appetite and weight loss. Which antidepressant would address all three issues?

choices.feedback: Fluoxetine can suppress appetite and cause insomnia — it would worsen two of this patient's problems.

Correct! Mirtazapine's side effect profile (sedation + increased appetite + weight gain) becomes therapeutic here. It treats depression, promotes sleep (H1 antagonism), and stimulates appetite. Ideal for elderly depressed patients with insomnia and poor oral intake. Low doses (7.5-15 mg) are more sedating than higher doses (paradoxically).
Bupropion is activating (can worsen insomnia) and does not increase appetite.
Venlafaxine can cause insomnia and GI side effects, not ideal for this patient's symptom profile.

questions.stem: What distinguishes atypical depression from typical MDD?

choices.feedback: Suicidal ideation can occur in both typical and atypical depression.

choices.text: Mood reactivity with hypersomnia, hyperphagia, and rejection sensitivity
choices.feedback: Correct! Atypical depression features mood REACTIVITY (mood improves with positive events) plus ≥2 of: hypersomnia, hyperphagia/weight gain, leaden paralysis (heavy feeling in limbs), and interpersonal rejection sensitivity. This is the opposite of melancholic depression (mood non-reactive, worse in AM, insomnia, anorexia).
Depressed mood is still present in atypical depression, but it is reactive (can temporarily improve with good news).
Psychotic features define 'depression with psychotic features,' a different specifier from atypical features.

questions.stem: Before starting an antidepressant, what condition must ALWAYS be screened for?

choices.feedback: TSH should be checked as part of depression workup, but it's not the critical screening before antidepressant initiation.

Correct! ALWAYS screen for history of mania/hypomania before starting an antidepressant. Antidepressants in undiagnosed bipolar disorder can trigger mania, rapid cycling, or mixed episodes. Use the MDQ (Mood Disorder Questionnaire). If bipolar is suspected, use mood stabilizers, not antidepressant monotherapy.
Personality disorders may affect treatment planning but are not a mandatory screen before antidepressant initiation.
While important to assess, substance use does not contraindicate antidepressants the way undiagnosed bipolar disorder does.

questions.stem: A 62-year-old with Type 2 DM presents with glucose 1,100 mg/dL, osmolality 380, pH 7.38, and no ketones. What is the most important initial treatment?

choices.feedback: Insulin is important but fluids come first in HHS. The extreme hyperosmolality and massive fluid deficit (8-12 L) make volume resuscitation the priority.

Correct! HHS treatment starts with aggressive IV fluids. The extreme dehydration and hyperosmolality are the immediate threat. 1-1.5 L NS in the first hour, then adjust based on corrected sodium. Insulin can be started after initial fluid resuscitation.
pH is 7.38 (normal) — bicarbonate is not indicated.
Potassium should be checked and replaced as needed, but the primary intervention is fluid resuscitation.

questions.stem: During DKA treatment, a patient's serum potassium is 3.1 mEq/L. What should you do?

choices.text: Start insulin immediately and add potassium to the fluids
choices.feedback: Starting insulin with K <3.3 is dangerous — insulin drives K further into cells, risking fatal arrhythmia.

choices.text: Hold insulin, aggressively replace potassium first
choices.feedback: Correct! K <3.3 = HOLD insulin until K is ≥3.3. Aggressively replace K (20-40 mEq/hr in severe hypokalemia). All DKA patients are total-body potassium depleted. Insulin + rehydration will drive K even lower.

choices.text: Give sodium bicarbonate to shift K out of cells
choices.feedback: Bicarbonate would worsen hypokalemia by shifting K intracellularly.

choices.text: Continue current management and recheck K in 4 hours
choices.feedback: Waiting 4 hours with K of 3.1 and no replacement risks cardiac arrhythmia.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old being treated for DKA develops headache, vomiting, and bradycardia 6 hours into treatment. What is the most likely complication?

choices.feedback: Hypoglycemia would cause tachycardia, diaphoresis, and altered mental status but not bradycardia.
Hypokalemia would cause cardiac arrhythmias (tachycardia, not bradycardia) and muscle weakness.

Correct! Cerebral edema: the most feared complication of pediatric DKA treatment. Headache + vomiting + bradycardia (Cushing triad) 4-12 hours after starting treatment = cerebral edema until proven otherwise. Immediate treatment: mannitol or 3% saline, slow IV fluids, elevate head of bed.
PE is not a typical complication of pediatric DKA treatment.

questions.stem: What is the mechanism by which DKA causes an elevated anion gap metabolic acidosis?

choices.feedback: Lactic acidosis can contribute but is not the primary mechanism. The primary anion gap elevation is from ketoacids.

Correct! In DKA, absolute insulin deficiency → unrestrained lipolysis → free fatty acids → hepatic ketogenesis → β-hydroxybutyrate (main ketoacid) and acetoacetate. These unmeasured anions increase the anion gap. β-hydroxybutyrate:acetoacetate ratio is 3:1 in DKA.
RTA causes a non-anion gap acidosis, not an elevated anion gap.
Hyperchloremia from saline causes a non-anion gap acidosis (which can occur during DKA treatment, not as the presenting acidosis).

questions.stem: What X-ray finding is expected?

choices.feedback: Correct! The double bubble sign shows air in the stomach (first bubble) and proximal duodenum (second bubble) with NO distal bowel gas if the atresia is complete. This is pathognomonic for duodenal atresia.
Multiple air-fluid levels suggest distal small bowel obstruction (jejunal/ileal atresia), not duodenal atresia.
Free air indicates perforation, not atresia. Duodenal atresia doesn't typically perforate.
Ground glass is a pulmonary finding (RDS/HMD), not abdominal. The vomiting here is a GI problem.

questions.stem: Why is duodenal atresia embryologically different from jejunal/ileal atresia?

choices.feedback: Both are endoderm-lined. The difference is in the mechanism of atresia, not germ layer.

choices.text: Duodenal = failed recanalization; jejunal/ileal = vascular accident
choices.feedback: Correct! Duodenal atresia = INTRINSIC (failure of lumen recanalization during solid cord stage). Jejunal/ileal atresia = EXTRINSIC (mesenteric vascular accident causing ischemic necrosis of the bowel segment in utero). This is why jejunal/ileal atresia can have 'apple peel' deformity and is NOT associated with Down syndrome.
Neural crest cell migration failure causes Hirschsprung disease (aganglionosis), not duodenal atresia.
The duodenum forms at the foregut-midgut junction earlier, not later. The key difference is the mechanism (recanalization vs vascular).

questions.stem: What causes annular pancreas?

choices.text: Failure of ventral pancreatic bud rotation
choices.feedback: Correct! Normally, the ventral pancreatic bud rotates posteriorly around the duodenum to fuse with the dorsal bud. If the ventral bud fails to rotate properly (or bifid ventral bud wraps around both sides), it encircles the duodenum → extrinsic compression → obstruction. Annular pancreas is a common co-finding with duodenal atresia in T21.
It's not overgrowth — it's abnormal positioning of normal pancreatic tissue around the duodenum due to rotational failure.

choices.text: Ectopic pancreatic tissue in the duodenal wall
choices.feedback: Heterotopic pancreas (pancreatic rest) is a different entity — usually found in the stomach or Meckel's and is typically asymptomatic.
The pancreas doesn't fuse with the duodenal wall. It wraps around the outside due to failed rotation of the ventral bud.

questions.stem: Why is the annular pancreas not divided?

choices.feedback: Annular pancreas does not regress. But dividing it is not the answer either.

choices.text: Dividing it risks pancreatic duct injury and fistula
choices.feedback: Correct! The pancreatic duct often courses through the annular ring. Dividing the ring risks cutting the duct → pancreatic fistula, pancreatitis, or duct stricture. Instead, the obstruction is bypassed with a duodenoduodenostomy (connecting proximal to distal duodenum around the obstruction).

choices.text: The annular tissue provides structural support
choices.feedback: The annular tissue provides no structural benefit — it's the cause of obstruction. But bypass is safer than division.

choices.text: The pancreas will atrophy after bypass
choices.feedback: The annular pancreas remains viable after bypass. It simply no longer causes obstruction.

questions.stem: Which type can present with the 'windsock' deformity?

choices.feedback: Correct! In Type 1, an intraluminal membrane/web can be stretched distally by peristalsis, creating a 'windsock' deformity that may cause partial obstruction. Some Type 1 webs have a central perforation → duodenal stenosis instead of complete atresia. This variant may present later in childhood with intermittent vomiting.
Type 2 has two blind-ending pouches connected by a fibrous cord. There's no intraluminal structure to create a windsock.
Type 3 has complete separation — no connection at all. Windsock requires an intraluminal membrane.
Only Type 1 with its intraluminal web can create the windsock deformity.

questions.stem: Most common location for ectopic pregnancy?

choices.feedback: Ovarian ectopics are rare (<3%).

Correct! Ampulla (~70%), then isthmus (~12%), fimbria (~11%), interstitial/cornual (~2%). Ampullary ectopics are most common because fertilization normally occurs here — the embryo may implant before reaching the uterus if tubal transport is impaired.
Cervical ectopics are rare (<1%) but dangerous (massive hemorrhage).
Abdominal pregnancy is extremely rare.

questions.stem: β-hCG above discriminatory zone + empty uterus on TVUS means:

choices.feedback: Above discriminatory zone, a normal IUP should be visible.

Correct! If β-hCG >1500-3500 mIU/mL and TVUS shows no IUP, presume ectopic. Further evaluation: adnexal mass? Free fluid? If β-hCG below discriminatory zone with empty uterus = pregnancy of unknown location → serial β-hCG q48h to determine trajectory.
Miscarriage shows intrauterine contents (POC), not empty uterus with high β-hCG.
Molar pregnancy shows intrauterine 'snowstorm' pattern, not empty uterus.

questions.stem: Methotrexate for ectopic is appropriate when:

choices.feedback: Instability = ruptured ectopic → emergent surgery, not MTX.

choices.text: Stable, unruptured, β-hCG <5000, no fetal cardiac activity
choices.feedback: Correct! MTX criteria: hemodynamically stable, unruptured, β-hCG <5000 (best results), no fetal cardiac activity on US, ectopic mass <3.5 cm, reliable patient for follow-up. Folic acid antagonist → inhibits trophoblastic proliferation. Monitor β-hCG to zero. Contraindicated: hepatic/renal disease, immunodeficiency, breastfeeding.

choices.text: Any ectopic regardless of size or stability
choices.feedback: MTX has specific criteria and contraindications.

choices.text: Only if β-hCG is declining spontaneously
choices.feedback: Declining β-hCG may qualify for expectant management instead of MTX.

questions.stem: Normal intrauterine pregnancy β-hCG doubling time?

choices.feedback: 24h is too fast for normal β-hCG doubling.

Correct! Normal early IUP: β-hCG doubles approximately every 48h. Abnormal rise (<35% increase in 48h) suggests ectopic or nonviable pregnancy. Declining β-hCG suggests miscarriage. Used for pregnancy of unknown location (PUL) to determine viability and location.
Weekly is too slow for normal doubling.

choices.text: β-hCG doesn't change in normal pregnancy
choices.feedback: β-hCG rises steadily in early pregnancy, peaking at 8-10 weeks.

questions.stem: #1 risk factor for ectopic pregnancy?

choices.feedback: Smoking impairs tubal motility but is not the highest risk factor.
Correct! Prior ectopic = 10-25% recurrence risk (strongest risk factor). Other major risks: PID/STI (Chlamydia causes tubal scarring), prior tubal surgery, IVF, smoking, DES exposure, endometriosis.
Age mildly increases risk but is not the #1 factor.
OCPs are protective against pregnancy and thus ectopic.

questions.stem: Rh-negative patient with ectopic pregnancy should receive:

choices.feedback: Rh status is always relevant in pregnancy-related bleeding.

choices.text: RhoGAM (anti-D immunoglobulin) to prevent Rh sensitization
choices.feedback: Correct! ALL Rh-negative women with any pregnancy event (ectopic, miscarriage, abortion, trauma, delivery) should receive RhoGAM within 72h to prevent Rh isoimmunization. Standard dose: 300 μg IM. Prevents formation of anti-D antibodies that could cause hemolytic disease in future pregnancies.
Transfusion only if hemodynamically unstable from hemorrhage.
Folate prevents neural tube defects but isn't specifically indicated for ectopic.

questions.stem: After methotrexate for ectopic, β-hCG should decline by ≥15% between:

choices.feedback: Too early — β-hCG may initially rise after MTX.

Correct! β-hCG often RISES initially (days 1-4) as trophoblast lyses. Measure on days 4 and 7. If ≥15% decline from day 4 to 7 → successful response → weekly monitoring until zero. If <15% decline → repeat MTX dose or surgery. Treatment failure rate: ~10-15%.
Monitoring starts earlier than this.
Day 4 to 7 comparison is the standard monitoring window.

questions.stem: IUD and ectopic pregnancy — the relationship is:

choices.feedback: IUDs don't cause ectopics — they prevent intrauterine pregnancy.

choices.text: If pregnancy occurs WITH IUD in place, higher proportion are ectopic
choices.feedback: Correct! IUDs are highly effective at preventing intrauterine pregnancy but less effective at preventing ectopic. If a patient gets pregnant with an IUD, there's a higher proportion of ectopics (not higher absolute risk vs no contraception). Most IUD pregnancies are still intrauterine, but always evaluate for ectopic.
IUDs reduce overall pregnancy risk but don't eliminate ectopic risk entirely.

choices.text: Only hormonal IUDs affect ectopic risk
choices.feedback: Both copper and hormonal IUDs have similar ectopic pregnancy considerations.

questions.stem: The 300 method for calculating heart rate uses:

choices.feedback: The 300 method divides, not multiplies.

choices.text: 300 ÷ number of large boxes between R waves
choices.feedback: Correct! Count large boxes between two consecutive R waves → 300 ÷ boxes = HR. Quick markers: 1 box = 300, 2 = 150, 3 = 100, 4 = 75, 5 = 60, 6 = 50. Only accurate for regular rhythms. For irregular rhythms: count R waves in a 6-second strip × 10.
The 300 method is an independent EKG-based calculation.
PR interval is used for assessing heart block, not heart rate.

questions.stem: Diffuse ST elevation with PR depression and no reciprocal changes suggests:

choices.feedback: STEMI has territorial ST elevation (corresponding to a coronary artery) WITH reciprocal ST depression in other leads.

Correct! Pericarditis: diffuse concave-up (smiley face) ST elevation in multiple non-contiguous leads, PR depression (most specific finding, best in II), NO reciprocal changes. STEMI: convex-up ST elevation in contiguous leads with reciprocal depression. This distinction is high-yield.
Hyperkalemia shows peaked T waves, widened QRS, and eventually sine wave — not diffuse ST elevation.
Brugada shows coved ST elevation in V1-V3 only, not diffuse.

questions.stem: WPW (Wolff-Parkinson-White) is characterized by:

choices.feedback: WPW has a SHORT PR interval (the accessory pathway bypasses the AV node delay).

choices.text: Short PR + delta wave + wide QRS
choices.feedback: Correct! WPW: accessory pathway (Bundle of Kent) bypasses the AV node → early ventricular depolarization. Short PR (<120 ms), delta wave (slurred QRS upstroke), wide QRS. Danger: if A-fib develops, rapid conduction through accessory pathway → VF. Treatment of A-fib in WPW: procainamide or cardioversion. AVOID AV nodal blockers (digoxin, verapamil, adenosine).

choices.text: Peaked T waves + absent P waves
choices.feedback: Peaked T waves = hyperkalemia. Absent P waves = A-fib.
Sawtooth = atrial flutter.

questions.stem: New LBBB with chest pain should be treated as:

choices.feedback: New LBBB + chest pain is potentially a STEMI equivalent and should not be observed.

Correct! New LBBB in the setting of ischemic symptoms is considered a STEMI equivalent because LBBB obscures ST-segment analysis. These patients should receive emergent reperfusion therapy (PCI). Old/chronic LBBB does not warrant emergent PCI.
LBBB is a conduction abnormality, not an arrhythmia.
PE has different EKG findings (S1Q3T3, right heart strain).

questions.stem: Torsades de pointes treatment:

choices.feedback: Amiodarone PROLONGS QT and can WORSEN torsades. Absolutely contraindicated.

Correct! IV magnesium sulfate 2g IV is first-line for torsades de pointes. It stabilizes cardiac membrane and suppresses early afterdepolarizations. For refractory cases: overdrive pacing (increases HR → shortens QT). Also correct underlying cause (stop QT-prolonging drugs, replace K+/Mg²+). AVOID amiodarone, procainamide, sotalol — all prolong QT.
Adenosine is for SVT, not ventricular arrhythmias.
If unstable/pulseless, defibrillate. But first-line pharmacotherapy is magnesium. Synchronized cardioversion is for stable re-entrant SVTs.

questions.stem: First EKG change in hyperkalemia?

choices.feedback: Wide QRS occurs later in the progression. The earliest change is different.

Correct! Hyperkalemia EKG progression: peaked T waves (earliest, K+ ~5.5-6.5) → PR prolongation → P wave flattening/loss → QRS widening → sine wave → VF/asystole. First intervention: IV calcium gluconate (cardioprotective — stabilizes membrane). Does NOT lower K+; add insulin/glucose and other shifting agents.
ST elevation suggests ischemia or pericarditis, not hyperkalemia.
Delta waves indicate WPW, not hyperkalemia.

questions.stem: MaRRoW/WiLLiaM mnemonics help identify:

choices.feedback: Heart blocks are classified by PR interval and P-QRS relationship.

Correct! MaRRoW = RBBB: M-shaped (rsR') in right precordial leads (V1-V2), W-shaped (wide S) in lateral leads (I, V6). WiLLiaM = LBBB: W-shaped in V1, M-shaped (broad notched R) in lateral leads (I, aVL, V5-V6). QRS must be >120 ms for either diagnosis.
ST patterns relate to ischemia/infarction, not bundle branch blocks.
Flutter has sawtooth pattern; A-fib has absent P waves with irregular R-R.

questions.stem: A-fib with WPW should NOT be treated with which drug?

choices.feedback: Procainamide is actually one of the preferred treatments — it slows accessory pathway conduction.

Correct! In A-fib with WPW, blocking the AV node forces ALL conduction through the accessory pathway → extremely rapid ventricular response → VF → cardiac arrest. NEVER give digoxin, verapamil, diltiazem, or adenosine. Safe options: procainamide (slows accessory pathway), cardioversion (if unstable).
Amiodarone can be considered cautiously but AV nodal blockers are the critical contraindication.
Cardioversion is safe and appropriate if the patient is hemodynamically unstable.

questions.stem: Classic presentation of GBS?

choices.text: Descending paralysis starting with cranial nerves
choices.feedback: Descending = botulism or Miller Fisher variant, not classic GBS.

choices.text: Ascending symmetric weakness with areflexia following a recent infection
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic GBS: 1-4 weeks post-infection → ascending symmetric weakness (legs → arms → respiratory → cranial nerves) + areflexia/hyporeflexia (LMN). Campylobacter jejuni is the #1 infectious trigger.

choices.text: Unilateral facial droop with forehead sparing
choices.feedback: Upper motor neuron facial palsy (stroke). GBS can cause bilateral facial weakness.

choices.text: Fluctuating weakness worse at end of day
choices.feedback: Fatigable weakness = myasthenia gravis, not GBS.

questions.stem: CSF findings in GBS?

choices.feedback: Elevated WBC suggests infection (meningitis), not GBS.

choices.text: Albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein, normal cell count)
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic CSF finding in GBS. Elevated protein from inflammation-related breakdown of blood-nerve barrier. Normal cell count distinguishes from infectious processes. May be normal in the first week — repeat LP if suspicion remains high.
Low glucose + high WBC = bacterial meningitis.
While possible early, elevated protein with normal cells is the classic finding.

questions.stem: When should you intubate a GBS patient?

choices.feedback: SpO2 drops LATE in neuromuscular respiratory failure — waiting for desaturation is too late.

choices.text: FVC <20 mL/kg or NIF < -30 cmH2O (20/30 rule)
choices.feedback: Correct! Monitor pulmonary function serially. Intubate when FVC <20 mL/kg or NIF (negative inspiratory force) weaker than -30 cmH2O. Also intubate for: rapid decline in FVC (>30% drop), bulbar dysfunction with aspiration risk, inability to cough effectively. 30% of GBS patients need ventilation.

choices.text: Only when the patient becomes unresponsive
choices.feedback: Waiting for unresponsiveness risks respiratory arrest.

choices.text: Based on arterial blood gas showing CO2 retention
choices.feedback: CO2 retention is a very late finding. FVC/NIF monitoring catches decline earlier.

questions.stem: Which treatment is NOT effective in GBS?

choices.feedback: IVIG is first-line treatment for GBS.

Correct! HIGH-YIELD: Steroids are NOT effective in GBS (unlike most other autoimmune conditions). Multiple trials show no benefit. IVIG + steroids = no additional benefit. Do NOT give steroids for GBS. This distinguishes GBS from CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), where steroids ARE effective.
Plasmapheresis is equally effective as IVIG for GBS.
Supportive care is essential for GBS management.

questions.stem: Miller Fisher variant of GBS presents with:

choices.feedback: That's classic GBS. Miller Fisher is different.

Correct! Miller Fisher triad: ophthalmoplegia (eye movement paralysis) + ataxia + areflexia. Associated with anti-GQ1b antibodies. Descending pattern (starts cranially). Generally better prognosis than classic AIDP. Treat with IVIG or plasmapheresis.
CNS symptoms suggest encephalitis, not GBS variants.
Systemic inflammatory signs suggest other autoimmune conditions, not GBS.

questions.stem: #1 infectious trigger for GBS?

choices.feedback: Influenza can trigger GBS but is not the #1 cause.

Correct! C. jejuni gastroenteritis precedes ~30% of GBS cases. The lipopolysaccharides on C. jejuni resemble gangliosides on peripheral nerves → molecular mimicry → autoimmune attack. C. jejuni-associated GBS tends to have worse prognosis (often axonal variant — AMAN).
E. coli is a common GI pathogen but not a major GBS trigger.
S. aureus is not typically associated with GBS.

questions.stem: Why should IVIG and plasmapheresis NOT be combined?

choices.text: They cause a dangerous drug interaction
choices.feedback: No pharmacologic interaction — the issue is more practical.

choices.text: Plasmapheresis removes the IVIG antibodies that were just infused
choices.feedback: Correct! Plasmapheresis filters out plasma proteins, including the therapeutic immunoglobulins from IVIG. Combining them is counterproductive. Choose one or the other. If one fails, can switch to the alternative. IVIG is generally preferred due to easier administration.
While IVIG can rarely cause renal issues, this is not the reason to avoid combining.

choices.text: They have identical mechanisms so combining is redundant
choices.feedback: They have different mechanisms (IVIG = immunomodulation, PLEX = antibody removal), but combining is counterproductive because PLEX removes IVIG.

questions.stem: GBS vs botulism — key distinguishing feature?

choices.feedback: GBS ascends; botulism DESCENDS. This is the key distinction.

choices.text: GBS: ascending. Botulism: DESCENDING paralysis starting with cranial nerves
choices.feedback: Correct! GBS = ascending (legs → arms → respiratory). Botulism = descending (cranial nerves → arms → legs). Botulism also has: dilated/fixed pupils, autonomic dysfunction, associated with home-canned foods (C. botulinum toxin blocks ACh release). Both cause flaccid paralysis and areflexia.

choices.text: GBS has UMN signs; botulism has LMN signs
choices.feedback: Both are LMN patterns with flaccid weakness and areflexia.
Both GBS and botulism can have autonomic dysfunction.

questions.stem: Duodenal ulcer pain pattern?

choices.feedback: That's gastric ulcer — food stimulates acid directly on the ulcer.

choices.text: Improves with eating, worsens 2-3h after meals/at night
choices.feedback: Correct! Food buffers acid → temporary relief. When stomach empties, acid floods duodenum → pain returns. Night pain (empty stomach) is classic. Gastric ulcer: pain worsens with eating (acid + food directly irritate).
PUD pain characteristically relates to meals/fasting.
Spicy foods may exacerbate but the pattern is meal-timing dependent.

questions.stem: Gastric ulcers should ALWAYS be biopsied because:

choices.feedback: Most are benign, but malignancy must be excluded.

choices.text: They can be malignant (gastric cancer can present as an ulcer)
choices.feedback: Correct! Gastric ulcers can harbor adenocarcinoma. Always biopsy at EGD. Duodenal ulcers are virtually never malignant. Follow-up EGD in 8-12 weeks to document healing of gastric ulcers. Non-healing gastric ulcer = repeat biopsy (cancer concern).

choices.text: H. pylori can only be detected in gastric ulcers
choices.feedback: H. pylori can be detected from any gastric/duodenal biopsy.

choices.text: Gastric ulcers are always due to cancer
choices.feedback: Most are from H. pylori or NSAIDs, but cancer must be excluded.

questions.stem: Barrett esophagus is characterized by:

choices.feedback: Hyperplasia is not the defining feature of Barrett.

choices.text: Intestinal metaplasia (squamous → columnar with goblet cells)
choices.feedback: Correct! Barrett = intestinal metaplasia of distal esophagus. Normal squamous epithelium replaced by columnar with goblet cells. Premalignant condition → risk of esophageal adenocarcinoma. Risk factors: chronic GERD, male, white, obesity, smoking. Requires surveillance EGD with systematic biopsy.
Granulomas suggest Crohn disease, not Barrett.
Eosinophils suggest eosinophilic esophagitis, not Barrett.

questions.stem: Test-of-cure for H. pylori should use:

choices.feedback: Serology stays positive after successful treatment — cannot confirm cure.

choices.text: Urea breath test or stool antigen (≥4 weeks after treatment, ≥2 weeks off PPI)
choices.feedback: Correct! Both detect active infection. Must wait ≥4 weeks after antibiotics and ≥2 weeks off PPI (PPI suppresses H. pylori → false negative). Serology (IgG) remains positive for months-years after eradication — useless for test-of-cure.
H. pylori is not detected by blood culture.
Non-invasive tests suffice for test-of-cure unless EGD is needed for other reasons (e.g., gastric ulcer follow-up).

questions.stem: Posterior duodenal ulcer that erodes posteriorly causes:

choices.feedback: Free air = anterior perforation (into peritoneal cavity).

Correct! Posterior duodenal ulcers erode into the GDA → massive upper GI hemorrhage. Anterior duodenal ulcers perforate into the peritoneal cavity → pneumoperitoneum (free air on upright CXR). This anatomic distinction is high-yield.
While posterior ulcers are near the pancreas, the classic complication is GDA hemorrhage.
Posterior ulcers affect the GDA, not the portal vein.

questions.stem: Long-term PPI risks include all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: PPIs increase C. diff risk by reducing gastric acid barrier.

Correct! PPIs are NOT associated with significant hepatotoxicity. Known long-term risks: C. diff, hypomagnesemia, B12 deficiency, calcium malabsorption (fractures), fundic gland polyps, CKD (rare), community-acquired pneumonia. Despite these risks, PPIs remain safe when indicated.
PPIs can cause hypomagnesemia, sometimes severe.
PPIs impair acid-dependent B12 absorption.

questions.stem: Misoprostol for NSAID ulcer prevention is CONTRAINDICATED in:

choices.feedback: Age alone is not a contraindication.

Correct! Misoprostol is a prostaglandin E1 analog that promotes mucosal defense. It causes uterine contractions → abortion. Absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy or women of childbearing age without contraception. Used intentionally for medical abortion (with mifepristone). Alternative for NSAID gastroprotection: PPI co-prescription.
HTN is not a contraindication for misoprostol.
No significant interaction with antibiotics.

questions.stem: H. pylori first-line eradication regimen?

choices.feedback: PPI alone suppresses but does not eradicate H. pylori.

Correct! Triple therapy for 14 days (not 7 — longer duration improves eradication rates). Use if local clarithromycin resistance <15%. If resistance >15%, penicillin allergy, or prior macrolide exposure: bismuth quadruple therapy (PPI + bismuth + metronidazole + tetracycline × 14 days). Always confirm eradication after treatment.
Single antibiotic cannot eradicate H. pylori (resistance develops).
This is not a standard H. pylori regimen.

questions.stem: Melena indicates bleeding from:

choices.feedback: Melena (black tarry stool) = upper GI. Blood is digested during transit.

choices.text: Upper GI tract (above ligament of Treitz)
choices.feedback: Correct! Melena = oxidized hemoglobin from upper GI bleed. Only ~100-200 mL blood needed. As little as the duodenum can produce melena. Right-sided colonic bleeds can occasionally cause melena if transit is slow.
Hemorrhoids cause bright red blood per rectum, not melena.
Rectal bleeding is hematochezia, not melena.

questions.stem: Restrictive transfusion threshold in GI bleeding?

choices.feedback: Liberal threshold — TRIGGER trial showed worse outcomes, especially in variceal bleeding.

Correct! Transfuse pRBCs when Hb <7 (TRIGGER trial). Restrictive strategy improves survival, especially in variceal bleeding (excessive transfusion increases portal pressure → more bleeding). Exception: active massive hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability — transfuse to maintain perfusion.
Only consider this threshold for patients with active CAD.
Objective Hb threshold is used, not symptoms alone.

questions.stem: #1 cause of upper GI bleeding?

choices.feedback: Varices are #1 in cirrhotic patients but not overall.

Correct! PUD accounts for ~40% of upper GIB. H. pylori and NSAIDs are the two main causes. Endoscopic therapy + high-dose PPI + H. pylori eradication (if positive). Duodenal ulcers bleed from the gastroduodenal artery; gastric ulcers from the left gastric artery.
MW tears (mucosal tear at GE junction from forceful vomiting) account for ~5-10%.
Malignancy is an uncommon cause of acute upper GIB.

questions.stem: In variceal bleeding, which medication reduces mortality?

choices.feedback: PPIs are for ulcer bleeding, not effective for varices.

Correct! Prophylactic antibiotics (ceftriaxone 1g IV daily × 7 days) reduce bacterial infections, rebleeding, AND mortality in variceal hemorrhage. Always part of the variceal bleed bundle: octreotide + antibiotics + EBL. This is one of the most important evidence-based interventions.
TXA has not shown clear benefit in GI bleeding (HALT-IT trial).
Calcium is given during massive transfusion but doesn't specifically reduce variceal mortality.

questions.stem: #1 cause of lower GI bleeding in adults over 60?

choices.feedback: Cancer causes chronic/occult bleeding more than acute hemorrhage.

Correct! Diverticulosis is the #1 cause of significant lower GIB in older adults. Painless, large-volume hematochezia. 80% self-limited. Left-sided diverticula are more common, but RIGHT-sided diverticula bleed more often. Angiodysplasia is #2.
Hemorrhoids are the most common cause of minor rectal bleeding but not significant lower GIB.
Ischemic colitis causes bloody diarrhea with pain, usually self-limited.

questions.stem: Heyde syndrome is the association between:

choices.feedback: That's portal hypertension, not Heyde syndrome.

choices.text: Aortic stenosis and GI bleeding from angiodysplasia
choices.feedback: Correct! Heyde syndrome: aortic stenosis → acquired von Willebrand syndrome (high shear forces destroy vWF multimers) → bleeding from angiodysplasia (AVM). Resolves with aortic valve replacement. Classic boards association.

choices.text: Peptic ulcer disease and H. pylori
choices.feedback: That's a direct causative relationship, not Heyde syndrome.
Gallstone pancreatitis is a separate entity.

questions.stem: Massive hematochezia with hemodynamic instability — first step?

choices.feedback: Colonoscopy requires prep and hemodynamic stability.

choices.text: Resuscitate + consider upper GI source (EGD first)
choices.feedback: Correct! Massive upper GIB can present as hematochezia (rapid transit). With hemodynamic instability, always consider upper source first — EGD before colonoscopy. Simultaneously resuscitate with 2 large-bore IVs, crystalloid, type & crossmatch, transfuse if Hb <7.
Barium enema is obsolete for acute GI bleeding evaluation.
Hemodynamic instability requires urgent intervention, not observation.

questions.stem: Forrest Ia ulcer classification means:

choices.feedback: Clean base = Forrest III (very low rebleed risk ~5%).

Correct! Forrest classification: Ia = spurting (highest risk ~90% rebleed without treatment), Ib = oozing, IIa = visible vessel (50% rebleed), IIb = adherent clot, IIc = flat spot, III = clean base (5% rebleed). Ia/Ib/IIa require endoscopic therapy. IIb controversial. IIc/III = medical management only.
Adherent clot = Forrest IIb.
Visible vessel = Forrest IIa.

questions.stem: Joint aspiration of an acutely swollen 1st MTP shows needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Pseudogout shows rhomboid-shaped, weakly positively birefringent CPPD crystals, not needle-shaped negatively birefringent crystals.

Correct! Needle-shaped, strongly negatively birefringent crystals = monosodium urate (MSU) = gout. The 1st MTP (podagra) is the classic location for acute gouty arthritis. Arthrocentesis with polarized microscopy is the gold standard for diagnosis.
Septic arthritis is diagnosed by positive Gram stain/culture. Crystals are absent in purely infectious arthritis (though both can coexist).
Hydroxyapatite crystals are not birefringent under polarized microscopy and cause calcific tendinitis, not classic crystal arthropathy.

questions.stem: A patient with gout and CKD stage 4 has an acute flare. NSAIDs are contraindicated. What is the best treatment?

choices.feedback: High-dose colchicine has significant toxicity in CKD (accumulates → diarrhea, bone marrow suppression). Low-dose may be considered with dose adjustment, but steroids are safer.

Correct! Corticosteroids (oral prednisone burst or intra-articular injection) are the best option when NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated due to CKD. Intra-articular injection is preferred for monoarticular flares to minimize systemic effects.
Allopurinol is for chronic urate-lowering, NOT acute flare treatment. Starting ULT during a flare can worsen or prolong it.
Probenecid is contraindicated in CKD (requires adequate GFR for uricosuric effect) and is not an acute flare treatment.

questions.stem: Which genetic marker should be screened before starting allopurinol in Southeast Asian and Black patients?

choices.feedback: HLA-B27 is associated with ankylosing spondylitis and spondyloarthropathies, not allopurinol hypersensitivity.

Correct! HLA-B*5801 screening is recommended before allopurinol in Southeast Asian, Korean, and Black patients due to high carrier rates. Positive HLA-B*5801 → risk of severe allopurinol hypersensitivity syndrome (DRESS, SJS/TEN) with 25% mortality. Use febuxostat instead if positive.
HLA-B*5701 is screened before abacavir (HIV medication) for hypersensitivity, not allopurinol.
CYP2D6 testing is relevant for drug metabolism (codeine, tamoxifen) but not allopurinol hypersensitivity.

questions.stem: What radiographic finding is characteristic of CPPD (pseudogout)?

choices.feedback: This radiographic pattern is characteristic of chronic tophaceous gout, not pseudogout.

Correct! Chondrocalcinosis = linear calcification within articular cartilage (menisci, triangular fibrocartilage of wrist) and is the hallmark radiographic finding of CPPD. Most commonly seen in the knee and wrist. Triggers for acute pseudogout include surgery, illness, and metabolic derangements.
Bamboo spine is characteristic of ankylosing spondylitis (syndesmophytes bridging vertebral bodies).
Pencil-in-cup is seen in psoriatic arthritis (arthritis mutilans), not pseudogout.

questions.stem: Why should urate-lowering therapy NOT be initiated during an acute gout flare?

choices.feedback: The diagnosis should already be confirmed by crystal analysis before considering ULT.

choices.text: Rapid uric acid changes can prolong or trigger flares
choices.feedback: Correct! Rapidly lowering uric acid during an acute attack causes crystal shedding from tophi into joints, prolonging or worsening the current flare and potentially triggering new attacks. Wait 2-4 weeks after flare resolution. When starting ULT, co-prescribe prophylactic colchicine for 3-6 months.
While allopurinol can cause hepatotoxicity, this is not the reason for avoiding initiation during flares.

choices.text: ULT requires fasting uric acid levels
choices.feedback: While fasting can affect levels, the primary concern is clinical — destabilizing crystal deposits during active inflammation.

questions.stem: Which condition is associated with pseudogout in younger patients and should be screened for?

choices.feedback: Correct! CPPD/pseudogout in patients under 55 should prompt evaluation for underlying metabolic conditions: hemochromatosis (iron overload), hyperparathyroidism (hypercalcemia), hypomagnesemia, hypothyroidism, and Wilson disease. The 2nd-3rd MCP hooklike osteophytes are classic for hemochromatosis arthropathy.
Marfan syndrome causes connective tissue abnormalities but is not associated with CPPD deposition.
Celiac disease is not directly linked to pseudogout.
Turner syndrome is associated with horseshoe kidney and coarctation, not crystal arthropathy.

questions.stem: A patient with recurrent gout attacks has a serum uric acid of 5.8 mg/dL during an acute flare. Does this rule out gout?

choices.text: Yes — uric acid must be elevated for gout diagnosis
choices.feedback: This is a common misconception. Serum uric acid can be misleadingly normal or even low during acute attacks.

choices.text: No — uric acid can be normal during acute flares
choices.feedback: Correct! Serum uric acid is UNRELIABLE during acute attacks — it can be normal in up to 40% of acute flares due to cytokine-mediated uricosuria (IL-6 increases renal uric acid excretion). The gold standard is synovial fluid crystal analysis, not serum uric acid. Recheck uric acid 2-4 weeks after the flare resolves.

choices.text: Yes — any level below 6.8 mg/dL excludes gout
choices.feedback: The solubility threshold of 6.8 mg/dL is for chronic supersaturation. During acute attacks, levels fluctuate and can be falsely normal.

choices.text: Only if the uric acid was also normal before the attack
choices.feedback: The timing of uric acid measurement matters — during flares, levels are unreliable regardless of prior values.

questions.stem: Colchicine works by which mechanism?

choices.feedback: COX inhibition is the mechanism of NSAIDs (ibuprofen, indomethacin, naproxen), not colchicine.
Xanthine oxidase inhibition is the mechanism of allopurinol and febuxostat (urate-lowering therapy), not colchicine.

Correct! Colchicine binds tubulin and disrupts microtubule polymerization → inhibits neutrophil chemotaxis, adhesion, and NLRP3 inflammasome activation. This prevents the inflammatory cascade triggered by MSU crystals. Most effective when started within 36 hours of flare onset.
IL-1β receptor blockade is the mechanism of anakinra, used for refractory gout. Colchicine acts upstream by disrupting microtubules.

questions.stem: Classic CSF finding in GBS?

choices.feedback: That's bacterial meningitis. GBS has normal cell count.

Correct! Elevated protein from nerve root inflammation + normal WBC count. This dissociation is characteristic of GBS. May be normal in the first week — repeat LP if initial is normal and clinical suspicion remains high.
Xanthochromia indicates subarachnoid hemorrhage.
Oligoclonal bands are characteristic of multiple sclerosis.

questions.stem: Most common preceding infection in GBS?

choices.feedback: E. coli is associated with UTI/meningitis, not GBS.

Correct! C. jejuni gastroenteritis precedes ~30% of GBS cases. Molecular mimicry between bacterial lipooligosaccharides and gangliosides on peripheral nerves triggers autoimmune attack. Anti-GM1 antibodies associated with axonal variants and worse prognosis.
GAS → rheumatic fever/glomerulonephritis, not GBS.
Pneumococcus causes pneumonia/meningitis, not typically GBS.

questions.stem: When to intubate a GBS patient?

choices.feedback: Hypoxia is a LATE sign — intubate based on respiratory mechanics, not oxygenation.

Correct! The 20/30 rule: FVC <20 mL/kg or negative inspiratory force (NIF) weaker than -30 cmH2O. Monitor FVC serially q4-6h. Rapid decline (>30% over 24h) also warrants intubation. 30% of GBS patients will need mechanical ventilation.
Intubation is based on objective respiratory parameters, not patient preference.
Far too late — proactive monitoring and early intubation are essential.

questions.stem: Treatment NOT effective in GBS?

choices.feedback: IVIG is first-line treatment for GBS.

Correct! Unlike most autoimmune conditions, corticosteroids are NOT effective in GBS and may worsen outcomes. This distinguishes GBS from CIDP (chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy), where steroids ARE effective. Treatment options: IVIG OR plasmapheresis (not both together).
Plasmapheresis is equally effective as IVIG for GBS.
Supportive care including respiratory monitoring is essential.

questions.stem: GBS weakness pattern is:

choices.text: Descending from cranial nerves to legs
choices.feedback: Descending pattern is more typical of myasthenia gravis or botulism.

Correct! Classic ascending paralysis: starts in feet/legs → thighs → trunk → arms → cranial nerves → respiratory muscles. Bilateral and relatively symmetric. Areflexia is the hallmark physical exam finding. Peak severity reached within 2-4 weeks.
Unilateral weakness suggests stroke, not GBS.
Fatigable weakness is characteristic of myasthenia gravis, not GBS.

questions.stem: Why should IVIG and plasmapheresis NOT be combined?

choices.feedback: There's no pharmacologic interaction per se.

choices.text: Plasmapheresis removes the infused IVIG from circulation
choices.feedback: Correct! PLEX removes plasma proteins including the therapeutic IVIG antibodies, negating its benefit. Choose one treatment. Both are equally effective as monotherapy. IVIG is often preferred due to ease of administration (no need for large-bore vascular access/apheresis machine).
Not the primary reason for avoiding combination.
The clinical rationale, not coverage, is why they shouldn't be combined.

questions.stem: Key feature distinguishing GBS from myasthenia gravis?

choices.feedback: GBS has areflexia/hyporeflexia; MG has normal reflexes.

choices.text: GBS has areflexia and ascending pattern; MG has normal reflexes and descending/fatigable weakness
choices.feedback: Correct! GBS: ascending, areflexic, can have sensory symptoms, CSF protein elevated, preceding infection. MG: descending (starts bulbar — ptosis, diplopia, dysarthria → limbs), fatigable weakness (worsens with repetitive use), normal reflexes, no sensory involvement, anti-AChR/MuSK antibodies.
Only myasthenic crisis requires ventilation; many MG patients are well-controlled.
