Cholinesterase inhibitors (pyridostigmine) treat MG, not GBS.

questions.stem: Prognosis of GBS — what percentage walk independently at 1 year?

choices.feedback: GBS has a much better prognosis than this.

Correct! ~80% of GBS patients walk independently at 1 year. Most recovery occurs in the first 6-12 months. Poor prognostic factors: older age, rapid progression, need for ventilation, preceding C. jejuni infection (axonal variant), low compound muscle action potential amplitudes on EMG.
While most recover well, 5-10% have significant residual disability and 3-5% die.
Recovery rate is higher than 50%.

questions.stem: Most common gynecologic cancer in the US?

choices.feedback: Ovarian is the deadliest but not the most common.

Correct! Endometrial cancer is #1 in incidence. Ovarian is #1 in mortality. Key risk factor: unopposed estrogen (obesity, PCOS, nulliparity, tamoxifen). Presentation: postmenopausal bleeding → always perform endometrial biopsy.
Cervical cancer incidence has declined significantly due to screening and HPV vaccination.
Vulvar cancer is rare.

questions.stem: #1 cause of gynecologic cancer death?

choices.feedback: Endometrial cancer generally has good prognosis due to early detection.

Correct! Ovarian cancer is the #1 GYN cancer killer because it presents late (75% stage III-IV at diagnosis). No effective screening test. Vague symptoms (bloating, satiety, pelvic pain) lead to delayed diagnosis. 5-year survival for advanced disease: ~30%.
Cervical cancer deaths declining due to Pap screening and HPV vaccination.
Uterine sarcomas are aggressive but rare.

questions.stem: Postmenopausal bleeding warrants what evaluation?

choices.text: Reassurance — spotting is normal after menopause
choices.feedback: ANY postmenopausal bleeding is abnormal and requires evaluation.

Correct! All postmenopausal bleeding needs evaluation to rule out endometrial cancer. Transvaginal US first: endometrial thickness ≤4mm has high negative predictive value. If >4mm or symptomatic → endometrial biopsy (office pipelle). ~10% of postmenopausal bleeding is endometrial cancer.
Pap screens for cervical cancer, not endometrial.
Imaging alone is insufficient — tissue sampling is needed.

questions.stem: HPV types most associated with cervical cancer?

choices.feedback: HPV 6 and 11 cause genital warts (low-risk), not cancer.

Correct! HPV 16 and 18 are high-risk types responsible for ~70% of cervical cancers. HPV 16 alone accounts for ~50%. The 9-valent HPV vaccine (Gardasil 9) covers HPV 6, 11, 16, 18, 31, 33, 45, 52, 58 — preventing ~90% of cervical cancers.
HPV 1 and 2 cause common skin warts.
These are low-risk HPV types.

questions.stem: BRCA1/2 mutation carriers should be offered:

choices.feedback: Mammograms are important but ovarian cancer risk also needs addressing.

Correct! BRCA1/2 carriers have 15-45% lifetime ovarian cancer risk. RRSO after completing childbearing reduces ovarian cancer risk by ~80% and breast cancer risk by ~50% (premenopausal oophorectomy). Also: enhanced breast screening (MRI + mammography), consider PARP inhibitors if cancer develops.
BRCA mutations don't increase cervical cancer risk.
Chemoprevention is not standard for BRCA carriers.

questions.stem: CIN 2-3 on colposcopy-directed biopsy. Treatment?

choices.feedback: CIN 1 can be observed. CIN 2-3 requires treatment due to progression risk.

Correct! CIN 2-3 (high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion) has significant progression risk to invasive cancer. Excision: LEEP (loop electrosurgical excision) preferred, or cold knife cone. Evaluates margins. Follow-up with co-testing at 12 and 24 months. CIN 1: observe (60% regress spontaneously).
Hysterectomy is for invasive cancer, not CIN.
Topical agents are not standard for CIN treatment.

questions.stem: Lynch syndrome increases lifetime risk of which GYN cancer to 40-60%?

choices.feedback: Lynch increases ovarian risk to ~10-12%, not 40-60%.

Correct! Lynch syndrome (HNPCC): mismatch repair mutations (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) increase endometrial cancer risk to 40-60% (higher than colon cancer risk in women!). Screen: annual endometrial biopsy starting age 30-35. Prophylactic hysterectomy + BSO after childbearing.
Lynch doesn't significantly increase cervical cancer risk.
Vulvar cancer is not a Lynch-associated cancer.

questions.stem: OCPs reduce ovarian cancer risk by:

choices.feedback: The protective effect is much larger.

Correct! Combined OCPs reduce ovarian cancer risk by 40-50% with ≥5 years of use. Protection lasts 15-20 years after stopping. Mechanism: suppression of ovulation reduces repetitive ovarian epithelial injury. Also reduces endometrial cancer risk by 50%. One of the most significant non-contraceptive benefits of OCPs.
Benefit occurs regardless of starting age, though longer use = greater protection.

choices.text: The effect is only seen in BRCA carriers
choices.feedback: Protection applies to all women, not just BRCA carriers.

questions.stem: Most common type of headache?

choices.feedback: Migraines are common but tension-type is the most prevalent.

Correct! Tension-type headache is the most common primary headache disorder. Bilateral, band-like, pressing/tightening quality, mild-moderate. No nausea/vomiting, no aura. Distinguished from migraine by the absence of unilateral pulsating quality, nausea, and photo/phonophobia.
Cluster headaches are relatively rare but extremely severe.
Medication overuse headache is common but secondary, not primary.

questions.stem: First-line ACUTE treatment for cluster headache?

choices.feedback: Oral triptans are too slow for cluster attacks (peak 15-180 min).

Correct! High-flow O2 (12-15 L/min × 15-20 min) is the first-line abortive. SC sumatriptan (faster than oral) is the second key treatment. Prevention: verapamil (#1 preventive). Cluster = the only primary headache where O2 is a primary treatment.
Opioids are NOT effective for cluster headache and risk dependency.
Acetaminophen is ineffective for the severe pain of cluster headache.

questions.stem: New headache in a patient >50 with jaw claudication and elevated ESR. Immediate step?

choices.feedback: MRI doesn't diagnose GCA. The immediate concern is preventing vision loss.

Correct! Giant cell arteritis (temporal arteritis): risk of irreversible vision loss from ophthalmic artery involvement. Start prednisone 60-80 mg/day IMMEDIATELY. Do NOT wait for temporal artery biopsy (gold standard — remains positive for 1-2 weeks after starting steroids). Biopsy may show skip lesions (sample ≥2 cm).

choices.text: Temporal artery biopsy first, then treat based on results
choices.feedback: Delaying treatment risks permanent vision loss.
GCA requires immediate steroids, not NSAIDs.

questions.stem: Cushing triad indicates:

choices.feedback: Cushing disease/syndrome is adrenal. Cushing TRIAD is a separate neurologic entity.

choices.text: Imminent brainstem herniation (HTN + bradycardia + irregular respirations)
choices.feedback: Correct! Cushing triad = last-ditch effort by brainstem to maintain cerebral perfusion against rising ICP: hypertension (to force blood past elevated ICP), reflexive bradycardia (baroreceptor response), irregular respirations (brainstem compression). This is a pre-terminal sign — emergent intervention required.
Not related to Cushing triad.
Not related to Cushing triad.

questions.stem: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri) typical patient?

choices.feedback: IIH predominantly affects young obese women.

choices.text: Obese young woman with headache and papilledema
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic demographics: obese women of childbearing age. Headache, papilledema (bilateral), pulsatile tinnitus, transient visual obscurations. Normal MRI. LP: opening pressure >25 cm H₂O with normal CSF. Associated drugs: tetracyclines, vitamin A/retinoids, growth hormone. Treatment: weight loss, acetazolamide.

choices.text: Thin elderly woman with temporal headache
choices.feedback: That profile suggests temporal arteritis/GCA.
Exercise headache is a different entity.

questions.stem: CN VI palsy with increased ICP is called a 'false localizing sign' because:

choices.text: CN VI is never affected by ICP
choices.feedback: CN VI is actually commonly affected, which is why it's a false localizer.

choices.text: The long intracranial course makes CN VI vulnerable to diffuse ICP elevation, not a focal lesion
choices.feedback: Correct! CN VI (abducens) has the longest intracranial course of any cranial nerve. Diffusely elevated ICP stretches it over the petrous ridge → lateral rectus palsy → inability to abduct the eye. This occurs with generalized ICP elevation, not necessarily a lesion at that specific location — hence 'false localizing.'

choices.text: CN VI is too small to be affected by mass lesions
choices.feedback: Size is not the issue; it's the vulnerability from the long course.

choices.text: The palsy is always bilateral, indicating diffuse disease
choices.feedback: Can be unilateral or bilateral. The key is that it doesn't indicate the lesion location.

questions.stem: Triptans are contraindicated in:

choices.feedback: Triptans are simply ineffective for tension headache, not necessarily contraindicated.

choices.text: Patients with coronary artery disease or uncontrolled HTN
choices.feedback: Correct! Triptans are 5-HT1B/1D agonists → vasoconstriction. Contraindicated in: CAD, prior MI/stroke, uncontrolled HTN, basilar/hemiplegic migraine, peripheral vascular disease. Also avoid within 24h of ergotamine. Risk of serotonin syndrome if combined with SSRIs (low but real).
Age alone is not a contraindication — cardiovascular risk assessment determines eligibility.

choices.text: Patients who have already taken NSAIDs
choices.feedback: Triptans can be safely combined with or used after NSAIDs.

questions.stem: Hyperventilation reduces ICP by:

choices.text: Increasing oxygen delivery to the brain
choices.feedback: The mechanism is CO2-mediated vasoconstriction, not oxygen delivery.

Correct! Reducing PCO2 from ~40 to 30-35 mmHg causes cerebral arteriolar vasoconstriction → decreased cerebral blood volume → rapid ICP reduction. TEMPORARY effect only (hours) — brain compensates. Used as a bridge to definitive treatment (mannitol, surgery). Target PCO2 30-35 mmHg. Excessive hyperventilation (<25) risks cerebral ischemia.
Hyperventilation does not directly affect CSF dynamics.
While hyperventilation affects metabolism, the ICP mechanism is vasoconstriction.

questions.stem: Most common type of primary headache?

choices.feedback: Migraine is the second most common. Tension-type is more prevalent.

Correct! Tension-type is the most common primary headache (~70% of primary headaches). Bilateral, band-like pressure, mild-moderate severity. No aura, typically no N/V or photophobia. Associated with pericranial muscle tenderness and stress.
Cluster is the least common of the three major primary headaches.
TN is relatively uncommon and classified differently.

questions.stem: Behavioral difference between migraine and cluster headache?

choices.text: Both patients lie still in dark rooms
choices.feedback: Only migraine patients prefer stillness. Cluster patients are agitated.

Correct! Migraine → photophobia, worsened by movement, patients lie in dark quiet room. Cluster → severe pain causes restlessness — patients pace, rock, bang head. This behavioral difference is a high-yield board pearl for distinguishing the two.

choices.text: Cluster patients prefer standing; migraine patients prefer sitting
choices.feedback: It's more about agitation (cluster) vs stillness (migraine).
The behavioral distinction is one of the most reliable clinical clues.

questions.stem: First-line acute treatment for cluster headache?

choices.feedback: Oral triptans are too slow for cluster headache (peaks in 15-180 min). SC sumatriptan or high-flow O2 needed.

Correct! 100% O2 at 12-15 L/min via non-rebreather × 15-20 min is first-line. ~70% response rate within 15 min. Also effective: subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg (fastest triptan route). Prophylaxis: verapamil (first-line). Prednisone for cluster period bridge.
Opioids are ineffective for cluster headache and should be avoided.
Simple analgesics are ineffective for the severe pain of cluster headache.

questions.stem: New headache in a 65-year-old with scalp tenderness and jaw claudication. Immediate concern?

choices.feedback: Tension headache doesn't cause jaw claudication or scalp tenderness in this pattern.

Correct! GCA: age >50, new headache, scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, visual symptoms (amaurosis fugax, vision loss). Elevated ESR (often >50) and CRP. START HIGH-DOSE STEROIDS IMMEDIATELY — do NOT wait for biopsy (prevents irreversible blindness from ophthalmic artery vasculitis). Temporal artery biopsy confirms diagnosis.
New headache at 65 with these features is not typical migraine.
MOH presents differently — progressive daily headache from analgesic overuse.

questions.stem: Triptans are CONTRAINDICATED in:

choices.feedback: Some triptans are approved for adolescents. Age alone is not a contraindication.

choices.text: Coronary artery disease, uncontrolled HTN, hemiplegic migraine
choices.feedback: Correct! Triptans are 5-HT1B/1D agonists that cause vasoconstriction. Contraindicated in: CAD, uncontrolled HTN, prior stroke/TIA, hemiplegic or basilar migraine, peripheral vascular disease, concurrent ergotamines or MAOIs. Risk: coronary vasospasm, MI, stroke.
Photophobia is a migraine symptom — triptans are the treatment for migraine.
Triptans are category C — can be used with caution. They're not absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy.

questions.stem: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (pseudotumor cerebri) classic patient?

choices.feedback: IIH predominantly affects young obese women.

choices.text: Obese young woman with headache, papilledema, and elevated LP opening pressure
choices.feedback: Correct! Classic: young obese woman (often on tetracyclines, vitamin A, or OCP) with headache, papilledema, visual changes (enlarged blind spot), pulsatile tinnitus. LP: opening pressure >25 cmH2O with normal CSF composition. MRI: normal (or empty sella, flattened posterior sclera). Treatment: weight loss, acetazolamide, serial LP, optic nerve sheath fenestration if vision threatened.

choices.text: Child with fever and neck stiffness
choices.feedback: This suggests meningitis, not IIH.
This pattern suggests normal pressure hydrocephalus or dementia.

questions.stem: First-line treatment for trigeminal neuralgia?

choices.feedback: Triptans treat migraine and cluster headache, not TN.

Correct! Carbamazepine (sodium channel blocker) is first-line for trigeminal neuralgia. Voltage-dependent sodium channel stabilization reduces neuronal hyperexcitability. Oxcarbazepine is an alternative with fewer side effects. For refractory: microvascular decompression (surgical — addresses vascular compression of CN V). MRI with CISS/FIESTA sequence to evaluate for compression.
Amitriptyline is used for tension headache and migraine prophylaxis.
Verapamil is used for cluster headache prophylaxis.

questions.stem: SNOOP red flags in headache — the 'O' for Onset refers to:

choices.feedback: While concerning, the SNOOP 'O' refers to sudden onset.

choices.text: Sudden onset thunderclap headache (SAH until proven otherwise)
choices.feedback: Correct! Thunderclap headache = maximal intensity within seconds. SAH must be excluded: CT head (95-98% sensitive within 6h) → LP if CT negative (xanthochromia). Other causes of thunderclap: cerebral venous thrombosis, pituitary apoplexy, cervical artery dissection, RCVS. Never dismiss a thunderclap headache as benign.
New headache after age 50 (the second 'O' in SNOOP) is the age concern.
Exertional headache can be concerning but 'O' specifically refers to sudden/thunderclap onset.

questions.stem: A patient has EF 30%, dilated LV, and S3 gallop. Classification?

choices.feedback: HFpEF has EF ≥50% with stiff/non-compliant ventricle. EF 30% is reduced.
Correct! EF ≤40% = HFrEF. Dilated LV with impaired contraction. S3 gallop from rapid ventricular filling (volume overload). This patient needs GDMT: ARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i.
Cor pulmonale = right heart failure from pulmonary disease, not primary LV dilation.
Restrictive = stiff ventricle with preserved size but impaired filling (diastolic).

questions.stem: Which diuretic REDUCES MORTALITY in HFrEF?

choices.feedback: Loop diuretics relieve congestion symptoms but have NO mortality benefit.

Correct! Spironolactone (MRA) reduces mortality by ~30% in HFrEF (RALES trial). It's an aldosterone antagonist with anti-fibrotic and anti-remodeling effects beyond diuresis. Monitor for hyperkalemia. Eplerenone is an alternative with fewer anti-androgenic side effects.
HCTZ is a thiazide diuretic used for HTN; no mortality benefit in HF.
Metolazone augments loop diuretic effect but does not reduce mortality.

questions.stem: Only three beta-blockers are proven to reduce mortality in HFrEF. They are:

choices.feedback: None of these three have been proven in HFrEF mortality trials.

Correct! ONLY these 3 BBs are evidence-based for HFrEF mortality reduction: carvedilol (COPERNICUS), metoprolol succinate (MERIT-HF), bisoprolol (CIBIS-II). Metoprolol tartrate is NOT the same — only the succinate (extended-release) formulation is proven.
Metoprolol tartrate (immediate-release) is NOT proven. Sotalol is primarily antiarrhythmic.
HF mortality benefit is specific to these 3 agents only.

questions.stem: SGLT2 inhibitors in HFrEF benefit which patients?

choices.feedback: A common misconception. SGLT2i benefit HFrEF patients regardless of diabetes status.

Correct! DAPA-HF (dapagliflozin) and EMPEROR-Reduced (empagliflozin) showed mortality and HF hospitalization reduction in HFrEF patients WITH and WITHOUT diabetes. SGLT2i are now a standard pillar of HF therapy. Mechanism includes natriuresis, improved cardiac energetics, and reduced fibrosis.
CKD patients benefit from SGLT2i, but the HF benefit extends to patients with normal kidney function.
Benefit seen across NYHA classes II-IV.

questions.stem: S4 gallop is caused by:

choices.feedback: That describes S3. S3 = passive rapid filling in early diastole.

choices.text: Atrial contraction against a stiff ventricle
choices.feedback: Correct! S4 = late diastolic sound from forceful atrial contraction pushing blood into a non-compliant ventricle. Causes: HTN (LVH), HCM, aortic stenosis, acute MI. S4 disappears in A-fib (no organized atrial kick). S4 can be normal in elderly.
MVP causes a mid-systolic click, not S4.
VSD causes a holosystolic murmur, not a gallop.

questions.stem: BNP may be FALSELY LOW in which condition?

choices.feedback: Renal failure causes falsely HIGH BNP (reduced clearance).

Correct! BNP is falsely LOW in obesity — adipose tissue expresses neprilysin which degrades BNP. Use lower diagnostic threshold in obese patients. NT-proBNP may be more reliable. BNP falsely HIGH in: renal failure, A-fib, PE, older age, sepsis.
A-fib causes falsely elevated BNP from atrial stretch.
BNP naturally increases with age (falsely high).

questions.stem: Acute decompensated HF with pulmonary edema. First-line treatment?

choices.feedback: Do NOT start beta-blockers during acute decompensation — may worsen hemodynamics.

Correct! Acute pulmonary edema: sit upright, O2/BiPAP (NIV preferred over intubation), IV furosemide (diuresis), IV nitroglycerin (preload/afterload reduction). BiPAP reduces work of breathing and preload. If cardiogenic shock (hypotension): inotropes (dobutamine) ± mechanical support.
Fluid bolus worsens pulmonary edema! These patients are volume overloaded.
Cardioversion is for arrhythmia-driven instability, not primary pulmonary edema.

questions.stem: In HFrEF, sacubitril-valsartan (ARNI) is preferred over ACEi because:

choices.text: It is cheaper and more widely available
choices.feedback: ARNIs are actually more expensive than ACEi.

choices.text: PARADIGM-HF showed superior mortality and HF hospitalization reduction
choices.feedback: Correct! PARADIGM-HF: sacubitril-valsartan reduced CV death and HF hospitalization by 20% vs enalapril. Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin (increases natriuretic peptides → vasodilation, natriuresis). Key safety: 36-hour washout when switching FROM ACEi TO ARNI (risk of angioedema). Never combine ARNI + ACEi.

choices.text: It has fewer side effects than all other HF drugs
choices.feedback: ARNIs can cause hypotension and have the angioedema risk, especially without washout from ACEi.

choices.text: It replaces the need for beta-blockers
choices.feedback: ARNI does NOT replace BBs. All four GDMT pillars should be used together.

questions.stem: Hepatitis B serology: HBsAg(-), Anti-HBs(+), Anti-HBc(-). Interpretation?

choices.feedback: Acute would have HBsAg+ and IgM anti-HBc+.

Correct! Anti-HBs ALONE positive (no anti-HBc) = immune from vaccination. The vaccine produces only anti-HBs. Natural infection produces both anti-HBs AND anti-HBc. This serologic pattern is the single most tested HBV question.
Chronic = HBsAg+ and anti-HBc IgG+.
Window: HBsAg cleared, anti-HBs not yet positive, IgM anti-HBc positive.

questions.stem: Which hepatitis virus is most likely to become chronic?

choices.feedback: Hep A NEVER becomes chronic.

Correct! HCV → chronic in ~80% of infections. Most patients are asymptomatic during acute phase. Chronic HCV → cirrhosis (20-30% over 20-30 years) → HCC. NOW curable with DAAs (>95% SVR in 8-12 weeks). Screen all adults 18-79 at least once (USPSTF).
Hep E rarely becomes chronic (only in immunosuppressed).
Hep D requires Hep B — cannot exist independently.

questions.stem: Hepatitis E is particularly dangerous in:

choices.feedback: HEV is usually mild in children.

choices.text: Pregnant women (up to 20% mortality in 3rd trimester)
choices.feedback: Correct! HEV in pregnancy, especially 3rd trimester: fulminant hepatic failure in up to 20%. Fecal-oral transmission via contaminated water. Endemic in South/Southeast Asia, Africa, Mexico. Generally self-limited in non-pregnant adults. No approved vaccine in US.
Elderly may have complications but pregnancy is the key risk factor.
Vertical transmission of HEV is rare compared to HBV.

questions.stem: Hepatitis C is now curable with:

choices.feedback: Old regimen — poorly tolerated, ~50% cure rate. Replaced by DAAs.

Correct! DAAs target HCV proteins directly: NS5B polymerase inhibitors (sofosbuvir), NS5A inhibitors (velpatasvir, ledipasvir), NS3 protease inhibitors (glecaprevir). Sofosbuvir/velpatasvir = pan-genotypic, 12 weeks, >95% SVR. Revolution in hepatitis treatment.
Transplant is for end-stage liver disease. DAAs can cure the infection and prevent progression.
Tenofovir treats Hepatitis B, not C.

questions.stem: HBV can cause HCC even without cirrhosis because:

choices.text: All hepatitis viruses cause cancer equally
choices.feedback: HCC risk varies by virus. HCV typically requires cirrhosis first.

choices.text: HBV DNA integrates into host genome → direct oncogenesis
choices.feedback: Correct! HBV is a DNA virus that integrates into hepatocyte genome → insertional mutagenesis, chromosomal instability, direct activation of oncogenes (including HBx protein). This means HBV carriers need HCC screening even WITHOUT cirrhosis (especially if Asian male >40, Asian female >50, African >20, family history of HCC).

choices.text: HBV always causes rapid progression to cancer
choices.feedback: HBV-related HCC develops over years-decades.

choices.text: Only in patients co-infected with HDV
choices.feedback: HBV alone has oncogenic potential via DNA integration.

questions.stem: Infant born to HBsAg-positive mother. Immediate management?

choices.feedback: Delay increases chronic infection risk from ~90% to nearly certain.

choices.text: HBIG + HBV vaccine within 12 hours of birth
choices.feedback: Correct! Both HBIG (passive immunity) + first dose of HBV vaccine (active immunity) within 12h of birth. Reduces vertical transmission from ~90% to <5%. Complete the 3-dose vaccine series. Check HBsAg and anti-HBs at 9-12 months to confirm protection. Mother should receive antiviral if high viral load in 3rd trimester.
Too late. HBIG + vaccine must be given within 12h.
C-section does not prevent HBV transmission (unlike HCV, where it may help).

questions.stem: Window period in HBV serology means:

choices.feedback: Vaccinated = anti-HBs alone positive.

choices.text: HBsAg cleared but anti-HBs not yet positive; only IgM anti-HBc detectable
choices.feedback: Correct! Window period: HBsAg has been cleared by immune system, but anti-HBs hasn't risen to detectable levels yet. The only positive marker is IgM anti-HBc. If you don't test for anti-HBc, you'll MISS the diagnosis. Window period typically lasts weeks. Resolves when anti-HBs appears.
That doesn't occur — HBsAg and anti-HBs are mutually exclusive (mostly).
Chronic: HBsAg+ persisting >6 months with IgG anti-HBc+.

questions.stem: Hepatitis D requires which co-factor?

choices.feedback: HAV and HDV have no relationship.

choices.text: Hepatitis B surface antigen (HBsAg) — HDV is a defective virus needing HBV envelope
choices.feedback: Correct! HDV is a defective RNA virus that requires HBsAg to form its viral envelope. Can only infect HBV-positive patients. Two patterns: coinfection (both acute simultaneously — usually self-limited) vs superinfection (HDV on chronic HBV — worse prognosis, accelerated fibrosis). HBV vaccination prevents HDV.
HCV and HDV are independent. HDV specifically needs HBV.
Immunosuppression isn't required for HDV, though it may worsen outcomes.

questions.stem: Most COMMON type of hernia?

choices.feedback: Second most common inguinal; older males through Hesselbach triangle.
Correct! Most common in ALL ages and BOTH sexes. Through deep ring, lateral to epigastric vessels. May descend into scrotum.
Less common but highest strangulation risk.
Common in infants but less common overall.

questions.stem: Inferior epigastric vessels distinguish:

choices.feedback: Inguinal ligament separates those.
Correct! Indirect = LATERAL. Direct = MEDIAL. MDs are Direct = Medial = Direct.
Distinguished by surgical history.
Rare types, different anatomy.

questions.stem: Which hernia has HIGHEST strangulation risk?

choices.feedback: Wide inguinal ring usually allows reduction.
Correct! ~40% incarceration rate — narrow, rigid canal. ALL femoral hernias should be repaired regardless of symptoms.
Lower strangulation rate than femoral.
LOWEST incarceration rate — wide area of floor weakness.

questions.stem: Tender, non-reducible groin mass with fever and vomiting, erythematous skin. Management?

choices.feedback: NEVER reduce suspected strangulated hernia — may push necrotic bowel intraperitoneally.
Correct! Strangulated hernia = surgical emergency. OR for exploration, bowel assessment, ± resection and repair.
Clinically obvious strangulation should not be delayed for imaging.
Observation risks bowel necrosis and death.

questions.stem: Infant umbilical hernias close spontaneously by age:

choices.feedback: Most still present at 1 year.
Correct! Most close by age 4-5 (especially if <1.5 cm). Surgery if: persists past 5, defect >1.5-2 cm, symptomatic, or incarcerated.
Too early for spontaneous closure.
By age 5 the surgical decision should already be made.

questions.stem: Structure at greatest risk during inguinal hernia repair?

choices.feedback: Below inguinal ligament, not in operative field.
Correct! Runs through inguinal canal. Injury → chronic inguinodynia (most common long-term complication). Also at risk: vas deferens, testicular vessels.
Deep retroperitoneal, not in inguinal field.
Intra-abdominal vessel, not in inguinal field.

questions.stem: Hesselbach triangle boundaries?

choices.text: Inguinal ligament, external oblique, internal oblique
choices.feedback: Those form inguinal canal walls.

choices.text: Epigastric vessels (lateral), rectus sheath (medial), inguinal ligament (inferior)
choices.feedback: Correct! Direct hernias protrude through this triangle. Understanding boundaries distinguishes direct from indirect intraoperatively.

choices.text: Femoral vein, Cooper ligament, lacunar ligament
choices.feedback: Those border the femoral canal.

choices.text: Transversus abdominis, conjoint tendon, iliopubic tract
choices.feedback: Canal structures but not Hesselbach triangle borders.

questions.stem: Why is watchful waiting NOT appropriate for femoral hernias?

choices.feedback: Not always, but the narrow canal is the issue.
Correct! Rigid, narrow femoral canal cannot accommodate swelling → rapid incarceration/strangulation. All femoral hernias should be repaired electively.
Recurrence isn't the reason; strangulation risk is.
Some can be reduced, but high recurrence/strangulation risk mandates repair.

questions.stem: A patient with CD4 count of 80 presents with headache, fever, and neck stiffness. LP shows elevated opening pressure (35 cm H2O), low glucose, and positive India ink prep. What is the diagnosis and initial treatment?

choices.feedback: Bacterial meningitis would show elevated WBC (PMNs) and gram stain positivity, not India ink positivity.

Correct! India ink positive = Cryptococcus neoformans. Treatment: induction with amphotericin B + flucytosine (2 weeks), then consolidation with fluconazole (8 weeks), then maintenance fluconazole. Elevated opening pressure needs therapeutic LPs (serial large-volume taps) — NOT ventriculostomy as first-line.
Toxoplasmosis presents with ring-enhancing brain lesions, not meningitis with India ink positivity.
TB meningitis has similar CSF but India ink would be negative. AFB stain/culture would be needed.

questions.stem: Before starting abacavir, which test must be performed?

choices.feedback: G6PD testing is needed before dapsone or rasburicase, not abacavir.
Correct! HLA-B*5701 testing is MANDATORY before abacavir. Positive HLA-B*5701 → hypersensitivity reaction (fever, rash, GI symptoms, potentially fatal rechallenge). If positive, NEVER give abacavir.
CYP2D6 is relevant for some pain medications and antidepressants, not abacavir.
Troponin is not relevant to abacavir prescribing.

questions.stem: A newly diagnosed HIV+ patient starts ART. Four weeks later, a previously known small pulmonary TB lesion dramatically worsens with high fevers and new lymphadenopathy. What is happening?

choices.feedback: ART failure would show rising viral load and falling CD4, not worsening of a known OI.
Drug resistance is possible but the timing (4 weeks after ART initiation) is classic for IRIS.

Correct! IRIS: paradoxical worsening of a known infection after ART initiation due to immune reconstitution. TB-IRIS is common. Continue ART + TB treatment + consider steroids if severe. Most common within 4-8 weeks of starting ART, especially if CD4 was very low.
PJP would show bilateral ground-glass opacities, not worsening of a known TB lesion.

questions.stem: What is the maximum time window for starting post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) after a high-risk HIV exposure?

choices.feedback: 24 hours is ideal but the window extends further.
Correct! PEP should be started within 72 hours of exposure. The sooner the better — efficacy decreases with time. 3-drug regimen for 28 days. Beyond 72 hours, PEP is generally not recommended.
7 days is too late for PEP to be effective.
14 days is far outside the PEP window.

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Alobar holoprosencephaly = complete failure of the prosencephalon to cleave into two hemispheres. Findings: single monoventricle, fused thalami, absent falx, absent interhemispheric fissure. The facial features reflect the adage: 'The face predicts the brain.'
Hydranencephaly has cerebral hemispheres replaced by CSF-filled sacs, but the falx is PRESENT and the thalami are SEPARATE. It's a destructive lesion, not a cleavage failure.
Hydrocephalus has DILATED but SEPARATE ventricles with preserved midline structures. The fused single ventricle here indicates failure of cleavage.
ACC has absent corpus callosum but SEPARATE hemispheres and ventricles (often with colpocephaly). The hemispheres cleave normally.

questions.stem: Which signaling pathway is critical for forebrain cleavage?

choices.feedback: Correct! SHH from the prechordal plate and notochord patterns the ventral forebrain. It induces the midline cleavage that separates hemispheres. SHH mutations are the most common genetic cause of HPE. Cholesterol is required for SHH processing — this is why teratogens that affect cholesterol (cyclopamine, alcohol) cause HPE.
Wnt signaling patterns the dorsal brain but is not the primary driver of ventral midline cleavage.
Notch is important for neuronal differentiation but not specifically for forebrain cleavage.
FGFs contribute to brain development but SHH is the critical pathway for ventral forebrain patterning and midline cleavage.

questions.stem: Which facial anomaly is most specific to severe holoprosencephaly?

choices.feedback: Cleft lip/palate occurs in HPE but is not specific — it occurs in many other conditions. Midline clefting is more suggestive than lateral clefting.

Correct! Cyclopia (single fused eye) with a proboscis (tube-like nasal structure above the eye) is pathognomonic for alobar HPE. The facial midline fails to develop, just as the brain midline fails. Less severe facial features in the HPE spectrum: hypotelorism, single central incisor, flat nose, median cleft lip.
Macroglossia is associated with Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome and Down syndrome, not HPE.
Micrognathia is associated with Pierre Robin sequence and Treacher Collins, not specifically HPE.

questions.stem: Which HPE subtype has the best prognosis?

choices.feedback: Alobar HPE has the worst prognosis regardless of intervention. VP shunting does not address the fundamental brain malformation.
Semilobar has intermediate prognosis — better than alobar but still significant developmental disability.

Correct! Lobar HPE has the best prognosis within the spectrum. The hemispheres are nearly completely separated. Some individuals with lobar HPE have mild-moderate intellectual disability and can survive to adulthood. Some may have only a single central incisor as the only external clue (microform HPE).
MIH (syntelencephaly) has a variable prognosis but is not the classic answer for 'best prognosis.' Lobar HPE is the standard answer.

questions.stem: Why does this child have temperature instability?

choices.feedback: While seizures can affect temperature, the persistent instability suggests a hypothalamic cause.

Correct! The hypothalamus develops from the diencephalon, which is the midline structure affected by HPE. Even in lobar HPE, hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction is common: temperature dysregulation, diabetes insipidus, growth hormone deficiency, cortisol deficiency, thyroid dysfunction. All HPE patients need endocrine screening.
HPE does not typically cause immunodeficiency. The temperature instability pattern (both hypo- and hyperthermia) suggests hypothalamic, not infectious, origin.
If this is the first presentation with seizures, medications haven't been started yet. Temperature instability preceded seizure treatment.

questions.stem: FIRST intervention in hyperkalemia with EKG changes?

choices.feedback: Insulin shifts K+ into cells but doesn't immediately stabilize the heart.

Correct! Calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane within 1-3 minutes by raising the threshold potential (antagonizes K+ effect on cardiac conduction). Does NOT lower serum K+. Repeat in 5 min if EKG unchanged. Then proceed to K+-lowering measures.
Kayexalate removes K+ but takes hours — too slow for acute emergency.
Diuretics help excrete K+ but don't protect the heart immediately.

questions.stem: Earliest EKG change in hyperkalemia?

choices.feedback: Wide QRS occurs later (K+ 7.0-8.0).

Correct! Peaked, tent-shaped T waves are the earliest and most sensitive EKG finding (K+ ~5.5-6.5). Progression: peaked T → PR prolongation → P wave loss → QRS widening → sine wave → VF/asystole. Any EKG change = treat immediately.
ST elevation is associated with ischemia/pericarditis, not hyperkalemia.
Prolonged QT is more associated with HYPOkalemia (U waves).

questions.stem: Insulin lowers K+ by:

choices.feedback: Insulin doesn't increase renal K+ excretion. That's diuretics.

choices.text: Driving K+ into cells via Na/K-ATPase activation
choices.feedback: Correct! Insulin stimulates the Na+/K+-ATPase pump → K+ moves from ECF into cells. Give 10 units regular insulin IV + 25g dextrose (D50) to prevent hypoglycemia. Most RELIABLE shifting agent. Onset 15-30 min, lasts 4-6h. Monitor glucose q1h.

choices.text: Binding K+ in the GI tract
choices.feedback: That's the mechanism of Kayexalate/patiromer.
K+ is not chelated by any medication.

questions.stem: #1 cause of hyperkalemia?

choices.feedback: Dietary excess alone rarely causes hyperkalemia with normal kidney function.

Correct! The kidneys are responsible for 90% of K+ excretion. Impaired renal function is the #1 cause of hyperkalemia. Other common causes: ACEi/ARBs + spironolactone (especially in CKD patients), acidosis, cell lysis (rhabdomyolysis, TLS).
Insulin LOWERS K+, not raises it.
Alkalosis causes HYPOkalemia (K+ shifts into cells).

questions.stem: Sine wave pattern on EKG with K+ >8 indicates:

choices.feedback: Sine wave = pre-arrest rhythm. Immediate action needed.

Correct! Sine wave = K+ >8.0, fusion of widened QRS with peaked T waves. VF or asystole imminent. IV calcium gluconate STAT + insulin/glucose + emergent hemodialysis. Defibrillation may be needed if VF develops.
Sine wave in context of known hyperkalemia is real and deadly.

choices.text: Typical finding — no action needed if asymptomatic
choices.feedback: Even 'asymptomatic' patients with sine wave are in immediate danger.

questions.stem: Which medications commonly CAUSE hyperkalemia?

choices.feedback: These cause HYPOkalemia from renal K+ wasting.

Correct! ACEi/ARBs reduce aldosterone → decreased K+ excretion. K+-sparing diuretics (spironolactone, eplerenone, amiloride, triamterene) directly block K+ secretion. The combination is particularly dangerous in CKD. Also: NSAIDs, beta-blockers, calcineurin inhibitors, heparin, TMP-SMX (blocks ENaC).
CCBs don't significantly affect potassium levels.
Diabetes medications don't typically cause hyperkalemia directly.

questions.stem: Pseudohyperkalemia should be suspected when:

choices.text: K+ is mildly elevated with symptoms
choices.feedback: Symptomatic hyperkalemia is usually real.

choices.text: K+ is elevated but EKG is normal and result is unexpected
choices.feedback: Correct! Pseudohyperkalemia: falsely elevated K+ from hemolyzed sample (most common), prolonged tourniquet, excessive fist clenching, extreme leukocytosis (>100K), thrombocytosis (>500K). Always repeat the test if unexpected elevation with normal EKG and no clinical cause.
K+ >8 with cardiac arrest is real hyperkalemia, not pseudo.

choices.text: Any hyperkalemia in a dialysis patient
choices.feedback: Dialysis patients frequently have real hyperkalemia.

questions.stem: For every 0.1 decrease in pH, K+ rises approximately:

choices.feedback: The relationship is more pronounced than this.

Correct! Acidosis causes transcellular K+ shift: H+ enters cells to be buffered → K+ exits to maintain electroneutrality. This means a patient in DKA with pH 7.0 may have artificially elevated K+ that will DROP dramatically once acidosis is corrected with insulin. Monitor K+ closely during correction.
This overstates the relationship.
While approximate, the 0.6 mEq/L per 0.1 pH rule is clinically useful.

questions.stem: What DEFINES a hypertensive emergency vs urgency?

choices.feedback: The absolute number doesn't define it — a patient at 190/115 WITH stroke is an emergency.

Correct! End-organ damage (encephalopathy, ACS, HF, dissection, AKI, eclampsia, retinopathy) is what makes it an EMERGENCY. Without end-organ damage, even very high BP is urgency (treated with oral meds). The BP number alone is insufficient.
Duration is not the distinguishing factor.
Treatment resistance doesn't define emergency vs urgency.

questions.stem: How fast should BP be lowered in most hypertensive emergencies?

choices.feedback: Too rapid — risks watershed infarcts from cerebral hypoperfusion.

choices.text: Reduce MAP by ≤25% in first hour, then 160/100 over 2-6 hours
choices.feedback: Correct! Gradual reduction prevents hypoperfusion injury. The brain has adapted autoregulatory curves to higher pressures. Exceptions: aortic dissection (aggressive — SBP <120 ASAP), eclampsia (<160 within 30-60 min), ischemic stroke pre-tPA (<185/110).

choices.text: Do not lower BP at all for 24 hours
choices.feedback: Ongoing end-organ damage requires prompt (but controlled) BP reduction.
50% reduction risks ischemic complications from hypoperfusion.

questions.stem: In hypertensive emergency with aortic dissection, the BP target is:

choices.feedback: That's the general rule. Dissection requires MORE aggressive reduction.

choices.text: SBP <120 mmHg and HR <60 bpm within minutes
choices.feedback: Correct! Dissection is the exception to gradual reduction. Immediate goal: SBP 100-120, HR <60 with IV BB (esmolol/labetalol) to minimize aortic wall shear stress. Add vasodilator only after HR controlled.
Far too slow for dissection — mortality increases 1-2% per hour.

choices.text: No specific target — just start oral medications
choices.feedback: Dissection requires IV medications and aggressive targets.

questions.stem: PRES (Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome) shows what on MRI?

choices.feedback: PRES primarily affects posterior (parieto-occipital) regions.

Correct! PRES = vasogenic edema from loss of cerebral autoregulation in the posterior circulation (watershed zone). MRI: bilateral parieto-occipital T2/FLAIR hyperintensities. Usually reversible with BP control. Causes: hypertensive emergency, eclampsia, immunosuppressants (tacrolimus, cyclosporine).
Gray matter necrosis suggests ischemic injury, not PRES.
Midline shift suggests mass effect (tumor, hemorrhage), not PRES.

questions.stem: Which IV antihypertensive is preferred in hypertensive emergency with AKI?

choices.feedback: Nitroprusside is renally cleared and risks cyanide toxicity. Not ideal with AKI.

Correct! Fenoldopam is a dopamine-1 receptor agonist that lowers BP while improving renal blood flow and GFR. It's the preferred agent when hypertensive emergency is accompanied by acute kidney injury. No cyanide toxicity risk (unlike nitroprusside).
Esmolol is effective for HR/BP but doesn't specifically improve renal perfusion.
Hydralazine has unpredictable response and reflex tachycardia — not preferred.

questions.stem: A pregnant patient at 36 weeks with BP 180/110, headache, and proteinuria. Diagnosis and first-line antihypertensive?

choices.feedback: ACE inhibitors are CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (teratogenic). This is also an emergency, not urgency.

choices.text: Severe preeclampsia — IV labetalol or hydralazine
choices.feedback: Correct! Severe preeclampsia: BP ≥160/110 + proteinuria + end-organ signs (headache, visual changes). First-line: IV labetalol or IV hydralazine (both safe in pregnancy). Also give IV magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis. Definitive treatment: delivery. ACEi/ARBs absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy.
This is acute severe preeclampsia requiring emergency management, not routine chronic HTN.
Eclampsia = preeclampsia + seizures. Treatment is IV magnesium, not diazepam. Also, this patient hasn't seized yet.

questions.stem: Nitroprusside risk with prolonged use?

choices.feedback: Nitroprusside is metabolized to cyanide, not a direct hepatotoxin.

Correct! Nitroprusside → cyanide → metabolized to thiocyanate. Prolonged use (>48-72h) or high doses → cyanide accumulation → lactic acidosis, AMS, seizures. Treatment: hydroxocobalamin (cyanide antidote) or sodium thiosulfate. Monitor thiocyanate levels if used >48h.
Bronchospasm is associated with non-selective beta-blockers, not nitroprusside.
Nitroprusside doesn't cause permanent renal damage directly.

questions.stem: Sublingual nifedipine should be AVOIDED in hypertensive emergencies because:

choices.text: It is ineffective at lowering BP
choices.feedback: Nifedipine is actually very effective but too unpredictable.

choices.text: It causes unpredictable precipitous BP drops → stroke/MI
choices.feedback: Correct! Sublingual/bite-and-swallow nifedipine causes rapid, uncontrolled BP drops that can precipitate cerebral ischemia, MI, or death. Banned from use in hypertensive emergencies. Use IV titratable agents instead for controlled, predictable BP reduction.

choices.text: It only works for pulmonary hypertension
choices.feedback: Nifedipine works systemically but is too dangerous in this context.
Nifedipine is oral/sublingual. The issue is unpredictable absorption and BP drop.

questions.stem: Most common cause of euvolemic hyponatremia?

choices.feedback: Psychogenic polydipsia causes euvolemic hyponatremia but is less common than SIADH.

Correct! SIADH is the #1 cause of euvolemic hyponatremia. Criteria: hypo-osmolar serum + concentrated urine (Uosm >100) + UNa >20 + euvolemic + normal thyroid/adrenal. Must exclude hypothyroidism and adrenal insufficiency first. Common causes: CNS/pulmonary disease, drugs, SCLC (ectopic ADH).
CHF causes hypervolemic hyponatremia (edema, JVD).
Adrenal insufficiency can cause euvolemic hyponatremia but is less common than SIADH.

questions.stem: Maximum safe rate of Na+ correction in chronic hyponatremia?

choices.feedback: This rate risks osmotic demyelination syndrome.

Correct! Maximum 8-10 mEq/L in 24h for chronic hyponatremia. Brain has adapted by losing intracellular osmoles. Rapid correction → water exits brain cells → pontine demyelination (ODS/CPM). If overcorrected: administer D5W or desmopressin (DDAVP) to re-lower Na+.
Too slow — even chronic hyponatremia needs some correction.

choices.text: No limit — correct as fast as possible
choices.feedback: Rapid correction of chronic hyponatremia causes ODS.

questions.stem: Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS) presents with:

choices.feedback: Seizures are a symptom of severe hyponatremia, not ODS.

Correct! ODS/CPM: destruction of myelin in central pons from overly rapid Na+ correction. Develops 2-6 days after overcorrection. Locked-in syndrome: conscious but unable to move or speak (only eye movements preserved). Largely irreversible. Prevention: strict adherence to correction rate limits.
ODS affects the central pons, not peripheral nerves.
ODS is a devastating irreversible complication, not a minor symptom.

questions.stem: Severe symptomatic hyponatremia (seizures) requires:

choices.feedback: NS may not raise Na+ fast enough for acute neurologic emergency.

Correct! Hypertonic saline for acute severe symptoms (seizures, coma, herniation). 100-150 mL bolus over 10-20 min, repeat 1-2× as needed. Goal: raise Na+ by 4-6 mEq/L in first hours to stop neurologic symptoms. Then slow correction to stay within 8-10 mEq/L per 24h total.
Fluid restriction is too slow for acute neurologic emergency.
DDAVP worsens hyponatremia (ADH analog). Used to PREVENT overcorrection, not treat acute hyponatremia.

questions.stem: Corrected sodium formula for hyperglycemia:

choices.feedback: The formula uses a specific ratio, not simple subtraction.

choices.text: Measured Na+ + 1.6 mEq/L for every 100 mg/dL glucose above 100
choices.feedback: Correct! Hyperglycemia causes pseudohyponatremia (dilutional). Glucose draws water osmotically → dilutes Na+. Corrected Na+ = measured Na+ + [1.6 × (glucose - 100)/100]. Example: glucose 500, Na+ 128 → corrected Na+ = 128 + [1.6 × 4] = 134.4 (nearly normal). Always correct before diagnosing true hyponatremia in DKA.
This is not the correction formula.

choices.text: No correction needed — just treat the glucose
choices.feedback: The correction is essential for clinical decision-making.

questions.stem: SIADH treatment includes all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Fluid restriction (800-1000 mL/day) is first-line for SIADH.

Correct! NS can WORSEN SIADH hyponatremia. In SIADH, kidneys retain free water. NS provides Na+ that kidneys excrete, but the water is retained → dilution worsens. Use: fluid restriction, salt tabs + loop diuretics (force free water excretion), tolvaptan (V2 receptor antagonist) for refractory cases.
Tolvaptan blocks ADH receptor → aquaresis. Used for refractory SIADH (with close Na+ monitoring).
This combination forces renal free water excretion — effective in SIADH.

questions.stem: Thiazide diuretics cause hyponatremia more than loop diuretics because:

choices.feedback: Loops are actually more potent diuretics.

choices.text: Thiazides impair diluting ability without impairing concentrating ability
choices.feedback: Correct! Thiazides block NaCl reabsorption in the distal convoluted tubule (diluting segment) → can't dilute urine → retain free water. Loops block in the thick ascending limb → impair BOTH concentrating AND diluting → net effect is free water loss (less hyponatremia risk). This is why thiazides are the #1 drug cause of hyponatremia.
Thiazides may mildly increase ADH but the primary mechanism is diluting segment blockade.
Loops can cause either hypo- or hypernatremia but less often hyponatremia than thiazides.

questions.stem: Hypovolemic hyponatremia with UNa <20 suggests:

choices.feedback: Renal losses have UNa >20.

Correct! UNa <20 in hypovolemic hyponatremia = kidneys are avidly retaining Na+ in response to volume depletion from extra-renal losses (GI, third-spacing, burns). UNa >20 in hypovolemic hyponatremia = renal losses (diuretics, adrenal insufficiency, cerebral salt wasting). Treatment: IV NS to restore volume.
SIADH is euvolemic with UNa >20.
Nephrotic syndrome causes hypervolemic hyponatremia.

questions.stem: Terminal ileum involvement with skip lesions and non-caseating granulomas. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: UC is continuous from rectum, mucosal only, no granulomas.

Correct! Crohn hallmarks: skip lesions (discontinuous), transmural inflammation, non-caseating granulomas, terminal ileum predilection. Can affect any part of GI tract (mouth to anus). Complications: fistulas, strictures, abscesses.
Ischemic colitis affects watershed areas, no granulomas.
Celiac affects duodenum/jejunum with villous atrophy, not granulomas.

questions.stem: Which IBD is CURABLE with surgery?

choices.feedback: Crohn recurs at anastomosis — surgery only for complications.

Correct! UC is confined to the colon. Total proctocolectomy with ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA) is curative. Crohn can affect any part of the GI tract and recurs after surgery (endoscopic recurrence at neo-terminal ileum in ~70% by 1 year).
Only UC is surgically curable.
UC is curable with total proctocolectomy.

questions.stem: Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is most associated with:

choices.feedback: PSC has some Crohn association but is predominantly linked to UC.

Correct! ~70% of PSC patients have UC. PSC = progressive fibrosis and stricturing of intrahepatic/extrahepatic bile ducts. ERCP: beading pattern. Increased risk of cholangiocarcinoma. UC patients with PSC have higher CRC risk → annual colonoscopy from PSC diagnosis.
Celiac is associated with dermatitis herpetiformis, not PSC.
IBS has no hepatobiliary complications.

questions.stem: Smoking's effect on IBD?

choices.feedback: Only protective in UC. Worsens Crohn.

choices.text: Worsens Crohn, paradoxically protective in UC
choices.feedback: Correct! One of the most counter-intuitive IBD facts. Smoking increases Crohn flares, recurrence after surgery, and need for immunosuppression. Nicotine may be protective in UC (mechanism unclear — possibly via anti-inflammatory effects on colonic mucosa). Former smokers have highest UC risk. Nicotine patches studied as UC adjunct.
Smoking significantly affects both diseases, in opposite directions.
It's the opposite: worsens Crohn, protective in UC.

questions.stem: Toxic megacolon — which interventions should be AVOIDED?

choices.feedback: These are part of the treatment for toxic megacolon.

Correct! Colonoscopy risks perforation. Opioids and anticholinergics decrease colonic motility → worsen dilation. Treatment: NPO, NG suction, IV fluids, IV steroids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, serial abdominal X-rays. Surgery (colectomy) if no improvement in 48-72h or signs of perforation.
NG decompression is part of the treatment.
Aggressive IV hydration is essential in toxic megacolon management.

questions.stem: CRC surveillance in UC should begin:

choices.feedback: CRC risk increases over time; surveillance starts later.

choices.text: 8-10 years after diagnosis, then every 1-2 years
choices.feedback: Correct! CRC risk increases significantly after 8-10 years of UC. Surveillance colonoscopy with chromoendoscopy and random biopsies every 1-2 years. Risk factors for earlier/more frequent screening: pancolitis, PSC, family history of CRC, prior dysplasia. PSC patients: annual colonoscopy from PSC diagnosis.
UC-related CRC can develop at any age based on disease duration, not patient age.
CRC surveillance is scheduled regardless of symptoms.

questions.stem: Fecal calprotectin is useful because:

choices.feedback: Calprotectin doesn't distinguish between the two types of IBD.

choices.text: It distinguishes IBD (organic inflammation) from IBS (functional)
choices.feedback: Correct! Fecal calprotectin is a neutrophil-derived protein elevated in intestinal inflammation. High sensitivity for IBD vs IBS. Calprotectin <50 μg/g essentially rules out active IBD. Also useful for monitoring disease activity and treatment response without colonoscopy.
Iron studies assess absorption, not calprotectin.
FIT (fecal immunochemical test) screens for CRC, not calprotectin.

questions.stem: Anti-TNF therapy is first-line biologic for moderate-severe IBD. Example?

choices.feedback: Rituximab is anti-CD20 (used in RA, MS, lymphoma), not for IBD.

Correct! Anti-TNF agents: infliximab (IV infusion) and adalimumab (SC injection) are first-line biologics for moderate-severe Crohn and UC. Check for latent TB before starting (risk of reactivation). Other biologics: vedolizumab (gut-selective anti-integrin), ustekinumab (anti-IL-12/23), tofacitinib (JAK inhibitor, UC).
MTX is an immunomodulator used as steroid-sparing agent in Crohn, not a biologic.
Omalizumab is anti-IgE for asthma/allergies, not IBD.

questions.stem: Biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdensity on CT that does NOT cross suture lines:

choices.feedback: Subdural is crescent-shaped and crosses suture lines.

Correct! Epidural: between skull and dura. Biconvex/lenticular shape because blood is confined by dural attachments at suture lines. Usually arterial (middle meningeal artery from temporal bone fracture). Classic: lucid interval → rapid deterioration → uncal herniation.
SAH fills sulci and cisterns, not a discrete biconvex collection.
IVH is within the ventricular system, not extra-axial.

questions.stem: 'Lucid interval' followed by rapid deterioration is classic for:

choices.feedback: SDH typically has gradual progressive symptoms (especially chronic), not the classic lucid interval pattern.

Correct! Epidural: trauma → brief LOC → lucid interval (minutes to hours) → rapid deterioration from arterial bleeding expanding hematoma → uncal herniation (ipsilateral dilated pupil, contralateral hemiparesis). Surgical emergency requiring craniotomy. The lucid interval can be deceptive — always image after head trauma with LOC.
Concussion resolves without deterioration.
SAH has progressive headache, not a lucid interval pattern.

questions.stem: Crescent-shaped hyperdensity crossing suture lines on CT:

choices.feedback: Epidural is biconvex and does NOT cross sutures.

Correct! Subdural: between dura and arachnoid. Crescent-shaped (conforms to brain surface), crosses suture lines (dura not attached at sutures). Acute = hyperdense (white). Caused by bridging vein rupture. Surgery if >10 mm thickness or >5 mm midline shift.
SAH fills cisterns and sulci, not a crescent-shaped collection.
Contusion is intraparenchymal, not extra-axial crescent.

questions.stem: Most common location for hypertensive ICH:

choices.feedback: Frontal lobe ICH is less common from HTN.

Correct! Hypertensive ICH: basal ganglia/putamen (#1, ~35-50%), thalamus (~15%), pons (~5-10%), cerebellum (~5-10%). These are deep brain structures supplied by small penetrating arteries (lenticulostriate) that are damaged by chronic hypertension. Lobar hemorrhage in elderly → think cerebral amyloid angiopathy.
Occipital hemorrhage is less common from hypertension.
Corpus callosum hemorrhage is rare and suggests vascular malformation.

questions.stem: Reversal agent for dabigatran-associated hemorrhage:

choices.feedback: Vitamin K reverses warfarin (factor synthesis), not dabigatran.

Correct! Idarucizumab is a monoclonal antibody fragment that specifically binds dabigatran. Complete reversal within minutes. For factor Xa inhibitors (rivaroxaban, apixaban): andexanet alfa. For warfarin: 4-factor PCC + IV vitamin K. For heparin: protamine sulfate.
Protamine reverses heparin, not DOACs.
TXA is antifibrinolytic but does not specifically reverse dabigatran.

questions.stem: Chronic subdural hematoma appears on CT as:

choices.feedback: Hyperdense = acute (<3 days) blood.

Correct! CT density changes over time: acute (<3d) = hyperdense (white), subacute (3-21d) = isodense (can be missed!), chronic (>21d) = hypodense (dark). Chronic SDH: elderly patients, often bilateral, may not recall trauma. Symptoms: progressive confusion, personality changes, headache, gait instability. Treatment: burr hole drainage if symptomatic.
Enhancement pattern suggests tumor, not chronic SDH.
Isodense = subacute (3-21 days), not chronic.

questions.stem: Cerebellar hemorrhage requires surgical evacuation when:

choices.feedback: Small asymptomatic cerebellar hemorrhages can be managed conservatively.

Correct! Posterior fossa is a confined space — cerebellar hemorrhage >3 cm or with brainstem compression/hydrocephalus requires emergent surgical evacuation (suboccipital craniectomy). Unlike supratentorial ICH (STICH trials: no surgical benefit), cerebellar ICH has clear surgical indications.
Anticoagulant status affects urgency of reversal but not surgical indication.
Medical management alone for large cerebellar hemorrhage risks brainstem herniation.

questions.stem: ICH BP target per INTERACT2:

choices.feedback: 180 is the post-tPA target for ischemic stroke, not ICH.

Correct! INTERACT2 trial: intensive SBP lowering to <140 within 1 hour showed trend toward improved functional outcomes in ICH. Safe and well-tolerated. IV nicardipine or labetalol for rapid control. Note: avoid SBP <110 (risks hypoperfusion).
Too aggressive — risks cerebral hypoperfusion.
Uncontrolled hypertension promotes hematoma expansion.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with proximal muscle weakness, violaceous eyelid discoloration, and papules over her knuckles. CK is 5,000 U/L. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: PM has proximal weakness and elevated CK but does NOT have skin findings. The heliotrope rash and Gottron papules are diagnostic of DM.

Correct! Heliotrope rash (violaceous eyelid discoloration) + Gottron papules (papules over knuckles/MCPs) + proximal weakness + elevated CK = classic dermatomyositis. Must screen for underlying malignancy (15-30% association).
IBM affects elderly men, causes distal (finger flexor) + proximal weakness asymmetrically, and does not have skin findings.
Fibromyalgia causes diffuse pain and fatigue but has NORMAL CK and no muscle weakness on exam.

questions.stem: Which antibody is associated with antisynthetase syndrome?

choices.feedback: Anti-centromere is associated with limited scleroderma (CREST), not antisynthetase syndrome.

Correct! Anti-Jo-1 (anti-histidyl-tRNA synthetase) is the classic antisynthetase antibody. The syndrome includes: myositis + interstitial lung disease + mechanic's hands (cracked, fissured fingers) + non-erosive arthritis + Raynaud phenomenon + fever. ILD is often the most life-threatening component.
Anti-Mi-2 is associated with classic DM with prominent skin findings and GOOD prognosis (good steroid response, low cancer risk).
Anti-Scl-70 is specific for diffuse systemic sclerosis, not myositis.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with slowly progressive weakness of grip strength and knee extension. CK is mildly elevated. Muscle biopsy shows rimmed vacuoles. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: PM shows endomysial CD8+ T cell infiltration on biopsy, not rimmed vacuoles. PM also responds to immunosuppression.

Correct! IBM: elderly men, insidious onset, asymmetric weakness of finger flexors (grip) and quadriceps (knee extensors), mildly elevated CK, rimmed vacuoles + inclusion bodies on biopsy. RESISTANT to immunosuppressive therapy — physical therapy is the mainstay.
DM has characteristic skin findings and perifascicular atrophy on biopsy, not rimmed vacuoles.
Statin myopathy causes elevated CK and weakness but biopsy shows necrosis without rimmed vacuoles or inflammation.

questions.stem: What is the most appropriate next step in a 50-year-old woman newly diagnosed with dermatomyositis?

choices.feedback: While physical therapy is important, it is not the most critical immediate step. Cancer screening is essential given the strong DM-cancer association.

Correct! Dermatomyositis has a 15-30% malignancy association, highest in the first 1-3 years. Age-appropriate cancer screening PLUS CT chest/abdomen/pelvis is recommended. Most common: ovarian, lung, GI, breast, and lymphoma. Anti-TIF1γ and anti-NXP2 antibodies increase cancer suspicion further.
Steroids (not MTX alone) are first-line. More importantly, cancer screening should occur at diagnosis.
HLA typing is not part of the standard DM workup or cancer screening.

questions.stem: A patient with DM on chronic prednisone develops worsening proximal weakness. CK is normal. What is the most likely cause?

choices.feedback: In a disease flare, CK would be elevated. Normal CK with worsening weakness on steroids suggests steroid myopathy.

Correct! Normal CK + worsening weakness on chronic steroids = steroid myopathy. The treatment is to TAPER steroids (counterintuitive). In contrast, disease flare shows elevated CK → increase immunosuppression. EMG and clinical context help differentiate.
DM does not transform into IBM. They are distinct entities.
While hypothyroidism causes myopathy with elevated CK, this scenario points to steroid myopathy given the normal CK and steroid use.

questions.stem: Gottron papules are pathognomonic for which condition?

choices.feedback: SLE has a malar rash (spares nasolabial folds) but not Gottron papules.

Correct! Gottron papules (erythematous-violaceous papules/plaques over MCPs, PIPs, elbows, knees — extensor surfaces) are PATHOGNOMONIC for dermatomyositis. Note: Gottron papules are over the knuckles, while SLE rash spares the knuckles and involves the skin between joints.
PsA has psoriatic plaques, nail pitting, and dactylitis — not Gottron papules.
RA causes rheumatoid nodules on extensor surfaces but not the violaceous papular rash of Gottron papules.

questions.stem: Which myositis antibody carries the highest cancer risk?

choices.feedback: Anti-Jo-1 is associated with antisynthetase syndrome and ILD, not particularly increased cancer risk.

Correct! Anti-TIF1γ (anti-p155/140) is the antibody most strongly associated with cancer in adult DM. Up to 50-75% of anti-TIF1γ-positive DM patients have an underlying malignancy. Anti-NXP2 is also cancer-associated. These antibodies should prompt aggressive malignancy workup.
Anti-Mi-2 is actually associated with classic DM and GOOD prognosis — lower cancer risk and good treatment response.
Anti-SRP is associated with necrotizing autoimmune myopathy (severe, refractory) but not specifically cancer.

questions.stem: Which inflammatory myopathy does NOT respond to immunosuppressive therapy?

choices.feedback: DM generally responds well to steroids, IVIG, and steroid-sparing agents.
PM responds to steroids and immunosuppressive agents like azathioprine and methotrexate.

Correct! IBM is characteristically resistant to all forms of immunosuppressive therapy, including steroids, MTX, AZA, and IVIG. Physical therapy and assistive devices are the mainstay of management. Dysphagia management is also important. IBM is increasingly recognized as having both inflammatory and degenerative components.
Antisynthetase syndrome responds to immunosuppression, though ILD management may require aggressive multi-agent therapy.

questions.stem: What X-ray finding distinguishes jejunal atresia from duodenal atresia?

choices.feedback: Double bubble is specific to DUODENAL atresia. Jejunal atresia shows multiple dilated loops because the obstruction is further downstream.

choices.text: Multiple dilated loops with air-fluid levels
choices.feedback: Correct! Jejunal/ileal atresia shows multiple dilated bowel loops proximal to the obstruction with air-fluid levels and absent distal gas. The more distal the atresia, the more loops are dilated. This differs from the characteristic double bubble of duodenal atresia.
Pneumatosis is the hallmark of necrotizing enterocolitis (NEC), not intestinal atresia.
Bird-beak sign suggests volvulus or intussusception on contrast studies, not atresia.

questions.stem: Why does this distinction matter clinically?

choices.text: It explains why jejunal atresia is NOT associated with chromosomal anomalies
choices.feedback: Correct! Duodenal atresia is intrinsic (recanalization failure) and associated with Down syndrome (30%). Jejunal/ileal atresia is extrinsic (vascular accident) — a random event NOT associated with chromosomal anomalies. This means karyotype and extensive association screening are less critical for jejunal/ileal atresia compared to duodenal atresia.

choices.text: It means surgery can be delayed
choices.feedback: Vascular accidents cause permanent atresia — they do not heal. Surgery is required and should not be delayed.

choices.text: It predicts the patient will have short bowel syndrome
choices.feedback: Some patients develop short bowel syndrome (especially Type 3b/apple peel), but the distinction matters more for associated anomaly screening, not predicting bowel length.

choices.text: It determines which antibiotics to give
choices.feedback: Intestinal atresia is not infectious. The vascular accident happens in utero and is sterile.

questions.stem: What defines the apple peel (Christmas tree) deformity?

choices.text: A web-like membrane across the bowel lumen
choices.feedback: An intraluminal membrane/web is Type 1 atresia, similar to duodenal Type 1. Apple peel is far more complex.

choices.text: Proximal jejunal atresia with absent SMA; distal bowel spirals around a marginal vessel
choices.feedback: Correct! Type 3b (apple peel/Christmas tree): The proximal jejunum ends blindly near the ligament of Treitz. The superior mesenteric artery is absent beyond this point. The remaining distal small bowel spirals around a single marginal artery from the ileocolic or right colic vessels, resembling an apple peel. These patients are at highest risk for short bowel syndrome.

choices.text: Multiple atresias along the bowel like a string of sausages
choices.feedback: Multiple atresias is Type 4, not Type 3b. Type 4 resembles a 'string of sausages' with multiple atretic segments.

choices.text: Two blind ends connected by a fibrous cord
choices.feedback: That describes Type 3a (mesenteric gap) — no apple peel deformity.

questions.stem: Why is the dilated proximal bowel resected rather than used for the anastomosis?

choices.text: It has abnormal peristalsis and poor motility
choices.feedback: Correct! The proximal dilated segment has hypertrophied but dysfunctional muscle. It acts as a functional obstruction even after anastomosis if not resected. The dilated-to-normal size mismatch also makes anastomosis difficult. Tapering (reducing the caliber) is an alternative to resection when bowel length must be preserved.

choices.text: It is at risk for malignant transformation
choices.feedback: Neonatal bowel does not develop malignancy from dilation. The issue is purely functional — poor motility.

choices.text: It contains meconium that cannot be cleared
choices.feedback: While meconium may be present, it can be irrigated. The real reason is the dysfunctional motility of the dilated segment.

choices.text: The blood supply is always compromised
choices.feedback: The proximal segment typically has adequate blood supply — it's dilated because it received intestinal contents but couldn't pass them distally. Motility, not blood supply, is the issue.

questions.stem: What determines whether this baby will develop short bowel syndrome?

choices.text: Presence of the ileocecal valve and remaining bowel length
choices.feedback: Correct! Short bowel syndrome risk depends on: remaining bowel length (<75 cm without ICV or <40 cm with ICV in neonates is high-risk), presence of ileocecal valve (prevents bacterial reflux, improves transit time), and intestinal adaptation capacity. This baby at 80 cm WITH ICV has a favorable prognosis. The microcolon is simply a disused colon — it will grow to normal caliber with enteral feeding.
Formula type matters for feeding tolerance but doesn't determine whether short bowel syndrome develops. Anatomy is the primary determinant.
Gestational age affects overall prognosis but doesn't determine short bowel syndrome — that depends on remaining bowel length and ICV status.

choices.text: Whether the atresia was Type 1 or Type 3
choices.feedback: While Type 3b has the highest short bowel risk, the actual remaining length and ICV preservation are what matter for the individual patient.

questions.stem: Active phase arrest of labor is defined as no cervical change for:

choices.feedback: 2h arrest criteria are for the 2nd stage, not active phase.

Correct! Active phase (6-10 cm): arrest = no cervical change for ≥6h with adequate contractions OR ≥4h with adequate contractions + ruptured membranes + oxytocin. This defines failed induction/arrest of dilation → indication for cesarean delivery.
1h without progress in active labor is not arrest.
12h exceeds the criteria — intervention would occur much sooner.

questions.stem: Late decelerations indicate:

choices.feedback: Head compression = early decelerations (mirror contractions). Benign.

Correct! Late decels: gradual onset AFTER contraction peak, gradual return. Reflect decreased placental perfusion during contractions. Concerning for fetal hypoxia. Management: left lateral positioning, IV fluids, O2, stop oxytocin. If persistent with absent variability (category III) → emergent delivery.
Cord compression = variable decelerations (abrupt, variable shape/timing).
Fetal sleep causes decreased variability, not late decelerations.

questions.stem: #1 cause of postpartum hemorrhage?

choices.feedback: Lacerations are the #2 cause (trauma).

Correct! Uterine atony accounts for ~80% of PPH. The uterus fails to contract after delivery → exposed spiral arteries bleed massively. Treatment ladder: bimanual uterine massage + oxytocin IV → methylergonovine IM (contraindicated in HTN) → carboprost IM (contraindicated in asthma) → misoprostol PR → tamponade (Bakri balloon) → surgery (B-Lynch suture, uterine artery ligation, hysterectomy).
Retained placenta is the #3 cause. Manual extraction or curettage needed.
DIC/coagulopathy is the #4 cause (4 T's: Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin).

questions.stem: Shoulder dystocia — FIRST maneuver?

choices.feedback: NEVER apply fundal pressure — worsens impaction and increases injury risk.

Correct! McRoberts: sharp flexion of maternal thighs toward abdomen → widens pelvic outlet, flattens sacral promontory. Resolves ~40% of shoulder dystocias. Simultaneously apply suprapubic pressure (NOT fundal). If McRoberts fails → Rubin maneuver, Wood's corkscrew, posterior arm delivery.
Zavanelli is a last resort (cephalic replacement → emergency C-section). Not first-line.
C-section after head delivery is extremely difficult. Vaginal maneuvers first.

questions.stem: First-line maneuver for shoulder dystocia results in Erb palsy affecting:

choices.feedback: Klumpke affects C8-T1: hand/wrist weakness ('claw hand'). Less common.

Correct! Erb-Duchenne palsy: upper brachial plexus (C5-C6) injury → loss of shoulder abduction/external rotation, elbow flexion, forearm supination. Arm hangs limply at side in 'waiter's tip' position. Most common brachial plexus injury from shoulder dystocia. ~90% resolve spontaneously.
C3-C5 = phrenic nerve → diaphragm paralysis. Would cause respiratory distress.
Lumbar plexus injury doesn't occur in shoulder dystocia.

questions.stem: Bishop score is used to assess:

choices.feedback: Biophysical profile and NST assess fetal well-being.

Correct! Bishop score (0-13): cervical dilation, effacement, station, consistency, position. Score ≥6 = favorable for induction (high success rate). <6 = unfavorable → cervical ripening needed first (misoprostol, dinoprostone, or mechanical Foley bulb).
Preeclampsia risk is assessed by clinical factors, not Bishop score.
Gestational age is determined by LMP and ultrasound dating.

questions.stem: Category III FHR tracing requires:

choices.feedback: Category III is immediately concerning — not safe to observe.

Correct! Category III: absent variability + recurrent late or variable decels, OR bradycardia, OR sinusoidal pattern. Indicates potential fetal acidemia. Immediate intrauterine resuscitation (positioning, fluids, O2, stop oxytocin). If no improvement → emergent cesarean delivery. Category I = normal, Category II = indeterminate.
Amniotomy alone won't resolve category III tracings.
Terbutaline (tocolytic) may briefly help by relaxing the uterus but category III requires preparation for delivery.

questions.stem: Sinusoidal FHR pattern is most associated with:

choices.feedback: Sleep causes decreased variability but not a sinusoidal pattern.

choices.text: Severe fetal anemia (Rh isoimmunization, massive fetomaternal hemorrhage)
choices.feedback: Correct! Sinusoidal pattern: smooth, undulating, sine wave-like FHR pattern without variability. Most ominous when caused by severe fetal anemia (Rh disease, fetomaternal hemorrhage, ruptured vasa previa). Category III tracing → emergent delivery. Can also be seen with narcotics (pseudosinusoidal — less concerning).
Sinusoidal pattern is never normal.
Chorioamnionitis causes fetal tachycardia, not sinusoidal pattern.

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis, and what is the embryological basis?

choices.text: Laryngomalacia — immature laryngeal cartilage from 4th/6th arch
choices.feedback: Correct! Laryngomalacia is the most common cause of stridor in newborns (60-70%). The laryngeal cartilages (derived from pharyngeal arches 4 and 6) are immature/flaccid. The supraglottic structures (omega-shaped epiglottis, aryepiglottic folds, arytenoids) collapse inward during inspiration. Most cases (>90%) resolve spontaneously by age 18-24 months as cartilage matures. Severe cases: supraglottoplasty.
Subglottic stenosis causes biphasic stridor (inspiratory AND expiratory), not purely inspiratory. It's also more commonly acquired (from intubation) than congenital.

choices.text: Vocal cord paralysis — recurrent laryngeal nerve injury
choices.feedback: Vocal cord paralysis causes a weak cry and stridor but laryngoscopy would show immobile cords, not omega epiglottis with arytenoid prolapse.
Tracheomalacia causes expiratory stridor/wheezing, not inspiratory stridor. The lesion is below the larynx.

questions.stem: Which nerve is injured, and why is the LEFT side more commonly affected?

choices.text: Left recurrent laryngeal nerve — longer course looping under the aortic arch
choices.feedback: Correct! The left recurrent laryngeal nerve loops under the aortic arch (4th arch derivative on the left), giving it a longer intrathoracic course than the right (which loops under the subclavian). This makes the left RLN vulnerable to: birth trauma, thoracic surgery, mediastinal masses, PDA ligation, and aortic arch anomalies. The RLN innervates all intrinsic laryngeal muscles EXCEPT the cricothyroid (which is innervated by the external branch of the superior laryngeal nerve).

choices.text: Right recurrent laryngeal nerve — compressed by the right subclavian
choices.feedback: The right RLN has a shorter course (loops under the right subclavian) and is actually LESS commonly affected than the left.

choices.text: Superior laryngeal nerve — damage above the larynx
choices.feedback: SLN injury causes voice fatigue and pitch changes (cricothyroid dysfunction), not vocal cord paralysis.

choices.text: Vagus nerve trunk — complete vagal injury
choices.feedback: Complete vagal injury would cause both cord paralysis and pharyngeal dysfunction. Isolated cord paralysis points to the recurrent laryngeal branch specifically.

questions.stem: Why is the subglottis the most common site for acquired stenosis after intubation?

choices.text: The cricoid is the only complete cartilage ring — it cannot expand
choices.feedback: Correct! The cricoid cartilage is the ONLY complete ring in the airway (all tracheal rings are C-shaped with a posterior membranous portion). Because it cannot expand, any mucosal edema or scarring in the subglottis causes significant obstruction. This is also why the subglottis is the narrowest point of the pediatric airway. Endotracheal tube pressure against the non-expandable cricoid ring → pressure necrosis → scarring → stenosis.

choices.text: The subglottis has the poorest blood supply
choices.feedback: Blood supply is adequate. The issue is the mechanical rigidity of the complete cricoid ring that prevents expansion.

choices.text: The subglottic mucosa is squamous epithelium
choices.feedback: The subglottis is actually lined by pseudostratified columnar (respiratory) epithelium. The epithelial type isn't the primary issue — it's the rigid cartilaginous ring.

choices.text: The vocal cords protect the upper airway but not the subglottis
choices.feedback: The ET tube passes through both the glottis and subglottis. The issue is the non-distensible nature of the cricoid, not differential exposure.

questions.stem: What is the normal shape of tracheal cartilage rings, and why does it matter?

choices.text: C-shaped with a posterior membranous portion — allows esophageal bolus passage and cough
choices.feedback: Correct! Normal tracheal rings are C-shaped (horseshoe) with a posterior membranous wall (trachealis muscle). This allows: 1) The esophagus to expand during swallowing (shared posterior wall). 2) Dynamic narrowing during cough (increasing velocity to clear secretions). Complete rings (O-shaped) are rigid, cannot expand, and cause fixed stenosis. Associated with PA sling (50-65%) and cardiac anomalies. Treatment: slide tracheoplasty (divide trachea, slide segments to widen).

choices.text: O-shaped (complete circles) — to maintain airway patency
choices.feedback: O-shaped complete rings are ABNORMAL. Normal tracheal rings are C-shaped with a posterior membranous portion.
Tracheal cartilage is specifically C-shaped, not flat. The shape is critical for function.
Tracheal cartilage consists of discrete C-shaped rings, not a continuous spiral.

questions.stem: Which is the ONLY intrinsic laryngeal muscle NOT innervated by the recurrent laryngeal nerve?
