choices.feedback: The PCA is the only abductor of the cords and IS innervated by the RLN. Loss of PCA = inability to open cords = airway compromise.

Correct! The cricothyroid muscle is innervated by the EXTERNAL branch of the SUPERIOR laryngeal nerve (from arch 4), NOT the RLN (arch 6). It tenses the vocal cords (increases pitch). All OTHER intrinsic laryngeal muscles are RLN (arch 6). The INTERNAL branch of the SLN is sensory above the cords. Memory aid: The cricothyroid is the odd one out — 4th arch muscle with 4th arch nerve.
The lateral cricoarytenoid is innervated by the RLN. It adducts the cords (closes for phonation/protection).
The thyroarytenoid is innervated by the RLN. It relaxes and shortens the vocal cords.

questions.stem: AST:ALT ratio >2:1 suggests which etiology?

choices.feedback: Hep C typically has ALT > AST.

Correct! AST:ALT >2:1 is classic for alcoholic hepatitis. Alcohol depletes pyridoxine (B6), which is required for ALT synthesis → ALT is disproportionately low. AST rarely exceeds 300 in alcoholic hepatitis. If transaminases >1000, think viral hepatitis, ischemia, or toxin.
MASLD typically has mildly elevated ALT > AST.
AIH has very high transaminases but no specific AST:ALT pattern.

questions.stem: SAAG ≥1.1 indicates:

choices.feedback: Malignancy typically has SAAG <1.1 (except hepatic metastases causing portal HTN).

Correct! SAAG ≥1.1 = portal hypertension with 97% accuracy. Cirrhosis is the most common cause. Also: right heart failure, Budd-Chiari, portal vein thrombosis. SAAG <1.1: peritoneal carcinomatosis, TB peritonitis, nephrotic syndrome, pancreatitis.
Nephrotic ascites has SAAG <1.1.
TB peritonitis: SAAG <1.1, high protein, lymphocytic predominance.

questions.stem: SBP is diagnosed when ascitic fluid PMN count is:

choices.feedback: Small numbers of WBCs are normal in ascitic fluid.

Correct! PMN >250/mm³ = SBP. Start empiric ceftriaxone immediately — cultures are positive in only ~40% of cases. IV albumin (1.5 g/kg day 1 + 1 g/kg day 3) reduces hepatorenal syndrome and mortality. After recovery: lifelong SBP prophylaxis with daily fluoroquinolone or TMP-SMX.
>1000 is likely but the diagnostic threshold is 250.
Waiting for culture delays treatment and increases mortality.

questions.stem: Hepatic encephalopathy first-line treatment?

choices.feedback: Systemic antibiotics are not first-line for HE.

Correct! Lactulose: osmotic laxative that acidifies colonic lumen → converts NH3 to NH4+ (non-absorbable) → fecal excretion. Titrate to 2-3 soft BMs/day. Rifaximin: non-absorbable antibiotic reducing ammonia-producing gut bacteria. ALSO: identify and treat precipitant (infection, GI bleed, constipation, hypoK, sedatives).
Transplant is definitive for cirrhosis but HE episodes are manageable medically.
Outdated practice! Protein restriction worsens sarcopenia and outcomes. Normal/high protein diet is now recommended.

questions.stem: Wilson disease classic findings?

choices.feedback: That's hemochromatosis (iron overload).

choices.text: Kayser-Fleischer rings, neuropsych symptoms, low ceruloplasmin
choices.feedback: Correct! Wilson disease: autosomal recessive copper overload (ATP7B mutation). Kayser-Fleischer rings (copper in Descemet membrane — slit lamp exam), neuropsychiatric symptoms (tremor, dysarthria, personality change), hepatic disease (hepatitis → cirrhosis), hemolytic anemia (Coombs-negative). Low ceruloplasmin, elevated 24h urine copper. Treatment: D-penicillamine, trientine, zinc.
These are general signs of chronic liver disease, not specific to Wilson.

choices.text: Positive ANA and anti-smooth muscle antibodies
choices.feedback: That's autoimmune hepatitis.

questions.stem: Hemochromatosis screening test?

choices.feedback: Biopsy is confirmatory but not the screening test.

Correct! Transferrin saturation >45% is the best screening test. Elevated ferritin supports the diagnosis. Confirm with HFE gene testing (C282Y homozygote). Liver biopsy/MRI for iron quantification. 'Bronze diabetes': skin hyperpigmentation + diabetes + cirrhosis. Treatment: therapeutic phlebotomy (target ferritin 50-100).
Ceruloplasmin screens for Wilson disease (copper), not hemochromatosis (iron).
These are autoimmune hepatitis markers.

questions.stem: HCC screening in cirrhotic patients?

choices.feedback: CT has radiation exposure and is not the recommended screening modality.

Correct! All patients with cirrhosis (any cause) should be screened with liver ultrasound + alpha-fetoprotein every 6 months. Also screen: chronic HBV carriers (even without cirrhosis — HBV can cause HCC without cirrhosis). If suspicious lesion: multiphase CT or MRI for characterization (arterial enhancement + venous washout = diagnostic for HCC).
AFP alone has poor sensitivity (~60%). Combined with US improves detection.

choices.text: No screening needed until symptoms develop
choices.feedback: HCC is often asymptomatic until advanced. Screening catches treatable disease.

questions.stem: Hepatorenal syndrome type 1 treatment?

choices.feedback: Diuretics are contraindicated in HRS — further reduce renal perfusion.

Correct! HRS type 1: rapidly progressive (Cr doubles in 2 weeks). Splanchnic vasodilation → reduced effective circulating volume → renal vasoconstriction. Treatment: vasoconstrictors (midodrine + octreotide, or norepinephrine) + IV albumin (volume expansion). Definitive: liver transplant. Terlipressin (vasopressin analog) is also effective.
Dialysis is a bridge but doesn't treat the underlying pathophysiology.
ACEi reduce renal perfusion pressure — absolutely contraindicated in HRS.

questions.stem: What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.text: Meckel diverticulum with ectopic gastric mucosa
choices.feedback: Correct! The classic presentation of Meckel diverticulum: painless rectal bleeding in a young child, often with significant blood loss. Ectopic gastric mucosa in the diverticulum produces acid → ulceration of adjacent ileal mucosa → bleeding. This is the most common cause of significant painless GI bleeding in children under 5.
Intussusception causes 'currant jelly' stools (blood + mucus) WITH colicky abdominal pain and vomiting. This child is pain-free between episodes.
Juvenile polyps cause painless bleeding but typically produce small amounts of bright red blood on the stool surface, not significant enough to drop hemoglobin.
IBD is rare at age 2 and typically presents with chronic diarrhea, abdominal pain, and weight loss — not isolated painless bleeding.

questions.stem: Where is a Meckel diverticulum located?

choices.feedback: The duodenum is at the foregut-midgut junction. The vitelline duct connects to the MIDGUT, specifically the ileum.

choices.text: Antimesenteric border of the ileum, ~2 feet from the ileocecal valve
choices.feedback: Correct! Meckel diverticulum is found on the ANTIMESENTERIC border of the ileum, approximately 2 feet (60cm) from the ileocecal valve in adults (shorter in children). It's antimesenteric because the vitelline duct connected to the ventral (antimesenteric) side of the midgut. Rule of 2s: 2% of population, 2 feet from ICV, 2 inches long, 2 types of ectopic tissue (gastric and pancreatic).
The vitelline duct remnant is in the ILEUM, not jejunum, and on the ANTIMESENTERIC border.
The diverticulum is 2 feet PROXIMAL to the ileocecal valve, not at the cecum. The appendix is a completely different structure.

questions.stem: What does the Meckel scan detect?

choices.feedback: Correct! Tc-99m pertechnetate has affinity for gastric parietal cells (and thyroid follicular cells). A positive Meckel scan shows ectopic gastric mucosa in the diverticulum. The ectopic gastric tissue secretes acid, which ulcerates the adjacent normal ileal mucosa, causing bleeding. Note: the scan detects the GASTRIC tissue, not the diverticulum itself — a Meckel without ectopic gastric mucosa may be scan-negative.
Tc-99m pertechnetate doesn't detect bleeding. A tagged RBC scan detects active bleeding sites. The Meckel scan detects ectopic gastric tissue.
This isn't an inflammation scan. It specifically detects gastric mucosal tissue. Inflammation would be better seen on CT or tagged WBC scan.
Obstruction is diagnosed on X-ray or CT. The Meckel scan is nuclear medicine, designed to find ectopic gastric mucosa.

questions.stem: When should an incidentally discovered Meckel diverticulum be removed?

choices.text: Always — all Meckels should be removed
choices.feedback: Routine removal of incidental Meckels is controversial. Most (95%) remain asymptomatic throughout life. Prophylactic resection adds surgical risk.

choices.text: Selectively — based on age, size, and palpable abnormalities
choices.feedback: Correct! Current consensus: Remove incidental Meckels if (1) patient is young (higher lifetime risk of complications), (2) the diverticulum has palpable thickening (suggesting ectopic tissue), (3) there's a fibrous band to the umbilicus (obstruction risk), (4) the diverticulum is long or has a narrow base (higher complication risk). Lifetime risk of complications from an incidental Meckel is ~4-6%.

choices.text: Never — incidental Meckels should always be left alone
choices.feedback: Not always left alone. In children, the lifetime risk of complications is higher, and certain features warrant removal.

choices.text: Only if the patient has Down syndrome
choices.feedback: Meckel diverticulum is not specifically associated with Down syndrome. The decision is based on anatomic features and patient age.

questions.stem: Which two types of ectopic tissue are found in Meckel diverticulum?

choices.feedback: Hepatic and renal ectopic tissue in a Meckel would be extremely unusual. The common types relate to the foregut derivatives.

Correct! The two most common types of ectopic tissue: GASTRIC (most common, ~50% of symptomatic Meckels — causes bleeding) and PANCREATIC (~5% — usually incidental). Why these two? Because the vitelline duct connects to the foregut-midgut junction, where gastric and pancreatic tissue are neighbors. These tissues get incorporated into the persistent duct remnant.
Colonic and esophageal ectopic tissue are not typical in Meckel diverticulum.
Thyroid and adrenal tissue are not found in Meckel diverticulum. These arise from different embryologic precursors.

questions.stem: Most common cause of bacterial meningitis in adults?

choices.feedback: N. meningitidis is #1 in adolescents/young adults but not overall adults.

Correct! S. pneumoniae is the #1 cause of bacterial meningitis in adults overall. Highest mortality of all bacterial causes (~20-30%). N. meningitidis is #1 in adolescents and associated with petechial rash + waterhouse-friderichsen (adrenal hemorrhage). S. pneumoniae also #1 in elderly.
Listeria is important in neonates, elderly, immunocompromised, and pregnant but not #1 overall.
Dramatically reduced since Hib vaccination.

questions.stem: When should you get CT BEFORE LP?

choices.text: Always — CT is required before every LP
choices.feedback: LP can be done without CT in most cases of suspected meningitis.

choices.text: Immunocompromised, focal deficits, papilledema, altered consciousness, new seizure, CNS disease history
choices.feedback: Correct! CT before LP to rule out mass effect/risk of herniation. In these high-risk scenarios, draw blood cultures → give empiric antibiotics + dexamethasone FIRST → CT → LP when safe. NEVER delay antibiotics to get imaging or LP.

choices.text: Only if the patient is over 65
choices.feedback: Age alone doesn't mandate CT before LP.
Temperature doesn't determine need for pre-LP imaging.

questions.stem: CSF glucose is LOW in which type of meningitis?

choices.feedback: Viral meningitis has NORMAL CSF glucose.

Correct! Bacterial meningitis: low glucose (<40 mg/dL, CSF/serum ratio <0.4) because bacteria consume glucose + impaired glucose transport across inflamed meninges. Also low in TB and fungal meningitis. Viral meningitis has normal glucose. Low glucose + high PMN WBC + high protein = bacterial.

choices.text: Neither — glucose is always normal in meningitis
choices.feedback: Glucose is characteristically low in bacterial, TB, and fungal meningitis.

choices.text: Viral meningitis has the lowest glucose
choices.feedback: Viral meningitis has normal glucose. Bacterial has low glucose.

questions.stem: HSV encephalitis has predilection for which brain region?

choices.feedback: Occipital involvement suggests PRES or PCA stroke, not HSV.

Correct! HSV-1 enters CNS via olfactory or trigeminal nerve → preferentially affects temporal and frontal lobes (limbic system). Presents: fever + altered mental status + behavioral changes + seizures + aphasia. MRI: T2 hyperintensity in medial temporal lobes (often asymmetric). CSF PCR for HSV is diagnostic gold standard.
Cerebellar involvement suggests different infectious or autoimmune etiologies.
Basal ganglia involvement is more typical of hypertensive hemorrhage or Wilson disease.

questions.stem: Empiric treatment for suspected bacterial meningitis in a 65-year-old?

choices.feedback: This covers pneumococcus but misses Listeria, which is important in patients >50.

Correct! Age >50, immunocompromised, alcoholic, or pregnant → add ampicillin to cover Listeria monocytogenes. Listeria is intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins. Vancomycin covers resistant pneumococcus. Also give dexamethasone BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose.
Insufficient coverage for the range of organisms in elderly meningitis.
This covers anaerobes and gram-negatives but misses the key meningitis organisms.

questions.stem: Dexamethasone in bacterial meningitis should be given:

choices.feedback: Waiting defeats the purpose — dexamethasone must be given early.

choices.text: BEFORE or WITH the first antibiotic dose
choices.feedback: Correct! Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h × 4 days. Must be given before or simultaneously with first antibiotic — antibiotics cause bacterial lysis → massive inflammatory response → cerebral edema. Dexamethasone blunts this. Primary benefit: reduces hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis. No benefit if given after antibiotics started.

choices.text: Only if CSF glucose is normal
choices.feedback: Dexamethasone is given empirically, not based on CSF glucose.
Dexamethasone is specifically for bacterial meningitis, not viral.

questions.stem: Close contacts of N. meningitidis meningitis should receive:

choices.feedback: Close contacts have significantly elevated risk and need prophylaxis.

Correct! Chemoprophylaxis for close contacts (household, kissing contacts, exposure to secretions): rifampin 600 mg PO BID × 2 days, OR ciprofloxacin 500 mg PO × 1 dose, OR ceftriaxone 250 mg IM × 1 dose (preferred in pregnancy). Give within 24h of diagnosis. Also vaccinate if serogroup covered by available vaccine.
IV antibiotics are for treatment, not prophylaxis of contacts.
Steroids are not used for contact prophylaxis.

questions.stem: A patient with fever, altered mental status, and seizures has MRI showing T2 hyperintensity in bilateral temporal lobes. First action?

choices.feedback: NEVER wait for PCR — HSV encephalitis mortality is ~70% untreated. Start acyclovir immediately.

Correct! HSV encephalitis is the most likely diagnosis (fever + AMS + seizures + temporal lobe MRI findings). Start IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h × 14-21 days IMMEDIATELY. Early treatment reduces mortality from ~70% to <20%. Send CSF for HSV PCR but do NOT delay treatment.
Bacterial meningitis antibiotics don't cover HSV. The temporal lobe MRI is classic for HSV encephalitis.

choices.text: Observe and repeat MRI in 48 hours
choices.feedback: Observation allows disease progression and increases mortality.

questions.stem: Most common initial presentation of MS?

choices.feedback: Transverse myelitis is a common MS presentation but not the most common initial one.

Correct! Unilateral optic neuritis is the most common first presentation: painful vision loss (pain with eye movement), afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil — swinging flashlight test shows paradoxical dilation of affected eye). ~50% of optic neuritis patients develop MS within 15 years.
Seizures are uncommon in MS — they occur in <5% of patients.
MS affects CNS myelin (oligodendrocytes), NOT peripheral nerves. Peripheral neuropathy suggests GBS, diabetes, etc.

questions.stem: MRI hallmark of MS?

choices.feedback: Basal ganglia calcification suggests Fahr disease or hypoparathyroidism.

Correct! Multiple ovoid lesions perpendicular to the ventricles (Dawson fingers), best seen on T2/FLAIR sequences. McDonald criteria require dissemination in space (≥2 CNS areas) AND time (new lesions on follow-up MRI or simultaneous enhancing + non-enhancing lesions). Spinal cord lesions also common.
Cortical ribbon sign suggests Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease (prion disease).
Ring enhancement suggests abscess, toxoplasmosis, or glioblastoma — not typical MS.

questions.stem: Uhthoff phenomenon refers to:

choices.feedback: MS symptoms don't specifically follow a diurnal pattern.

Correct! Heat (exercise, hot baths, fever) worsens conduction in demyelinated axons by further slowing already impaired saltatory conduction. Symptoms improve when the body cools. This is pathognomonic for MS and can be used as a diagnostic clue. Not a true relapse — no new inflammation.
Progressive vision loss in MS is a sign of disease progression, not Uhthoff phenomenon.
Steroid response is expected in acute relapses but not the definition of Uhthoff.

questions.stem: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) in a young patient suggests:

choices.feedback: MG causes fatigable ptosis and diplopia but not INO specifically.

Correct! Bilateral INO in a young patient is highly suggestive of MS (MLF lesion). INO: impaired ADDuction of ipsilateral eye + nystagmus of ABDucting contralateral eye. Unilateral INO in an older patient suggests brainstem stroke. Convergence is preserved in INO (distinguishes from CN III palsy).
PD affects basal ganglia causing movement disorder, not specific eye movement patterns.
Migraine can cause visual symptoms but not INO.

questions.stem: CSF findings in MS?

choices.text: Elevated protein with normal cell count
choices.feedback: That's albuminocytologic dissociation of GBS. MS may have mildly elevated protein but the key finding is different.

choices.text: Oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index
choices.feedback: Correct! Oligoclonal bands (>90% of MS patients) represent intrathecal immunoglobulin synthesis — bands in CSF NOT present in serum. Elevated IgG index confirms intrathecal antibody production. Mild lymphocytic pleocytosis may be present. CSF is supportive but not required if MRI meets McDonald criteria.
Xanthochromia indicates SAH, not MS.
High WBC suggests bacterial meningitis, not MS.

questions.stem: Risk of natalizumab in MS treatment?

choices.feedback: Hepatotoxicity is associated with some DMTs but PML is the key natalizumab concern.

Correct! Natalizumab (anti-α4 integrin) prevents lymphocyte CNS entry but suppresses CNS immune surveillance → JC virus reactivation → PML. Risk factors: JCV antibody positive + treatment >2 years + prior immunosuppression. Monitor JCV antibody status. PML is often fatal or causes severe disability. Risk stratification guides treatment decisions.
Optic neuritis is a symptom of MS itself, not a natalizumab side effect.
Osteoporosis is associated with chronic steroid use, not natalizumab.

questions.stem: Treatment of acute MS relapse?

choices.feedback: DMTs prevent future relapses but don't treat acute ones.

Correct! High-dose IV steroids shorten relapse duration and speed recovery but do NOT change long-term disability outcomes. Oral taper may follow. For steroid-refractory relapses: plasmapheresis. Not all relapses require treatment — minor sensory symptoms may be observed.
IVIG has limited evidence for acute MS relapses compared to steroids.
Low-dose oral steroids are insufficient for acute relapse management.

questions.stem: Ocrelizumab is unique among MS DMTs because:

choices.text: It is the only injectable DMT
choices.feedback: Multiple injectable DMTs exist (IFN-beta, glatiramer). Ocrelizumab is actually IV infusion.

choices.text: It is approved for primary progressive MS (PPMS)
choices.feedback: Correct! Ocrelizumab (anti-CD20 — depletes B cells) is the FIRST and currently only DMT approved for PPMS (ORATORIO trial). Also effective in RRMS (OPERA trials). Infused every 6 months. Screening required: hepatitis B (risk of reactivation), tuberculosis. Most DMTs only work for relapsing forms.
Side effects include infusion reactions, increased infection risk, and hepatitis B reactivation.
No current therapy cures MS. Ocrelizumab slows progression but requires ongoing treatment.

questions.stem: Most common initial presentation of MS?

choices.feedback: Transverse myelitis can occur but is not the most common initial presentation.

choices.text: Optic neuritis (unilateral vision loss with pain on eye movement)
choices.feedback: Correct! Optic neuritis is the most common initial MS presentation — unilateral vision loss, pain with eye movement, afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil). ~50% of optic neuritis patients develop MS within 15 years. MRI brain at presentation determines risk.
Seizures are uncommon in MS (cortical gray matter is relatively spared).
Intention tremor can occur in MS (cerebellar) but is not the most common initial symptom.

questions.stem: Bilateral INO in a young adult is highly suggestive of:

choices.feedback: MG causes fatigable ptosis and diplopia but not INO specifically.

Correct! Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) from MLF lesion: impaired adduction ipsilateral + abducting nystagmus contralateral. Bilateral INO in a young adult = MS until proven otherwise. In elderly: most commonly caused by brainstem stroke.
GBS is a peripheral nerve disease; INO is a CNS brainstem finding.
Tumors can cause INO but bilateral INO in a young adult strongly suggests MS.

questions.stem: MS symptoms worsening with heat exposure is called:

choices.feedback: Lhermitte = electric shock sensation with neck flexion (posterior column).

Correct! Uhthoff phenomenon: heat slows conduction velocity in demyelinated nerves → transient worsening of existing MS symptoms. Classic: vision worsens after hot bath, exercise, or fever. Pseudoexacerbation (not a true relapse). Symptoms resolve with cooling.
Romberg tests proprioception (dorsal column) — positive when balance worsens with eyes closed.
Babinski (upgoing plantar response) indicates UMN lesion — can be present in MS but is not specific to heat.

questions.stem: CSF finding most specific for MS?

choices.feedback: That's albuminocytologic dissociation — classic for GBS.

choices.text: Oligoclonal bands (IgG) present in CSF but not serum
choices.feedback: Correct! Oligoclonal bands (OCBs) present in CSF but absent in serum indicate intrathecal IgG synthesis — present in >95% of MS patients. Not 100% specific (also seen in neurosyphilis, Lyme, SSPE), but in the right clinical context, strongly supports MS diagnosis. Elevated IgG index is also supportive.
Low CSF glucose = bacterial meningitis or TB meningitis, not MS.
Xanthochromia indicates prior subarachnoid hemorrhage.

questions.stem: Acute MS relapse treatment?

choices.feedback: Low-dose oral steroids are less effective than IV pulse therapy for MS relapses.

Correct! High-dose IV steroids shorten relapse duration and speed recovery. Does NOT change long-term disease progression or prevent future relapses. For steroid-refractory relapses: plasmapheresis (PLEX). Long-term disease modification requires DMTs, not steroids.
Natalizumab is a DMT for relapse prevention, not acute relapse treatment.
IFN-β prevents future relapses (DMT) but does not treat acute attacks.

questions.stem: Which DMT carries risk of PML from JC virus reactivation?

choices.feedback: Glatiramer has a very favorable safety profile with no PML risk.

Correct! Natalizumab (α4-integrin antibody) prevents lymphocyte CNS entry — highly effective but carries PML risk (JC virus reactivation). Risk factors: JCV antibody positive, treatment >2 years, prior immunosuppression. Screen JCV antibody before starting. Consider switching after 2 years if JCV+.
DMF has rare PML reports but it's primarily associated with natalizumab.
IFN-β does not carry significant PML risk.

questions.stem: Ocrelizumab is significant because it is:

choices.feedback: First oral MS drug was fingolimod. Ocrelizumab is IV infusion.

choices.text: The first DMT approved for primary progressive MS (PPMS)
choices.feedback: Correct! Ocrelizumab (anti-CD20, B-cell depleting) is the FIRST and currently only DMT with proven benefit in PPMS (ORATORIO trial). Also highly effective in RRMS. PPMS has historically been treatment-resistant, making this a landmark approval.
No DMT cures MS. They reduce relapse frequency and slow disability progression.
Ocrelizumab is effective in both sexes.

questions.stem: MS lesion on MRI that enhances with gadolinium indicates:

choices.feedback: Chronic plaques show T2 hyperintensity but do NOT enhance with gadolinium.

Correct! Gadolinium enhancement = breakdown of blood-brain barrier = active inflammation. Enhancing lesions represent new or actively inflamed plaques (within ~6 weeks). Non-enhancing T2 lesions = older/chronic plaques. Simultaneous enhancing + non-enhancing lesions on single MRI can satisfy dissemination in time criterion.
Normal white matter does not enhance with gadolinium.
While tumors enhance, ring enhancement and clinical context distinguish them from MS plaques.

questions.stem: Hallmark feature that distinguishes MG from other causes of weakness?

choices.text: Weakness is constant throughout the day
choices.feedback: Constant weakness suggests other myopathies. MG fluctuates.

choices.text: Fatigable weakness — worsens with use, improves with rest
choices.feedback: Correct! Fatigability is the hallmark of MG. Antibodies block AChR → fewer functional receptors → repeated stimulation depletes available ACh → progressive weakness. Rest allows ACh to rebuild. Classic: ptosis worsens with sustained upgaze, voice fades during conversation, difficulty chewing at end of meal.
Improvement with use = Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (pre-synaptic, facilitating).
Elevated CK with proximal weakness = inflammatory myopathy (polymyositis/dermatomyositis).

questions.stem: Most SENSITIVE diagnostic test for MG?

choices.feedback: Most specific but only ~85% sensitive for generalized MG and 50% for ocular MG.

Correct! Single-fiber EMG detects increased 'jitter' (variability in neuromuscular transmission time) — most sensitive test (>95%). Used when clinical suspicion is high but antibodies and RNS are negative. Repetitive nerve stimulation (decremental response) is more commonly used but less sensitive.
CT evaluates for thymoma but doesn't diagnose MG.
Historically used but less sensitive and rarely performed now due to cardiac risks.

questions.stem: ALL MG patients should get which imaging study?

choices.feedback: MG is a peripheral NMJ disorder, not a CNS disease.

Correct! 10% of MG patients have thymoma, 65% have thymic hyperplasia. ALL patients need CT chest at diagnosis. Thymoma requires surgical resection regardless. Even without thymoma, thymectomy is beneficial in generalized AChR+ MG (MGTX trial — improved outcomes over 3 years).
MG is not associated with abdominal pathology.
MRI spine evaluates for spinal cord pathology, not NMJ disease.

questions.stem: Lambert-Eaton vs MG — key distinction?

choices.text: LEMS has autoantibodies against AChR receptors
choices.feedback: LEMS has anti-VGCC antibodies (pre-synaptic), not anti-AChR.

choices.text: LEMS: weakness IMPROVES with use (pre-synaptic VGCC antibodies, associated with SCLC)
choices.feedback: Correct! LEMS = pre-synaptic (VGCC antibodies block calcium-mediated ACh release). Repeated stimulation → calcium accumulates → MORE ACh released → strength improves (facilitating response). Associated with small cell lung cancer (paraneoplastic). RNS shows INCREMENTAL response. MG = post-synaptic, decremental response, associated with thymoma.
LEMS typically causes proximal limb weakness, autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth), and hyporeflexia.
LEMS treatment includes 3,4-DAP (amifampridine) to increase ACh release, plus treating underlying cancer.

questions.stem: Drugs that can worsen MG include all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Aminoglycosides worsen MG — they impair neuromuscular transmission.

Correct! Pyridostigmine is the primary TREATMENT for MG (AChE inhibitor → increases ACh at NMJ). Drugs that worsen MG: aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, macrolides, beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, magnesium, D-penicillamine, botulinum toxin. Always check the drug list before prescribing to MG patients.
Fluoroquinolones can worsen MG and carry an boxed warning.
Magnesium impairs neuromuscular transmission and can precipitate myasthenic crisis.

questions.stem: #1 trigger for myasthenic crisis?

choices.feedback: Stress can worsen MG but is not the #1 trigger.

Correct! Infection (especially respiratory) is the #1 trigger for myasthenic crisis. Others: surgery, medication changes (stopping immunosuppression, starting MG-worsening drugs), pregnancy, emotional stress. Management: ICU, monitor FVC/NIF, IVIG or plasmapheresis, treat infection, adjust MG medications.
Exercise worsens MG symptoms but rarely triggers full crisis.
Heat can worsen MG but is not the primary crisis trigger.

questions.stem: Repetitive nerve stimulation in MG shows:

choices.feedback: Incremental response = Lambert-Eaton (facilitating, pre-synaptic).

Correct! RNS at 2-3 Hz → progressive decrease in compound muscle action potential amplitude (>10% decrement). Reflects post-synaptic AChR depletion with repeated stimulation. This is the most commonly used electrodiagnostic test for MG. Compare: LEMS shows incremental response with rapid repetitive stimulation.
Normal RNS occurs in ~50% of ocular MG; doesn't rule out disease if clinical suspicion high.
Fibrillations suggest denervation (ALS, radiculopathy), not NMJ disease.

questions.stem: Cholinergic crisis from excess pyridostigmine presents with:

choices.feedback: Those are anticholinergic effects — the OPPOSITE of cholinergic excess.

choices.text: DUMBBELSS (diarrhea, urination, miosis, bronchospasm, bradycardia, emesis, lacrimation, salivation, sweating) + weakness
choices.feedback: Correct! Excess AChE inhibitor → too much ACh at muscarinic AND nicotinic receptors. Muscarinic: DUMBBELSS. Nicotinic: fasciculations + weakness (depolarization block). Distinguishing from myasthenic crisis is critical — both cause weakness, but cholinergic crisis has cholinergic signs. Treatment: stop pyridostigmine, atropine for muscarinic effects.
Fever + rigidity suggests NMS or serotonin syndrome, not cholinergic crisis.
Cholinergic excess causes bradycardia and hypotension, not the reverse.

questions.stem: Hallmark clinical feature of myasthenia gravis?

choices.feedback: That's GBS. MG has a different pattern.

choices.text: Fatigable weakness that worsens with use and improves with rest
choices.feedback: Correct! Fatigable weakness is pathognomonic for MG. Ptosis worsens through the day or with sustained upward gaze. Improvement after rest distinguishes MG from other neuromuscular conditions. Ice pack test: ptosis improves with cold application (reduces AChE activity at NMJ).
Resting tremor is characteristic of Parkinson disease.

choices.text: Fixed weakness worse in the morning
choices.feedback: MG is typically worse later in the day with sustained activity.

questions.stem: Most common antibody in myasthenia gravis?

choices.feedback: Anti-MuSK is found in 5-8%, typically with more severe bulbar symptoms.

Correct! Anti-AChR antibodies in 85% of generalized MG. They block, cross-link, and destroy postsynaptic receptors via complement activation. Most specific diagnostic test. If negative, check anti-MuSK. All MG patients need CT chest to screen for thymoma.
Anti-Jo1 is associated with antisynthetase syndrome/inflammatory myopathy.
Anti-dsDNA is specific for SLE.

questions.stem: #1 trigger for myasthenic crisis?

choices.feedback: Stress can worsen MG but is not the #1 trigger.

Correct! Infection (especially respiratory) is the most common precipitant of myasthenic crisis. The inflammatory response and physiologic stress exacerbate NMJ dysfunction. Always workup and treat infection in crisis. Other triggers: surgery, medication changes, certain drugs (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones).
Temperature can affect NMJ function but is not the primary trigger.
Exercise exacerbates fatigable weakness but rarely triggers full crisis without another factor.

questions.stem: Medications that can WORSEN myasthenia gravis include:

choices.feedback: These are treatments for MG, not exacerbating medications.

Correct! These drugs worsen NMJ transmission. Aminoglycosides/fluoroquinolones: impair presynaptic ACh release. Beta-blockers: worsen fatigue. Magnesium: blocks NMJ calcium channels. Also: succinylcholine (prolonged paralysis), D-penicillamine, phenytoin, telithromycin.
Standard analgesics do not affect NMJ transmission.
These medications are safe in MG patients.

questions.stem: DUMBBELSS symptoms indicate:

choices.feedback: Myasthenic crisis = weakness from insufficient treatment. DUMBBELSS = excessive cholinergic stimulation.

Correct! Cholinergic crisis from excessive pyridostigmine: Diarrhea, Urination, Miosis, Bradycardia, Bronchospasm/Bronchorrhea, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation, Sweating. Treatment: hold AChE inhibitors, atropine for muscarinic symptoms, supportive care.
Serotonin syndrome: hyperthermia, rigidity, clonus, agitation.
Organophosphates cause similar cholinergic symptoms but from external toxin, not overtreatment.

questions.stem: Repetitive nerve stimulation in MG shows:

choices.feedback: Incrementing response is seen in Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome.

Correct! Low-frequency (2-3 Hz) repetitive nerve stimulation: progressive decrease in compound muscle action potential amplitude >10% = decremental response. This reflects progressive depletion of available AChR at the NMJ. Lambert-Eaton shows INCREMENTAL response at HIGH frequency (post-tetanic facilitation).
Fibrillation potentials suggest denervation (ALS, radiculopathy), not MG.
Conduction block is seen in GBS/CIDP demyelinating conditions.

questions.stem: Thymectomy in MG is recommended for:

choices.feedback: Thymectomy benefits even non-thymoma MG patients.

choices.text: All thymoma patients AND non-thymoma generalized MG (ages 18-65)
choices.feedback: Correct! MGTX trial: thymectomy + prednisone was superior to prednisone alone in non-thymoma generalized AChR+ MG (ages 18-65). All thymoma patients should have thymectomy regardless. CT chest screening is mandatory for all MG patients.
Seronegative patients may benefit but the strongest evidence is for AChR+ MG.

choices.text: Only patients who fail all other treatments
choices.feedback: Thymectomy is recommended early, not as a last resort.

questions.stem: In myasthenic crisis, which treatments provide rapid improvement?

choices.feedback: Steroids take weeks to work and can initially worsen weakness.

Correct! Both provide rapid improvement (days). IVIG: 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days. Plasmapheresis: removes circulating antibodies. Choose one (don't combine — PLEX removes IVIG). Also: intubate if FVC <15-20 mL/kg, treat trigger (infection), hold/reduce pyridostigmine (may worsen secretions during crisis).
Excessive pyridostigmine can cause cholinergic crisis. Typically held or reduced during crisis.
Azathioprine takes 3-6 months for full effect — too slow for crisis.

questions.stem: What is the most likely explanation for elevated maternal serum AFP?

choices.feedback: Correct! Elevated MSAFP (>2.5 MoM) is the classic screening marker for open NTDs. AFP leaks from exposed fetal tissue into amniotic fluid and then maternal serum.
Down syndrome causes LOW AFP. The triple screen pattern for T21 is: low AFP, low unconjugated estriol, high hCG.
GDM doesn't significantly affect AFP levels. Elevated AFP should prompt evaluation for structural defects.
Twins can elevate AFP, but ultrasound would have already identified twins. This needs NTD evaluation.

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Meningocele contains only meninges and CSF, no neural elements. It has better prognosis. This sac contains neural tissue.

Correct! Myelomeningocele = meninges + spinal cord/nerve roots protruding through the defect. The lemon and banana signs indicate associated Chiari II malformation (hindbrain herniation). This is the most common clinically significant NTD.
Spina bifida occulta has intact skin coverage and would NOT elevate AFP (it's closed). It's usually an incidental finding.
Anencephaly is failure of the CRANIAL neuropore — absent calvarium and cerebral hemispheres. This defect is lumbosacral (caudal neuropore failure).

questions.stem: Which factor most likely contributed to this NTD?

choices.feedback: Correct! Valproic acid is one of the strongest risk factors for NTDs (1-2% risk vs 0.1% baseline). It interferes with folate metabolism and histone deacetylase. Women on valproic acid need high-dose folic acid (4 mg/day) starting before conception.
Late folic acid is a contributing factor, but the neural tube closes by day 28 (~week 6). The valproic acid exposure during this critical window is the primary risk factor.
Age 28 is not a risk factor for NTDs. NTD risk is not age-dependent (unlike trisomies).
Multiparity itself doesn't increase NTD risk. Prior NTD-affected pregnancy does increase recurrence risk (2-5%).

questions.stem: What did the MOMS trial demonstrate about fetal surgery for myelomeningocele?

choices.feedback: The MOMS trial showed clear benefits for prenatal repair in specific measures.

choices.text: Reduced need for VP shunt at 12 months
choices.feedback: Correct! The MOMS trial (2011) showed prenatal repair reduced VP shunt placement from 82% to 40% at 12 months, and improved motor function at 30 months. However, it increased risk of preterm delivery and uterine dehiscence.
Fetal surgery improves outcomes but does not restore full function. Most children still have some motor and bladder dysfunction.
Fetal repair can improve hindbrain herniation but doesn't eliminate it completely.

questions.stem: What is the most urgent concern in the first 24 hours?

choices.feedback: Correct! The open myelomeningocele must be closed within 24-72 hours to prevent meningitis and further neural damage. Until surgery, the sac is kept moist with sterile saline-soaked gauze and the baby positioned prone.
Orthopedic issues are important but not urgent. Preventing CNS infection from the open defect takes priority.
Bladder management is crucial but the immediate priority is closing the defect to prevent infection.
MRI is important for long-term planning but should not delay surgical closure.

questions.stem: What does the functional motor level predict for ambulation?

choices.feedback: L4 motor level typically allows community ambulation but usually needs ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs) and may need crutches.

Correct! L4 motor level (intact quadriceps) is a favorable prognostic indicator. These children typically achieve community ambulation with AFOs. Higher levels (L1-L2) are usually wheelchair-dependent; lower levels (L5-S1) may walk with minimal support.
Wheelchair dependence is typical for thoracic and high lumbar (L1-L2) levels. L4 has preserved quadriceps function, enabling walking.
The neurological deficit is permanent. The absent ankle function means normal gait is not achievable.

questions.stem: What embryological process has failed in lissencephaly?

choices.feedback: Proliferation occurs in the ventricular zone and is normal in lissencephaly. The neurons are produced but fail to reach their destination.

choices.text: Neuronal migration from the ventricular zone to the cortical plate
choices.feedback: Correct! Lissencephaly results from defective neuronal migration. Normally, neurons born in the ventricular/subventricular zone migrate along radial glial fibers to form the 6-layered cortex (inside-out pattern — later neurons pass earlier ones). In lissencephaly, migration fails → smooth cortex with only 4 disorganized layers instead of 6. Caused by LIS1 (17p13.3) or DCX (X-linked) mutations.
Neural tube closure defects (NTDs) cause spina bifida and anencephaly — different from migration disorders.
Excessive neurons would cause megalencephaly, not a smooth cortex. Lissencephaly is about WHERE neurons end up, not how many.

questions.stem: A female infant has MRI showing bands of gray matter lining the ventricles underneath an apparently normal cortex. What is this finding called?

choices.feedback: Polymicrogyria involves abnormal cortical folding with many small, fused gyri. The finding described is heterotopic neurons lining the ventricles — a different pattern.

Correct! Band heterotopia (subcortical band heterotopia / double cortex) = a band of neurons that stopped migrating partway, creating a layer of gray matter between the ventricular surface and the cortex. Classically seen in females with DCX mutations (X-linked). Males with DCX get full lissencephaly; females (one normal X) get band heterotopia. Presentation: seizures, variable intellectual disability.
Schizencephaly involves full-thickness clefts lined by gray matter extending from ventricle to surface — not bands.
PVL is hypoxic-ischemic white matter injury, not a migration disorder.

questions.stem: What is the key embryological difference between polymicrogyria and lissencephaly?

choices.text: PMG affects migration; lissencephaly affects proliferation
choices.feedback: Both can involve migration, but PMG is primarily a post-migrational cortical organization defect.

choices.text: PMG is a late cortical organization defect; lissencephaly is early migration failure
choices.feedback: Correct! Lissencephaly = early migration failure (weeks 12-16) → neurons don't reach the cortex. Polymicrogyria = late organization/lamination defect (weeks 20-24) → neurons reach the cortex but organize abnormally, forming many small fused gyri with disordered lamination. PMG can also result from in utero vascular injury or infection (CMV). Bilateral perisylvian PMG → pseudobulbar palsy (drooling, dysphagia, dysarthria).

choices.text: They are the same process with different severity
choices.feedback: They are distinct processes occurring at different developmental timepoints with different genetic causes.

choices.text: PMG is always infectious; lissencephaly is always genetic
choices.feedback: PMG can be genetic (SRPX2, GPR56) or acquired (CMV, ischemia). Lissencephaly is primarily genetic but the distinction is the developmental stage affected.

questions.stem: What distinguishes open-lip from closed-lip schizencephaly?

choices.text: Open-lip: cleft walls separated, CSF-filled. Closed-lip: walls apposed.
choices.feedback: Correct! Open-lip schizencephaly: the walls of the cleft are separated with CSF flowing between ventricle and subarachnoid space. Worse prognosis — motor deficits, seizures, often severe. Closed-lip: the gray matter-lined walls are in contact (fused lips). Better prognosis — may present later with seizures or mild hemiparesis. Both are lined by polymicrogyric gray matter (key distinction from porencephaly, which is lined by white matter/gliosis).

choices.text: Open-lip involves both hemispheres; closed-lip is unilateral
choices.feedback: Both open and closed-lip can be unilateral or bilateral. The distinction is whether the cleft walls are separated (open) or in contact (closed).

choices.text: Open-lip is caused by infection; closed-lip is genetic
choices.feedback: Both types share similar etiologies (EMX2 mutations, in utero ischemia). The morphology, not the etiology, determines the type.

choices.text: Open-lip has white matter lining; closed-lip has gray matter
choices.feedback: Both types are lined by gray matter (polymicrogyric cortex). A CSF-filled cavity lined by WHITE matter/gliosis is porencephaly (a destructive lesion), not schizencephaly.

questions.stem: What is the most common cause of medically refractory focal epilepsy in children that benefits from surgical resection?

choices.feedback: Mesial temporal sclerosis is the most common cause in adults. In children, focal cortical dysplasia is more common.

Correct! Focal cortical dysplasia (FCD) is the #1 cause of refractory focal epilepsy in children and the most common finding in pediatric epilepsy surgery specimens. FCD is a localized organization defect (disordered lamination, dysmorphic neurons, ± balloon cells). Unlike diffuse PMG, FCD can be surgically resected if the focus is well-defined. Seizure-free rates after FCD resection: 50-80%.
Tubers can cause epilepsy but are less common than FCD overall as a cause of refractory pediatric epilepsy.
Tumor-associated epilepsy is important but less common than FCD as the substrate for refractory focal pediatric epilepsy.

questions.stem: The tetrad of NMS (FARM) includes:

choices.feedback: Ataxia, rash, and myoclonus are not core features of NMS.

choices.text: Fever, Autonomic instability, Rigidity (lead-pipe), Mental status changes
choices.feedback: Correct! FARM: Fever (often >40°C), Autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis), Rigidity (generalized lead-pipe — distinguishes from serotonin syndrome), Mental status changes (confusion → obtundation → coma). CK is markedly elevated.

choices.text: Fever, Anemia, Renal failure, Mucosal bleeding
choices.feedback: This describes TTP/HUS-like features, not NMS.
These are nonspecific systemic symptoms, not NMS.

questions.stem: What MOST reliably distinguishes NMS from serotonin syndrome?

choices.feedback: Both NMS and serotonin syndrome cause hyperthermia.

Correct! NMS: generalized lead-pipe rigidity with NORMAL or decreased reflexes. Serotonin syndrome: clonus (especially lower extremity), hyperreflexia, and tremor. Clonus is the single most distinguishing feature of serotonin syndrome. Also: NMS develops over days-weeks; serotonin syndrome within hours.
Both cause altered mental status.
Both cause diaphoresis as part of autonomic dysfunction.

questions.stem: Treatment of NMS includes:

choices.feedback: Cyproheptadine is the treatment for serotonin syndrome, not NMS.

Correct! Dantrolene (muscle relaxant — reduces rigidity/heat) + bromocriptine (dopamine agonist — reverses dopamine blockade). Also: stop offending agent, aggressive cooling, IV fluids, ICU monitoring. Amantadine can substitute for bromocriptine. Benzodiazepines for agitation.
The antipsychotic is the CAUSE — it must be stopped immediately.
Naloxone reverses opioids, not NMS.

questions.stem: NMS can be triggered by withdrawal of which medication?

choices.feedback: SSRI withdrawal causes discontinuation syndrome, not NMS.

Correct! Abrupt withdrawal of dopaminergic agents (levodopa, dopamine agonists) in Parkinson patients can trigger NMS-like syndrome. Same pathophysiology: sudden central dopamine depletion. Never abruptly stop Parkinson medications. Treat the same as NMS + resume dopaminergic therapy.
Benzodiazepine withdrawal causes seizures and autonomic instability but not NMS.
Opioid withdrawal causes GI symptoms and autonomic changes but not NMS.

questions.stem: Masseter spasm during induction of anesthesia suggests:

choices.feedback: NMS develops over days, not acutely during anesthesia.

Correct! Masseter spasm (trismus) during anesthesia, especially with succinylcholine, is an early warning of malignant hyperthermia. MH: genetic (RYR1 mutation, autosomal dominant), triggered by volatile anesthetics + succinylcholine. Treatment: stop triggers, dantrolene IV, active cooling.
Serotonin syndrome is caused by serotonergic drugs, not anesthetics.
Cholinergic crisis has DUMBBELSS symptoms, not masseter spasm during anesthesia.

questions.stem: CK elevation in NMS is typically:

choices.feedback: Mild elevation is more typical of statin myopathy. NMS causes severe elevation.

Correct! Massive CK elevation from generalized muscle rigidity → rhabdomyolysis. CK >1000 is common; levels >100,000 occur in severe cases. Rhabdomyolysis → myoglobinuria → AKI (leading cause of death in NMS). Aggressive IV hydration is essential for renal protection.
Normal CK essentially rules out NMS.

choices.text: Elevated only if patient has seizures
choices.feedback: CK is elevated from sustained muscle rigidity, not seizures (NMS rarely causes seizures).

questions.stem: After NMS resolves, can the patient ever take antipsychotics again?

choices.feedback: Re-challenge is possible with careful precautions.

choices.text: Yes — after 2+ weeks, using a low-potency or atypical agent with slow titration
choices.feedback: Correct! Re-challenge is reasonable after full NMS resolution (minimum 2 weeks, ideally longer). Use a different antipsychotic (low-potency typical or atypical, different class from trigger), start at low dose with very slow titration, close monitoring. Recurrence risk: ~10-30%. Shared decision-making with patient.

choices.text: Only if CK returns to normal within 24 hours
choices.feedback: CK normalization takes days-weeks. Re-challenge timing is based on clinical resolution, not just CK.
Route of administration doesn't change NMS risk — it's the dopamine blockade mechanism.

questions.stem: Dantrolene works by:

choices.feedback: Bromocriptine is the dopamine agonist. Dantrolene works peripherally on muscle.

choices.text: Blocking calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum in skeletal muscle
choices.feedback: Correct! Dantrolene inhibits ryanodine receptor (RYR1) → reduces intracellular calcium release → decreases muscle contraction → reduces rigidity and heat generation. Used in both NMS and malignant hyperthermia. Side effects: hepatotoxicity, muscle weakness. Only acts on skeletal muscle — does not affect cardiac or smooth muscle.
That's the mechanism of benzodiazepines and barbiturates.
AChE inhibition is the mechanism of pyridostigmine (treats MG) and nerve agents.

questions.stem: Which diagnosis applies to each patient?

choices.feedback: Correct! Omphalocele: midline defect AT the umbilicus, covered by a membrane (peritoneum + amnion), often contains liver. Gastroschisis: defect to the RIGHT of the umbilicus, NO covering membrane, bowel floating freely in amniotic fluid. Note the demographics: omphalocele is associated with advanced maternal age, gastroschisis with young mothers.
Reversed! The membrane-covered central defect = omphalocele. The uncovered paraumbilical defect = gastroschisis.

choices.text: Both are omphaloceles of different sizes
choices.feedback: These are fundamentally different conditions with different embryology, associations, and outcomes.
Umbilical hernia is a postnatal condition with intact skin coverage. Omphalocele is a congenital defect with a membranous sac, present at birth.

questions.stem: What is the embryologic mechanism of omphalocele?

choices.text: Failure of midgut return to the abdomen
choices.feedback: Correct! During weeks 6-10, the midgut herniates into the umbilical cord (physiologic herniation) for growth and rotation. Omphalocele occurs when the gut FAILS TO RETURN to the abdominal cavity by week 10-12. The contents remain in the umbilical sac, covered by the membrane.
This is not the mechanism. The abdominal wall lateral folds (cephalic, caudal, lateral) fail to completely close, leaving the midline defect.

choices.text: Involution of the right umbilical vein
choices.feedback: This is the proposed mechanism for GASTROSCHISIS, not omphalocele. Gastroschisis may result from right umbilical vein involution leading to ischemic weakness in the body wall.
Neural crest cells don't contribute to the anterior abdominal wall. They're relevant for heart, face, and enteric nervous system.

questions.stem: What percentage of omphaloceles have associated chromosomal anomalies?

choices.feedback: Much higher than 5%. This is the rate for gastroschisis.

Correct! 30-50% of omphaloceles have chromosomal anomalies (T13, T18, T21 most common). Additional associations: Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (omphalocele + macroglossia + macrosomia + hypoglycemia), pentalogy of Cantrell (midline defects), cardiac defects in 30-50%. This is why genetic workup is essential for omphalocele.
The rate is higher. Combined chromosomal + syndromic associations approach 50%.
While the rate is high, it's not that high. About half of omphaloceles are isolated.

questions.stem: What is the immediate priority for exposed gastroschisis bowel?

choices.feedback: Immediate closure is rarely possible — the abdominal cavity is often too small for the edematous bowel. Staged reduction is usually needed.

choices.text: Wrap bowel in sterile bag, prevent heat/fluid loss
choices.feedback: Correct! The exposed bowel loses massive fluid and heat. Priorities: (1) Place bowel in sterile plastic bag (silo/spring-loaded or improvised); (2) Position baby on right side to prevent mesenteric kinking; (3) IV fluids at 2-3× maintenance (insensible losses); (4) NG tube for decompression; (5) Broad-spectrum antibiotics.
Gauze is not ideal — a plastic bag/silo is preferred because it allows visualization, reduces evaporative losses more effectively, and doesn't adhere to the bowel.
The exposed, matted bowel is non-functional. The baby needs TPN until bowel function returns after repair (often weeks).

questions.stem: What is the most common long-term GI complication of gastroschisis?

choices.feedback: While the bowel may not rotate normally, clinically significant volvulus after gastroschisis repair is uncommon.

Correct! The bowel's serosal damage from amniotic fluid exposure causes long-term dysmotility in many gastroschisis patients. Feeding intolerance, GERD, and need for prolonged TPN are common. About 10-15% develop intestinal atresia or short bowel syndrome requiring long-term nutritional support.
Incisional hernia can occur but is not the most common long-term complication.
Adhesive bowel obstruction can occur but is less common than chronic dysmotility.

questions.stem: Classic opioid overdose triad?

choices.feedback: That's a sympathomimetic toxidrome.

choices.text: CNS depression, respiratory depression, miosis (pinpoint pupils)
choices.feedback: Correct! The triad is pathognomonic. Respiratory depression is the lethal mechanism — opioids suppress the brainstem respiratory drive (mu receptors). Pinpoint pupils (miosis) is characteristic but not universal (exceptions: meperidine, severe hypoxia, co-ingestions).
That suggests serotonin syndrome or sympathomimetic.
These can occur but aren't the classic triad.

questions.stem: Naloxone should be titrated to:

choices.feedback: Full reversal in dependent patients precipitates withdrawal and is unnecessary.

Correct! Goal: reverse life-threatening respiratory depression without precipitating full withdrawal. Start low (0.04-0.4 mg IV), titrate up. Opioid-dependent patients: use smaller initial doses. Complete reversal causes acute withdrawal (non-lethal but causes agitation, vomiting, aspiration risk).
Naloxone shouldn't be titrated to pain endpoints in overdose.
Pupil size is not the target of naloxone titration.

questions.stem: Why is re-sedation a major concern after naloxone?

choices.feedback: Naloxone is a pure antagonist with no sedative effects.

choices.text: Naloxone's duration (30-90 min) is shorter than most opioids
choices.feedback: Correct! Most opioids last longer than naloxone → as naloxone wears off, opioid effects return → re-sedation with respiratory depression. Especially dangerous with: methadone (half-life 24-36h), extended-release formulations, fentanyl patches. Observe ≥4h (short-acting) to ≥24h (long-acting). Consider naloxone infusion.
Tolerance to naloxone doesn't develop with single doses.
Re-sedation can occur with any opioid, especially long-acting agents.

questions.stem: Methadone overdose has unique risks including:

choices.feedback: Hepatotoxicity is more associated with acetaminophen.

choices.text: QTc prolongation → torsades de pointes + prolonged toxicity requiring extended observation
choices.feedback: Correct! Methadone uniquely prolongs QTc (blocks hERG K+ channels) → risk of torsades de pointes. Also: very long half-life (24-36h) means toxicity outlasts naloxone → need extended observation (≥24h) and possible naloxone infusion. Unlike most opioids, methadone requires cardiac monitoring.
Blindness is associated with methanol poisoning.

choices.text: Seizures as the primary lethal mechanism
choices.feedback: Respiratory depression remains the primary lethal mechanism. Meperidine is the opioid most associated with seizures.

questions.stem: If a patient doesn't respond to 10 mg naloxone total, you should:

choices.feedback: If 10 mg total doesn't work, opioids are unlikely the sole cause.

choices.text: Reconsider the diagnosis (may not be opioid overdose)
choices.feedback: Correct! No response to 10 mg IV naloxone → strongly reconsider diagnosis. Consider: co-ingestions (benzodiazepines, GHB, barbiturates), non-opioid causes of CNS depression (stroke, hypoglycemia, head trauma, sepsis), polysubstance use. Exception: massive fentanyl exposure may require higher doses.
Flumazenil should be used cautiously and only if benzodiazepine overdose is suspected.
Charcoal is unlikely to help at this point and is dangerous in an obtunded patient without airway protection.

questions.stem: Opioid withdrawal is distinguished from alcohol/benzo withdrawal by:

choices.feedback: Opioid withdrawal is extremely uncomfortable but not life-threatening.

choices.text: Opioid withdrawal is NOT life-threatening (no seizures/DT), unlike alcohol/benzo withdrawal
choices.feedback: Correct! Opioid withdrawal: dysphoria, myalgias, diarrhea, vomiting, piloerection, yawning, lacrimation, rhinorrhea. Extremely uncomfortable but NOT fatal. Alcohol and benzodiazepine withdrawal CAN kill (seizures, delirium tremens → autonomic instability → death). This distinction is critical for management.

choices.text: Opioid withdrawal causes seizures while alcohol doesn't
choices.feedback: It's the opposite — alcohol/benzo withdrawal causes seizures.
The clinical presentations and risks differ significantly.

questions.stem: Non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema in opioid overdose is most associated with:

choices.feedback: Any opioid can cause it but heroin is the classic association.

Correct! Heroin is classically associated with non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema (NCPE). Mechanism: acute alveolar-capillary membrane damage + negative-pressure pulmonary edema from airway obstruction. Can occur even after successful naloxone reversal. Treatment: supportive (oxygen, NIV/intubation if needed). Usually resolves within 24-48h.
While possible, NCPE is most classically linked to heroin/methadone.
Tramadol is more associated with seizures and serotonin syndrome than NCPE.

questions.stem: Intranasal naloxone dose for bystander administration?

choices.feedback: 0.04 mg is a low IV starting dose for opioid-dependent patients.

Correct! naloxone (Narcan) nasal spray: 4 mg into one nostril, may repeat after 2-3 min in alternating nostrils. Available OTC for community/bystander use. Slightly slower onset than IV (~3-5 min vs 1-2 min) but no needles required. Key public health intervention for fentanyl crisis. All patients should receive rescue naloxone prescription at opioid prescribing.
That's an IV dose, not intranasal.
40 mg is far too much and not a standard dosing.

questions.stem: DUMBBELSS mnemonic: the most life-threatening muscarinic effect?

choices.feedback: Diarrhea is uncomfortable but not immediately lethal.

Correct! The 'B's in DUMBBELSS are the killers: Bronchospasm (airway constriction) + Bronchorrhea (massive secretion production — can produce liters). Combined with respiratory muscle weakness (nicotinic) and central respiratory depression (CNS) → respiratory failure = #1 cause of death. This is why atropine endpoint is drying of secretions.
Incontinence is distressing but not life-threatening.
Tearing is a minor symptom.

questions.stem: Atropine treats which receptor effects in OP poisoning?

choices.feedback: Atropine has NO nicotinic effect — pralidoxime treats nicotinic symptoms.

Correct! Atropine = competitive muscarinic antagonist. Treats: bronchorrhea, bronchospasm, bradycardia, salivation, miosis, GI/GU hypermotility. Does NOT treat: fasciculations, muscle weakness, paralysis (nicotinic — need pralidoxime). Does NOT treat CNS effects (need benzodiazepines for seizures).
Atropine only blocks muscarinic receptors.
Atropine has limited CNS penetration.

questions.stem: Pralidoxime (2-PAM) works by:

choices.feedback: That's atropine's mechanism.

choices.text: Reactivating acetylcholinesterase by cleaving the OP-enzyme bond
choices.feedback: Correct! 2-PAM is a nucleophilic oxime that attacks the OP-AChE bond → frees the enzyme → restores AChE activity. Must be given BEFORE 'aging' (irreversible conformational change of the OP-enzyme complex). Aging time varies: sarin ~5h, VX ~40h, some pesticides longer. Treats nicotinic effects (weakness, fasciculations).
2-PAM doesn't destroy the OP — it separates it from the enzyme.
That's the mechanism of benzodiazepines.

questions.stem: Atropine dosing endpoint in OP poisoning:

choices.feedback: Tachycardia is an unreliable endpoint and may be from nicotinic stimulation.

Correct! Clear the lungs — bronchorrhea is the lethal muscarinic effect. Pupil dilation and tachycardia are poor endpoints (may be caused by nicotinic effects or fear). Dose: 2-4 mg IV, double q5 min until secretions clear. Massive doses may be needed (10-100+ mg). Do NOT under-dose atropine.
Pupil size is an unreliable endpoint.
BP normalization is not the atropine endpoint.

questions.stem: Difference between organophosphate and carbamate poisoning?

choices.feedback: Both cause cholinergic crisis (muscarinic + nicotinic).

choices.text: OPs: irreversible AChE inhibition. Carbamates: reversible (spontaneous recovery)
choices.feedback: Correct! Carbamates reversibly bind AChE → enzyme spontaneously reactivates over hours. OPs irreversibly bind → require pralidoxime before aging. Clinical implication: carbamate poisoning is shorter duration, less severe CNS effects, and pralidoxime is generally not needed. Atropine is still first-line for both.
Both cause miosis via muscarinic stimulation.

choices.text: Carbamates are not found in pesticides
choices.feedback: Many pesticides contain carbamates (carbaryl, aldicarb).

questions.stem: Intermediate syndrome occurs:

choices.feedback: Immediate effects are the cholinergic crisis.

choices.text: 24-96 hours after exposure: proximal weakness and respiratory failure
choices.feedback: Correct! Intermediate syndrome: 24-96h after initial cholinergic crisis resolves. Proximal muscle weakness, neck flexor weakness, cranial nerve palsies, respiratory muscle weakness → may need ventilation. Due to persistent NMJ dysfunction (nicotinic receptor desensitization). Can be fatal if respiratory support not provided.
That timeline suggests delayed polyneuropathy (OPIDN), a different complication.

choices.text: Only with nerve agents, not pesticides
choices.feedback: Intermediate syndrome occurs with both pesticides and nerve agents.

questions.stem: Healthcare worker exposure risk in OP poisoning:

choices.text: No risk — OPs only enter through ingestion
choices.feedback: OPs can be absorbed through skin and inhaled.

choices.text: Significant — OPs absorb through skin; PPE is essential before decontamination
choices.feedback: Correct! OPs (especially nerve agents and concentrated pesticides) can be absorbed through skin, inhaled, and through mucous membranes. Healthcare workers MUST wear full PPE (impervious gowns, gloves, respiratory protection) before contact. Remove patient's clothing (removes ~80% of contamination) + copious soap/water decontamination. Secondary contamination of healthcare workers is well-documented.

choices.text: Risk only from nerve agents, not pesticides
choices.feedback: Both nerve agents and concentrated pesticides pose dermal absorption risk.

choices.text: Risk is limited to the first 5 minutes
choices.feedback: Contaminated clothing and skin can off-gas and expose others for extended periods.

questions.stem: A farmer presents with miosis, excessive salivation, muscle fasciculations, and garlic odor. Treatment order?

choices.feedback: Wrong order — decontamination and atropine are more urgent.

choices.text: PPE + decontamination → atropine (for muscarinic) → pralidoxime (for nicotinic) → benzos if seizures
choices.feedback: Correct! Sequence: (1) PPE for providers + patient decontamination, (2) ABCs/airway, (3) Atropine 2-4 mg IV doubled q5 min until secretions dry, (4) Pralidoxime 1-2g IV (before aging), (5) Benzodiazepines for seizures. The garlic odor + agricultural setting + cholinergic symptoms = classic OP presentation.
Flumazenil is for benzodiazepines. Charcoal has limited role.
Naloxone is for opioids. This presentation is cholinergic, not opioid.

questions.stem: Diagnosis of acute pancreatitis requires 2 of 3 Atlanta criteria. Which lab is MOST specific?

choices.feedback: Amylase rises early but is less specific (elevated in salivary disease, renal failure, DKA, bowel obstruction).

Correct! Lipase is more sensitive AND specific than amylase for acute pancreatitis. Stays elevated longer (7-14 days vs 3-5 for amylase). Lipase ≥3× upper limit of normal meets the lab criterion. The degree of elevation does NOT correlate with severity.
CRP is a prognostic marker (>150 at 48h predicts severity) but not diagnostic.
ALT >150 suggests gallstone etiology but doesn't diagnose pancreatitis.

questions.stem: #1 cause of acute pancreatitis?

choices.feedback: Alcohol is #2 (~30%). Historically overestimated.

Correct! Gallstones cause ~40% of acute pancreatitis. Stone impacts ampulla of Vater → pancreatic duct obstruction → acinar cell injury. ALT >150 has a positive predictive value >95% for gallstone etiology. Same-admission cholecystectomy prevents recurrence.
Hypertriglyceridemia is #3. Requires TG >1000 mg/dL.
Drug-induced is uncommon (<5%).

questions.stem: IV fluid of choice in acute pancreatitis?

choices.feedback: NS is acceptable but LR is preferred based on evidence.

Correct! LR reduces SIRS compared to NS (likely due to anti-inflammatory effect of lactate). Goal-directed: 5-10 mL/kg/hr initially, titrate to UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr. Early aggressive hydration (first 12-24h) is critical for preventing necrotizing pancreatitis.
Dextrose solutions provide no volume resuscitation benefit.
Albumin may be used as adjunct in severe cases but is not first-line fluid.

questions.stem: When should cholecystectomy be performed for gallstone pancreatitis?

choices.feedback: Delayed cholecystectomy has higher readmission rates for recurrent pancreatitis.

choices.text: During the SAME admission (after pancreatitis resolves)
choices.feedback: Correct! Same-admission cholecystectomy reduces recurrent gallstone pancreatitis by 75%. Perform after clinical improvement (pain resolving, labs trending down). Exception: severe necrotizing pancreatitis — delay until collections resolve. ERCP acutely if concurrent cholangitis or persistent CBD stone.

choices.text: Only if there are recurrent episodes
choices.feedback: Waiting for recurrence exposes patient to unnecessary risk.
Without cholecystectomy, ~30-60% have recurrent gallstone pancreatitis.

questions.stem: Nutrition in acute pancreatitis — current evidence supports:

choices.text: Prolonged NPO (7-10 days) to rest the pancreas
choices.feedback: Prolonged NPO increases infection risk, delays recovery, and harms gut barrier.

choices.text: Early oral feeding within 24h if tolerated
choices.feedback: Correct! Early feeding (within 24h) is safe and reduces hospital stay, infection, and organ failure. Start low-fat solid diet (clear liquid diet is unnecessary as a step). If unable to eat: enteral feeding (NG/NJ tube) is superior to TPN (maintains gut barrier, reduces infections).

choices.text: TPN is preferred over enteral nutrition
choices.feedback: Enteral > TPN. TPN increases infection rates and is reserved for enteral-intolerant patients.

choices.text: High-fat diet to stimulate pancreatic recovery
choices.feedback: High-fat diet can worsen symptoms. Low-fat is recommended initially.

questions.stem: Infected pancreatic necrosis should be suspected when:

choices.feedback: Amylase changes early don't indicate infection.

choices.text: Fever/WBC rise occurs after 7-10 days of illness
choices.feedback: Correct! Early fever/WBC = SIRS from pancreatitis itself. New fever/clinical deterioration after 7-10 days = suspect infected necrosis. Confirm: CT-guided FNA for gram stain/culture, or gas bubbles in necrosis on CT. Treatment: step-up approach (percutaneous drainage → endoscopic necrosectomy → surgery as last resort). Prophylactic antibiotics are NOT recommended.
Lipase level does not correlate with necrosis or infection.
Hiccups can occur with diaphragmatic irritation but don't indicate infected necrosis.

questions.stem: Prophylactic antibiotics in acute pancreatitis are:

choices.feedback: No evidence supports routine prophylactic antibiotics.

choices.text: NOT recommended (no mortality benefit, increase fungal infections)
choices.feedback: Correct! Multiple RCTs and meta-analyses show prophylactic antibiotics do NOT reduce mortality or infected necrosis rates, but DO increase Candida infections and resistance. Only give antibiotics if: confirmed infected necrosis, concurrent cholangitis, or other documented infection.
Etiology doesn't determine antibiotic use.

choices.text: Given for 21 days in all severe cases
choices.feedback: No indication for prolonged prophylactic antibiotics.

questions.stem: BISAP score component that predicts severity?

choices.feedback: Amylase level is not part of BISAP and doesn't predict severity.

choices.text: BUN >25, Impaired mental status, SIRS, Age >60, Pleural effusion
choices.feedback: Correct! BISAP ≥3 at 24h predicts severe pancreatitis with similar accuracy to APACHE II but much simpler to calculate. Each component = 1 point. BUN >25 reflects third-spacing/dehydration. Pleural effusion reflects systemic inflammation.

choices.text: Heart rate, respiratory rate, and blood pressure only
choices.feedback: Those are SIRS criteria, just one component of BISAP.

choices.text: Lipase level and CT findings only
choices.feedback: Neither lipase level nor CT findings are part of BISAP.

questions.stem: Cardinal motor features of Parkinson disease (TRAP):

choices.feedback: Aphasia and paralysis are stroke features.

Correct! TRAP: resting Tremor (pill-rolling), Rigidity (cogwheel), Akinesia/bradykinesia (most disabling — slowness + reduced amplitude), Postural instability (late feature, increases fall risk). Asymmetric onset is characteristic.
This combination doesn't characterize PD.
This pattern suggests NMS or another autonomic disorder.

questions.stem: Resting tremor vs essential tremor — key difference:

choices.feedback: They have distinct characteristics.

choices.text: PD tremor is resting + asymmetric; ET is action/postural + bilateral and improves with alcohol
choices.feedback: Correct! PD: resting pill-rolling tremor, asymmetric, with bradykinesia and rigidity. ET: action/postural tremor (holding a cup, writing), bilateral, no rigidity or bradykinesia, improves with alcohol, positive family history. ET is the most common movement disorder.
ET and PD are distinct disorders.
That's essential tremor, not PD.

questions.stem: Most effective medication for Parkinson disease:

choices.feedback: Dopamine agonists are less effective than levodopa, though they have less dyskinesia risk.

Correct! Levodopa is converted to dopamine in the brain — MOST effective treatment for motor symptoms. Carbidopa prevents peripheral conversion (reduces nausea). Limitation: chronic use leads to motor complications (wearing-off between doses, on-off phenomenon, dyskinesias) after 5-10 years. Gold standard therapeutic trial.
Anticholinergics help tremor but are less effective overall and cause cognitive side effects in elderly.
Propranolol is first-line for essential tremor, not PD.

questions.stem: Dopamine agonist side effect that is unique and important:

choices.feedback: Hepatotoxicity is associated with tolcapone (COMT inhibitor), not dopamine agonists.

Correct! Dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) cause impulse control disorders in ~15-20%: pathological gambling, compulsive shopping, hypersexuality, binge eating. Must screen for and counsel about these. Also: daytime somnolence (sudden sleep attacks — dangerous for driving), hallucinations.
Not a dopamine agonist side effect.
Seizures are not a typical dopamine agonist side effect.

questions.stem: Drug-induced parkinsonism is most commonly caused by:

choices.feedback: SSRIs rarely cause parkinsonism.

Correct! D2 receptor blockers → decreased dopaminergic transmission → parkinsonism. Antipsychotics (typical > atypical), metoclopramide, prochlorperazine. Key distinction from PD: symmetric, often bilateral onset, temporal relationship to drug initiation. Reversible with drug discontinuation (may take weeks-months).
Benzodiazepines cause sedation but not parkinsonism.
ACEi don't affect dopaminergic pathways.

questions.stem: Non-motor symptom that may PRECEDE motor Parkinson symptoms by years:

choices.feedback: Hearing loss is not an early PD feature.

Correct! RBD (acting out dreams during REM sleep) may precede motor PD by 10-15 years. Also early: anosmia (loss of smell), constipation, depression. These are prodromal PD markers and reflect early α-synuclein deposition in brainstem and enteric nervous system before it reaches the substantia nigra.
Seizures are not associated with PD.
PD is a central disorder, not peripheral neuropathy.

questions.stem: Why must dopaminergic medications NEVER be stopped abruptly?

choices.feedback: Seizures are not the primary risk (that's benzodiazepine/alcohol withdrawal).

choices.text: Can cause NMS-like syndrome (rigidity, fever, AMS, rhabdomyolysis)
choices.feedback: Correct! Abrupt withdrawal of levodopa or dopamine agonists → dopamine depletion → NMS-like syndrome with severe rigidity, fever, altered consciousness, autonomic instability, elevated CK/rhabdomyolysis. Can be fatal. Always taper gradually. Also occurs when antipsychotics are given to PD patients.
Vision is not affected by dopaminergic withdrawal.
While motor function worsens, NMS-like syndrome is the critical danger.

questions.stem: Amantadine is specifically used in PD for:

choices.feedback: Amantadine is not preferred as initial monotherapy.

Correct! Amantadine (NMDA antagonist) is the only medication with good evidence for treating levodopa-induced dyskinesias (involuntary choreiform movements from chronic levodopa). Also has mild anti-parkinsonian effect. Extended-release formulation (Gocovri) approved specifically for dyskinesias.
PD depression is treated with SSRIs/SNRIs, not amantadine.
Orthostatic hypotension in PD is treated with fludrocortisone, midodrine, or droxidopa.

questions.stem: Cardinal features of Parkinson disease (TRAP)?

choices.feedback: Aphasia and ptosis are not PD features.

Correct! TRAP. Resting tremor (4-6 Hz pill-rolling, improves with action), cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia (MOST disabling — slowness and reduced amplitude), postural instability (late feature). Asymmetric onset is key diagnostic feature.
These are systemic symptoms, not related to PD.
Not the PD cardinal features.

questions.stem: Most effective medication for Parkinson disease?

choices.feedback: Dopamine agonists are less effective than levodopa.

Correct! Levodopa remains the most effective drug for PD motor symptoms. Carbidopa prevents peripheral conversion (reduces nausea). Long-term complications: wearing-off (shorter duration of effect), dyskinesias (involuntary movements), on-off fluctuations. Balance quality of life now vs motor complications later.
Amantadine has modest benefit and treats dyskinesias.
Anticholinergics help tremor but are less effective overall and cause cognitive side effects.

questions.stem: Resting tremor in Parkinson disease characteristically:

choices.feedback: Action/intention tremor is cerebellar. PD resting tremor actually improves with movement.

Correct! PD resting tremor: present at rest, diminishes with voluntary action (pill-rolling stops when patient reaches for an object). Cerebellar/intention tremor: absent at rest, WORSENS with target-directed movement. Essential tremor: present with action/posture, improves with alcohol.
PD tremor typically starts UNILATERAL — asymmetric onset is a key feature.
REM sleep behavior disorder occurs in PD but the tremor is a waking phenomenon.

questions.stem: Drug-induced parkinsonism differs from PD by being:

choices.feedback: Unilateral onset + resting tremor favors true PD, not drug-induced.

choices.text: Symmetric, often without resting tremor, and reversible
choices.feedback: Correct! Drug-induced parkinsonism: bilateral symmetric onset (vs PD's unilateral), often lacks resting tremor, reversible with discontinuation of offending drug (over weeks to months). Caused by dopamine antagonists: antipsychotics, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine.

choices.text: Associated with Lewy bodies on biopsy
choices.feedback: Lewy bodies are pathognomonic for PD/Lewy body dementia, not drug-induced parkinsonism.
Drug-induced parkinsonism can affect all extremities.

questions.stem: Levodopa-induced dyskinesias are treated with:

choices.feedback: Higher doses typically WORSEN dyskinesias.

Correct! Amantadine (NMDA receptor antagonist) is the primary treatment for levodopa-induced dyskinesias. Other strategies: reduce individual levodopa doses (give smaller doses more frequently), add COMT inhibitor to smooth response, consider deep brain stimulation for refractory motor fluctuations.
Anticholinergics treat tremor but don't help dyskinesias.
SSRIs treat PD-associated depression but not dyskinesias.

questions.stem: What distinguishes Lewy body dementia from Parkinson disease dementia?

choices.feedback: LBD has parkinsonism — the key is timing of dementia relative to motor symptoms.

choices.text: In LBD, dementia occurs BEFORE or within 1 year of motor symptoms
choices.feedback: Correct! The '1-year rule': if cognitive decline precedes or occurs within 1 year of motor symptoms = Lewy body dementia. If motor symptoms precede dementia by >1 year = Parkinson disease dementia. Both have Lewy bodies pathologically. LBD also features: visual hallucinations (well-formed), fluctuating cognition, REM sleep behavior disorder.

choices.text: PD dementia is always more severe
choices.feedback: Severity varies in both conditions.
LBD patients are HYPERSENSITIVE to antipsychotics — can cause severe NMS-like reactions. Avoid typical antipsychotics.

questions.stem: Why should levodopa NEVER be abruptly discontinued?

choices.feedback: That's a concern with clonidine withdrawal, not levodopa.

Correct! Abrupt levodopa withdrawal → acute akinesia, rigidity, fever, autonomic instability, elevated CK = parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia syndrome (NMS-like). Can be fatal. Always taper dopaminergic drugs gradually. This also applies to other dopamine agonists and amantadine.
Dopamine receptors recover; the concern is the acute withdrawal syndrome.
Seizures are associated with benzodiazepine/alcohol withdrawal, not levodopa.
