questions.stem: Impulse control disorders (gambling, hypersexuality) are a side effect of:

choices.feedback: Levodopa can contribute but these side effects are more strongly associated with another drug class.

Correct! Dopamine agonists stimulate D3 receptors in the mesolimbic reward pathway → impulse control disorders in 15-20%: pathological gambling, compulsive shopping, hypersexuality, binge eating. Screen for these side effects. May require dose reduction or switch to levodopa.
MAO-B inhibitors have different side effect profiles.
Anticholinergics cause cognitive side effects, not impulse control disorders.

questions.stem: Most common symptom of pulmonary embolism?

choices.feedback: Hemoptysis occurs in <20% — less common.

Correct! Sudden-onset dyspnea is #1 symptom (~80%). Tachycardia is the most common sign. PE should be suspected in any patient with unexplained dyspnea + risk factors. Classic triad (dyspnea + pleuritic chest pain + hemoptysis) occurs in only ~20%.
Pleuritic chest pain occurs in ~50% but dyspnea is more common.
Syncope suggests massive PE with RV failure but occurs in only ~10%.

questions.stem: When can D-dimer RULE OUT PE?

choices.feedback: D-dimer should NOT be used if high probability (proceed directly to CTPA).

choices.text: Low pre-test probability with negative D-dimer
choices.feedback: Correct! D-dimer has high sensitivity/NPV. Negative D-dimer in low-probability patient effectively excludes PE. Do NOT order D-dimer if high probability (too many false positives). Age-adjusted threshold (age × 10 for patients >50) improves specificity without reducing sensitivity.
D-dimer in high-probability patients wastes time and leads to unnecessary further testing.
D-dimer works across ages with appropriate interpretation.

questions.stem: Gold standard imaging for PE?

choices.feedback: V/Q is used when CTPA is contraindicated (contrast allergy, renal failure, pregnancy).

Correct! CTPA: sensitivity >95%, specificity >97%. Shows intraluminal filling defects. Also evaluates RV size (RV/LV ratio >0.9 = RV strain = submassive PE). V/Q scan is alternative when CTPA is contraindicated.
CXR is often normal or non-specific in PE. Hampton hump and Westermark sign are classic but uncommon.
Echo shows RV strain but cannot directly visualize thrombus in pulmonary arteries.

questions.stem: Massive PE with hemodynamic instability requires:

choices.feedback: Anticoagulation alone is insufficient for massive PE with hemodynamic collapse.

Correct! Massive PE (SBP <90 + RV dysfunction) = thrombolytics indicated despite bleeding risk. Alteplase 100 mg IV over 2h. Mortality >50% without intervention. If thrombolytics contraindicated: catheter-directed therapy or surgical embolectomy. Start heparin simultaneously.
IVC filter prevents future PE but doesn't treat current massive PE.
Massive PE requires aggressive intervention — observation risks cardiac arrest.

questions.stem: Most common inherited thrombophilia?

choices.feedback: Less common than Factor V Leiden.

Correct! Factor V Leiden = point mutation making Factor V resistant to activated protein C (natural anticoagulant). Present in ~5% of Caucasians. Heterozygous: 5-10× VTE risk. Homozygous: 50-100×. Most common inherited cause of hypercoagulability.
AT III deficiency is rare but causes heparin resistance.
Second most common inherited thrombophilia after FVL.

questions.stem: First-line anticoagulation for PE?

choices.feedback: Warfarin requires bridging with LMWH/heparin. DOACs are now preferred.

Correct! DOACs are first-line for non-massive PE. Rivaroxaban and apixaban can be started without heparin bridging (single-drug approach). Advantages: fixed dosing, no monitoring, fewer drug interactions. Exception: cancer-associated VTE (LMWH or edoxaban/rivaroxaban) and renal failure (warfarin).
Aspirin is for arterial thrombosis prevention, not VTE treatment.
UFH is for initial treatment of massive/submassive PE but not the definitive oral anticoagulant.

questions.stem: IVC filter is indicated ONLY when:

choices.text: Patient has recurrent PE on anticoagulation
choices.feedback: This requires anticoagulation intensification, not necessarily IVC filter.

choices.text: Anticoagulation is absolutely contraindicated with acute VTE
choices.feedback: Correct! IVC filter only if active hemorrhage or other absolute contraindication to anticoagulation AND patient has acute PE/DVT. Use retrievable filters — remove once anticoagulation can be started. IVC filters do NOT replace anticoagulation and increase DVT risk long-term.
Anticoagulation is standard first-line, not IVC filter.
Pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (LMWH/heparin) is standard, not IVC filter.

questions.stem: S1Q3T3 on EKG suggests:

choices.feedback: LVH shows tall R waves in lateral leads and deep S in V1-V2.

Correct! S1Q3T3: deep S wave in lead I, Q wave + inverted T in lead III. Classic but found in only ~20% of PE. Other EKG findings: sinus tachycardia (MOST COMMON), right axis deviation, RBBB, T-wave inversions V1-V4 (right ventricular strain pattern).
MI shows ST elevation/depression in coronary territories.
A-fib shows irregularly irregular rhythm without P waves.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old woman presents with unstable relationships ('I love my therapist' → 'she's the worst'), fear of abandonment, chronic emptiness, impulsive cutting, and identity confusion. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Bipolar II has distinct mood episodes (hypomania + depression) lasting days-weeks. BPD has rapid mood shifts (hours), identity disturbance, and splitting — these are different patterns.

Correct! Classic BPD features: unstable relationships with splitting (idealization/devaluation), fear of abandonment, chronic emptiness, impulsive self-harm, identity disturbance. 75% female. Defense mechanism = splitting. Treatment of choice = DBT (Dialectical Behavior Therapy).
HPD involves attention-seeking and exaggerated emotions but lacks the identity disturbance, self-harm, and splitting seen in BPD.
MDD can coexist with BPD but doesn't explain the splitting, identity disturbance, and relational instability pattern.

questions.stem: What is the gold standard treatment for borderline personality disorder?

choices.feedback: Medications are symptom-targeted adjuncts in BPD but no medication treats the core personality pathology. No approved medication exists for BPD.

Correct! DBT was developed specifically for BPD by Marsha Linehan. It combines CBT with mindfulness and teaches four skill modules: mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation, and interpersonal effectiveness. DBT reduces self-harm, suicide attempts, and hospitalization. It is the most evidence-based treatment for BPD.
Low-dose antipsychotics may help with transient stress-related psychotic symptoms in BPD but do not treat the core disorder.
Traditional psychoanalysis is generally not recommended for BPD and may worsen symptoms. Mentalization-based therapy (MBT) is a modified psychodynamic approach with evidence.

questions.stem: What distinguishes OCPD from OCD?

choices.feedback: OCD has obsessions and compulsions. OCPD has a pervasive pattern of perfectionism and control WITHOUT specific obsessions/compulsions.

choices.text: OCPD is ego-syntonic; OCD is ego-dystonic
choices.feedback: Correct! OCPD: the person sees their perfectionism, orderliness, and need for control as rational and desirable (ego-syntonic — 'I'm just thorough'). OCD: the person recognizes obsessions as intrusive, irrational, and distressing (ego-dystonic — 'I know this is crazy but I can't stop'). OCPD is a personality pattern; OCD is an anxiety-related disorder.

choices.text: OCD is more common than OCPD
choices.feedback: OCPD is actually the most common personality disorder (3-8% of population). OCD affects ~2-3%.

choices.text: They are the same condition with different names
choices.feedback: They are distinct diagnoses. Many patients with OCD do NOT have OCPD, and vice versa, though comorbidity can occur.

questions.stem: A patient can ONLY be diagnosed with antisocial personality disorder at what minimum age?

choices.feedback: Before 18, the diagnosis is conduct disorder, not ASPD.

Correct! ASPD requires the patient to be ≥18 years old AND have evidence of conduct disorder with onset before age 15 (lying, stealing, aggression, animal cruelty, truancy, property destruction). Below age 18, the diagnosis is conduct disorder. This age requirement exists because personality patterns must be demonstrated as enduring across adolescence into adulthood.
The minimum age for ASPD diagnosis is 18, not 21.
ASPD has a strict minimum age requirement of 18, with evidence of childhood conduct disorder.

questions.stem: How does avoidant personality disorder differ from schizoid personality disorder?

choices.text: Avoidant patients are indifferent to relationships
choices.feedback: This describes SCHIZOID PD. Avoidant patients desperately want relationships but fear rejection.

choices.text: Avoidant patients desire relationships but fear rejection; schizoid patients prefer isolation
choices.feedback: Correct! Key distinction: Avoidant PD = wants relationships but avoids due to fear of criticism and rejection (anxiety-driven). Schizoid PD = genuinely indifferent to social relationships, content being alone (not anxious about it). Avoidant PD overlaps significantly with social anxiety disorder and may respond to similar treatments (CBT + SSRIs).
Magical thinking and eccentric behavior characterize SCHIZOTYPAL PD, not schizoid PD.
The motivation behind social withdrawal is fundamentally different: desire with fear (avoidant) vs. genuine disinterest (schizoid).

questions.stem: 'Splitting' as a defense mechanism is most associated with which personality disorder?

choices.feedback: NPD's primary defense mechanisms are grandiosity and devaluation, though splitting can occur.

Correct! Splitting = viewing people and situations as all-good or all-bad with no middle ground. In BPD: 'My doctor is the best ever!' → (perceived slight) → 'She's incompetent and doesn't care!' This primitive defense mechanism drives the unstable relationships characteristic of BPD. Staff splitting (turning treatment team members against each other) is a common inpatient challenge.
HPD uses dramatic emotional expression and attention-seeking, not splitting.
Dependent PD's defense mechanism is submission and clinging, not splitting.

questions.stem: Which personality disorder cluster has a genetic link to schizophrenia?

choices.feedback: Cluster B (dramatic/erratic) is not genetically linked to schizophrenia.

Correct! Cluster A (odd/eccentric) — especially schizotypal PD — has the strongest genetic association with schizophrenia. Schizotypal PD is considered part of the 'schizophrenia spectrum.' First-degree relatives of schizophrenia patients have higher rates of schizotypal PD, and some schizotypal patients may progress to full schizophrenia.
Cluster C (anxious/fearful) is related to anxiety disorders, not schizophrenia.
The genetic link to schizophrenia is specific to Cluster A, particularly schizotypal PD.

questions.stem: Conduct disorder before age 15 is a prerequisite for which adult diagnosis?

choices.feedback: BPD does not require childhood conduct disorder, though childhood trauma is common in BPD patients.

Correct! ASPD requires: 1) Age ≥18, 2) Evidence of conduct disorder with onset before age 15, 3) Pattern of disregard for others' rights. Conduct disorder features: aggression toward people/animals, property destruction, deceitfulness/theft, rule violations. Without documented childhood conduct problems, ASPD cannot be diagnosed.
NPD does not require childhood conduct disorder.
Schizotypal PD has no specific childhood behavioral prerequisite.

questions.stem: Classic presentation of placental abruption:

choices.feedback: Painless bleeding = placenta previa, not abruption.

choices.text: Painful vaginal bleeding + rigid tender uterus + fetal distress
choices.feedback: Correct! Abruption triad: painful dark bleeding + board-like/tender uterus + fetal distress. 20% are concealed (no external bleeding). Cocaine use, trauma, and hypertension are key risk factors.
Watery discharge suggests PPROM, not abruption.
Itching in pregnancy suggests cholestasis of pregnancy.

questions.stem: Placental abruption vs placenta previa — key difference:

choices.text: Abruption is painless; previa is painful
choices.feedback: It's the OPPOSITE.

choices.text: Abruption: PAINFUL bleeding, tender uterus. Previa: PAINLESS bleeding, soft uterus
choices.feedback: Correct! This is the most tested distinction. Abruption = painful, dark blood, rigid uterus, fetal distress, high DIC risk. Previa = painless, bright red blood, soft uterus, fetus usually ok. Both require different management: abruption often needs emergent delivery; previa needs expectant if preterm/stable.
They have distinctly different presentations.

choices.text: Previa always requires cesarean; abruption never does
choices.feedback: Not absolute — previa usually requires CS but abruption may need CS for fetal distress.

questions.stem: Abruption diagnosis is primarily:

choices.feedback: US is only ~25% sensitive for abruption.

Correct! Abruption is a clinical diagnosis based on: painful bleeding, tender/rigid uterus, fetal distress. US may show retroplacental clot but MISSES most cases. Do not delay delivery waiting for US if clinical picture is clear. Normal US does NOT exclude abruption.
CT is not used for diagnosing abruption in pregnancy.

choices.text: Made only at time of delivery
choices.feedback: While confirmed at delivery, clinical diagnosis should be made before delivery to guide management.

questions.stem: DIC in abruption — most sensitive lab marker:

choices.feedback: Platelets drop in DIC but fibrinogen is more sensitive/earlier.

Correct! Fibrinogen is the most sensitive marker for DIC in abruption. Normal pregnancy fibrinogen is elevated (300-600 mg/dL), so a 'normal' level of 200 may actually indicate significant consumption. Fibrinogen <200 predicts severe hemorrhage. Replace with cryoprecipitate (each unit raises fibrinogen ~5-10 mg/dL).
D-dimer is elevated in normal pregnancy, making it less useful.
INR rises in DIC but later than fibrinogen drops.

questions.stem: Cocaine-associated abruption mechanism:

choices.feedback: While cocaine crosses the placenta, the abruption mechanism is vascular.

choices.text: Vasospasm → acute hypertension → shearing of placenta from uterine wall
choices.feedback: Correct! Cocaine inhibits norepinephrine reuptake → intense vasoconstriction → acute hypertension → shearing forces at placental-uterine interface → abruption. Always screen for cocaine in young women with unexpected abruption. Also increases risk of preterm labor, IUGR, and fetal distress.
Cocaine's mechanism is vascular, not immune-mediated.
Cocaine actually increases uterine contractility.

questions.stem: Couvelaire uterus refers to:

choices.text: Uterine rupture along prior cesarean scar
choices.feedback: Uterine rupture is a separate condition.

choices.text: Blood infiltrating the myometrium causing purple discoloration
choices.feedback: Correct! Couvelaire uterus: blood from abruption dissects into the myometrium → purple/blue discoloration of uterine surface (seen at cesarean delivery). May impair uterine contraction → contributes to PPH. Alone is NOT an indication for hysterectomy unless uterus fails to contract.
Uterine prolapse is a separate condition.
Uterine inversion is a different obstetric emergency.

questions.stem: Fetal demise from abruption — preferred delivery route:

choices.feedback: If the fetus is already deceased and mother has DIC, vaginal delivery is preferred.

choices.text: Vaginal delivery (avoid surgery in setting of DIC)
choices.feedback: Correct! When fetal demise has occurred, the priority is maternal safety. Vaginal delivery avoids surgical hemorrhage in the setting of DIC (coagulopathy makes surgery extremely high-risk). Correct coagulopathy aggressively (cryoprecipitate, FFP, platelets). CS only if maternal hemorrhage is life-threatening and uncontrolled.
Delivery is still necessary to stop the ongoing abruption process.
Hysterotomy is not standard management.

questions.stem: #1 risk factor for recurrent abruption:

choices.feedback: Smoking is a risk factor but not the #1 for recurrence.

Correct! History of prior abruption carries ~10% recurrence risk in subsequent pregnancies. These patients need close monitoring: serial growth US, fetal surveillance, early delivery planning. Modifiable risk factors: control HTN, stop cocaine/smoking.
Advanced age increases risk slightly but isn't the strongest predictor.
Fetal sex does not significantly affect abruption risk.

questions.stem: Painless bright red vaginal bleeding in the third trimester. Most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Abruption is PAINFUL with dark blood and uterine tenderness.

Correct! Painless, bright red bleeding in 2nd-3rd trimester = placenta previa until proven otherwise. Diagnose with ultrasound. NEVER do digital cervical exam. Most common risk factors: prior C-section, multiparity, smoking, prior previa.
Bloody show is a small amount of blood-tinged mucus, not significant bright red bleeding.
Cervical cancer can cause bleeding but is rare in pregnancy and not episodic bright red.

questions.stem: Painful bleeding with rigid 'board-like' uterus suggests:

choices.feedback: Previa = painless bleeding without uterine rigidity.

Correct! Abruption: painful bleeding + rigid/tender uterus + fetal distress. Severe (grade III): board-form uterus, fetal demise, DIC, maternal shock. #1 risk: HTN (chronic or preeclampsia). Cocaine use is also high-yield association. Can have concealed hemorrhage (retroplacental clot, no visible bleeding).
Atony causes postpartum hemorrhage with a soft, boggy uterus.
Braxton-Hicks don't cause bleeding or board-like rigidity.

questions.stem: Placenta accreta spectrum is most associated with:

choices.feedback: Accreta is extremely rare without prior uterine surgery.

Correct! Risk increases with each prior C-section: 1 prior C-section + previa = 3% accreta risk, 3 prior = 40%, 5+ prior = 67%. Abnormal placental invasion into myometrium. Diagnosis: MRI or US (loss of retroplacental clear space, placental lacunae). Management: planned cesarean hysterectomy at 34-36 wks at tertiary center.
Age alone doesn't significantly increase accreta risk without prior C-sections.
Twins increase previa risk but accreta specifically relates to prior uterine scarring.

questions.stem: Vasa previa rupture — key finding?

choices.feedback: Maternal pain/bradycardia is not the hallmark.

choices.text: Painless bleeding at ROM with sudden fetal bradycardia
choices.feedback: Correct! Vasa previa: fetal vessels cross internal os unprotected by placenta or cord. When membranes rupture → fetal vessel tears → fetal hemorrhage → sudden fetal bradycardia/death. Apt test distinguishes fetal (pink with NaOH) from maternal (brown) blood. If diagnosed prenatally → elective C-section at 34-36 weeks.
That's eclampsia, not vasa previa.
Vasa previa bleeding is from the fetus — it's an emergency.

questions.stem: Risk of uterine rupture is highest with:

choices.feedback: Low-transverse has ~0.5-1% rupture risk during TOLAC — lowest of C-section types.

Correct! Classical (vertical) uterine incision has 4-9% rupture risk — contraindication to trial of labor (TOLAC). Low-transverse: 0.5-1% risk → TOLAC is generally safe. Other rupture risks: excessive oxytocin, grand multiparity, uterine anomaly, prior myomectomy with cavity entry.
Nulliparous women without prior uterine surgery have negligible rupture risk.
D&C causes minimal uterine wall disruption.

questions.stem: Placenta previa management at 34 weeks with stable patient?

choices.feedback: Vaginal delivery is contraindicated with previa overlapping the os.

choices.text: Continue expectant management, planned cesarean at 36-37 weeks
choices.feedback: Correct! Stable previa: pelvic rest (no intercourse, no vaginal exams), antenatal corticosteroids if <37 wks, planned cesarean at 36-37 wks. Earlier delivery if: significant hemorrhage, unstable mother, or fetal distress. Low-lying placenta (edge >2 cm from os) may resolve and allow vaginal delivery.
At 34 weeks without active bleeding or instability, continue expectant management.
ECV is contraindicated with placenta previa.

questions.stem: Concealed abruption means:

choices.text: No visible vaginal bleeding despite placental separation
choices.feedback: Correct! Blood is trapped behind the placenta (retroplacental hematoma). No visible bleeding but patient has pain, uterine tenderness, fetal distress, and possibly DIC. Can be more dangerous than visible bleeding because diagnosis is delayed. Suspect when: painful rigid uterus + fetal distress + minimal visible bleeding.
That's placenta previa.
Concealed refers to the hemorrhage pattern during abruption, not incidental finding.
Concealed doesn't mean mild — it can be the most severe form.

questions.stem: Cocaine use in pregnancy increases risk of:

choices.feedback: Cocaine is not associated with GDM.

Correct! Cocaine causes intense vasoconstriction → acute hypertension → placental abruption. Also associated with: preterm labor, IUGR, spontaneous abortion. Always ask about substance use in any pregnant patient presenting with abruption, especially if young and previously healthy.
Previa is associated with prior C-sections and smoking, not cocaine.
Cocaine doesn't cause molar pregnancy.

questions.stem: Light criteria classify effusion as exudative if:

choices.feedback: Only ONE criterion needs to be met for exudate classification.

Correct! Exudate if ANY: pleural/serum protein >0.5, pleural/serum LDH >0.6, or pleural LDH > 2/3 upper limit of normal serum LDH. High sensitivity (98%) but may misclassify some transudates (especially CHF on diuretics). If borderline, check serum-pleural albumin gradient (>1.2 = transudate).
Bloody fluid suggests malignancy or trauma but doesn't define Light criteria.
Low glucose helps characterize exudates but isn't part of Light criteria.

questions.stem: Most common cause of pleural effusion overall?

choices.feedback: Malignancy is a common exudative cause but not #1 overall.

Correct! CHF is the most common cause of transudative effusion and the most common cause of pleural effusion overall. Typically bilateral (right > left). Responds to diuretics. Thoracentesis NOT needed if bilateral + known CHF + responding to treatment.
Parapneumonic effusion is the most common exudative cause but not #1 overall.
TB effusion is common worldwide but not #1 overall.

questions.stem: Milky pleural fluid with TG >110 mg/dL indicates:

choices.feedback: Empyema is purulent, not milky. Differentiated by TG levels.

Correct! Chylothorax = thoracic duct disruption → chyle leak into pleural space. Milky/opalescent fluid, TG >110 mg/dL, chylomicrons present. Causes: trauma/surgery (#1), malignancy (lymphoma), congenital. Treatment: NPO + TPN (reduces chyle production), octreotide, surgical ligation if refractory.
Malignant effusions are typically bloody or serosanguinous, not milky.
Hemothorax is bloody, not milky.

questions.stem: Parapneumonic effusion requires chest tube drainage when:

choices.feedback: Simple parapneumonic effusions resolve with antibiotics alone.

choices.text: pH <7.2, glucose <60, positive Gram stain, or pus (empyema)
choices.feedback: Correct! These indicate complicated parapneumonic effusion or empyema — will NOT resolve with antibiotics alone. Chest tube drainage is essential. Loculated effusions may need fibrinolytics (tPA + DNase) instilled through the chest tube, or VATS decortication for organized empyema.

choices.text: Only when the patient has fever
choices.feedback: Fever alone doesn't determine drainage need — fluid characteristics do.
Elevated protein defines exudate but doesn't specifically indicate drainage need.

questions.stem: Maximum volume to remove during therapeutic thoracentesis?

choices.feedback: Often need more than 500 mL for symptomatic relief.

Correct! Limit removal to 1-1.5L per session to prevent re-expansion pulmonary edema (sudden re-expansion of collapsed lung → increased capillary permeability → unilateral pulmonary edema). Symptoms: cough, chest tightness, dyspnea during procedure. Stop if patient develops symptoms.
Excessive drainage risks re-expansion pulmonary edema.
100 mL may be sufficient for diagnostic but not therapeutic thoracentesis.

questions.stem: Bilateral pleural effusions in a CHF patient responding to diuretics. Next step?

choices.feedback: Thoracentesis is unnecessary if the clinical picture is clear and patient is improving.

Correct! Bilateral effusions in known CHF responding to diuresis = clinical diagnosis. Thoracentesis NOT indicated. Tap if: unilateral, febrile, not responding to diuretics, atypical presentation, concern for alternative diagnosis. Unnecessary thoracentesis exposes patient to procedural risk.
Imaging may be helpful but thoracentesis decision is the key question.
No infection concern in typical bilateral CHF effusions.

questions.stem: Pleural fluid cytology is most useful for diagnosing:

choices.feedback: Parapneumonic is diagnosed by pH, glucose, Gram stain, and culture.

Correct! Cytology detects malignant cells in pleural fluid. Sensitivity ~60% on first thoracentesis (increases to ~75% with repeat). Most common causes: lung cancer (#1), breast cancer, lymphoma. If cytology negative but malignancy suspected: pleural biopsy (CT-guided or thoracoscopic).
CHF effusions are diagnosed clinically and by Light criteria (transudate).
Chylothorax is diagnosed by milky appearance and elevated triglycerides.

questions.stem: Costophrenic angle blunting on CXR requires approximately how much fluid?

choices.feedback: 50 mL is typically undetectable on standard upright CXR.

Correct! Upright CXR: >200 mL needed for costophrenic blunting. Lateral decubitus: can detect as little as 50-100 mL (fluid layers dependently). US is most sensitive for detecting small effusions and guiding thoracentesis.
1 liter would cause significant opacification beyond just blunting.
2 liters would cause near-complete hemithorax opacification.

questions.stem: Most common pathogen in community-acquired pneumonia?

choices.feedback: Mycoplasma is common in young adults but not the overall #1.

Correct! S. pneumoniae (pneumococcus) is the #1 cause of CAP in all age groups. Presents with acute onset, productive cough (rusty sputum), consolidation on CXR. Urine antigen test available. Vaccination: PCV20 or PCV15 + PPSV23.
S. aureus is more common in post-influenza pneumonia and HAP/VAP (MRSA).
Pseudomonas is nosocomial — HAP/VAP and CF/bronchiectasis patients.

questions.stem: Aspiration pneumonia most commonly affects which lobe?

choices.feedback: LUL aspiration is uncommon due to the angle of the left main bronchus.

Correct! The right main bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertical than the left, making the right lower lobe the most common site for aspiration. If supine: posterior segment of right upper lobe or superior segment of right lower lobe.
Less common than right lower lobe aspiration.
Can be affected but RLL is most common.

questions.stem: CURB-65 score of 3 in a pneumonia patient suggests:

choices.feedback: Score 3-5 indicates severe disease requiring hospitalization.
Correct! CURB-65 ≥3: consider ICU. Score 0-1: outpatient. Score 2: short inpatient stay or closely supervised outpatient. Each point = 1 of: Confusion, Uremia (BUN>20), RR≥30, low BP, age ≥65.

choices.text: No antibiotics needed, viral etiology likely
choices.feedback: CURB-65 assesses severity, not etiology. Score 3 needs aggressive treatment.
Score 3 is too high for discharge.

questions.stem: PCP pneumonia prophylaxis is indicated when CD4 count is:

choices.feedback: Below 500: some OIs become risks, but PCP prophylaxis threshold is lower.

Correct! PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX when CD4 <200 (or CD4% <14%, or history of oropharyngeal candidiasis). TMP-SMX also provides toxoplasma prophylaxis. Alternatives: dapsone, atovaquone, aerosolized pentamidine.
<50 is the threshold for MAC prophylaxis (azithromycin), not PCP.
<350 is the threshold for starting ART (though current guidelines recommend ART for all HIV+).

questions.stem: CAP in alcoholic patient: upper lobe cavitation, currant jelly sputum. Pathogen?

choices.feedback: Pneumococcus rarely causes cavitation or currant jelly sputum.

Correct! Klebsiella: classic in alcoholics and diabetics. Upper lobe cavitary pneumonia with thick, bloody 'currant jelly' sputum. Gram-negative rod with large mucoid capsule. Bulging fissure sign on CXR (heavy inflammatory exudate expands the lobe).
Mycoplasma causes atypical walking pneumonia in young adults.
Legionella: contaminated water exposure, GI symptoms, hyponatremia.

questions.stem: Legionella pneumonia clue on labs?

choices.feedback: Eosinophilia suggests parasitic or allergic disease.

Correct! Legionella associations: contaminated water (cooling towers, hot tubs), elderly/immunocompromised, GI symptoms (diarrhea, nausea), high fever, hyponatremia (SIADH). Urine antigen test detects serogroup 1 only. Treatment: fluoroquinolone or macrolide.
Not associated with Legionella.
Not a feature of Legionella.

questions.stem: HAP/VAP empiric antibiotics must cover:

choices.feedback: HAP/VAP pathogens are nosocomial — pneumococcus is less relevant.

Correct! HAP/VAP: anti-pseudomonal coverage (pip-tazo, cefepime, meropenem, or aztreonam) + MRSA coverage (vancomycin or linezolid). De-escalate based on cultures. Risk factors for MDR: prior IV antibiotics within 90 days, hospitalization ≥5 days, high MDR prevalence.
Anaerobic coverage is more important for aspiration pneumonia.
Atypicals are community pathogens, not typical HAP/VAP organisms.

questions.stem: PCP treatment includes steroids when:

choices.feedback: Steroids only when moderate-severe hypoxemia is present.

Correct! Adjunctive prednisone reduces mortality in moderate-severe PCP. Start within 72h of antibiotics. Taper over 21 days. Treatment: high-dose TMP-SMX × 21 days. CXR: bilateral diffuse ground-glass opacities. Elevated LDH supports diagnosis. Beta-glucan often elevated.
Steroids are based on oxygenation thresholds, not intubation status.
Steroids improve outcomes in moderate-severe PCP.

questions.stem: What is the embryologic basis for postaxial polydactyly?

choices.text: Excessive SHH signaling from the ZPA
choices.feedback: Correct! Postaxial (ulnar/fibular side) polydactyly involves the posterior (SHH-producing) side of the limb. Excessive or expanded SHH signaling can cause duplication of posterior (pinky-side) structures. Isolated postaxial polydactyly Type B is the most common congenital hand anomaly in Black populations (~1/300) and is inherited as autosomal dominant with variable penetrance.
Failure of apoptosis causes SYNDACTYLY (fused digits), not polydactyly. Polydactyly involves extra digit formation, not failed separation.
AER duplication would cause limb duplication more broadly, not just an extra digit. The AER controls proximal-distal growth, not digit number.
Neural crest cells contribute to the face and heart, not limb digit formation. Limb mesenchyme comes from lateral plate mesoderm.

questions.stem: What molecular signal triggers interdigital apoptosis?

choices.feedback: SHH patterns the anterior-posterior axis (which digit is which) but doesn't directly trigger interdigital apoptosis.

Correct! BMPs (especially BMP2, BMP4, BMP7) in the interdigital mesenchyme trigger apoptosis. This is why BMP signaling inhibition → failed apoptosis → syndactyly. Retinoic acid also promotes interdigital apoptosis. FGFs from the AER INHIBIT apoptosis (keeping digits alive while the interdigital tissue dies).
Wnt7a is involved in dorsal-ventral patterning (extensor vs flexor) but not interdigital apoptosis.
Notch is important in many developmental contexts but is not the primary driver of interdigital apoptosis.

questions.stem: What distinguishes simple from complex syndactyly?

choices.text: Simple = skin/soft tissue only; Complex = bone fusion
choices.feedback: Correct! SIMPLE syndactyly = skin and soft tissue connection only, no bony fusion. COMPLEX syndactyly = shared or fused bones, often with abnormal nails. Additionally: COMPLETE syndactyly = fusion extends to fingertip; INCOMPLETE = fusion doesn't reach the tip. Complex syndactyly is more likely to be syndromic (Apert, Poland).
Laterality doesn't define simple vs complex. The classification is based on whether bone is involved.
The number of digits involved doesn't determine simple vs complex. Even two-digit syndactyly can be complex if bones are fused.
Both forms can be familial or sporadic. The classification is anatomical (skin vs bone involvement).

questions.stem: Which syndrome should be considered with radial-side limb anomalies + cardiac defect?

choices.feedback: Correct! Holt-Oram syndrome = radial ray defects (thumb anomalies, absent radius, preaxial polydactyly) + congenital heart defects (ASD most common, VSD, conduction defects). Caused by TBX5 mutations (autosomal dominant). TBX5 is a transcription factor critical for both upper limb and cardiac development.
T21 has a single transverse palmar crease and AVSD, but not preaxial polydactyly or radial ray defects.
Turner has short 4th metacarpals and coarctation of aorta, not preaxial polydactyly.
Marfan has arachnodactyly (long thin fingers), not polydactyly, and mitral valve prolapse/aortic root dilation.

questions.stem: Why does thumb duplication require more complex surgery than postaxial nubbin removal?

choices.text: The thumb is critical for grip; reconstruction must optimize function
choices.feedback: Correct! Thumb function (opposition, pinch grip) is critical for hand function. Simple amputation of one thumb often leaves a suboptimal result. The Bilhaut-Cloquet procedure combines elements from both duplicated thumbs (bone, nail, soft tissue) to create one well-aligned, stable, functional thumb. Postaxial nubbin removal is simple because the extra digit has no functional importance.

choices.text: The radial artery is always involved
choices.feedback: While vascular anatomy is considered, the primary reason is functional importance of the thumb, not vascular complexity.

choices.text: Preaxial digits have more nerve fibers
choices.feedback: While sensation matters, the primary reason for complex reconstruction is the functional importance of the thumb for hand use.

choices.text: The growth plate is always shared
choices.feedback: The growth plates may be separate or shared depending on the type of duplication. The complexity is driven by functional reconstruction needs.

questions.stem: #1 cause of postpartum hemorrhage?

choices.feedback: Lacerations account for ~20% — trauma is #2.

Correct! Uterine atony (failure to contract after delivery) accounts for 70-80% of PPH. Boggy, soft uterus on exam. Risk factors: uterine overdistension, prolonged labor, chorioamnionitis, MgSO4 use. First treatment: bimanual uterine massage + oxytocin.
Retained tissue accounts for ~10%.
Coagulopathy accounts for ~1% of PPH.

questions.stem: Carboprost (hemabate) is CONTRAINDICATED in:

choices.feedback: Methylergonovine is contraindicated in HTN. Carboprost is safe in HTN.

Correct! Carboprost (15-methyl PGF2α) causes bronchospasm — contraindicated in asthma. Other side effects: diarrhea, fever, vomiting. Methylergonovine (ergot alkaloid) is contraindicated in hypertension (vasoconstriction). Know the contraindication pairs: carboprost = asthma, methylergonovine = HTN.
Diabetes is not a contraindication to carboprost.
Accreta requires surgical management, but carboprost isn't specifically contraindicated.

questions.stem: TXA for PPH should be given within:

choices.feedback: Benefit diminishes significantly after 3 hours.

Correct! WOMAN trial: TXA given within 3 hours reduces death from PPH. 1g IV over 10 min, repeat at 30 min if still bleeding. No benefit if given >3h after onset. TXA is an antifibrinolytic (inhibits plasminogen activation → stabilizes clots). Now a standard part of PPH management protocols.
TXA is for acute PPH management, not delayed use.

choices.text: Only after all other uterotonics fail
choices.feedback: TXA should be given early alongside uterotonics, not as last resort.

questions.stem: Placenta accreta spectrum — highest risk with:

choices.feedback: First pregnancy has very low accreta risk.

Correct! The combination of prior CS (scarred lower segment) + placenta previa (placenta over the scar) creates the highest risk. Risk increases with number of prior CS: 1 prior CS + previa = 3%, 2 prior = 11%, 3 prior = 40%, 4+ prior = 61%. Planned cesarean hysterectomy with multidisciplinary team is standard of care.
Multiparity alone without prior uterine surgery has minimal accreta risk.
GDM is not a risk factor for placenta accreta.

questions.stem: Bakri balloon is used for:

choices.feedback: Bakri is a tamponade device, not a dilator.

choices.text: Intrauterine tamponade in PPH unresponsive to uterotonics
choices.feedback: Correct! Bakri balloon: inserted into uterus, inflated with 250-500 mL saline → direct pressure on uterine wall → controls bleeding. Used when uterotonics + massage fail. Effective in >80% of cases. Bridge to surgery if needed. Remove after 12-24h. Alternative: Ebb balloon, condom catheter.
Different balloons (Foley/Cook) are used for cervical ripening.
IUDs are for contraception, not Bakri balloons.

questions.stem: Placenta percreta invades:

choices.feedback: Endometrial attachment without invasion = normal placentation.

choices.text: Through the myometrium to the serosa (and possibly into bladder)
choices.feedback: Correct! Percreta = most severe: placenta invades completely through the myometrium to the serosal surface. Can invade into adjacent organs (bladder most common). Accreta = superficial attachment. Increta = into myometrium. Percreta = through myometrium. Mnemonic: A/I/P = Attach/Into/Permeate.
Into myometrium = increta.
Decidual attachment is normal.

questions.stem: B-Lynch suture is a:

choices.feedback: Episiotomy repair uses standard suture techniques.

Correct! B-Lynch is a surgical compression suture that mechanically compresses the uterus to control hemorrhage. Used when conservative measures (uterotonics, tamponade) fail but before hysterectomy. Uterine-sparing procedure. Other surgical options: uterine artery ligation, internal iliac artery ligation.
Cesarean closure uses standard layered closure.
B-Lynch is a surgical procedure, not a device.

questions.stem: First uterotonic for PPH?

choices.feedback: Carboprost is second/third-line after oxytocin.

Correct! Oxytocin is first-line uterotonic for both PPH treatment and prevention (10-40 units IV infusion). Acts on oxytocin receptors → myometrial contraction. If oxytocin fails → methylergonovine (avoid in HTN), carboprost (avoid in asthma), misoprostol (rectally — universal availability). Step up as needed.
Misoprostol is used when oxytocin is unavailable or as adjunct.
Methylergonovine is second-line after oxytocin.

questions.stem: Preeclampsia is defined as hypertension after 20 weeks PLUS:

choices.feedback: Edema is common in pregnancy and no longer part of the diagnostic criteria.

Correct! Proteinuria is classic but NOT required if end-organ damage present (thrombocytopenia <100K, elevated Cr >1.1, elevated LFTs, pulmonary edema, cerebral/visual symptoms). The 2013 ACOG task force updated criteria to not require proteinuria.
Seizures = eclampsia, a progression beyond preeclampsia.
GDM and preeclampsia are separate conditions.

questions.stem: First-line treatment for eclamptic seizures?

choices.feedback: Benzodiazepines are NOT first-line for eclamptic seizures.

Correct! MgSO4 is first-line for both treatment and prophylaxis of eclamptic seizures (MAGPIE trial). Superior to diazepam (Eclampsia Trial Collaborative) and phenytoin. Loading 4-6g IV → maintenance 1-2g/hr. Also has tocolytic and neuroprotective effects on fetus.
Phenytoin is inferior to MgSO4 for eclampsia.
Lorazepam is first-line for status epilepticus but NOT for eclampsia.

questions.stem: HELLP syndrome is characterized by:

choices.feedback: That's not what HELLP stands for.

choices.text: Hemolysis, Elevated Liver enzymes, Low Platelets
choices.feedback: Correct! HELLP: microangiopathic hemolysis (schistocytes, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin), elevated AST/ALT, thrombocytopenia. Can occur without severe HTN. RUQ pain from hepatic capsule distension. Risk of hepatic rupture, DIC, placental abruption. Definitive treatment: delivery regardless of gestational age.

choices.text: Heart failure, Eclampsia, Liver failure, Low Potassium
choices.feedback: Incorrect expansion of the HELLP acronym.

choices.text: Hematemesis, Edema, Lung disease, Low Pressure
choices.feedback: Not related to HELLP.

questions.stem: Antidote for magnesium toxicity?

choices.feedback: Bicarb is for TCA overdose and salicylate poisoning.

Correct! Calcium gluconate antagonizes magnesium at the cardiac membrane. Give 10 mL of 10% calcium gluconate IV over 3 min. Signs of Mg toxicity (in order): loss of DTRs (first) → respiratory depression → cardiac arrest. Monitor: reflexes, RR >12, UOP >30 mL/hr.
Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines, not magnesium.
Naloxone reverses opioids.

questions.stem: Low-dose aspirin for preeclampsia prevention should start at:

choices.feedback: Evidence supports starting at 12-16 weeks, not earlier.

Correct! ACOG recommends low-dose aspirin (81 mg daily) starting at 12-16 weeks (before 16 weeks for optimal benefit) in patients with high-risk factors. Reduces preeclampsia risk by ~17%. High-risk: prior preeclampsia, multifetal pregnancy, chronic HTN, DM, renal disease, autoimmune disease.
Too late — placental development occurs in first/second trimesters.
Aspirin is for PREVENTION, not treatment of established preeclampsia.

questions.stem: Definitive treatment for preeclampsia?

choices.feedback: MgSO4 prevents seizures but doesn't cure preeclampsia.

choices.text: Delivery of the fetus and placenta
choices.feedback: Correct! Preeclampsia is a placental disease — removing the placenta is curative. Timing depends on severity and gestational age. Mild <37 wks: expectant management, deliver at 37 wks. Severe ≥34 wks: deliver after stabilization. Severe <34 wks: corticosteroids for fetal lungs → deliver at 34 wks or sooner if worsening.
Bed rest is no longer recommended as treatment.
Antihypertensives manage BP but don't treat the underlying disease.

questions.stem: BP goal when treating severe preeclampsia?

choices.feedback: Aggressive normalization can compromise uteroplacental perfusion.

Correct! Target <160/110 to prevent maternal stroke while maintaining adequate uteroplacental blood flow. Agents: IV labetalol (first-line), IV hydralazine, PO nifedipine (immediate-release). Do NOT use ACEi/ARBs (teratogenic), nitroprusside (fetal cyanide), or atenolol (IUGR).
Too aggressive — risks fetal compromise.

choices.text: No treatment needed — hypertension is expected in pregnancy
choices.feedback: Severe HTN (≥160/110) requires treatment to prevent maternal stroke and organ damage.

questions.stem: Preeclampsia occurring BEFORE 20 weeks suggests:

choices.feedback: Preeclampsia by definition occurs after 20 weeks.

Correct! Classic preeclampsia occurs after 20 weeks. If preeclampsia-like features before 20 weeks, suspect molar pregnancy (complete hydatidiform mole). Also: very elevated β-hCG ('snowstorm' on US, uterus larger than dates, no fetal parts). Treatment: suction evacuation + monitor β-hCG for GTN.
GDM doesn't present with hypertension/proteinuria before 20 weeks.
Twins increase preeclampsia risk but still present after 20 weeks.

questions.stem: Post-op fever on day 1 is MOST commonly caused by:

choices.feedback: SSI occurs POD 5-7, not day 1.
Correct! Atelectasis is #1 cause of fever in first 24-48h post-op. Collapsed alveoli → cytokine release → fever. Treatment: incentive spirometry, ambulation.
DVT/PE presents POD 5-7 or later.
C. diff develops POD 7+ after antibiotic exposure.

questions.stem: A patient on chronic prednisone 20 mg daily needs surgery. What perioperative steroid management?

choices.feedback: Abrupt stop risks adrenal crisis from HPA suppression.
Correct! Chronic steroids (>5 mg/day for ≥3 weeks) suppress HPA axis. Stress dose: hydrocortisone 100 mg IV at induction, then 50 mg q8h × 24-48h, taper to home dose. Prevents adrenal crisis.
Home dose may be insufficient for surgical stress demand.
Inappropriately high dose. Standard stress dosing uses hydrocortisone.

questions.stem: Prophylactic antibiotics should be given:

choices.feedback: Too early — not at therapeutic levels at incision time.
Correct! Within 60 min (120 for vancomycin). Ensures adequate tissue levels during procedure. Redose q4h for long cases. Discontinue within 24h post-op.
Post-incision antibiotics miss the contamination prevention window.
That's treatment, not prophylaxis.

questions.stem: Warfarin should be held how many days before elective surgery?

choices.feedback: Half-life is 36-42h. One day insufficient to normalize INR.
Correct! Hold 5 days for INR <1.5. Bridge with LMWH/UFH for high-risk patients (mechanical valve, recent VTE). Check INR day before surgery.
Excessively long, increases VTE risk without benefit.
Appropriate for some DOACs, not warfarin.

questions.stem: POD 6: fever, tachycardia, distension after colonic anastomosis. CT shows contrast extravasation. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Ileus causes distension but not fever/tachycardia/contrast leak.
Correct! POD 5-7 with fever + tachycardia + peritonitis + contrast extravasation = anastomotic leak. Surgical emergency → OR for washout ± diversion. Mortality 6-22%.
C. diff causes watery diarrhea, not contrast extravasation on CT.
Dehiscence is superficial/external, not intra-abdominal.

questions.stem: The 4-2-1 rule for a 70 kg adult calculates to:

choices.feedback: That's 1 mL/kg/hr — too low.
Correct! First 10 kg × 4 = 40. Next 10 kg × 2 = 20. Remaining 50 kg × 1 = 50. Total = 110 mL/hr.
Exceeds maintenance; approaches resuscitation rates.
Only the first-10-kg contribution, not the total.

questions.stem: Transfusion threshold for Hb in most stable post-op patients?

choices.feedback: Liberal strategy — shown no better than restrictive in most patients.
Correct! Restrictive strategy (Hb <7) recommended for stable patients (TRICC, TRACS trials). Exceptions: active ACS, symptomatic anemia (threshold ~8-9).
Inappropriate — increases transfusion complications without benefit.
Dangerously low to wait; standard is 7.

questions.stem: NPO guideline for clear liquids before elective surgery?

choices.feedback: Outdated for clear liquids.
Correct! Current ASA: clear liquids up to 2h before surgery. Light meal: 6h. Full meal: 8h. Liberal policy improves comfort without aspiration risk increase.
That's for a light meal, not clear liquids.
Unnecessarily restrictive.

questions.stem: A patient on an MAOI eats aged cheese and develops severe headache, hypertension (BP 220/130), and diaphoresis. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Serotonin syndrome involves neuromuscular hyperactivity (clonus, hyperreflexia) and is triggered by serotonergic drugs, not tyramine-containing foods.

Correct! MAOIs inhibit intestinal and hepatic MAO → inability to metabolize tyramine from aged foods → massive norepinephrine release → hypertensive crisis. Aged cheese, cured meats, red wine, fermented foods, and tap/draft beer are the main culprits. Treatment: phentolamine (alpha-blocker) or nitroprusside for acute hypertension.
While the presentation resembles pheo, the context (MAOI + aged cheese) makes tyramine crisis the clear diagnosis.
NMS is caused by antipsychotics and features rigidity + fever, not specifically triggered by dietary tyramine.

questions.stem: What is the treatment for TCA overdose with QRS widening?

choices.feedback: Charcoal may help if given early (within 1-2 hours) but does not address the cardiotoxicity.

Correct! Sodium bicarbonate is the specific antidote for TCA cardiotoxicity. Mechanism: 1) Sodium loading overcomes sodium channel blockade → narrows QRS. 2) Alkalinization reduces free drug binding to sodium channels. Target blood pH 7.45-7.55. Also: IV benzos for seizures, intralipid for refractory cases. QRS >100 ms = seizure risk; >160 ms = arrhythmia risk.
Flumazenil reverses benzodiazepine overdose, not TCA overdose. It is contraindicated in TCA ingestion (may precipitate seizures).
Magnesium treats torsades de pointes but sodium bicarbonate is the specific treatment for TCA-induced QRS widening.

questions.stem: Which antidepressant causes priapism?

choices.feedback: SSRIs cause sexual dysfunction (decreased libido, anorgasmia) but not priapism.

Correct! Trazodone (5-HT2A antagonist/reuptake inhibitor) is associated with priapism — a prolonged, painful erection that constitutes a urologic emergency. Risk is ~1 in 10,000 but patients should be counseled. Trazodone is primarily used off-label for insomnia at low doses (25-100 mg) and for depression at higher doses (150-400 mg).
Bupropion is notable for having no sexual side effects (and no weight gain), making it attractive for patients with SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction.
Mirtazapine causes sedation and weight gain but is not associated with priapism.

questions.stem: Acute dystonia from an antipsychotic should be treated with:

choices.feedback: Propranolol treats akathisia, not acute dystonia.

Correct! Acute dystonia (torticollis, oculogyric crisis, jaw dystonia, potentially life-threatening laryngospasm) is treated with anticholinergic agents: benztropine (Cogentin) or diphenhydramine (Benadryl) IM/IV. Response is rapid (minutes). Most common in young males started on high-potency typical antipsychotics.
Dantrolene is for NMS and malignant hyperthermia, not simple acute dystonia.
Valbenazine (VMAT2 inhibitor) is for tardive dyskinesia (a late EPS), not acute dystonia.

questions.stem: What is the MAOI washout period required BEFORE starting an SSRI?

choices.feedback: 48 hours is far too short. MAO regeneration takes approximately 2 weeks.

Correct! A 2-week washout is required after stopping an MAOI before starting any serotonergic drug (SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs, tramadol). This allows new MAO enzyme to be synthesized. Combining MAOIs with serotonergic drugs without adequate washout risks fatal serotonin syndrome. Special case: fluoxetine to MAOI requires 5 weeks due to its long half-life.
24 hours is insufficient for MAO regeneration.
6 months is excessively long. The standard MAOI washout is 2 weeks.

questions.stem: Tardive dyskinesia is treated with which class of medications?

choices.feedback: Anticholinergics treat acute EPS (dystonia, parkinsonism) but can WORSEN tardive dyskinesia.

Correct! VMAT2 (vesicular monoamine transporter 2) inhibitors are indicated for tardive dyskinesia. They reduce dopamine release in the striatum. Valbenazine and deutetrabenazine are first-line. TD develops after months-years of antipsychotic use and may be irreversible. Also important: switch to a lower-risk antipsychotic (quetiapine, clozapine).
SSRIs do not treat movement disorders like tardive dyskinesia.
Dopamine agonists would be counterproductive — TD is related to dopamine receptor supersensitivity from chronic D2 blockade.

questions.stem: Which SSRI has the WORST discontinuation syndrome?

choices.feedback: Fluoxetine has the LONGEST half-life (4-6 days including active metabolite norfluoxetine), giving it the BEST discontinuation profile — self-tapering effect.

Correct! Paroxetine has the shortest half-life among SSRIs (~21 hours) AND is the most anticholinergic, leading to the worst discontinuation syndrome (FINISH: flu-like, insomnia, nausea, imbalance, sensory disturbances/brain zaps, hyperarousal). Venlafaxine (SNRI) is similarly problematic. Always taper gradually.
Sertraline has an intermediate half-life and moderate discontinuation risk — less than paroxetine.
Escitalopram has a moderate half-life and relatively smooth discontinuation compared to paroxetine.

questions.stem: Serotonin syndrome vs NMS — how do you distinguish them clinically?

choices.feedback: While they share features (fever, altered mental status), key differences in muscle findings and causative agents distinguish them.

choices.text: SS has clonus/hyperreflexia; NMS has lead-pipe rigidity
choices.feedback: Correct! Key distinctions: Serotonin syndrome: rapid onset (hours), CLONUS (especially lower extremity), hyperreflexia, tremor, mydriasis, diarrhea. Caused by serotonergic drugs. NMS: slower onset (days-weeks), LEAD-PIPE RIGIDITY, bradyreflexia, massively elevated CK (>1000), caused by antipsychotics. SS treatment: cyproheptadine. NMS treatment: dantrolene + bromocriptine.
SS can occur with any combination of serotonergic drugs (SSRIs + tramadol, SSRIs + triptans, SSRIs + linezolid), not just MAOIs.

choices.text: NMS only occurs with typical antipsychotics
choices.feedback: NMS can occur with both typical AND atypical antipsychotics, though typical high-potency agents carry higher risk.

questions.stem: What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Correct! Intralobar sequestration (ILS) = non-functioning lung tissue within the visceral pleura that receives systemic arterial supply (usually from the aorta) and has NO bronchial connection. Recurrent infections in the same lobe are the classic presentation because secretions pool with no drainage.
Extralobar sequestration has its OWN pleural covering (separate from the lung) and typically presents in infancy, not with recurrent pneumonia.
CPAM communicates with the bronchial tree and receives pulmonary (not systemic) arterial supply. The key finding here is the anomalous aortic supply.
Bronchogenic cysts are fluid-filled and don't typically cause recurrent pneumonia with a solid mass and systemic arterial supply.

questions.stem: Why does sequestered lung maintain systemic arterial supply instead of developing pulmonary arterial supply?

choices.feedback: While molecular signals play a role, the primary explanation is anatomical — the tissue buds off before pulmonary vasculature develops.

choices.text: It separates before pulmonary arteries develop and retains primitive aortic supply
choices.feedback: Correct! The accessory lung bud separates from the foregut early, before the pulmonary vascular plexus incorporates it. It retains its primitive connection to the dorsal aorta (which supplies all embryonic tissues initially). The normal lung acquires pulmonary arterial supply as the pulmonary vasculature develops — the sequestration misses this transition.
The ductus arteriosus is unrelated to sequestration blood supply. The supply comes directly from the aorta via embryonic segmental arteries.

choices.text: Inflammation redirects blood supply after birth
choices.feedback: The systemic supply is congenital, not acquired. It's present from embryologic development.

questions.stem: Which type is more commonly associated with other congenital anomalies?

choices.feedback: Intralobar sequestration is usually ISOLATED with few associated anomalies (only ~15%). It's the extralobar type that has more associations.

Correct! Extralobar sequestration has associated anomalies in 50-65% of cases: congenital diaphragmatic hernia (most common — the sequestration may be below the diaphragm), cardiac defects, foregut duplications, and vertebral anomalies. Intralobar is usually isolated. Mnemonic: 'Extra-lobar has EXTRA problems.'
The rates are quite different — extralobar has 3-4× higher association rate than intralobar.
Extralobar sequestrations have a very high rate of associated congenital anomalies.

questions.stem: Why is identifying the systemic arterial supply the most critical surgical step?

choices.text: Massive hemorrhage if the aortic branch retracts unligated
choices.feedback: Correct! The aberrant artery comes directly from the aorta (systemic pressure). If cut without ligation, it retracts into the mediastinum/retroperitoneum where hemostasis is extremely difficult → potentially fatal hemorrhage. Preoperative CTA to map the feeding vessel is essential. Some sequestrations have multiple feeding arteries.

choices.text: The artery must be preserved for collateral circulation
choices.feedback: The artery supplies only the sequestered tissue, which is being removed. It serves no purpose for the remaining normal lung.

choices.text: It contains the pathologic tissue that caused infections
choices.feedback: The infections are caused by the sequestered lung tissue, not the vessel itself. The vessel is a surgical hazard, not the disease focus.
AV fistula is not a significant risk. The danger is uncontrolled bleeding from a high-pressure aortic branch.

Correct! CPAM (formerly CCAM) = hamartomatous overgrowth of terminal bronchioles forming cysts. Key differences from sequestration: communicates with bronchial tree, receives PULMONARY artery supply, and represents abnormal branching morphogenesis rather than an accessory lung bud.
Sequestration receives SYSTEMIC (aortic) blood supply and does NOT communicate with the bronchial tree. This lesion has pulmonary supply and bronchial communication.
CLE causes hyperinflation of a lobe (not cystic), and is typically diagnosed postnatally when the lobe fails to deflate properly.
Bronchogenic cysts are typically single, fluid-filled, and mediastinal. They don't usually appear as multicystic parenchymal lesions.

questions.stem: Most important treatment for rhabdomyolysis?

choices.feedback: Dialysis is reserved for complications (refractory hyperkalemia, severe AKI).

Correct! IV NS 1-1.5 L/hr initially, targeting UOP 200-300 mL/hr. Fluids dilute myoglobin, maintain renal perfusion, and prevent tubular precipitation. This is the single most important intervention to prevent AKI. Start immediately upon diagnosis.
Calcium is generally AVOIDED in rhabdomyolysis (deposits in damaged muscle).
Stopping the offending agent is important but IV fluids are the priority.

questions.stem: Urinalysis shows blood on dipstick but NO red blood cells on microscopy. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: True hematuria would show RBCs on microscopy.

Correct! Myoglobin cross-reacts with the heme-detecting dipstick reagent (peroxidase activity), giving a false-positive for 'blood.' Microscopy confirms no RBCs. Cola/tea-colored urine + dipstick positive for blood + no RBCs = myoglobinuria. Check CK to confirm rhabdomyolysis.
UTI would show WBCs and bacteria, not isolated positive blood dipstick.
Positive blood on dipstick without RBCs is abnormal and suggests myoglobinuria or hemoglobinuria.

questions.stem: Why should calcium NOT be supplemented in rhabdomyolysis despite low levels?

choices.feedback: Calcium doesn't directly worsen renal function in this context.

choices.text: Exogenous calcium deposits in damaged muscle and worsens injury
choices.feedback: Correct! Hypocalcemia is EXPECTED in rhabdomyolysis (calcium deposits in damaged muscle). Giving IV calcium increases heterotopic calcification in injured tissue. Only supplement if: symptomatic hypocalcemia (tetany, seizures, Chvostek/Trousseau signs) or hyperkalemia with EKG changes requiring calcium for cardiac membrane stabilization. Calcium often rebounds HIGH during recovery phase.
The concern is deposition in damaged muscle, not myoglobin clearance.
Hypocalcemia is common and expected.

questions.stem: Statin-induced rhabdomyolysis risk is increased when combined with:

choices.feedback: ACEi + statins is a common and safe combination.

Correct! Statin + fibrate (especially gemfibrozil) significantly increases rhabdomyolysis risk. Gemfibrozil inhibits statin glucuronidation → elevated statin levels → myotoxicity. Fenofibrate is safer with statins. Other rhabdomyolysis-risk combinations: statin + CYP3A4 inhibitors (azole antifungals, macrolides, grapefruit juice), statin + cyclosporine.
Statin + metformin is common and safe.
Statin + aspirin is standard cardiovascular combination.

questions.stem: CK level typically peaks at what time after muscle injury?

choices.feedback: CK begins rising within hours but doesn't peak that quickly.

Correct! CK peaks at 24-72 hours post-injury, then gradually declines over 3-5 days. If CK is not trending down → ongoing muscle damage (compartment syndrome, continued crush, recurrent seizures). CK half-life is ~36 hours. AKI risk correlates with peak CK level (highest risk >15,000-20,000).
CK should be declining by this point if injury has stopped.
CK takes hours to begin rising and days to peak.

questions.stem: Target urine output in rhabdomyolysis fluid resuscitation?

choices.feedback: Standard maintenance UOP, but rhabdomyolysis requires much more.

Correct! Aggressive diuresis target: 200-300 mL/hr (or ~3 mL/kg/hr) to flush myoglobin through tubules before it can precipitate. May require 1-1.5 L/hr of IV fluid initially. Monitor for fluid overload (pulmonary edema) especially in patients with cardiac or renal compromise.
Excessive — risks fluid overload without additional benefit.

choices.text: No specific target — just maintain hydration
choices.feedback: A specific high UOP target is critical to prevent AKI.

questions.stem: Compartment syndrome as both a CAUSE and COMPLICATION of rhabdomyolysis:

choices.text: This is incorrect — they are unrelated
choices.feedback: They are closely interconnected.

choices.text: Compartment syndrome causes muscle ischemia → rhabdo. Rhabdo causes muscle swelling → compartment syndrome
choices.feedback: Correct! Bidirectional relationship: 1) Compartment syndrome → muscle ischemia → necrosis → rhabdomyolysis. 2) Rhabdomyolysis → massive muscle edema (especially with aggressive IV fluids) → increased compartment pressure → compartment syndrome. Monitor compartment pressures. Fasciotomy if pressure >30 mmHg or within 30 mmHg of diastolic pressure.

choices.text: Compartment syndrome only causes rhabdo, never the reverse
choices.feedback: Both directions of causation occur.

choices.text: Rhabdomyolysis prevents compartment syndrome by softening muscle
choices.feedback: Muscle swelling from rhabdomyolysis actually INCREASES compartment syndrome risk.

questions.stem: Electrical injury and crush injury both cause rhabdomyolysis. What unique risk does electrical injury add?

choices.feedback: Electrical injury goes far deeper than skin.

Correct! Electrical current passes through the body causing deep tissue injury (external appearance underestimates internal damage). Unique risks: cardiac arrhythmias (VF most dangerous), spinal cord injury, internal organ damage. Requires: continuous cardiac monitoring ×24h, serial CK levels, aggressive IV fluids, and monitoring for myoglobinuria. Both low and high voltage can cause rhabdomyolysis.
Electrical injury causes thermal damage, not hypothermia.
Electrical injury causes deep tissue destruction along the current path.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with 4 months of symmetric joint pain and swelling in her MCPs, PIPs, and wrists. Morning stiffness lasts 2 hours. DIP joints are spared. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: OA affects DIPs, 1st CMC, and weight-bearing joints. Morning stiffness is <30 minutes. Symmetric small joint involvement with prolonged stiffness points to RA.
Correct! Symmetric inflammatory polyarthritis of MCPs, PIPs, and wrists with >1 hour morning stiffness is classic RA. DIP sparing distinguishes it from OA and psoriatic arthritis.
PsA more commonly involves DIPs and can be asymmetric. Look for nail pitting, dactylitis, and skin psoriasis.
Gout is typically monoarticular (1st MTP initially) with acute flares, not chronic symmetric polyarthritis.

questions.stem: Which test is MOST SPECIFIC for rheumatoid arthritis?

choices.feedback: RF is ~70-80% sensitive but only ~50% specific. It is positive in many conditions: Sjögren, HCV, endocarditis, sarcoidosis, and even healthy elderly.
Correct! Anti-CCP has 95-98% specificity for RA, making it the most specific serologic test. It can be positive years before clinical onset and predicts erosive, aggressive disease.
ANA is associated with SLE and other connective tissue diseases, not specific for RA.
ESR is a non-specific inflammatory marker elevated in many conditions.

questions.stem: What is the FIRST-LINE DMARD for rheumatoid arthritis?

choices.feedback: HCQ is a mild DMARD used for early/mild RA or in combination. Not the first-line monotherapy for moderate-severe RA.

Correct! Methotrexate is the anchor DMARD for RA. Start within 3 months of diagnosis. Dose 15-25 mg weekly with folic acid supplementation. Monitor LFTs and CBC. It serves as the foundation for combination and biologic strategies.
TNF inhibitors are second-line, used when MTX is inadequate. They are not first-line monotherapy.
Steroids are bridge therapy while DMARDs take effect, not a disease-modifying strategy.

questions.stem: A patient on methotrexate for RA develops pancytopenia. What is the RESCUE agent?

choices.feedback: Daily folic acid prevents MTX side effects but is insufficient to rescue acute toxicity with pancytopenia.

Correct! Leucovorin bypasses the dihydrofolate reductase block caused by MTX, providing reduced folate directly. It is the rescue agent for MTX toxicity (pancytopenia, mucositis). Folic acid supplementation is preventive, not rescue.
B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia but is not related to MTX toxicity rescue.
G-CSF stimulates neutrophil production but does not address the underlying MTX toxicity mechanism.

questions.stem: What must be screened for BEFORE starting a TNF inhibitor for RA?

choices.feedback: While important in general care, HIV screening is not specifically mandated before TNF inhibitors. TB and hepatitis B are the critical screens.

Correct! Latent TB MUST be screened for (PPD or IGRA) before starting TNF inhibitors. TNF-α is critical for granuloma maintenance. TNF inhibition → TB reactivation with disseminated or extrapulmonary disease. If LTBI positive → treat with INH before starting the biologic. Also screen for hepatitis B.
HCV screening is important but TNF inhibitors do not significantly worsen HCV. TB is the critical mandatory screen.
Malaria screening is not part of pre-biologic workup.

questions.stem: Felty syndrome consists of which triad?

choices.feedback: Felty involves splenomegaly (not hepatomegaly) and neutropenia (not thrombocytopenia).
This combination is not Felty syndrome.
Correct! Felty syndrome = RA + splenomegaly + neutropenia. Occurs in longstanding seropositive RA. The neutropenia increases infection risk. Treatment: MTX (first-line DMARD) or rituximab.
This combination does not constitute Felty syndrome.

questions.stem: Which radiographic finding is characteristic of RA but NOT osteoarthritis?

choices.feedback: Osteophytes (bone spurs) are characteristic of OA, not RA.
Subchondral sclerosis is an OA finding from altered stress distribution in degenerating joints.

Correct! Marginal (periarticular) erosions with periarticular osteopenia are hallmarks of RA. OA shows osteophytes, subchondral sclerosis, and subchondral cysts. Joint space narrowing occurs in both but is uniform in RA vs. asymmetric in OA.
Subchondral cysts (geodes) are more characteristic of OA, though they can occur in RA.

questions.stem: A patient with longstanding RA develops upper extremity weakness and difficulty walking. What cervical spine complication must be evaluated?

choices.feedback: While disc herniation can cause myelopathy, the specific RA concern is atlantoaxial instability.

Correct! RA causes erosion of the transverse ligament and odontoid process → C1-C2 instability → potential spinal cord compression. Flexion-extension X-rays show atlantodental interval >3 mm. Critical to assess before intubation/surgery. Can cause myelopathy, quadriplegia, or sudden death.
Bamboo spine with ankylosis is characteristic of AS, not RA.
While osteoporosis is common in RA (steroids), the myelopathic symptoms specifically suggest C1-C2 instability.

questions.stem: Earliest symptom of salicylate toxicity?

choices.feedback: Seizures are a LATE and severe finding.

Correct! Tinnitus (ringing in the ears) is the earliest and most reliable symptom of salicylate toxicity. Occurs at relatively low levels (~30 mg/dL). Followed by nausea, vomiting, hyperventilation. Late: confusion, seizures, coma. Tinnitus in a patient with unexplained acid-base disturbance = check salicylate level.
Hyperthermia occurs later from uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation.
Renal involvement is a late complication.

questions.stem: Classic acid-base disturbance in salicylate poisoning?

choices.feedback: Metabolic alkalosis is not a feature of salicylate toxicity.

choices.text: Mixed respiratory alkalosis + high anion gap metabolic acidosis
choices.feedback: Correct! Pathognomonic pattern: early respiratory alkalosis (direct medullary stimulation → hyperventilation) + late HAGMA (lactic acid, ketoacids from uncoupled oxidative phosphorylation). The mixed pattern on ABG should always prompt checking a salicylate level. Children may present with predominant HAGMA.
Salicylates cause hyperventilation (respiratory alkalosis), not hypoventilation.
Significant salicylate poisoning always disrupts acid-base balance.

questions.stem: Why is sodium bicarbonate the key treatment?

choices.text: It neutralizes aspirin in the stomach
choices.feedback: Bicarb works systemically, not as a GI antacid.

choices.text: Alkalinization traps ionized salicylate in blood (prevents CNS entry) and in urine (enhances excretion)
choices.feedback: Correct! Ion trapping: alkaline pH → salicylate ionized → cannot cross BBB (reduces CNS toxicity) and cannot be reabsorbed in renal tubules (enhances urinary excretion). Goal pH: serum 7.45-7.55, urine 7.5-8.0. Must correct hypokalemia first — kidneys cannot alkalinize urine when K is low.
The respiratory alkalosis is actually protective — don't suppress it.
Bicarb doesn't bind salicylate — it changes pH to trap the ionized form.

questions.stem: Why is intubation DANGEROUS in salicylate poisoning?

choices.feedback: Laryngospasm is not a feature of salicylate toxicity.

choices.text: Loss of hyperventilation → CO2 rises → acidemia → increased brain salicylate → cardiac arrest
choices.feedback: Correct! The patient's hyperventilation is a critical compensatory mechanism maintaining alkaline pH. Intubation + sedation → loss of this compensation → rapid CO2 accumulation → acute acidemia → un-ionized salicylate floods CNS → seizures, cerebral edema, cardiac arrest. Many salicylate deaths occur peri-intubation. If must intubate: pre-bolus bicarb, match high minute ventilation, emergency HD.
While salicylate can cause pulmonary edema, that's not the main intubation risk.
Drug interaction is not the concern.

questions.stem: Hypokalemia must be corrected in salicylate poisoning because:

choices.feedback: While true, this isn't the primary concern in this context.

choices.text: Kidneys cannot alkalinize urine when potassium is low (K+/H+ exchange)
choices.feedback: Correct! In hypokalemia, kidneys reabsorb K+ and excrete H+ to compensate → urine becomes acidic → bicarb therapy fails (can't trap salicylate in urine). Must replete K aggressively for alkalinization to work. Target K >4.0. This is a frequently tested concept.

choices.text: Potassium is needed for activated charcoal to work
choices.feedback: Charcoal mechanism is unrelated to potassium.
Potassium doesn't affect salicylate absorption.

questions.stem: Indications for hemodialysis in salicylate poisoning:

choices.feedback: 30 mg/dL is mild toxicity — bicarb is sufficient.

choices.text: Level >100, renal failure, AMS, pulmonary edema, acidemia (pH <7.2), clinical deterioration
choices.feedback: Correct! HD directly removes salicylate from blood AND corrects acid-base disturbances. Definitive treatment for severe poisoning. Don't delay HD for bicarb to 'work' — if HD criteria met, initiate immediately. Also indicated: failure to respond to aggressive bicarb therapy, need for intubation (relative indication).

choices.text: Only after 24 hours of bicarb therapy
choices.feedback: Don't wait — HD should be initiated promptly when indicated.
HD is indicated for severe toxicity regardless of acute or chronic ingestion.

questions.stem: Multi-dose activated charcoal is uniquely useful in salicylate poisoning because:

choices.text: Salicylates are not absorbed by charcoal on first dose
choices.feedback: Charcoal does absorb salicylates on first dose.

choices.text: Salicylates form bezoars and have delayed/prolonged absorption, making later charcoal doses effective
choices.feedback: Correct! Salicylates (especially enteric-coated or large ingestions) form concretions/bezoars in the stomach → continued absorption over hours. Multi-dose charcoal (MDAC) can adsorb salicylate still in the GI tract AND may enhance elimination through 'gut dialysis' (salicylate excreted into the GI lumen is re-adsorbed by charcoal). Whole bowel irrigation also considered for large ingestions.

choices.text: Charcoal prevents vomiting in salicylate poisoning
choices.feedback: Charcoal doesn't prevent vomiting — salicylates commonly cause nausea.
MDAC use is based on clinical pharmacology, not regulation.

questions.stem: Chronic salicylate toxicity compared to acute:

choices.feedback: Chronic toxicity can be more dangerous.

choices.text: Often more dangerous — more CNS penetration, diagnosed later, higher mortality
choices.feedback: Correct! Chronic toxicity is often more severe because: (1) salicylate accumulates gradually → more time for CNS penetration, (2) patients are often elderly on therapeutic doses → diagnosis delayed (misattributed to other conditions), (3) presents with AMS/confusion without classic acute findings, (4) higher mortality than acute. Always check salicylate levels in elderly patients with unexplained altered mental status + acid-base disturbance.

choices.text: Only occurs with topical salicylate products
choices.feedback: Chronic toxicity occurs with oral aspirin and other salicylate formulations.
Chronic toxicity still causes acid-base disturbance, often HAGMA predominant.

questions.stem: Which type of hallucination is MOST characteristic of schizophrenia?

choices.feedback: Visual hallucinations are more suggestive of organic/medical causes (delirium, Lewy body dementia, substance intoxication). Always think medical etiology first with visual hallucinations.

Correct! Auditory hallucinations (especially voices commenting on behavior or two voices conversing) are the most common and characteristic hallucinations in schizophrenia. Present in ~70% of patients. Command auditory hallucinations require safety assessment.
Tactile hallucinations (formication — bugs crawling on skin) are most associated with substance use (cocaine, methamphetamine, alcohol withdrawal) and delirium.
Olfactory hallucinations suggest temporal lobe epilepsy or brain tumor (uncinate fits), not typical schizophrenia.

questions.stem: What is the ONLY antipsychotic approved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia?

choices.feedback: Haloperidol is a first-generation antipsychotic with no special efficacy in treatment resistance.

Correct! Clozapine is the most effective antipsychotic and the only one approved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia (defined as failure of ≥2 adequate antipsychotic trials). Also approved for reducing suicide risk in schizophrenia. Requires mandatory ANC monitoring due to risk of agranulocytosis (~1%). Other risks: metabolic syndrome, seizures, myocarditis.
Aripiprazole is a D2 partial agonist with good tolerability but is not specifically indicated for treatment-resistant schizophrenia.
Olanzapine is effective but not approved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia and has significant metabolic side effects.

questions.stem: A patient on haloperidol develops high fever, lead-pipe rigidity, altered consciousness, and CK of 15,000 U/L. What is the diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Serotonin syndrome causes clonus, hyperreflexia, and myoclonus — NOT lead-pipe rigidity. It results from serotonergic drugs, not antipsychotics.

Correct! NMS tetrad: Fever + Rigidity (lead-pipe) + Autonomic instability + Mental status changes, with massively elevated CK. Caused by dopamine D2 blockade from antipsychotics (higher risk with high-potency typical agents like haloperidol). Treatment: STOP the antipsychotic, dantrolene + bromocriptine + supportive ICU care.
Malignant hyperthermia occurs with volatile anesthetics + succinylcholine, not antipsychotics. Though treatment with dantrolene is similar.

Catatonia can cause rigidity and altered mental status but typically without fever to this degree or massively elevated CK. NMS and catatonia can overlap but the context (antipsychotic use + CK elevation) points to NMS.

questions.stem: The negative symptoms of schizophrenia (5 A's) include all EXCEPT:

choices.feedback: Alogia (poverty of speech) is a core negative symptom.

Correct! Auditory hallucinations are a POSITIVE symptom, not negative. The 5 A's of negative symptoms: Affective flattening (flat affect), Alogia (poverty of speech), Avolition (lack of motivation), Anhedonia (inability to feel pleasure), Asociality (social withdrawal). Negative symptoms are harder to treat and carry worse prognosis than positive symptoms.
Anhedonia is a core negative symptom.
Avolition is a core negative symptom.

questions.stem: A 23-year-old woman presents with acute psychosis. Which medical condition must be specifically excluded, especially if a teratoma is found?

choices.feedback: SLE can cause neuropsychiatric symptoms but is not specifically associated with teratomas.

Correct! Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis classically affects young women, often associated with ovarian teratoma. Presents with psychiatric symptoms (psychosis, agitation), seizures, movement disorders, autonomic instability, and decreased consciousness. ALWAYS check for ovarian teratoma (pelvic ultrasound) and anti-NMDA antibodies in young women with new-onset psychosis. Treatment: tumor removal + immunotherapy.
Pheochromocytoma causes hypertensive crises and anxiety, not psychosis with teratoma.
Hashimoto encephalopathy (steroid-responsive encephalopathy) can cause psychiatric symptoms but is not associated with teratomas.

questions.stem: Which atypical antipsychotic has the WORST metabolic side effect profile?

choices.feedback: Aripiprazole (D2 partial agonist) has the LEAST metabolic effects among atypicals.

Correct! Olanzapine causes the most weight gain and metabolic syndrome (diabetes, dyslipidemia) among atypical antipsychotics. Patients can gain 10+ kg in the first year. Requires baseline and periodic monitoring of weight, BMI, fasting glucose, lipid panel, and waist circumference. Despite this, it is highly effective.
Lurasidone has relatively favorable metabolic effects compared to other atypicals.
Ziprasidone is weight-neutral but carries QTc prolongation risk.

questions.stem: What is the minimum total duration of illness required to diagnose schizophrenia?

choices.feedback: 1 month of active symptoms is required, but the TOTAL illness duration must be longer.

Correct! Schizophrenia requires ≥6 months total disturbance, including at least 1 month of active-phase symptoms (≥2 of: delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech, disorganized behavior, negative symptoms).
