choices.feedback: Isoniazid causes peripheral neuropathy and hepatotoxicity, not optic neuritis.
Rifampin causes hepatotoxicity and drug interactions, not optic changes.
Pyrazinamide causes hyperuricemia and hepatotoxicity, not visual changes.

Correct! Ethambutol causes dose-dependent optic neuritis → red-green color blindness, decreased visual acuity. Test baseline visual acuity and monitor monthly. Typically reversible if caught early and drug is stopped.

questions.stem: Active pulmonary TB is treated with RIPE for the initial 2 months, then what continuation phase?

choices.feedback: Correct! Standard active TB: RIPE × 2 months (intensive phase), then Rifampin + Isoniazid (RI) × 4 months (continuation phase) = 6 months total. Pyrazinamide and ethambutol are stopped after the initial phase.

choices.text: All four drugs for 4 more months
choices.feedback: All four drugs for 6 months total is unnecessary and increases toxicity risk.
Isoniazid alone for 9 months is a LTBI regimen, not active TB treatment.
Rifampin alone would risk resistance development.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old sexually active woman presents with dysuria and frequency. UA shows pyuria and positive nitrites. What is the most appropriate empiric treatment?

choices.feedback: Fluoroquinolones should be reserved for complicated UTI/pyelonephritis. Not first-line for uncomplicated cystitis.

Correct! Nitrofurantoin 5 days is first-line for uncomplicated cystitis. It concentrates in the urine, has good activity against common uropathogens, and preserves fluoroquinolones for more serious infections.
Amoxicillin has high resistance rates against E. coli and is not recommended empirically for UTI.
Ceftriaxone IM is for pyelonephritis or complicated UTI, not simple cystitis.

questions.stem: A patient with recurrent UTIs has a CT showing a large stone filling the renal pelvis (staghorn calculus). Urine pH is 8.0. What organism is most likely responsible?

choices.feedback: E. coli is the most common UTI cause but typically produces acidic urine and calcium oxalate stones, not staghorn calculi.
S. saprophyticus causes cystitis in young women but doesn't produce urease or staghorn stones.
Correct! Proteus mirabilis produces urease → splits urea to ammonia → alkaline urine (pH >7.5) → struvite (magnesium ammonium phosphate) staghorn calculi. Classic boards association.
Enterococcus causes UTI but doesn't produce urease or struvite stones.

questions.stem: A pregnant woman at her first prenatal visit has a urine culture growing 100,000 CFU/mL E. coli but no symptoms. What should you do?

choices.feedback: Delaying treatment risks progression to pyelonephritis, which can cause preterm labor.

choices.text: No treatment needed — asymptomatic bacteriuria is benign
choices.feedback: Asymptomatic bacteriuria IS benign in most populations, but pregnancy is the exception — 40% progress to pyelonephritis if untreated.

Correct! Treat ALL bacteriuria in pregnancy (even asymptomatic). Untreated ASB → 40% risk of pyelonephritis → preterm labor, low birth weight. Use pregnancy-safe antibiotics (nitrofurantoin, cephalexin, amoxicillin-clavulanate). Avoid fluoroquinolones and TMP-SMX (especially 1st trimester).
Cranberry supplements are not adequate treatment for bacteriuria in pregnancy.

questions.stem: Which UTI pathogen is characteristically novobiocin-resistant and most common in sexually active young women after E. coli?

choices.feedback: Klebsiella is common in diabetics and catheterized patients, not specifically young sexually active women.
Proteus is associated with staghorn calculi and alkaline urine, not as the second most common in young women.
Correct! S. saprophyticus = 2nd most common cause of UTI in young sexually active women. Novobiocin-resistant (vs S. epidermidis which is novobiocin-sensitive). Coagulase-negative.
Pseudomonas is associated with complicated/catheter-related UTIs, not uncomplicated UTI in young women.

questions.stem: Crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at RUSB radiating to carotids with delayed carotid upstroke. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: MR is holosystolic at apex radiating to axilla.

Correct! Classic AS: crescendo-decrescendo (diamond-shaped) systolic murmur, best at RUSB, radiating to carotids. Pulsus parvus et tardus (weak, delayed carotid upstroke). Murmur decreases with Valsalva/standing (decreased preload).
AR is early diastolic decrescendo at LUSB.
MVP = mid-systolic click ± late systolic murmur at apex.

questions.stem: Which murmurs get LOUDER with Valsalva?

choices.feedback: Both decrease with Valsalva (decreased preload → less flow across valve).

Correct! Valsalva decreases preload → smaller LV cavity → MORE obstruction in HCM (louder) and EARLIER click in MVP (more prolapse). Everything else gets softer. This is the most tested dynamic maneuver concept on boards.
Both decrease with Valsalva.
Only HCM and MVP increase; others decrease.

questions.stem: Mitral stenosis is almost always caused by:

choices.feedback: Degenerative calcification causes aortic stenosis, not MS.

Correct! Rheumatic heart disease (Group A Strep → rheumatic fever → autoimmune valve damage) is the cause of MS in ~99% of cases worldwide. Leads to valve thickening, commissural fusion, fish-mouth orifice. Complications: LA enlargement → A-fib → LA thrombus → stroke.
Endocarditis typically causes regurgitation (valve destruction), not stenosis.
Bicuspid valve causes aortic stenosis, not mitral stenosis.

questions.stem: Classic symptom triad of aortic stenosis (SAD)?

choices.feedback: Close but the 'D' = dyspnea on exertion, and the classic triad uses syncope rather than dizziness.

Correct! SAD: Syncope (exertional — fixed output can't meet demand), Angina (increased O2 demand from LVH), Dyspnea (HF as late manifestation). Once any symptom develops, prognosis is poor without valve replacement. Dyspnea/HF onset = <1 year survival untreated.
These can be consequences but are not the classic symptom triad.
These suggest right heart failure, not AS.

questions.stem: Right-sided murmurs increase with inspiration because:

choices.text: Inspiration increases systemic venous return to the right heart
choices.feedback: Close — this is the mechanism, but the answer should capture the full concept.

choices.text: Negative intrathoracic pressure increases venous return → more flow across right-sided valves
choices.feedback: Correct! Inspiration creates negative intrathoracic pressure → increases venous return to RA/RV → more flow across tricuspid/pulmonic valves → louder murmur. This is Carvallo sign (tricuspid regurgitation louder with inspiration). Left-sided murmurs typically decrease with inspiration.
Inspiration does not significantly compress the left atrium.

choices.text: The diaphragm pushes the heart closer to the chest wall
choices.feedback: This is not the mechanism for right-sided murmur augmentation.

questions.stem: When is endocarditis prophylaxis indicated before dental procedures?

choices.feedback: Most murmurs do NOT require prophylaxis. Only specific high-risk conditions.

choices.text: Prosthetic valve, prior endocarditis, certain congenital defects
choices.feedback: Correct! High-risk only: prosthetic valve, prior IE, unrepaired cyanotic CHD, repaired CHD with residual defects, cardiac transplant with valvulopathy. Give amoxicillin 2g PO 1h before dental procedures involving gingival manipulation. NOT required before GI/GU procedures.

choices.text: All patients with mitral valve prolapse
choices.feedback: MVP alone is NOT an indication for prophylaxis (changed in 2007 AHA guidelines).

choices.text: Any patient undergoing any surgical procedure
choices.feedback: Prophylaxis is only for specific high-risk cardiac conditions AND only before specific dental procedures.

questions.stem: Wide pulse pressure, bounding pulses, and early diastolic murmur at LUSB. Diagnosis?

choices.feedback: AS has a narrow pulse pressure and delayed upstroke, not wide pulse pressure.

Correct! AR: wide pulse pressure (elevated systolic from increased stroke volume, low diastolic from regurgitant flow back into LV). Named signs: bounding 'water hammer' pulses (Corrigan), head bobbing (de Musset), nail bed pulsations (Quincke). Blowing early diastolic decrescendo murmur at LUSB.
MS has a low-pitched diastolic rumble at apex with opening snap.
PDA causes a continuous 'machinery' murmur, not an isolated diastolic murmur.

questions.stem: For severe symptomatic aortic stenosis, TAVR is preferred over SAVR in which patients?

choices.feedback: SAVR remains standard for young, low-risk patients.

choices.text: Patients at high/prohibitive surgical risk or age ≥65
choices.feedback: Correct! TAVR (transcatheter aortic valve replacement) is preferred for high/prohibitive surgical risk patients and increasingly for those ≥65 years (PARTNER 3, Evolut Low Risk trials showed non-inferiority to SAVR). For patients <65 or with bicuspid valves, SAVR is still often preferred due to long-term durability data. Shared decision-making is key.
TAVR is a primary treatment option, not just for refusals.

choices.text: Only patients with concurrent coronary disease
choices.feedback: Concurrent CABG need may favor SAVR, but this is not the defining criterion for TAVR.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with new-onset severe headache, jaw claudication, and sudden vision loss in the right eye. ESR is 95 mm/hr. What should you do FIRST?

choices.text: Order a temporal artery biopsy before treatment
choices.feedback: NEVER delay treatment for biopsy in suspected GCA. Vision loss can become bilateral and permanent. Steroids do not significantly alter biopsy results within 2 weeks.

Correct! GCA with vision loss is an emergency. Start prednisone 60-80 mg/day (or IV methylprednisolone if acute vision loss) IMMEDIATELY. Then arrange biopsy within 2 weeks. Delay = risk of bilateral blindness from anterior ischemic optic neuropathy.
This is not a stroke protocol. GCA requires high-dose steroids, not antiplatelet therapy alone.

choices.text: Obtain CT angiography of the head
choices.feedback: While imaging can support GCA diagnosis, the immediate priority is starting steroids to prevent further vision loss.

questions.stem: Which ANCA pattern and target antigen is associated with GPA (Wegener granulomatosis)?

choices.feedback: p-ANCA/MPO is associated with MPA and EGPA (Churg-Strauss), not GPA.

Correct! GPA is associated with c-ANCA (cytoplasmic pattern) targeting proteinase 3 (PR3). This is >90% specific for GPA. The classic triad is upper airway (sinusitis, saddle nose) + lungs (cavitary lesions) + kidneys (crescentic GN).
c-ANCA targets PR3, not MPO. Anti-MPO produces a perinuclear (p-ANCA) pattern.
PR3 produces a cytoplasmic (c-ANCA) pattern, not perinuclear.

questions.stem: Which vasculitis spares the lungs and is associated with hepatitis B?

choices.feedback: GPA specifically involves the lungs (cavitary nodules) and is not associated with HBV.

Correct! PAN is a medium-vessel vasculitis that classically spares the lungs (no pulmonary involvement) and has a strong hepatitis B association (~30% of cases). Diagnosis: mesenteric/renal angiography showing microaneurysms. ANCA-negative.
MPA involves the lungs (pulmonary-renal syndrome) and is ANCA-positive (p-ANCA/MPO).
IgA vasculitis is immune complex-mediated and primarily affects children. Not associated with HBV.

questions.stem: A 4-year-old presents with fever for 6 days, bilateral conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, cervical lymphadenopathy, and desquamating rash. What is the most serious complication to screen for?

choices.feedback: While aseptic meningitis can occur in Kawasaki disease, it is not the major life-threatening complication.

Correct! Kawasaki disease causes coronary artery aneurysms in 25% of untreated cases (reduced to <5% with IVIG). Echocardiography is essential at diagnosis, 2 weeks, and 6-8 weeks. Giant aneurysms (>8 mm) carry risk of MI and sudden death.
Pulmonary fibrosis is not a complication of Kawasaki disease.
Renal involvement is uncommon in Kawasaki disease, unlike IgA vasculitis or ANCA-associated vasculitis.

questions.stem: A child presents with palpable purpura on the buttocks and legs, colicky abdominal pain, arthritis, and hematuria. What is the most likely diagnosis?

choices.feedback: Kawasaki presents with high fever, conjunctivitis, strawberry tongue, and coronary involvement — not purpura and nephritis.

Correct! Classic tetrad: palpable purpura (non-thrombocytopenic, buttocks/legs), arthritis/arthralgias, abdominal pain (intussusception risk), and renal involvement (IgA nephropathy). Most common vasculitis in children. Usually self-limited. Monitor urine for persistent nephropathy.
Meningococcemia causes purpura fulminans with DIC and septic shock, a much more acutely ill presentation.

TTP causes microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia + neurological symptoms + renal dysfunction. Purpura in TTP is due to thrombocytopenia, unlike the immune-mediated purpura in IgA vasculitis.

questions.stem: Which vasculitis is characterized by asthma, eosinophilia, and cardiac involvement?

choices.feedback: GPA involves sinuses, lungs, and kidneys but is NOT associated with asthma or eosinophilia.
MPA causes pulmonary-renal syndrome without asthma or eosinophilia.

Correct! EGPA triad: late-onset asthma + peripheral eosinophilia (often >10%) + vasculitis. Cardiac involvement (myocarditis, cardiomyopathy) is the leading cause of death. p-ANCA/MPO positive in ~40%. Treat with steroids ± mepolizumab (anti-IL-5).
Takayasu affects large vessels (aorta and branches) in young women. No association with asthma or eosinophilia.

questions.stem: Takayasu arteritis most commonly affects which patient population?

choices.feedback: GCA (not Takayasu) affects patients over 50. Takayasu affects younger patients.

Correct! Takayasu arteritis predominantly affects women under 40 (female:male ratio 9:1). It involves the aorta and its major branches → 'pulseless disease' with limb claudication, blood pressure discrepancies between arms (>10 mmHg), and vascular bruits. Diagnosis: MRA or CTA showing vessel wall thickening and stenosis.
Kawasaki disease (not Takayasu) primarily affects children under 5.
Takayasu has a strong female predominance in young adults.

questions.stem: In ANCA-associated vasculitis, what type of glomerulonephritis is seen on kidney biopsy?

choices.feedback: Membranous GN shows subepithelial deposits on IF and is associated with SLE Class V, PLA2R antibodies, or malignancy.
IgA nephropathy shows mesangial IgA deposits and is associated with IgA vasculitis, not ANCA-associated vasculitis.

Correct! ANCA-associated vasculitis causes pauci-immune crescentic (rapidly progressive) GN — meaning few or NO immune deposits on immunofluorescence. The crescents represent epithelial cell proliferation in Bowman capsule. This distinguishes it from anti-GBM disease (linear IgG) and lupus nephritis (full house pattern).
MCD causes nephrotic syndrome with normal light microscopy and foot process effacement on EM. Not associated with vasculitis.

questions.stem: PE primarily kills by:

choices.feedback: V/Q mismatch occurs but the lethal mechanism is hemodynamic.

Correct! PE → increased pulmonary vascular resistance → acute RV pressure overload → RV dilation → interventricular septum shifts left → decreased LV filling → cardiogenic shock. This is why massive PE requires emergent clot removal/lysis, not just oxygen.
The left ventricle fails secondarily from decreased filling, not primary infarction.
Hemorrhage is rare; the primary issue is obstruction.

questions.stem: Low clinical probability (Wells <2). Next step?

choices.feedback: Low probability should first undergo D-dimer to avoid unnecessary CT radiation/contrast.

Correct! Low Wells → D-dimer. Negative D-dimer effectively excludes PE (high sensitivity ~95%). Positive D-dimer → CTPA. D-dimer is NOT useful in high-probability patients (too many false positives; go directly to CTPA).
Empiric treatment for low-probability PE without confirmatory testing is inappropriate.
V/Q is an alternative to CTPA, not a screening test for low-probability PE.

questions.stem: Massive PE with SBP <90. Treatment?

choices.feedback: Heparin alone is insufficient for massive PE with hemodynamic compromise.

Correct! Massive PE (hemodynamic instability): systemic tPA is first-line. Reduces mortality despite bleeding risk. If thrombolytics contraindicated: catheter-directed therapy or surgical embolectomy. Also: IV fluids (cautious — excess may worsen RV dilation), vasopressors (norepinephrine), avoid intubation if possible (positive pressure worsens RV).
IVC filters prevent future embolization but don't treat current massive PE.
Warfarin takes days to work — inappropriate for acute massive PE.

questions.stem: Factor V Leiden is:

choices.feedback: Factor V Leiden is inherited, not acquired.

Correct! Factor V Leiden mutation: resistance to activated protein C → increased VTE risk. Heterozygous: 5-7x risk. Homozygous: 25-50x risk. Most common in Caucasians (5% carrier rate). Testing: activated protein C resistance assay, confirmed by genetic testing.
FVL affects the coagulation cascade, not platelet function.
FVL primarily increases VENOUS thromboembolism risk.

questions.stem: DOAC advantage over warfarin for PE treatment?

choices.feedback: DOACs are typically more expensive than warfarin.

choices.text: No bridging needed, no INR monitoring, fewer drug interactions
choices.feedback: Correct! Apixaban/rivaroxaban: can start immediately without heparin bridging. No routine monitoring needed (vs INR for warfarin). Fewer food/drug interactions. Exceptions: use heparin → warfarin for antiphospholipid syndrome (DOACs inferior — TRAPS trial), severe CKD, mechanical valves, pregnancy (LMWH only).

choices.text: They can be used in pregnancy
choices.feedback: DOACs are contraindicated in pregnancy. Use LMWH.
DOACs still carry bleeding risk, just easier to manage than warfarin.

questions.stem: Duration of anticoagulation for UNPROVOKED PE?

choices.feedback: Far too short for any PE.

Correct! Unprovoked (idiopathic) PE: recommended indefinite anticoagulation (reassess annually). Recurrence rate without anticoagulation ~10%/year. Provoked PE (surgery, immobility, OCP): 3 months. Cancer-associated: anticoagulate while cancer is active (LMWH or DOAC).
Duration depends on provoked vs unprovoked status.
Symptom resolution doesn't determine anticoagulation duration.

questions.stem: EKG finding most commonly associated with PE?

choices.feedback: That's anterior STEMI, not PE.

Correct! Sinus tachycardia is the most common EKG finding in PE. Classic findings (less common): S1Q3T3 (S wave in I, Q wave and inverted T in III), right heart strain pattern, right axis deviation, RBBB. A normal EKG does NOT exclude PE.
A-fib can occur with PE but is not the most common finding.
Heart block is not typically associated with PE.

questions.stem: ABG pattern in acute PE?

choices.feedback: PE causes respiratory alkalosis, not metabolic acidosis (unless shock develops).

choices.text: Respiratory alkalosis with elevated A-a gradient
choices.feedback: Correct! Hyperventilation from dyspnea → low PaCO2 → respiratory alkalosis. Elevated A-a gradient (normal <15 in young adults) reflects V/Q mismatch. However, up to 20% of PEs have normal A-a gradient — does NOT exclude PE.
Respiratory acidosis suggests hypoventilation (COPD, neuromuscular), not PE.
Most PEs show ABG abnormalities, though a normal ABG can occur.

questions.stem: What is the MOST IMPORTANT cell in wound healing?

choices.feedback: Neutrophils arrive first (24-48h) and clear debris/bacteria, but are not the most important coordinator.

Correct! Macrophages are the MOST IMPORTANT cell in wound healing. They coordinate the transition from inflammation to proliferation, release growth factors (PDGF, TGF-β, VEGF, FGF), phagocytose debris, and orchestrate all subsequent phases. Wound healing can proceed without neutrophils but NOT without macrophages.
Fibroblasts produce collagen (proliferative phase) but macrophages are more critical as overall coordinators.
Platelets initiate hemostasis but are not the key coordinator of wound healing.

questions.stem: Maximum tensile strength a healed wound reaches?

choices.feedback: Healed wounds NEVER reach full original strength.

Correct! Maximum tensile strength = 80% of unwounded tissue. Type III collagen (immature) is replaced by Type I (mature), and cross-linking increases strength over months. This is why surgical sites and healed wounds remain weaker than native tissue permanently.
50% is reached by about 6 weeks but continues to improve to ~80%.
Wounds do not reach 95%. 80% is the maximum.

questions.stem: Vitamin C deficiency impairs wound healing by affecting:

choices.feedback: DNA synthesis is primarily zinc-dependent, not vitamin C-dependent.

Correct! Vitamin C is an essential cofactor for prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase, enzymes that hydroxylate proline and lysine residues in collagen. Without hydroxylation, collagen cannot form stable triple helices. Deficiency = scurvy: wound breakdown, bleeding gums, poor healing.
Platelet function is not directly vitamin C-dependent.
Epithelialization is more dependent on vitamin A than vitamin C.

questions.stem: Which vitamin REVERSES steroid-impaired wound healing?

choices.feedback: Vitamin C is critical for collagen synthesis but does not specifically reverse steroid effects.

Correct! Vitamin A counteracts steroid-induced wound healing impairment by increasing macrophage activity, inflammation, and collagen synthesis. HIGH-YIELD pearl. Important: Vitamin A does NOT reverse impairment from diabetes, radiation, or other causes — only steroids.
Vitamin D is important for bone metabolism but does not reverse steroid wound impairment.
Vitamin K is essential for clotting factors but not for reversing steroid wound impairment.

questions.stem: What distinguishes a keloid from a hypertrophic scar?

choices.text: Keloids are painful; hypertrophic scars are not
choices.feedback: Both can be painful or pruritic.

choices.text: Keloids extend BEYOND wound borders; hypertrophic scars stay WITHIN
choices.feedback: Correct! Keloid = extends beyond original wound margins, does not regress, recurs after excision. Hypertrophic = raised but contained within wound borders, often improves over time. Keloids more common in dark-skinned individuals, earlobes, sternum, deltoid.
Both may require treatment depending on symptoms and cosmetic concerns.

choices.text: Hypertrophic scars occur only on the face
choices.feedback: Hypertrophic scars can occur anywhere, not limited to the face.

questions.stem: Wound left open to heal by granulation and contraction is:

choices.feedback: Primary = edges approximated and closed (surgical incision with sutures).

Correct! Secondary intention: wound left open, heals from bottom up with granulation tissue formation and contraction by myofibroblasts. Examples: abscess cavity after I&D, pressure ulcers, burns. Results in larger scar.
Tertiary (delayed primary) = wound left open initially then closed 3-5 days later after contamination risk passes.
There is no quaternary intention in wound healing classification.

questions.stem: Pre-albumin is preferred over albumin for assessing nutritional status because:

choices.feedback: Albumin is actually more widely available. Pre-albumin's advantage is different.

choices.text: It has a shorter half-life (2 days vs 20 days), reflecting acute nutritional changes
choices.feedback: Correct! Pre-albumin (transthyretin) half-life = 2-3 days vs albumin half-life = 20 days. Pre-albumin reflects recent nutritional changes, making it a better marker for acute nutritional status and response to nutritional support. Albumin reflects chronic status and is affected by many non-nutritional factors (inflammation, liver disease).
Pre-albumin does not measure collagen synthesis. It is a hepatic protein.
While malnutrition increases infection risk, pre-albumin specifically reflects acute nutritional status.

questions.stem: The #1 modifiable risk factor for impaired wound healing?

choices.feedback: Diabetes significantly impairs healing but is a chronic condition that is managed, not eliminated.

Correct! Smoking is the #1 modifiable risk factor. Nicotine causes vasoconstriction → tissue hypoxia, carbon monoxide reduces oxygen-carrying capacity, and cigarette chemicals impair inflammatory cell function. Smoking cessation at least 4-8 weeks before elective surgery significantly improves wound healing outcomes.
Age is a risk factor but is not modifiable.
Malnutrition is modifiable and important, but smoking has the most evidence as a modifiable risk factor.
